questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Plane angle and solid angle have:
questions.explanation: Radians and Steradians are measured in either $\pi$ (pi) or degrees. Because $\pi$ and degrees are pure numbers. Planes and solid angles have no dimensions. Plane and solid angles both have units but no dimensions.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: The unit of thermal conductivity is:
questions.explanation: Thermal conductivity, $$ \begin{aligned}&K=\frac{L}{A\Delta T}\frac{dQ}{dt}\\ &\quad=\frac{Metre}{(Metre)^{2}\times Kelvin}\times watt\\ &\quad=Wm^{-1}K^{-1}\\ \end{aligned} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Unit of Stefan's constant is:
questions.explanation: The value of the Stefan-Boltzmann constant is approximately $ 5.67 \times 10^{-8} $ watt per meter square per kelvin to the fourth of absolute temperature. (W.m $ ^{-2} $K $ ^{-4} $).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: The area of a rectangular field (in m²) of length 55.3 m and breadth 25 m after rounding off the value for correct significant digits is:
questions.explanation: Given that, length = 55.3 m breadth = 25 m As we know, resultant should have 2 significant digits. Area = length × breadth = 55.3 × 25 = 1382.5 m² = 13.8 × 10² m² ≈ 14 × 10² m²

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Taking into account of the significant figures, what is the value of 9.99 m - 0.0099 m?
questions.explanation: Value = 9.99 m - 0.0099 m = 9.9801 m Taking significant figure, both the values have two significant figures after decimal point so current answer is 9.98 m.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: The dimensions [MLT^{-2}A^{-2}] belong to the:
questions.explanation: Magnetic flux, $ \phi = B \cdot A $ As, F = ILB $$ [\mathbf{B}]={\frac{\mathbf{F}}{\mathbf{I L}}}={\frac{\mathbf{M L T}^{-2}}{\mathbf{A L}}}=[\mathbf{M T}^{-2}\mathbf{A}^{-1}] $$ From (i) $$ \phi=[\mathrm{M T}^{-2}\mathrm{A}^{-1}]\mathrm{L}^{2} $$ $$ [\phi]=[\mathrm{M L^{2}T^{-2}A^{-1}}] $$ Self inductance, $ \phi = LI $ $$ \mathrm{L}=\frac{\phi}{\mathrm{I}}=\frac{\mathrm{ML}^{2}\mathrm{T}^{-2}\mathrm{A}^{-1}}{\mathrm{A}} $$ $$ [{\mathrm L}]={\mathrm M}{\mathrm L}^{2}{\mathrm T}^{-2}{\mathrm A}^{-2} $$ Magnetic permeability, $ \mu = \frac{B \cdot 2\pi r}{I} $ $$ [\mu]=\frac{[\mathrm{MT}^{-2}\mathrm{A}^{-1}\mathrm{L}]}{[\mathrm{A}]}=\mathrm{ML}^{1}\mathrm{T}^{-2}\mathrm{A}^{-2} $$ Electric permittivity, $ \varepsilon_{0} = \frac{q_{1}q_{2}}{4\pi r^{2}F} $ $$ [\varepsilon_{0}]=[\mathrm{M L}^{-3}\mathrm{T}^{4}\mathrm{A}^{2}] $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Which of the following is a dimensional constant?
questions.optionD: Gravitational constant The topic number and names correspond to the exact topics of the NCERT Class XI textbook, so it's easy for you to find in NCERT.
questions.explanation: A quantity which has dimensions and also has a constant value is called dimensional constant. Here, gravitational constant (G) is a dimensional constant.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: The quantities which have the same dimensions as those of solid angle are:
questions.explanation: Dimensional formula of stress = ML^{-1} T^{-2} Dimensional formula of angle = Dimensionless Dimensional formula of strain = Dimensionless Dimensional formula of angular speed = T^{-1} Dimensional formula of arc = L So, strain and angle are dimensionless like solid angle.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Match List - I with List - II. Choose the correct answer from the options given below:

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: If E and G respectively denote energy and gravitational constant, then $ \frac{E}{G} $ has the dimensions of:
questions.explanation: Given. Energy = E Gravitational constant = G We know that. $$ \begin{aligned}[\mathrm{E}]\mathbf{F}&=\mathbf{F.S}\\&=\operatorname{kg}\ \mathbf{m}\mathbf{s}^{-2}\ \mathbf{m}\\&=\operatorname{kg}\ \mathbf{m}^{2}\ \mathbf{s}^{-2}\\&=[\mathbf{M}\mathbf{L}^{2}\mathbf{T}^{-2}]\end{aligned} $$ $$ \begin{array}{l} \text{[G]} =\displaystyle {\frac{F r^{2}}{m^{2}}} \\ \qquad = \displaystyle {\left[ \frac{k \text{g} \text{m s}^{-2} \text{m}^{2}}{k \text{g}^{2}} \right] } \\ \qquad = \displaystyle {\frac{m^{3} \text{s}^{-2}}{k \text{g}}} \\ \qquad = \displaystyle {[\text{M}^{-1} \text{L}^{3} \text{T}^{-2}]} \end{array} $$ $$ \begin{aligned}\frac{[E]}{[G]}=&\left[\frac{ML^{2}T^{-2}}{M^{-1}L^{3}T^{-2}}\right]\\=&[M^{1+1}L^{2-3}T^{-2+2}]\\=&[M^{2}L^{-1}T^{0}]\end{aligned} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: If force [F], acceleration [A] and time [T] are chosen as the fundamental physical quantities. Find the dimensions of energy:
questions.explanation: We know, Given, [E] = [Fα Aβ Tγ] [E] = FS = kg m² s⁻² = [ML²T⁻²] [F] = kg m s⁻² = [MLT⁻²] [A] = ms⁻² = [M⁰LT⁻²] [T] = s = [M⁰L⁰T¹] [ML²T⁻²] = [MLT⁻²]α [LT⁻²]β B[T⁻²] [M¹L²T⁻²] = [Mα Lα⁺β T⁻²α⁻²β⁺γ] On comparing the powers, α = 1 α + β = 2 -2α - 2β + γ = -2 β = 2 - α = 2 - 1 = 1 -2(1) - 2(1) + γ = -2 γ = 2 E = [FAT²]

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Dimensional formula of stress is:

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: If energy (E), velocity (v) and time (T) are chosen as the fundamental quantities, the dimensional formula of surface tension will be:
questions.explanation: Dimension of energy, $ E = [ML^2T^{-2}] $ Dimension of velocity, $ v = [LT^{-1}] $ Dimension of time, $ t = [T] $ From the question, Surface tension, $ S = E a v b T^c $ $$ [S] = [MT^{-2}] $ a, b, c are constants. Equating dimensions on both sides, $$ [\mathbf{M}\mathbf{L}^{0}\mathbf{T}^{-2}]=[\mathbf{M}\mathbf{L}^{2}\mathbf{T}^{-2}]^{a}\,[\mathbf{L}\mathbf{T}^{-1}]^{b}\,[\mathbf{T}]^{c} $$ $$ [\mathbf{M}\mathbf{L}^{0}\mathbf{T}^{-2}]=[\mathbf{M}^{a}\mathbf{L}^{2a+b}\mathbf{T}^{-2a-b+c}] $$ On comparing both sides, $$ a=1,2a+b=0\text{and}-2a-b+c=-2 $$ On solving these equations, we get, $$ a=1,b=-2,c=-2 $$ Putting the values in eq (i), we get, $$ [\mathbf{S}]=[\mathrm{E v}^{-2}\mathrm{T}^{-2}] $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: The dimensions of $(\mu_0\varepsilon_0)^{-1/2}$ are:
questions.explanation: We have, $$ c=\frac{1}{\sqrt{\mu_{0}\varepsilon_{0}}} $$ So, dimension of $ (\mu_0\varepsilon_0)^{-1/2} $ is equal to the dimension of $ c $. Therefore, dimension of $ c = [LT^{-1}] $.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: The dimension of $\frac{1}{2}\varepsilon_{0}E^{2}$, where $\varepsilon_{0}$ is permittivity of free space and E is electric field, is:
questions.explanation: Dimension of $ \varepsilon_0 = [\mathrm{M}^{-1}\mathrm{L}^{-3}\mathrm{T}^4\mathrm{A}^2] $ Dimension of $ \mathrm{E} = [\mathrm{MLT}^{-3}\mathrm{A}^{-1}] $ $ \therefore $ Dimension of $$ \begin{aligned}\frac{1}{2}\varepsilon_{0}\mathrm{E}^{2}&=\left[\mathbf{M}^{-1}\mathrm{L}^{-3}\mathrm{T}^{4}\mathbf{A}^{2}\right]\left[\mathbf{M}\mathrm{LT}^{-3}\mathbf{A}^{-1}\right]^{2}\\&=\left[\mathbf{M}\mathrm{L}^{-1}\mathrm{T}^{-2}\right]\end{aligned} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: If p represent momentum and d represents position, then the dimensions of Planck's constant (h) in terms of p and d is:
questions.explanation: Let Let $ h = [p^a d^p] $ $ [ML^2T^{-1}] = [MLT^{-1}]^a [L]^b $ $ [ML^2T^{-1}] = [MaL^{a+bT^{-a}}] $ Comparing powers on both sides, we get $ a = 1, a + b = 2, $ $ \Rightarrow $ $ b = 1 $ Putting these values in eq. (i), we get The dimension of $ h = [pd] $

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Which two of the following five physical parameters have the same dimensions? (1) Energy density (2) Refractive index (3) Dielectric constant (4) Young's modulus (5) Magnetic field Options:
questions.explanation: Energy density and Young's modulus have same dimension and equal to $ [ML^{-1}T^{-2}] $. Dielectric constant and refractive index are dimension less. #### △! Caution Students should know that the different quantities can have the same dimensional unit. Many students on finding the same dimension of a quantity as of some other quantity cancel out the process and redo the question.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Dimensions of resistance in an electrical circuit, in terms of dimension of mass M, of length L, of time T and of current I, would be:
questions.explanation: We know that. $$ \begin{aligned}R&=\frac{V}{I}=\frac{[ML^{2}T^{-2}]}{[I^{2}T]}\\&=[ML^{2}T^{-3}I^{-2}]\end{aligned} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: The ratio of the dimension of Planck's constant and that of moment of inertia is the dimension of:
questions.explanation: $$ \frac{h}{1}=\frac{E\times\lambda}{c\times I}=\frac{[ML^{2}T^{-2}][L]}{[LT^{-1}][ML^{2}]} $$ $$ \mathrm{\frac{h}{I}}=[\mathrm{T}^{-1}] $$ which is the dimension of frequency.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: The dimensions of universal gravitational constant are:
questions.explanation: We have, $ F = \frac{GMm}{R^{2}} $ $$ \begin{array}{l l}{\Rightarrow\quad}&{\mathrm{G}=\frac{\mathrm{F R}^{2}}{\mathrm{M}m}=\frac{[\mathrm{M L T}^{-2}][\mathrm{L}^{2}]}{[\mathrm{M}^{2}]}}\\ {}&{}\\ {}&{=[\mathrm{M}^{-1}\mathrm{L}^{3}\mathrm{T}^{-2}]}\\ \end{array} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Which of the following will have the dimensions of time?
questions.explanation: $ \frac{L}{R} $ is time constant of R-L circuit so, dimensions of $\frac{L}{R}$ is same as that of time. $$ \frac{\mathrm{D i m e n s i o n s~of~L}}{\mathrm{D i m e n s i o n s~of~R~}}=\frac{[\mathrm{M L}^{2}\mathrm{T}^{-2}\mathrm{A}^{-2}]}{[\mathrm{M L}^{2}\mathrm{T}^{-3}\mathrm{A}^{-2}]}=[\mathrm{T}] $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: The dimensional formula for permeability of free space, $ \mu_{0} $ is:
questions.explanation: From Biot-Savart law. $$ dB=\frac{\mu_{0}}{4\pi}\frac{Idl\sin\theta}{r^{2}} $$ Idl = current element r = displacement vector $$ \begin{aligned}\mu_{0}&=\frac{4\pi r^{2}(dB)}{\mathrm{Id}l\sin\theta}\mathrm{=}\frac{\left[\mathrm{L}^{2}\right]\left[\mathrm{MT}^{-2}\mathrm{A}^{-1}\right]}{[\mathrm{A}][\mathrm{L}]}\\&=[\mathrm{MLT}^{-2}\mathrm{A}^{-2}]\end{aligned} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: The dimensional formula of pressure is:
questions.explanation: $$ \begin{aligned}Pressure&=\frac{Force}{Area}\!=\!\frac{F}{A}\!=\!\frac{\left[MLT^{-2}\right]}{\left[L^{2}\right]}\\&=\left[ML^{-1}T^{-2}\right]\end{aligned} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: If C and R denote capacitance and resistance respectively, then the dimensional formula of CR is:
questions.explanation: $$ \begin{aligned}\mathrm{C}&=\frac{q}{\mathrm{V}}=\frac{q^{2}}{\mathrm{W}}=\frac{(\mathrm{I}t)^{2}}{\mathrm{F}.x}=\frac{[\mathrm{A}\mathrm{T}]^{2}}{[\mathrm{M L}^{2}\mathrm{T}^{-2}]}\\&=[\mathrm{M}^{-1}\mathrm{L}^{-2}\mathrm{T}^{4}\mathrm{A}^{2}]\end{aligned} $$ and $$ \begin{aligned}\mathbf{R}&=\frac{\mathbf{V}}{\mathbf{I}}=\frac{\mathbf{W}}{q\mathbf{I}}\\&=\frac{\mathbf{F}.x}{\mathbf{I}^{2}t}=[\mathbf{M}\mathbf{L}^{2}\mathbf{T}^{-3}\mathbf{A}^{-2}]\end{aligned} $$ ∴ Dimensional formula of CR, $ = [M^{-1}L^{-2}T^{4}A^{2}] [ML^{2}T^{-3}A^{-2}] $ $ = [M^{0}L^{0}T^{1}] $

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: The potential energy of a particle moving along x-direction varies as $ V = \frac{Px^2}{\sqrt{x} + Q} $. The dimensions of $ \frac{P^2}{Q} $ are:

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A force defined by F = \alpha t^{2} + \beta t acts on a particle at a given time t. The factor which is dimensionless, if \alpha and \beta are constants, is:
questions.explanation: $$ \mathrm{F}=\alpha t^{2}+\beta b $$ For $$ \mathrm{F}=\alpha t^{2} $$ $$ [\mathrm{M L T}^{-2}]=\alpha[\mathrm{T}^{2}] $$ $$ \alpha=[\mathrm{M L T^{-4}}] $$ For $$ \mathbf{F}=\boldsymbol{\beta t} $$ $$ [{\mathrm{M L T^{-2}}}]=\upbeta[{\mathrm{T}}] $$ $$ \boldsymbol{\beta}=[\mathrm{M L T}^{-3}] $$ $$ \begin{aligned}\frac{\alpha t}{\beta}&=\frac{[MLT^{-4}][T]}{[MLT^{-3}]}\\&=[M^{0}L^{0}T^{0}]\end{aligned} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A physical quantity of the dimensions of length that can be formed out of c, G and \frac{e^{2}}{4\pi\varepsilon_{0}} is [c is speed of light, G is universal constant of gravitation and e is charge]:
questions.explanation: $ L = [c]^x [G]^y \left[ \frac{e^2}{4\pi\varepsilon_0} \right]^z $ Since coulomb's force is $ F = \frac{e^{2}}{4\pi\varepsilon_{0}r^{2}} $ $$ \frac{e^{2}}{4\pi\varepsilon_{0}}\;=\left[\mathrm{ML}^{3}\mathrm{T}^{-2}\right] $$ L = [LT^{-1}]^{x} [M^{-1}L^{3}T^{-2}]^{y} [ML^{3}T^{-2}]^{z} $$ [\mathbf{L}]=[\mathbf{L}^{x}+3y+3z\mathbf{M}^{-y}+z\mathbf{T}^{-x}-2y-2z] $$ Comparing both sides, $$ -y+z=0 $$ $$ y=z $$ $$ x+3y+3z=1 $$ $$ -x-4z=0 $$ From (i), (ii) and (iii), $$ z=y=\frac{1}{2},x=-2 $$ Hence, $$ \mathrm{L}=\frac{1}{c^{2}}\left[\mathrm{G}.\frac{e^{2}}{4\pi\varepsilon_{0}}\right]^{\frac{1}{2}} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Planck's constant (h), speed of light in vacuum (c) and Newton's gravitational constant (G), these are the fundamental constants. Which of the following combinations of these quantities has the dimension of length?
questions.explanation: According to question. $$ \mathbf{L}=[h]^{a}\mathbf{\Sigma}[c]^{b}\mathbf{\Sigma}[\mathbf{G}]^{c} $$ Putting the dimensions on both sides, we get, $$ [\mathbf{M}^{0}\mathbf{L}\mathbf{T}^{0}]=[\mathbf{M}\mathbf{L}^{2}\mathbf{T}^{-1}]^{a}\;[\mathbf{L}\mathbf{T}^{-1}]^{b}\;[\mathbf{M}^{-1}\mathbf{L}^{3}\mathbf{T}^{-2}]^{c} $$ $$ [\mathbf{M}^{0}\mathbf{L}\mathbf{T}^{0}]=[\mathbf{M}^{a-c}]\;\left[\mathbf{L}^{2a+b+3c}\right]\;[\mathbf{T}^{-a-b-2c}] $$ Comparing powers on both sides, we get. $$ a-c=0 $$ $$ 2a+b+3c=1 $$ $$ -a-b-2c=0 $$ Solving the above equations, $$ a=c=\frac{1}{2}and b=-\frac{3}{2} $$ Putting these values in eq. (i), we get. $$ \mathrm{L}=[h^{1/2}\ c^{-3/2}\ G^{1/2}]=\frac{\sqrt{h\mathrm{G}}}{c^{3/2}} $$ $$ \mathrm{L}=\frac{\sqrt{h\mathrm{G}}}{c^{3/2}} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: If Force (F), velocity (v) and time (T) are taken as fundamental units, then the dimensions of mass are:
questions.explanation:
Dimension of force, $ F = [MLT^{-2}] $ Dimension of velocity, $ v = [LT^{-1}] $ and dimension of time, T = [T] According to the question, $$ [\mathbf{M}]=[\mathbf{F}^{a}\,\mathbf{v}^{b}\,\mathbf{T}^{c}] $$ Where $a$, $b$ and $c$ are constant putting dimensions. $$ [\mathbf{M}]=[\mathbf{M L T}^{-2}]^{a}\;[\mathbf{L T}^{-1}]^{b}[\mathbf{T}]^{c} $$ $$ [\mathbf{M}]=[\mathbf{M}^{a}\,\mathbf{L}^{a+b}\,\mathbf{T}^{-2a-b+c}] $$ Comparing powers on both side, we get: $$ a=1,a+b=0,-2a-b+c=0 $$ Solving the above equation, we get $$ a=1,b=-1\text{and}c=1 $$ Putting these values in eq. (i), we get $$ \mathbf{M}=[\mathbf{F}\mathbf{v}^{-1}\mathbf{T}] $$
##### related Theory Alternative method: $$ F=m a $$ $$ \Rightarrow $$ $$ F=\frac{m\mathbf{v}}{t} $$ $$ m=\frac{Ft}{\textbf{v}} $$ $$ M=\frac{[F][T]}{[\mathbf{v}]}=[F\mathbf{v}^{-1}T] $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: The density of material in CGS system of units is 4 g/cm $ ^{3} $. In a system of units in which unit of lengths is 10 cm and unit of mass is 100 g, the value of density of material will be:
questions.explanation: From the formula, $ d_{1} = \frac{m}{V} $ $$ \begin{array}{l}d_{2}=d_{1}\Bigg[\frac{\mathbf{M}_{1}}{\mathbf{M}_{2}}\Bigg]\Bigg[\frac{\mathbf{L}_{1}}{\mathbf{L}_{2}}\Bigg]^{-3}\\=4\Bigg[\frac{1}{100}\Bigg]\Bigg[\frac{1}{10}\Bigg]^{-3}=40\end{array} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: If the dimensional formula of a physical quantity is given $ [M^{a}L^{b}T^{c}] $, then the physical quantity will be:
questions.explanation: This question will be solved with the help of given options: $$X = [M^a L^b T^c]$$ (a) If $a = 1$, $b = -1$, $c = -2$ $$\Rightarrow$$ $X = [ML^{-1}T^{-2}] \rightarrow Pressure$$ (b) If $a = 1$, $b = 0$, $c = -1$ $$\Rightarrow$$ $X = [MT^{-1}]$$ It is not the dimension of velocity. (c) If $a = 1$, $b = 1$, $c = -2$ $$\Rightarrow$$ $X = [MLT^{-2}]$, It is not the dimension of acceleration. (d) If $a = 0$, $b = -1$, $c = -2$ $$\Rightarrow$$ $X = [L^{-1}T^{-2}]$, It is not the dimension of force.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: In Van der Waals' gas equation $ \left[\mathrm{P}+\frac{a}{\mathrm{V}^{2}}\right]\left[\mathrm{V}-b\right]=\mathrm{RT} $, the dimensions of Van der Waals's constant, a is:
questions.explanation: Using principle of homogeniety, $$ \left[\frac{a}{\mathbf{V}^{2}}\right]=\mathbf{P} $$ $$ \begin{aligned}{[a]}&{{}=[\mathbf{P}]\;[\mathbf{V}^{2}]}\\ {}&{{}=[\mathbf{M}\mathbf{L}^{-1}\mathbf{T}^{-2}]\;[\mathbf{L}^{3}]^{2}}\\ {}&{{}=[\mathbf{M}\mathbf{L}^{5}\mathbf{T}^{-2}]}\\ \end{aligned} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: The velocity v of a particle at time t is given by, v = at + $ \frac{b}{t+c} $ where a, b and c are constants. The dimensions of a, b and c are:
questions.explanation: Using principle of homogeniety. (B) Using principle of homogeniety. Dimension of v = Dimension of at = Dimension of $ \frac{b}{t+c} $ $ \therefore\quad at = [LT^{-1}] $ $ \Rightarrow\quad [a]=\frac{\left[LT^{-1}\right]}{[T]}=[LT^{-2}] $ And c = T \Rightarrow c = [T] And \frac{[b]}{[T]} = [LT^{-1}] \Rightarrow $ [b] = [LT^{-1}] [T] = [L] $

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: In a particular system, the unit of length, mass and time are chosen to be 10 cm, 10 g and 0.1 s respectively. The unit of force in this system will be equivalent to:
questions.optionD: 100 N *Topic/Qs are in NEET latest syllabus but have been removed from NCERT.
questions.explanation: Force. $$ \begin{aligned}\mathrm{F}&=[MLT^{-2}]\\&=(10\mathrm{~g})(10\mathrm{~cm})(0.1\mathrm{~s})^{-2}\end{aligned} $$ Changing these units into mks system. $$ \begin{aligned}\mathrm{F}&=\left(10^{-2}\mathrm{kg}\right)\left(10^{-1}\mathrm{m}\right)\left(10^{-1}\mathrm{s}\right)^{-2}\\&=10^{-1}\mathrm{N}=0.1\mathrm{~N}\end{aligned} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: If $x = at + bt^{2}$, where $x$ is the distance travelled by the body in kilometer, while $t$ is the time in second, then the unit of $b$ is:
questions.explanation: As. $$ x=a t+b t^{2} $$ According to the concept of dimensional analysis and principle of homogeneity. $$ \because $$ $$ unit~of~x=unit~of~bt^{2} $$ $$ unit of b=\frac{unit of x}{unit of t^{2}}=km/s^{2} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: In an electrical circuit, the voltage is measured as $ V = (200 \pm 4) \, \Omega $ volt and the current is measured as $ I = (20 \pm 0.2) \, A $. The value of the resistance is:
questions.explanation: Given, Voltage, $ V=(200\pm4)V $ Current, $ I = (20 \pm 0.2) $ A $$ R=\frac{V}{I}=\frac{200}{20}=10\ \Omega $$ $$ \left(\frac{\Delta\mathrm{R}}{\mathrm{R}}\right)^{2}=\left(\frac{\Delta\mathrm{V}}{\mathrm{V}}\right)^{2}+\left(\frac{\Delta\mathrm{I}}{\mathrm{I}}\right)^{2} $$ $$ \frac{\Delta\mathrm{V}}{\mathrm{V}}=\frac{4}{200}=0.02 $$ $$ \left(\frac{\Delta R}{R}\right)^{2}=(0.02)^{2}+(0.01)^{2} $$ $$ \frac{\Delta R}{R}=\sqrt{0.0005} $$ $$ \Delta\mathbf{R}=\mathbf{R}\times0.0224 $$ $$ \Delta R=0.224 $$ $$ \therefore\mathbf{R}=\left(10\pm0.224\right)\boldsymbol{\Omega}=\left(10\pm0.3\right)\boldsymbol{\Omega} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: The errors in the measurement which arise due to unpredictable fluctuation in temperature and voltage supply are:
questions.explanation:
Random errors are the errors which occur due to random and unpredictable fluctuations in temperature and voltage supply.
##### Related Theory Random errors can arise by some other experimental conditions (Example: u mechanical vibrations of experimental set-ups, etc, errors by the observer taking readings, etc.)

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A metal wire has mass $(0.4 \pm 0.002)$ g, radius $(0.3 \pm 0.001)$ mm and length $(5 \pm 0.02)$ cm. The maximum possible percentage error in the measurement of density will nearly be:
questions.explanation: Volume of the wire, $ V = \pi r^{2} L $ Density, $$ \begin{aligned}\rho&=\frac{m}{\mathrm{V}}\\&=\frac{m}{\pi r^{2}\mathrm{L}}\end{aligned}\quad(\because\mathrm{V}=\pi r^{2}\mathrm{L})\quad $$ For % error. $$ \frac{\Delta\rho}{\rho}\times100=\frac{\Delta m}{m}\times100+2\times\frac{\Delta r}{r}\times100+\frac{\Delta L}{L}\times100 $$ $$ \begin{aligned}\therefore\frac{\Delta\rho}{\rho}\times100&=\frac{0.002}{0.4}\times100+2\times\frac{0.001\times100}{0.3}+\frac{0.02}{5}\\&=0.5+0.67+0.4=1.57=1.6\%\end{aligned} $$ $$ \overline{a}=\frac{1.25+1.24+1.27+1.21+1.28}{5} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Time intervals measured by a clock gave the following readings: 1.25 s, 1.24 s, 1.27 s, 1.21 s and 1.28 s. What is the percentage error of the observation?
questions.explanation: Mean time interval, $$ \begin{aligned}\Delta\overline{a}&=\frac{6.25}{5}=1.25s\\&=\frac{|\Delta a_{1}|+|\Delta a_{2}|+|\Delta a_{3}|+|\Delta a_{4}|+|\Delta a_{5}|}{5}\\&\quad\quad|1.25-1.25|+|1.25-1.24|+|1.25\\&=\frac{-1.27|+|1.25-1.21|+|1.25-1.28|}{5}\\&=\frac{0+0.01+0.02+0.04+0.03}{5}=\frac{0.1}{5}\\&=0.02s\end{aligned} $$ Now, percentage error = $ \frac{\Delta \overline{a}}{\overline{a}} \times 100 $ $ =\frac{0.02}{1.25} \times 100 = 1.6\% $

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: In an experiment, the percentage of error occurred in the measurement of physical quantities A, B, C and D are 1%, 2%, 3% and 4% respectively. Then, the maximum percentage error in the measurement of X, where, $ X = \frac{A^{2}B^{1/2}}{C^{1/3}D^{3}} $, will be:
questions.explanation: According to the question, $$ \mathbf{X}=\frac{\mathbf{A}^{2}\mathbf{B}^{1/2}}{\mathbf{C}^{1/3}\mathbf{D}^{3}} $$ Using percentage error of X, $$ \begin{aligned}\frac{\Delta\mathrm{X}}{\mathrm{X}}\times100&=2\left(\frac{\Delta\mathrm{A}}{\mathrm{A}}\right)\times100+\frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{\Delta\mathrm{B}}{\mathrm{B}}\right)\times100\\ &+\frac{1}{3}\left(\frac{\Delta\mathrm{C}}{\mathrm{C}}\right)\times100+3\left(\frac{\Delta\mathrm{D}}{\mathrm{D}}\times100\right)\end{aligned} $$ Putting the given value, Maximum % error in $X=2(1\%)+\frac{1}{2}(2\%)+\frac{1}{3}(3\%)+3(4\%)$ $=2\%+1\%+1\%+12\%$ $=16\%$ ∴ Maximum error in X is 16%.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: In an experiment, four quantities a, b, c and d are measured with percentage error 1%, 2%, 3% and 4% respectively. Quantity P is calculated as follows, P = $ \frac{a^{3}b^{2}}{cd} $, % error in P is:
questions.explanation:
Given, $$ \mathrm{P}=\frac{a^{3}b^{2}}{cd} $$ Error in P can be written as: $$ \begin{aligned}\frac{\Delta\mathrm{P}}{\mathrm{P}}\times100=&\left(3\frac{\Delta a}{a}+2\frac{\Delta b}{b}+\frac{\Delta c}{c}+\frac{\Delta d}{d}\right)\times100\\=&3\times\frac{\Delta a}{a}\times100+2\frac{\Delta b}{b}\times100+\frac{\Delta c}{c}\times100\\&+\frac{\Delta d}{d}\times100\end{aligned} $$ Putting the given values of percentage errors in above equation, we get, $$ =3\times1+2\times2+3+4=14\% $$
##### Related Theory Student should know that the final absolute error in this case is taken to be equal to the least count of the measuring instrument.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A student measures the distance traversed in free fall of a body, initially at rest in a given time. He uses this data to estimate g, the acceleration due to gravity. If the maximum percentage errors in measurement of the distance and the time are $ e_{1} $ and $ e_{2} $ respectively, the percentage error in the estimation of g is:
questions.explanation: We know that, $$ h=\frac{1}{2}gt^{2} $$ $$ \Rightarrow $$ $$ g=\frac{2h}{t^{2}} $$ Using error formula $$ \begin{array}{l}\left(\frac{\Delta g}{g}\times100\right)_{\max}=\left(\frac{\Delta h}{h}\times100\right)+2\left(\frac{\Delta t}{t}\times100\right)\\ =e_{1}+2e_{2}\end{array} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: If the error in the measurement of radius of a sphere is 2%, then the error in the determination of volume of the sphere will be:
questions.explanation: We know that. Volume of sphere = $ V = \frac{4}{3}\pi R^{3} $ Taking log on both sides, $$ \log\mathrm{V}=\log\frac{4}{3}\pi+3\log\mathrm{R} $$ From error formula. $$ \begin{aligned}\frac{\Delta V}{V}&=0+\frac{3\Delta R}{R}\\&=3\times2\%=6\%\quad&[Given,\ \frac{\Delta R}{R}=2\%]\end{aligned} $$ #### Caution ➞ Power of the variables in the expression of a quantity is important.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: The density of a cube is measured by measuring its mass and length of its sides. If the maximum error in the measurement of mass and length are 4% and 3% respectively, the maximum error in the measurement of density will be:
questions.explanation: As density $ \rho = \frac{m}{V} = \frac{m}{l^{3}} $ $$ \begin{aligned}\therefore\quad\frac{\Delta\rho}{\rho}\times100&=\left(\frac{\Delta m}{m}+3\frac{\Delta l}{l}\right)\times100\%\\&=(4+3\times3)=13\%\end{aligned} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A certain body weighs 22.42 g and has a measured volume of 4.7 cc. The possible error in the measurement of mass and volume are 0.01 g and 0.1 cc. Then, maximum error in the density will be:
questions.explanation: $$ Density=\frac{Mass}{Volume} $$ $$ \rho=\frac{m}{\mathbf{V}} $$ $$ \frac{\Delta\rho}{\rho}=\frac{\Delta m}{m}+\frac{\Delta\mathbf{V}}{\mathbf{V}} $$ Here, $$ \Delta m=0.01\ g,m=22.42\ g $$ $$ \Delta V=0.1\ cc,V=4.7\ cc $$ $$ \frac{\Delta\rho}{\rho}=\left(\frac{0.01}{22.42}+\frac{0.1}{4.7}\right)\times100\approx2\% $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A particle moves along a straight line OX. At a time $t$ (in seconds) the distance $x$ (in metres) of the particle from O is given by $x = 40 + 12t - t^{3}$. How long would the particle travel before coming to rest?
questions.explanation: The given equation is $$ x=40+12t-t^{3} $$ On differentiating, we get, $$ u=\frac{dx}{dt}=12-3t^{2} $$ When particle comes to rest means, v = 0 $\therefore$ $12-3t^{2}=0$ $\Rightarrow$ $t=2s$ The distance travelled before coming to rest $$ \begin{aligned}\int_{0}^{s}ds&=\int_{0}^{2}\mathbf{v}dt\\\left[s\right]_{0}^{s}&=\int_{0}^{2}(12-3t^{2})dt\\s&=\left[12t-\frac{3t^{3}}{3}\right]_{0}^{2}\\&=12\times2-(8-0)\\&=16m.\end{aligned} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: The displacement $x$ of a particle varies with time $t$ as $x = ae^{-\alpha t} + be^{\beta t}$, where $a$, $b$, $\alpha$ and $\beta$ are positive constants. The velocity of the particle will:
questions.explanation: From question, $$ x=ae^{-\alpha t}+be^{\beta t} $$ $$ \mathrm{v}=\frac{dx}{dt}=-a\alpha e^{-\alpha t}+b\beta e^{\beta t} $$ $$ \mathbf{v}=-a\alpha e^{-\alpha t}+b\beta e^{\beta t} $$ ∴ Velocity will go on increasing with time.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: The displacement-time graph of moving particle is shown below. The instantaneous velocity of the particle is negative at the point:
questions.explanation: Instantaneous velocity is the slope of displacement-time graph. At point E, the slope is negative, so instantaneous velocity of the particle is negative. At points C and F, the slope is positive and at D, the slope is zero.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A particle is moving along, x-axis with its position (x) varying with time (t) as $ x = \alpha t^{4} + \beta t^{2} + \gamma t + \delta $. The ratio of its initial velocity to its initial acceleration, respectively, is:
questions.explanation: $$ \mathrm{v}(t)=\frac{dx}{dt}=4\alpha t^{3}+2\beta t+\gamma $$ $$ \alpha(t)=\frac{d v}{d t}=12\alpha t^{2}+2\beta $$ $$ \begin{aligned}&\mathbf{v}(0)=4\boldsymbol{\alpha}\times\boldsymbol{0}+2\boldsymbol{\beta}\times\boldsymbol{0}+\boldsymbol{\gamma}=\boldsymbol{\gamma}\\&\boldsymbol{\alpha}(0)=12\boldsymbol{\alpha}\times\boldsymbol{0}+2\boldsymbol{\beta}=2\boldsymbol{\beta}\\ \end{aligned} $$ The ratio of initial velocity to its initial acceleration is: $$ \frac{\mathrm{v}(\mathfrak{A})}{a(0)}=\frac{\gamma}{2\beta} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A particle of unit mass undergoes one-dimensional motion such that its velocity varies according to $ v(x) = \beta x^{-2n} $ where, $ \beta $ and n are constants and x is the position of the particle. The acceleration of the particle as a function of x, is given by:
questions.explanation: The velocity (v) as a function of position of particle is given by, Acceleration. $$ \begin{aligned}\mathbf{v}&=\beta\mathbf{x}^{-2n}\\a&=\frac{d\mathbf{v}}{dt}=\frac{dx}{dt},\frac{d\mathbf{v}}{dx}\\a&=\frac{d\mathbf{v}}{dx}.\mathbf{v}=\mathbf{v},\frac{d\mathbf{v}}{dx}\\&=\beta\mathbf{x}^{-2n}.(-2n\beta\mathbf{x}^{-2n-1})\\a&=-2n\beta^{2}\mathbf{x}^{-4n-1}\end{aligned} $$ $$ \begin{array}{c}C)\text{Acceleration,}\frac{d\mathbf{v}}{dt}=f=f_{0}\left(1-\frac{t}{\mathrm{T}}\right)\\ \Rightarrow\quad\int_{0}^{\mathrm{v}}d\mathbf{v}=f_{0}\int_{0}^{\mathrm{T}}\left(1-\frac{t}{\mathrm{T}}\right)dt\\ \Rightarrow\quad\mathbf{v}=f_{0}\left(t-\frac{t^{2}}{2\mathrm{T}}\right)_{0}^{\mathrm{T}}\\ =f_{0}\left(\mathrm{T}-\frac{\mathrm{T}^{2}}{2\mathrm{T}}\right)=\frac{1}{2}f_{0}\mathrm{T}\end{array} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A particle moving along x-axis has acceleration $f$, at time $t$, given by, $f = f_{0}\left(1 - \frac{t}{T}\right)$, where $f_{0}$ and T are constants. The particle at $t=0$ has zero velocity. In the time interval between $t=0$ and the instant when $f=0$, the particle's velocity $(\mathbf{v}_{x})$ is:

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: The position x of a particle varies with time t, as $ x = at^{2} - bt^{3} $. The acceleration of the particle will be zero at time t equals to:
questions.explanation: Acceleration. $$ \frac{dv}{dt}=\frac{d^{2}x}{dt^{2}} $$ Velocity, $$ \mathbf{v}=\frac{dx}{dt}. $$ The given equation is, $$ x=a t^{2}-b t^{3} $$ Velocity, $$ \mathrm{v}=\frac{dx}{dt}=2at-3bt^{2} $$ Acceleration. $$ a=\frac{d\mathbf{v}}{dt}=2a-6bt $$ but $$ a=0 $$ (given) $$ \begin{array}{l}\bullet\\ \bullet\bullet\end{array} $$ $$ 2a-6bt=0 $$ $$ 6bt=2a $$ $$ t=\frac{2a}{6b}=\frac{a}{3b} $$ or

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A car accelerates from rest at a constant rate $ \alpha $ for some time, after which it decelerates at a constant rate $ \beta $ and comes to rest. If the total time elapsed is t, then the maximum velocity acquired by the car is:
questions.explanation:
This situation is plotted on (v-t) graph. In (v-t) graph, OA represents the accelerated part and AB represents the decelerated part.
Let $ t_{1} $ and $ t_{2} $ be the times for part OA and AB respectively. At point A velocity is maximum and let it be $ v_{max} $ But, $$ t=t_{1}+t_{2} $$ $$ \mathbf{t}=\mathbf{v}_{\max}\left(\frac{1}{\alpha}+\frac{1}{\beta}\right)=\mathbf{v}_{\max}\left(\frac{\alpha+\beta}{\alpha\beta}\right) $$ or $$ \mathbf{v}_{\max}=t\left(\frac{\alpha\beta}{\alpha+\beta}\right) $$
##### Related Theory This problem can also be solved by checking the dimensions on both sides. On checking the dimensions, we note that the dimensions of option (d) match with that of velocity.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.subtopic: Kinematic Equation for Uniformly Accelerated Motion
questions.question: A horizontal bridge is built across a river. A student standing on the bridge throws a small ball vertically upwards with a velocity $4\ \mathrm{m s}^{-1}$. The ball strikes the water surface after 4 s. The height of bridge above water surface is: (Take, $g=10\ \mathrm{m s}^{-2}$)
questions.explanation: Given: $$ \begin{aligned}&u=4\mathrm{~m}\mathrm{s}^{-1},\mathrm{v}=0\\&g=10\mathrm{~m s}^{-2}\\ \end{aligned} $$ Time taken by ball to reach its maximum height, $$ \begin{aligned}v&=u+at\\0&=4+(-10)t\\ or4&=10t\\t&=0.4s\\ime,\\&=4s-2\times0.4s\\&=3.2s\end{aligned} $$ Remaining time, The height of bridge above water surface is, $$ \Downarrow\Downarrow $$ $$ \begin{aligned}S&=ut+\frac{1}{2}at^{2}\\ S&=4\times3.2+\frac{1}{2}\times10\times(3.2)^{2}\\ S&=64m\end{aligned} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.subtopic: Kinematic Equation for Uniformly Accelerated Motion
questions.question: The ratio of the distances travelled by a freely falling body in the $1^{st}, 2^{nd}, 3^{rd}$ and $4^{th}$ second:
questions.explanation: $$ \begin{aligned}\mathrm{S}_{\mathbf{n}}^{\mathrm{th}}&=u+\frac{a}{2}\left(2n-1\right)\\&=0+\frac{a}{2}\left(2n-1\right)\\\mathrm{S}_{\mathbf{n}}^{\mathrm{th}}&\propto(2n-1)\\&=\left[2(1)-1\right]:\left[2(2)-1\right]:\left[2(3)-1\right]:\left[2(4)-1\right]\\&=1:3:5:7\end{aligned} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.subtopic: Kinematic Equation for Uniformly Accelerated Motion
questions.question: A stone falls freely under gravity. It covers distances $ h_{1} $, $ h_{2} $ and $ h_{3} $ in the first 5 seconds, the next 5 seconds and the next 5 seconds respectively. The relation between $ h_{1} $, $ h_{2} $ and $ h_{3} $ is:

questions.subject: Physics
questions.subtopic: Kinematic Equation for Uniformly Accelerated Motion
questions.question: A ball is dropped from a high rise platform at $t=0$ starting from rest. After 6 s another ball is thrown downwards from the same platform with a speed $v$. The two balls meet at $t=18$ s. What is the value of $v$? (take $g=10\ \text{ms}^{-2}$)
questions.explanation: According to the question. For first ball, u = 0 $$ s_{1}=\frac{1}{2}gt_{1}^{2}=\frac{1}{2}\times g(18)^{2} $$ For second ball. $$ \begin{array}{l}s_{2}=\mathrm{vt}_{2}+\frac{1}{2}\mathrm{gr}^{2}\\ t_{2}=18-6=12\mathrm{s}\\ s_{2}=\mathrm{v}\times12+\frac{1}{2}g(12)^{2}\end{array} $$ Hence, $ s_{1} = s_{2} $ $$ \therefore\frac{1}{2}g(18)^{2}=12\mathrm{v}+\frac{1}{2}g(12)^{2} $$ On solving we get $$ \mathbf{v}=75~\mathbf{m s}^{-1} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.subtopic: Kinematic Equation for Uniformly Accelerated Motion
questions.question: A particle starts its motion from rest under the action of a constant force. If the distance covered in first 10 s is $ s_{1} $ and that covered in the first 20 s is $ s_{2} $, then:

questions.subject: Physics
questions.subtopic: Kinematic Equation for Uniformly Accelerated Motion
questions.question: The distance travelled by a particle starting from rest and moving with an acceleration $\frac{4}{3} \mathrm{~m s}^{-2}$, in the third second is:
questions.explanation: Distance covered in $ n^{\text{th}} $ second, $$ \begin{aligned}S_{n}&=u+\frac{1}{2}a(2n-1)\\&=\ 0+\frac{1}{2}\times\frac{4}{3}(2\times3-1)=\frac{1}{2}\times\frac{4}{3}\times5\\&=\frac{10}{3}m\end{aligned} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.subtopic: Kinematic Equation for Uniformly Accelerated Motion
questions.question: Two particles move with initial speeds of 4 ms^{-1} and 2 ms^{-1} and their accelerations are 1 ms^{-2} and 2 ms^{-2} respectively. If both reach at final position in same time, then the length of path travelled is:
questions.explanation: According to the question, two particles reach at the final position in same time, distance covered is S and time t will remain same. From equation of motion, $$ \mathrm{S}=u t+\frac{1}{2}a t^{2} $$ For $1^{st}$ particle $$ \begin{aligned}S&=4(t)+\frac{1}{2}\left(1\right)t^{2}\\ &=4t+\frac{t^{2}}{2}\end{aligned} $$ For $ 2^{nd} $ particle $$ \begin{aligned}\mathrm{S}&=2(t)+\frac{1}{2}\left(2\right)t^{2}\\&=2t+t^{2}\end{aligned} $$ Equating eq. (i) and eq. (ii) we get, $$ 4t+\frac{t^{2}}{2}=2t+t^{2} $$ $$ t=4s $$ Putting the value of $t$ in eq. (i) we have $$ \begin{aligned}S&=4(4)+\frac{16}{2}\\&=24m\end{aligned} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.subtopic: Kinematic Equation for Uniformly Accelerated Motion
questions.question: If a ball is thrown vertically with speed $u$, the distance covered during the last $t$ seconds of its ascent is:
questions.explanation: Let the total height = H T is time taken by ball to reach height H. So, $$ \mathrm{H}=u\mathrm{T}-\frac{1}{2}g\mathrm{T}^{2} $$ Distance covered by ball in time (T - t) sec. $$ y=u\left(\mathrm{T}-t\right)-\frac{1}{2}g\left(\mathrm{T}-t\right)^{2} $$ Distance covered in last $t$ sec; $$ \begin{aligned}h&=\mathrm{H}-y\\=[ut-\frac{1}{2}gt^{2}]-[u\left(\mathrm{T}-t\right)-\frac{1}{2}g\left(\mathrm{T}-t\right)^{2}]\end{aligned} $$ On solving and putting, $$ \mathrm{T}=\frac{u}{g} $$ We will get, $$ h=\frac{1}{2}gt^{2} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.subtopic: Kinematic Equation for Uniformly Accelerated Motion
questions.question: A man throws balls with the same speed vertically upwards one after the other at an interval of 2 seconds. What should be the speed of the throw so that more than two balls are in the sky at any time?
questions.explanation: From question, Interval of ball thrown = 2 s If we want that minimum three balls remain in air then time of flight of first ball must be greater than 4 sec. $$ \mathrm{T}>4\mathrm{~s} $$ $$ \bullet \quad \bullet\ast $$ $$ \mathrm{\frac{2u}{g}}>4 \mathrm{~s} $$ $$ \rightarrow $$ $$ u>19.6\mathrm{m/s} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.subtopic: Kinematic Equation for Uniformly Accelerated Motion
questions.question: A car moving with a speed of 40 km/h can be stopped after 2 m by applying brakes. If the same car is moving with a speed of 80 km/h, what is the minimum stopping distance?
questions.explanation:
Initial speed of car, u = 40 km/h $$ =40\times\frac{5}{18}m/s=\frac{100}{9}m/s $$ From $3^{\mathrm{rd}}$ equation of motion. $$ \mathbf{v}^{2}=u^{2}-2a s $$ ↓ $$ 0=\left(\frac{100}{9}\right)^{2}-2\times a\times2 $$ $$ 4a=\frac{100\times100}{81} $$ ↓ $$ a=\frac{2500}{81}m/s $$ Final speed of car = 80 km/h $ = 80 \times \frac{5}{18} = \frac{200}{9} \text{ m/s} $ Suppose car stops for a distance s'. Then $$ \mathbf{v}^{2}=\boldsymbol{u}^{2}-2a s^{\prime} $$ $$ 0=\left(\frac{200}{9}\right)^{2}-2\times\frac{2500}{81}s^{\prime} $$ $$ s^{\prime}=\frac{200\times200\times81}{9\times9\times2\times2500}=8m $$
#### ! Caution Students should not get confused by sign of acceleration or retardation. Here, brakes apply retarding force hence a is considered negative.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.subtopic: Kinematic Equation for Uniformly Accelerated Motion
questions.question: If a ball is thrown vertically upwards with a velocity of 40 m/s, then velocity of the ball after 2 s will be $ g = 10 \, \text{m/s}^2 $:
questions.explanation: Here, initial velocity of ball, $ u = 40 \, \text{m/s}^2 $ Acceleration of ball, $$ \begin{array}{r} \begin{aligned}a&=-g\ \mathrm{m/s^{2}}\end{aligned} \\ &=-10\ \mathrm{m/s^{2}}\end{array} $$ Time = 2 s From first equation of motion, $$ \mathbf{v}=\mathbf{u}+a t=40-10\times2 $$ $$ \mathbf{v}=20\ m/s $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.subtopic: Kinematic Equation for Uniformly Accelerated Motion
questions.question: A body starts from rest, what is the ratio of the distance travelled by the body during the 4^{\mathrm{th}} and 3^{\mathrm{rd}} s?
questions.explanation: Distance travelled by the body in $ n^{\text{th}} $ second is given by, $$ s_{n}=u+\frac{a}{2}(2n-1) $$ Here, $$ u=0 $$ $ \therefore $ For $ 4^{th} $ second, $$ s_{4}=\frac{a}{2}(2\times4-1) $$ and For $ 3^{rd} $ second, Hence, $$ s_{3}=\frac{a}{2}(2\times3-1) $$ $$ \frac{s_{4}}{s_{3}}=\frac{(2\times4-1)}{(2\times3-1)}=\frac{7}{5} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.subtopic: Kinematic Equation for Uniformly Accelerated Motion
questions.question: What will be the ratio of the distance moved by a freely falling body from rest in $4^{\text{th}}$ and $5^{\text{th}}$ second of journey?
questions.explanation: As distance travelled in $ n^{th} $ sec is given by, Here, u = 0, $$ s_{n}=u+\frac{1}{2}a(2n-1) $$ acceleration due to gravity, $ a = 9.8 \, \text{m/s}^2 $ $\therefore$ For $4^{\text{th}}$ second, $s_{4} = \frac{1}{2} \times 9.8(2 \times 4 - 1)$ and for $5^{\text{th}}$ second, $s_{5} = \frac{1}{2} \times 9.8(2 \times 5 - 1)$ $$ \mathrm{\frac{s_{4}}{s_{5}}} = \frac{7}{9} $$ $$ s_{4}:s_{5}=7:9 $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.subtopic: Kinematic Equation for Uniformly Accelerated Motion
questions.question: A car is moving along a straight road with a uniform acceleration. It passes through two points P and Q separated by a distance with velocity 30 km/h and 40 km/h respectively. The velocity of the car midway between P and Q is:
questions.explanation:
Let x be the total distance between points P and Q and v be the velocity of car while passing a certain middle point of PQ. If a is the acceleration of the car then,
For part PQ, $$ 40^{2}-30^{2}=2ax $$ or $$ a=\frac{350}{x} $$ For part RQ, $$ 40^{2}-\mathbf{v}^{2}=\frac{2ax}{2} $$ Putting value of $a$ from eq. (i) in eq. (ii), we have, $$ 40^{2}-v^{2}=2\left(\frac{350}{x}\right)\frac{x}{2} $$ $$ 40^{2}-v^{2}=350 $$ $$ \mathbf{v}^{2}=1250 $$ v = 35.35 km/h v ≈ 35 km/h

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: The speed of a swimmer in still water is 20 m/s. The speed of river water is 10 m/s and is flowing to east. If he is standing on the south bank and wishes to cross the river along the shortest path the angle at which he should make his strokes w.r.t. north is given by:
questions.explanation:
Here, $ v_{SR} = 20 \, m/s $
$$ \begin{array}{r}{\mathbf{v}_{\mathrm{R G}}\quad=10\mathrm{m/s}}\end{array} $$ $$ \mathbf{\tilde{v}_{SG}}\stackrel{\rightarrow}{=}\mathbf{\tilde{v}_{SR}}\stackrel{\rightarrow}{+}\mathbf{\tilde{v}_{RG}} $$ $$ \sin\theta=\left|\frac{\mathbf{v}_{\mathrm{RG}}}{\mathbf{v}_{\mathrm{SR}}}\right|=\frac{10}{20}=\frac{1}{2} $$ ↓ $$ \theta=30^{\circ}\mathrm{w e s t}. $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: The two bullets are fired horizontally and simultaneously towards each other from roof tops of two building 100 m apart and of the same height of 200 m with the same velocity of 25 m/s. When and where will the two bullets collides (g = 10 m/s²)?
questions.optionA: After 2 s at a height 180 m
questions.optionB: After 2 s at a height 20 m
questions.optionC: After 4 s at a height 120 m
questions.explanation: Let bullet collide at time t. $$ \because $$ $$ x_{1}+x_{2}=100m $$ $$ 25t+25t=100 $$ $$ t=2s $$ $$ \begin{aligned}y=\frac{1}{2}gt^{2}=\frac{1}{2}\times10\times2^{2}\\=20m\\h=200-20=180m\end{aligned} $$ Hence, bullets will collide after 2 s at the height 180 m above the ground.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Preeti reached the metro station and found that the escalator is not working. She walked up the stationary escalator in time t_{1}. On other days, if she remains stationary on the moving escalator, then the escalator takes up in time t_{2}. The time taken by her to walk up on the moving escalator will be:
questions.explanation: Velocity of girl with respect to elevator = $ \frac{d}{t_1} = v_1 $ Velocity of elevator with respect to ground = $ \frac{d}{t_2} = v_2 $ $ \therefore $ Velocity of girl with respect to ground = $ v_g = v_1 + v_2 $ $$ \begin{aligned}\frac{d}{t}&=\frac{d}{t_{1}}+\frac{d}{t_{2}}\\\frac{1}{t}&=\frac{1}{t_{1}}+\frac{1}{t_{2}}\\t&=\frac{t_{1}t_{2}}{t_{1}+t_{2}}\end{aligned} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A ship A is moving westwards with a speed of 10 km h^{-1} and a ship B 100 km south of A, is moving northwards with a speed of 10 km h^{-1}. The time after which the distance between them becomes shortest, is:
questions.explanation: From the diagram, the distance S between the ships is, $$ \mathrm{S}=\sqrt{(100-x)^{2}+x^{2}}[\mathrm{From~A~to~B}] $$ for $ S_{min} $, $$ \frac{d\mathbf{S}}{dx}=0 $$ $$ \frac{dS}{dx}=\frac{[-2(100-x)+2x]}{2[(100-x)^{2}+x^{2}]^{1/2}} $$ $$ \left[-2\left(100-x\right)+2x\right]=0 $$ $$ \implies\quad4x-200=0 $$ ⇨ $ x = 50 ~km $ So after both the ships have covered 50 km. The time taken is. $$ t=\frac{x}{10}=\frac{50}{10}=5h $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A bus is moving with a speed of 10 ms^{-1} on a straight road. A scooterist wants to overtake the bus in 100 s. If the bus is at a distance of 1 km from the scooterist, with what speed should be scooterist chase the bus?
questions.explanation: S $ \rightarrow $ 1 km B $ \rightarrow $ $ u = 10 \, ms^{-1} $ S = Scooter B = Bus Let v = relative velocity of S w.r.t. B $$ \begin{aligned}&\mathbf{V}=\mathbf{V}_{S}-\mathbf{V}_{\mathbf{B}}\\&\mathbf{V}_{S}=\mathbf{V}+\mathbf{V}_{\mathbf{B}}\end{aligned} $$ \therefore Relative velocity = \frac{Displacement}{time} = \frac{1000}{100} = 10 ms^{-1} Putting this value in eq. (i),

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Two boys are standing at the ends A and B of a ground where AB = a. The boy at B starts running in a direction perpendicular to AB with velocity $ v_{1} $. The boy at A starts running simultaneously with velocity v and catches the other in time t, where t is equal to:
questions.explanation:
$$ \mathbf{v}_{s}=10+10=20\;\mathbf{m}\mathbf{s}^{-1} $$
After time t,
$$ (\mathbf{v}t)^{2}=a^{2}+(\mathbf{v}_{1}t)^{2} $$ $$ \mathbf{v}^{2}t^{2}=a^{2}+(\mathbf{v}_{1}^{2}t)^{2} $$ $$ (\mathbf{v}^{2}-\mathbf{v}_{1}^{2})t^{2}=a^{2} $$ $$ t=\frac{a}{\sqrt{\mathbf{v}^{2}-\mathbf{v}_{1}^{2}}} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A vehicle travels half the distance with speed v and the remaining distance with speed 2v. Its average speed is:
questions.optionD: $ \frac{2v}{3} $ $ ^{*} $Topics / Qs are in NEET latest syllabus but have been removed from NCERT.
questions.explanation: Average speed = $ \frac{\text{Total distance}}{\text{Total time taken}} $ Average speed = $ \frac{2s}{\frac{s}{v}+\frac{s}{2v}} $ $ =\frac{2s\times2v}{2s+s}=\frac{4vs}{2s}=\frac{4v}{3} $

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A person travelling in a straight line moves with a constant velocity $v_{1}$ for certain distance 'x' and with a constant velocity $v_{2}$ for next equal distance. The average velocity $v$ is given by the relation:
questions.explanation: Time taken. $$ t_{1}=\frac{x}{\mathbf{v}_{1}}\quad\text{and}\quad t_{2}=\frac{x}{\mathbf{v}_{2}} $$ $\therefore$ Average velocity $\mathbf{v}=\frac{x+x}{t_{1}+t_{2}}=\frac{2x}{\frac{x}{v_{1}}+\frac{x}{v_{2}}}=\frac{2v_{1}v_{2}}{v_{1}+v_{2}}$ $$ \frac{2}{\mathbf{v}}=\frac{1}{\mathbf{v}_{1}}+\frac{1}{\mathbf{v}_{2}} $$ OR $$ \mathbf{v}=\frac{2\mathbf{v}_{1}\mathbf{v}_{2}}{\mathbf{v}_{1}+\mathbf{v}_{2}} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A car moves from X to Y with a uniform speed $ v_{u} $ and returns to Y with a uniform speed $ v_{d} $. The average speed for this round trip is:
questions.explanation: Average speed = $ \frac{\text{Total distance}}{\text{Total time}} $ $ =\frac{2d}{\frac{d}{v_u}+\frac{d}{v_d}}=\frac{2v_d v_u}{v_d+v_u} $

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A car covers the first-half of the distance between two places at 40 km/h and other half at 60 km/h. The average speed of the car is:
questions.explanation: Let the distance between two places be $d$ and $t_{1}$ is time taken by car to travel first-half length, $t_{2}$ is time taken by car to travel second-half length. Time taken by car to travel first-half length, $$ t_{1}=\frac{\left(\frac{d}{2}\right)}{40}=\frac{d}{80} $$ Time taken by car to travel second-half length. $$ t_{2}=\frac{\left(\frac{d}{2}\right)}{60}=\frac{d}{120} $$ ∴ Total time. $$ \begin{aligned}t&=t_{1}+t_{2}\\&=\frac{d}{80}+\frac{d}{120}\\&=d\left(\frac{1}{80}+\frac{1}{120}\right)=\frac{d}{48}\end{aligned} $$ $\therefore$ Average speed, $=\frac{d}{t_{1}+t_{2}}=\frac{d}{\left(\frac{d}{48}\right)}$ $=48\mathrm{~km/h}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Which of the following is NOT a vector quantity?
questions.explanation:
Speed is a scalar quantity. It gives no idea about the direction of motion of the object. Velocity is a vector quantity, as it has both magnitude and direction. Displacement is a vector as it possesses both magnitude and direction. When an object goes on the path ABC (in figure), then the displacement of the object is AC. The arrow head at C shows that the object is displaced from A to C.
Torque is turning effect of force, which is a vector quantity.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.subtopic: Addition and Subtraction of Vectors – Graphical Method
questions.question: Six vectors $ \overrightarrow{a} $ through $ \overrightarrow{f} $ have the magnitudes and direction indicated in the figure. Which of the following statements is true? $$ \begin{array}{ccc}\overrightarrow{a}&\overrightarrow{b}&&\overrightarrow{c}\\ \uparrow\overrightarrow{d}&\overleftarrow{e}&&\overrightarrow{f}\end{array} $$
questions.explanation: According to the parallelogram law of vector addition, we can say that, $$ \vec{d}+\vec{e}=\vec{f} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: If the magnitude of sum of two vectors is equal to the magnitude of difference of the two vectors, the angle between these vectors is:
questions.explanation: From the question, if $ \overrightarrow{A} $ and $ \overrightarrow{B} $ are two vectors, the $ |\overrightarrow{A}+\overrightarrow{B}|=|\overrightarrow{A}-\overrightarrow{B}| $ $ \therefore A^{2}+B^{2}+2AB\cos\theta=A^{2}+B^{2}-2AB\cos\theta $ $ \Rightarrow $ 4AB $ \cos\theta = 0 $ $ \Rightarrow $ $ \cos\theta = 0 $ $ \Rightarrow $ $ \theta = 90^{\circ} $

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A body is moving with velocity 30 m/s towards east. After 10 s its velocity becomes 40 m/s towards north. The average acceleration of the body is:
questions.explanation: Average acceleration $$ \overset{\rightarrow}{a_{\mathrm{a v g}}}=\frac{\overset{\rightarrow}{\mathbf{v}}_{f}-\overset{\rightarrow}{\mathbf{v}}_{i}}{t}=\frac{40\overset{\wedge}{j}-30\overset{\wedge}{i}}{10} $$ $$ \overset{\rightarrow}{a_{\mathrm{a v g}}}=\frac{\sqrt{40^{2}+30^{2}}}{10}=5\mathrm{m/s e c}^{2} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: The magnitudes of vectors A, B and C are 3, 4 and 5 units respectively. If $ \overrightarrow{A}+\overrightarrow{B}=\overrightarrow{C} $, the angle between $ \overrightarrow{A} $ and $ \overrightarrow{B} $ is:
questions.explanation:
From figure, $ \overrightarrow{A} + \overrightarrow{B} = \overrightarrow{C} $
Also, $ |\overrightarrow{A}| = 3 $, $ |\overrightarrow{B}| = 4 $, $ |\overrightarrow{C}| = 5 $ As $ \overrightarrow{A}+\overrightarrow{B}=\overrightarrow{C} $ So, $ 5^{2}=3^{2}+4^{2}+2\times4\times3\cos\theta $ $ \cos\theta=0 $ $ \Rightarrow $ $ \theta=\frac{\pi}{2} $ $ \Rightarrow $ $ \overrightarrow{A} $ is perpendicular to $ \overrightarrow{B} $

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: The two-dimensional motion of a particle, described by $\overrightarrow{r} = (\hat{i} + 2\hat{j}) \text{ A } \cos \omega t \text{ is a/an:}$ (I) parabolic path (II) elliptical path (III) periodic motion (IV) simple harmonic motion Choose the correct answer from the options given below:
questions.explanation: Given: $ \overrightarrow{r} = (i + 2\overrightarrow{j}) $ A cos ωt The motion is periodic and exhibits characteristics of simple harmonic mention due to cosine function in the y direction. Thus, the correct answer is periodic motion and SHM.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: The displacement-time graphs of two moving particles make angles of $ 30^{\circ} $ and $ 45^{\circ} $ with the x-axis as shown in the figure. The ratio of their respective velocity is:
questions.explanation: Let displacement be $s$ and time be $t$ and $y$-intercept is zero. $\therefore$ $s = ut + 0$ $\Rightarrow$ $s = ut$, where $u$ is velocity as per laws of motion Also u is slope of the graph s vs t. $$ u=\tan\theta $$ $$ u_{1}=\tan30^{\circ} $$ $$ u_{1}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}} $$ $$ u_{2}=\tan45^{\circ} $$ $$ u_{2}=1 $$ $$ u_{1}:u_{2}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}:1 $$ $$ u_{1}:u_{2}=1:\sqrt{3} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A car starts from rest and accelerates at $ 5 \, m/s^{2} $. At $ t = 4 \, s $, a ball is dropped out of a window by a person sitting in the car. What is the velocity and acceleration of the ball at $ t = 6 \, s $?
questions.explanation: Given. Initial velocity, u = 0 Acceleration, $ a = 5 \, \text{m/s}^2 $ At t = 4 s ball is dropped. velocity of car at $ t = 4 \, s $, $$ \mathbf{v}=\boldsymbol{u}+a t $$ $$ \mathbf{v}=0+5\ (4)\ =20\ m/s $$ At $t=6\mathrm{~s}$, acceleration is due to gravity. $$ a=g=10m/s $$ $$ \begin{aligned}\mathbf{v}_{x}&=20\mathrm{~m/s}\text{(due to car)}\\\mathbf{v}_{y}&=u+at=0+g(2)\text{(downward)}\\&=20\mathrm{~m/s}\text{(downward)}\\\mathbf{v}&=\sqrt{(20^{2}+20^{2})}=20\sqrt{2}\mathrm{~m/s}\end{aligned} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A particle moves in $x-y$ plane according to rule $x=a\sin\omega t$ and $y=a\cos\omega t$. The particle follows:
questions.optionD: a straight line path inclined equally to x and y-axis
questions.explanation: It is given that, $$ x=a\sin\omega t $$ $$ \frac{x}{a}=\sin\omega t $$ $$ y=a\cos\omega t $$ $$ \frac{y}{a}\;=\cos\omega t $$ On squaring and adding eq. (i) and (ii), we get, $$ \begin{aligned}\frac{x^{2}}{a^{2}}+\frac{y^{2}}{a^{2}}&=1\\\Rightarrow\quad x^{2}+y^{2}&=a^{2}\end{aligned} $$ This is the equation of circle. Hence, particle follows a circular path.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: When an object is shot from the bottom of a long smooth inclined plane kept at an angle $ 60^{\circ} $ with horizontal it can travel a distance $ x_{1} $ along the plane. But when the inclination is decreased to $ 30^{\circ} $ and the same object is shot with the same velocity, it can travel $ x_{2} $ distance. Then $ x_{1} $: $ x_{2} $ will be:
questions.explanation: $\therefore$ Stopping distance, $x_{1}=\frac{u^{2}}{2g\sin60^{\circ}}$ Stopping distance, $ x_{2}=\frac{u^{2}}{2g\sin30^{\circ}} $ $$ \begin{aligned}\frac{x_{1}}{x_{2}}&=\frac{\sin30^{\circ}}{\sin60^{\circ}}\\&=\frac{1\times2}{2\times\sqrt{3}}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}\\&=1:\sqrt{3}\end{aligned} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Two particles A and B move with constant velocities $ \vec{v}_1 $ and $ \vec{v}_2 $. At the initial moment, their position vectors are $ \vec{r}_1 $ and $ \vec{r}_2 $ respectively. The condition for particles A and B for their collision is:
questions.explanation: Displacement of particle is, $$ \begin{array}{r}{\vec{\mathrm{~\boldmath~R~}}=\vec{r_{1}}+\vec{\mathrm{~\boldmath~v_{1}~}}t=\vec{r_{2}}+\vec{\mathrm{~\boldmath~v_{2}~}}t}\end{array} $$ i.e. $$ \overset{\to}{r}_{1}-\overset{\to}{r}_{2}=(\overset{\to}{\mathbf{v}}_{2}-\overset{\to}{\mathbf{v}}_{1})t $$ $$ \begin{array}{r}{\overset{\to}{r}_{1}-\overset{\to}{r}_{2}=\big(\mathbf{v}_{2}\overset{\to}{--}\mathbf{v}_{1}\big)\frac{\overset{\to}{\mid}\boldsymbol{r}_{1}-\boldsymbol{r}_{2}\mid}{\overset{\to}{\mid}\mathbf{v}_{2}-\mathbf{v}_{1}\mid}}\end{array} $$ $$ \begin{array}{c}\rightarrow\rightarrow\\\left.\begin{array}{l}\boldsymbol{r}_{1}-\boldsymbol{r}_{2}\end{array}\right.=\frac{\left(\mathbf{v}_{2}-\mathbf{v}_{1}\right)}{\rightarrow\rightarrow}\left|\begin{array}{l}\mathbf{v}_{2}\rightarrow\\\mathbf{v}_{1}\rightarrow\end{array}\right.\end{array} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A particle is moving such that its position coordinates $ (x, y) $ are $ (2\ m, 3\ m) $ at time $ t = 0 $, $ (6\ m, 7\ m) $ at time $ t = 2\ s $ and $ (13\ m, 14\ m) $ at time $ t = 5\ s $. Average velocity vector $ (v_{av}) $ from $ t = 0 $ to $ t = 5\ s $ is:
questions.explanation: Here, net displacement, $$ \begin{aligned} \vec{s}_{av} = (13-2)\hat{i} + (14-3)\hat{j} = 11\hat{i} + 11\hat{j} \\ \text{and time taken, } t = (5-0) = 5 \\ \therefore \text{Average velocity, } \vec{v}_{av} = \frac{\text{Net displacement}}{\text{Time taken}} \\ = \frac{11\hat{i} + 11\hat{j}}{5} \\ = \frac{11}{5}(\hat{i} + \hat{j}) \end{aligned} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: The velocity of a projectile at the initial point A is $ (2\hat{i}+3\hat{j}) $ m/s. Its velocity (in m/s) at point B will be:
questions.explanation: From graph, x-component remains unchanged and y-component is reversed. So, velocity at point B is $(2\hat{i}-3\hat{j})\, \mathrm{ms}^{-1}$. [Here, x-component does not change and y-component changes]

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A particle starting from the origin (0, 0) moves in a straight line in the $(x, y)$ plane. Its coordinates at a later time are $(\sqrt{3}, 3)$. The path of the particle makes with the x-axis an angle of:
questions.explanation: Here, $ \tan\theta=\frac{3}{\sqrt{3}}=\sqrt{3} $ $$ \theta=60^{\circ} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Two particles A and B are connected by a rigid rod AB. The rod slides along perpendicular rails as shown here. The velocity of A to the right is 10 m/s. What is the velocity of B, when angle $\alpha = 60^{\circ}$?
questions.explanation:
Let the velocity along x and y-axes be $ v_{x} $ and $ v_{y} $ respectively. $$ \therefore\quad\mathbf{v}_{x}=\frac{dx}{dt}\text{and}\mathbf{v}_{y}=\frac{dy}{dt} $$
From figure. $$ \tan\alpha=\frac{y}{x} $$ $ y = x \tan \alpha $ Differentiating eq. (i), w.r.t. t we get. $$ \begin{aligned}\frac{dy}{dt}&=\frac{dx}{dt}\tan\alpha\\\Longrightarrow\quad\mathbf{v}_{y}&=\mathbf{v}_{x}\tan\alpha\\ Here,\quad\mathbf{v}_{x}&=10\mathrm{~m/s},\alpha=60^{\circ}\\\therefore\quad\mathbf{v}_{x}&=10\tan60^{\circ}\\&=10\sqrt{3}=17.3\mathrm{~m/s}\end{aligned} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.subtopic: Motion in a Plane with Constant Acceleration
questions.question: The position vector of a particle is $\boldsymbol{r} = (a \cos \omega t) \hat{\boldsymbol{i}} + (a \sin \omega t) \hat{\boldsymbol{j}}$. The velocity of the particle is:
questions.explanation:
Velocity is rate of change of position vector, i.e., $$ \overset{\rightarrow}{\mathbf{v}}=\frac{\boldsymbol{d}\overset{\rightarrow}{r}}{dt} $$ where, $ \overrightarrow{r} $ is the position vector. $$ \begin{aligned}\overrightarrow{\mathbf{v}}=&\frac{d}{dt}\left[\left(a\cos\omega t\right)\hat{i}+\left(a\sin\omega t\right)\hat{j}\right]\\=&\left(-a\omega\sin\omega t\right)\hat{i}+\left(a\omega\cos\omega t\right)\hat{j}\\=&\omega[(-a\sin\omega t)\hat{i}+(a\cos\omega t)\hat{j}]\end{aligned} $$ Slope of position vector $$ \frac{a\sin\omega t}{a\cos\omega t}=\tan\omega t $$ and slope of velocity vector = $ \frac{-a\cos\omega t}{a\sin\omega t} $ $ = -\frac{1}{\tan\omega t} $ Velocity is perpendicular to the displacement.
##### Related Theory Velocity vector and position vector are perpendicular to each other if their scalar product is zero, i.e. $$ \begin{array}{r}{\mathbf{\nabla}^{\rightarrow}\rightarrow}\\ {\mathbf{v}.r=0}\end{array} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.subtopic: Motion in a Plane with Constant Acceleration
questions.question: The $x$ and $y$ coordinates of a particle at any time are $x = 5t - 2t^{2}$ and $y = 10t$ respectively, where $x$ and $y$ are in metres and $t$ in seconds. The acceleration of the particle at $t = 2\,s$ is:
questions.explanation: Given. Velocity $$ x=51-2t^{2} $$ $$ \begin{array}{l}\text{v}_{x}=\displaystyle \frac{dx}{dt}=\frac{d}{dt}(5t-2t^{2})\\ =5-4t \end{array} $$ Acceleration, $$ \begin{array}{l}a_{x}=\displaystyle\frac{d}{d t}(\textbf{v}_{x})=\frac{d}{d t}(5-4t)\\ \quad=-\displaystyle4\mathrm{~m/s}^{2}\end{array} $$ Again, $$ y=10t $$ ∴ Velocity, $$ \mathbf{v}_{y}=\frac{d}{dt}(10t)=10 $$ Acceleration, $$ \begin{array}{l}a_{y}=\displaystyle\frac{d}{d t}(\mathbf{v}_{y})\\ =\displaystyle\frac{d}{d t}(10)=0\end{array} $$ ∴ Net acceleration of particle, $$ \begin{array}{l}\vec{a_{net}}=\vec{a_{x}}\hat{i}+\vec{a_{y}}\hat{j}\\ \quad=-\vec{4\hat{i}}+0=-\vec{4\hat{i}}\mathrm{m/s^{2}}\\ \quad\rightarrow\quad\quad\quad\quad t=t_{2}\end{array} $$ $$ \vec{a_{n e t}}=-4~\mathrm{m/s^{2}} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.subtopic: Motion in a Plane with Constant Acceleration
questions.question: The position vector of a particle R as a function of time is given by: \vec{R}=4\sin(2\pi t)\ \hat{i}+4\cos(2\pi t)\ \hat{j} where R is in metres, t is in seconds and \hat{i} and \hat{j} denote unit vectors along x and y-directions, respectively. Which one of the following statement is WRONG for the motion of particle?
questions.optionB: Magnitude of acceleration vector is  $ \frac{v^{2}}{R} $, where v is the velocity of particle.
questions.optionC: Magnitude of the velocity of particle is 8 m/s.
questions.optionD: Path of the particle is a circle of radius 4 m
questions.explanation: Here, $ \overrightarrow{R} = 4 \sin (2\pi t)i + 4 \cos (2\pi t)j $ The velocity of the particle is, $$ \begin{array}{l}\overrightarrow{v}=\frac{d\overrightarrow{R}}{dt}=\frac{d}{dt}\left[4\sin\left(2\pi t\right)\hat{i}+4\cos\left(2\pi t\right)\hat{j}\right]\\=8\pi\cos2\pi t\hat{i}-8\pi\sin\left(2\pi t\right)\hat{j}\end{array} $$ Its magnitude is, $$ \begin{align*}|\overrightarrow{v}|&=\sqrt{(8\pi\cos(2\pi t))^2+(-8\pi\sin(2\pi t))^2}\\&=\sqrt{64\pi^2\cos^2(2\pi t)+64\pi^2\sin^2(2\pi t)}\\&=\sqrt{64\pi^2[\cos^2(2\pi t)+\sin^2(2\pi t)]}\\&=\sqrt{64\pi^2}=8\pi\text{m/s}\\&\quad\text{(as}\sin^2\theta+\cos^2\theta=1\text{)}\end{align*} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.subtopic: Motion in a Plane with Constant Acceleration
questions.question: A particle has initial velocity $(2\hat{i}+3\hat{j})$ and acceleration $(0.3\hat{i}+0.2\hat{j})$. The magnitude of velocity after 10 s will be:
questions.explanation: Given that, velocity, $ \vec{u} = 2\hat{i} + 3\hat{j} $ Acceleration. $$ \vec{a}=(0.3\hat{i}+0.2\hat{j}) $$ By first equation of motion, $$ \begin{aligned}\mathbf{v}&=\mathbf{u}+at\\\mathbf{v}&=(2\hat{i}+3\hat{j})+(0.3\hat{i}+0.2\hat{j})\times10\\&=5\hat{i}+5\hat{j}\\\mathbf{v}&=5\sqrt{2}\text{units}\end{aligned} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.subtopic: Motion in a Plane with Constant Acceleration
questions.question: A particle has initial velocity $(3\hat{i}+4\hat{j})$ and has acceleration $(0.4\hat{i}+0.3\hat{j})$. Its speed after 10 s is:
questions.explanation: We know that, first equation of motion. $$ \begin{aligned}\mathbf{v}&=u+at\\\overrightarrow{\mathbf{v}}&=(3\overrightarrow{i}+4\overrightarrow{j})+(0.4\overrightarrow{i}+0.3\overrightarrow{j})10\\&=\ 3\overrightarrow{i}+4\overrightarrow{j}+4\overrightarrow{i}+3\overrightarrow{j}\\\overrightarrow{\mathbf{v}}&=7\overrightarrow{i}+7\overrightarrow{j}\\|\mathbf{v}|&=\ \sqrt{7^{2}+7^{2}}=\ \sqrt{49+49}\\&=7\sqrt{2}units\end{aligned} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A bullet is fired from a gun at the speed of $ 280 \, \text{ms}^{-1} $ in the direction $ 30^{\circ} $ above the horizontal. The maximum height attained by the bullet is: $ (g = 9.8 \, \text{ms}^{-2}, \sin 30^{\circ} = 0.5) $
questions.explanation: Max. height, H = $ \frac{u^{2}\sin^{2}\theta}{2g} $ Here: $$ u=280~\mathrm{m s}^{-1} $$ $$ \theta=30^{\circ} $$ $$ \begin{aligned}H&=\frac{(280)^{2}\times\sin^{2}30^{\circ}}{2\times9.8}\\&=\frac{(280)^{2}\times\frac{1}{4}}{2\times9.8}=1000m\end{aligned} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A ball is projected with a velocity, $10\ \mathrm{m s}^{-1}$, at angle of $60^{\circ}$ with the vertical direction. Its speed at the highest point of its trajectory will be:
questions.explanation: Given: velocity of ball = 10 m/s, Angle with vertical direction = $ 60^{\circ} $ From question. we can say that speed of the ball at the highest point = v $ \cos \theta $ where, $ \theta = 90^{\circ} - 60^{\circ} = 30^{\circ} $ $$ \mathbf{v}=10\times\cos\left(30^{\circ}\right)=10\times\left(\frac{\left(\sqrt{3}\right)}{2}\right) $$ $$ \mathbf{v}=5\sqrt{3}\mathrm{~m/s} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: The speed of a projectile at its maximum height is half of its initial speed. The angle of projection is:
questions.explanation: The speed of a projectile at its maximum height $$ \mathbf{v}^{\prime}=\mathbf{v}_{0}\cos\theta $$ According to given problem, $$ \mathbf{v}^{\prime}=\frac{\mathbf{v}_{0}}{2} $$ $$ \frac{\mathbf{v}_{0}}{2}=\mathbf{v}_{0}\cos\theta $$ $$ ↳\Downarrow $$ $$ \theta=\frac{1}{2} $$ $$ \theta=60^{\circ} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A projectile is fired from the surface of the Earth with a velocity of $ 5 \, m s^{-1} $ and angle $ \delta $ with the horizontal. Another projectile fired from another planet with a velocity of $ 3 \, m s^{-1} $ at the same angle follows a trajectory which is identical with the trajectory of the projectile fired from the Earth. The value of the acceleration due to gravity on the planet is $ \left(\mathrm{in} \, \mathrm{ms}^{-2}\right) $ is: (given, $ g = 9.8 \, \mathrm{m s}^{-2} $):
questions.explanation: The equation of trajectory is, $$ y=x\tan\theta-\frac{gx^{2}}{2u^{2}\cos^{2}\theta}, $$ where, $\theta$ is the angle of projection and $u$ is the velocity with which projectile is projected. For equal trajectories and for same angles of projection, $$ \frac{g}{u^{2}}=constant $$ As per question, $$ \frac{9.8}{\left(5\right)^{2}}=\frac{g^{\prime}}{\left(3\right)^{2}}, $$ where $g'$ is acceleration due to gravity on the planet.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: The horizontal range and the maximum height of a projectile are equal. The angle of projection of the projectile is:
questions.explanation: We know that, $$ g^{\prime}=\frac{9.8\times9}{25}\approx3.5\mathrm{~m/s^{2}} $$ Range, $$ R=\frac{u^{2}(2\sin\theta\cos\theta)}{g} $$ and Height, $$ \mathrm{H}=\frac{u^{2}\sin^{2}\theta}{2g} $$ According to the question, $$ \boldsymbol{R}=\boldsymbol{H} $$ $$ \frac{u^{2}(2\sin\theta\cos\theta)}{g}=\frac{u^{2}\sin^{2}\theta}{2g} $$ $$ 2\cos\theta=\frac{\sin\theta}{2} $$ $$ \Downarrow\Downarrow $$ $$ \begin{array}{l}\tan\theta=4\\\theta=\tan^{-1}\left(4\right)\end{array} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A projectile is fired at an angle of $ 45^{\circ} $ with the horizontal Elevation angle of the projectile at its highest point as seen from the point of projection is:
questions.explanation: Height of Projectile, $$ \begin{array}{l}\text{H}=\displaystyle \frac{u^{2} \sin^{2} \theta}{2g} \\ = \displaystyle \frac{u^{2} \sin^{2} 45^{\circ}}{2g} \\ = \displaystyle \frac{u^{2}}{4g}\end{array} $$ and Range of projectile, $$ \begin{aligned}R&=\frac{u^{2}\sin2\theta}{g}\\&=\frac{u^{2}\sin90^{\circ}}{g}\\&=\frac{u^{2}}{g}\end{aligned} $$ $$ \frac{\mathrm{R}}{2}=\frac{u^{2}}{2g} $$ $$ \tan\alpha=\frac{H}{R/2}=\frac{u^{2}/4g}{u^{2}/2g} $$ considering eq. (i) and eq. (ii), $$ \tan\alpha=\frac{H}{R/2} $$ $$ \alpha=\frac{1}{2} $$ $$ \alpha=\tan^{-1}\left(\frac{1}{2}\right) $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A missile is fired for maximum range with an initial velocity of 20 m/s, if $ g = 10 \, m/s^{2} $, the range of the missile is:
questions.explanation: As we know that, maximum range, $$ \mathrm{R}_{\max}=\frac{u^{2}}{g}=\frac{(20)^{2}}{10}=40\mathrm{~m} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: For angles of projection of projectile at angle $(45^{\circ}-\theta)$ and $(45^{\circ}+\theta)$, the horizontal range described by the projectile are in the ratio of:
questions.explanation: Horizontal range. $$ \mathrm{R}=\frac{u^{2}\sin2\theta}{g} $$ When angle of projection is $ (45^{\circ}-\theta) $ then, $$ \begin{aligned}R_{1}&=\frac{u^{2}\sin2(45^{\circ}-\theta)}{g}\\&=\frac{u^{2}\sin(90^{\circ}-2\theta)}{g}\\&=\frac{u^{2}\cos2\theta}{g}\end{aligned} $$ If angle of projection is $ (45^{\circ} + \theta) $ then, $$ \begin{array}{l}\text{R}_{2}=\displaystyle\frac{u^{2}\sin2(45^{\circ}+2\theta)}{g}\\ \quad=\displaystyle\frac{u^{2}\sin(90^{\circ}+2\theta)}{g}\\ \quad=\displaystyle\frac{u^{2}\cos2\theta}{g}\end{array} $$ Now, $$ \frac{R_{1}}{R_{2}}=\frac{u^{2}\cos\frac{2\theta}{g}}{u^{2}\cos\frac{2\theta}{g}}=1:1 $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: The maximum range of a gun of horizontal terrain is 16 km. If $g = 10\ \text{ms}^{-2}$, then muzzle velocity of a shell must be:
questions.explanation: Range of projectile is given by, $$ \mathrm{R}=\frac{u^{2}\sin2\theta}{g} $$ For maximum range, angle should be $ 45^{\circ} $. $$ \begin{array}{l}\text{R}_{\text{max}}=\displaystyle\frac{u^{2}\sin2\times45^{\circ}}{g}\\=\displaystyle\frac{u^{2}\sin90^{\circ}}{g}\end{array} $$ Or $$ R_{max}=\frac{u^{2}}{g} $$ Here, $$ \begin{aligned}R_{max}&=\frac{u^{2}}{g}=16km\\&=16000m\end{aligned} $$ or $$ \begin{array}{l}u=\sqrt{16000\times g}\\=\sqrt{16000\times10}\\=400\mathrm{~m s}^{-1}\end{array} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Let \omega_{1}, \omega_{2} and \omega_{3} be the angular speed of the second hand, minute hand and hour hand of a smoothly running analog clock, respectively. If x_{1}, x_{2} and x_{3} are their respective angular distances in 1 minute then the factor which remains constant (k) is:
questions.explanation: $ \omega_{1}x_{1}=\omega_{2}x_{2}=\omega_{3}x_{3}=k $ The product of angular speed and angular distance remains constant for each hand of the clock.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A particle moving with uniform speed in a circular path maintains:
questions.explanation: A particle moving with uniform speed in a circular path has varying velocity as the direction of motion changes continuously. The magnitude of velocity remains constant. As the direction of centripetal acceleration is changing. The particle has varying acceleration. ##### △! Caution Students should not get confused about the direction of acceleration. It is always directed towards centre but position of particle keep changing and hence overall direction of acceleration vector changes.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A wheel of a bullock cart is rolling on a level road as shown in the figure below. If its linear speed is v in the direction shown, which one of the following options is correct (P and Q are any highest and lowest points on the wheel, respectively)?
questions.optionA: Point P moves faster than point Q.
questions.optionB: Both the points P and Q move with equal speed.
questions.optionD: Point P moves slower than point Q.
questions.explanation: In case of rolling, the velocity of the point of contact with the surface is zero. Also, the velocity of the point of the highest point of the rolling body is twice the velocity of COM of the body. So. point body P moves faster then point Q.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A particle moving in a circle of radius R with a uniform speed takes a time T to complete one revolution. If this particle was projected with the same speed at an angle $\theta$ to the horizontal, the maximum height attained by it equals to 4R. The angle of projection, $\theta$, is then given by:
questions.explanation: Given, $$ \begin{array}{l}Radius=R\\Time=T for one revolution\end{array} $$ Angle of projection = $ \theta $ $$ H_{max}=4R $$ $$ \mathbf{v}=\frac{2\pi\mathbf{R}}{\mathbf{T}}\quad(from~circular~motion) $$ $$ \mathrm{H}_{\max}=\frac{\mathrm{v}_{y}^{2}}{2g}=\frac{\mathrm{v}^{2}\sin^{2}\theta}{2g}=4\mathrm{R} $$ $$ \frac{4\pi^{2}R^{2}}{T^{2}}\times\frac{\sin^{2}\theta}{2g}=4R $$ $$ \sin^{2}\theta=\frac{8gT^{2}}{4\pi^{2}R}=\frac{2gT^{2}}{\pi^{2}R} $$ $$ \theta=\sin^{-1}\left(\frac{2g\mathrm{T}^{2}}{\pi^{2}\mathrm{R}}\right)^{1/2} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A particle moves, so that its position vector is given by $\vec{r} = \cos \omega t \, \hat{x} + \sin \omega t \, \hat{y}$, where $\omega$ is a constant. Which of the following statement is true?
questions.optionA: Velocity and acceleration both are parallel for r.
questions.optionB: Velocity is perpendicular to $r$ and acceleration is directed towards the origin
questions.optionC: Velocity is perpendicular to $r$ and acceleration is directed away from the origin
questions.optionD: Velocity and acceleration both are perpendicular to r.
questions.explanation: $$ \begin{aligned}\overrightarrow{r}&=\cos\omega t\overrightarrow{x}+\sin\omega t\overrightarrow{y}\\\overrightarrow{v}&=\frac{\overrightarrow{d}\overrightarrow{r}}{dt}=-\omega\sin\omega t\overrightarrow{x}+\omega\cos\omega t\overrightarrow{y}\\\overrightarrow{a}&=\frac{\overrightarrow{d}\overrightarrow{v}}{dt}=-\omega^{2}\cos\omega t\overrightarrow{x}-\omega^{2}\sin\omega t\overrightarrow{y}\\&=-\omega^{2}\overrightarrow{r}\end{aligned} $$ $ \overrightarrow{a} $ is directed towards the origin. Also, $ \overrightarrow{r} \cdot \overrightarrow{v} = 0 $, Hence, $$ \overrightarrow{r}\perp\overrightarrow{v} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: An electric fan has blades of length 30 cm measured from the axis of rotation. If the fan is rotating at 120 rev/min, the acceleration of a point on the tip of the blade is:
questions.explanation: We have, centripetal acceleration of rotating body Since, $$ \begin{array}{l}a_{c}=\frac{v^{2}}{r}=\frac{v^{2}\omega^{2}}{r}=r\omega^{2}\quad(\text{as}v=r\omega)\\\omega=2\pi v,\end{array} $$ where v is frequency of rotation. ∴ $$ a_{c}=r\left(2\pi\mathbf{v}\right)^{2}=r\times4\pi^{2}\mathbf{v}^{2} $$ Here, $$ r=30cm=30\times10^{-2}m=0.30m $$ $$ \mathrm{v}=120\mathrm{rev}/\mathrm{m}=\frac{120}{60}\mathrm{rev}/\mathrm{s}=2\mathrm{rev}/\mathrm{s} $$ $$ \begin{array}{l}a_{c}=(0.30\times4\times3.14\times3.14\times2\times2)\\=47.4\mathrm{~m s}^{-2}\end{array} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Physical independence of force is a consequence of:
questions.explanation: Newton's first law of motion is related to physical independence of force.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A football player is moving southward, suddenly turns eastward with the same speed to avoid an opponent. The force that acts on the player while turning is:
questions.explanation: Given. Acceleration $$ \begin{array}{l}\overset{\rightarrow}{u}=-u\overset{\wedge}{j}\\ \to\quad\overset{\wedge}{v}=ui\end{array} $$ $$ \vec{a}=\frac{\mathbf{v}-\mathbf{u}}{t} $$ $$ \vec{a}=\frac{\hat{u i}+\hat{u j}}{t} $$ Direction of acceleration $$ \tan\theta=\frac{\mathbf{v}}{u}=1 $$ $ \theta = 45^{\circ} $, along northeast. Force is also acting towards north-east. $$ h=10m $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A ball of mass 0.15 kg is dropped from a height 10 m, strikes the ground and rebounds to the same height. The magnitude of impulse imparted to the ball is $ (g = 10 \, \text{m/s}^2) $ nearly:
questions.explanation: Given. $$ m=0.15\mathrm{~k g} $$ Velocity just before striking the ground. $$ \mathbf{v}_{1}=\sqrt{2gh} $$ $$ \mathbf{v}_{1}=\sqrt{2\times(10\times10)} $$ $$ =10\sqrt{2}\mathrm{~m/s} $$ $$ v_{1}=-10\sqrt{2} $$ If it reaches same height, speed remain same after collision only the direction changes. $$ v_{2}=10\sqrt{2}\ m/s $$ $$ \overrightarrow{v}_{2}^{}=10\sqrt{2} $$ $$ \begin{align*}\text{Impulse}&=m\left|\Delta\overrightarrow{v}\right|\\&=m\left|10\sqrt{2}\hat{j}-(-10\sqrt{2}\hat{j})\right|\\&=0.15\left[2\left(10\sqrt{2}\right)\right]\\&=3\sqrt{2}\text{kg m/s}\\&=4.2\text{kg m/s}.\end{align*} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A particle moving with velocity v is acted by three forces shown by the vector triangle PQR. The velocity of the particle will:
questions.optionC: change according to the smallest force QR
questions.explanation: As forces are forming closed loop in same order. So, Net force = 0 $$ ↳\;\downarrow\;↳: $$ $$ \begin{array}{l}\overrightarrow{\mathrm{F}_{\mathrm{net}}}=0\\m\frac{d\overrightarrow{\mathrm{v}}}{dt}=0\\\quad\rightarrow\\\quad\mathrm{v}=\mathrm{constant}\end{array} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A rigid ball of mass m strikes a rigid wall at 60^{\circ} and gets reflected without loss of speed as shown in the figure. The value of impulse imparted by the wall on the ball will be:
questions.explanation: Impulse, $$ \begin{aligned}|\overrightarrow{\Delta\mathrm{P}}|&=m|\overrightarrow{\Delta\mathrm{v}}|\\&=m(2\times\cos60^{\circ})=m\mathrm{v}\end{aligned} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: The force F acting on a particle of mass m is indicated by the force-time graph shown below. The change in momentum of the particle over the time interval from 0 s to 8 s is:
questions.explanation:
The F-t graph is,
Now, Change of momentum, For $$ I^{st}\rightarrow\Delta\mathbf{P}_{1}=\frac{1}{2}\times2\times6=6kg-m/s $$ For $\mathrm{II}^{\mathrm{nd}} \rightarrow \Delta \mathrm{P}_{2} = 2\left(-3\right) = -6\ \mathrm{kg} \cdot \mathrm{m/s}$ For $ \mathrm{III}^{\mathrm{rd}} \rightarrow \Delta \mathrm{P}_{3} = 4 \times 3 = 12 \, \mathrm{kg} - \mathrm{m/s} $ Total change in momentum, $$ \begin{aligned}\Delta\mathrm{P}&=\Delta\mathrm{P}_{1}+\Delta\mathrm{P}_{2}+\Delta\mathrm{P}_{3}\\&=6-6+12\\&=12\mathrm{~Ns}\end{aligned} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Three blocks with masses m, 2m and 3m are connected by strings, as shown in the figure. After and upward force F is applied on block m, the masses move upward constant speed v. What is the net force on the block of mass 2m? (g is the acceleration due to gravity)
questions.explanation: Using law of conservation of momentum, Initial momentum = mu₁ + mu₂ final momentum = nmv₁ + (M - nm)v₂ ∴ $ mu_1 + mu_2 = nmv_1 + (M - nm)v_2 $ $ 0 = \frac{10 \times 10}{1000} \times 800 + \left(100 - \frac{10 \times 10}{1000}\right)v_2 $ $ -80 = \frac{999}{10}v_2 $ $ \Rightarrow v_2 = -0.8 \, \text{ms}^{-1} $ And average force exerted on the person, $ =\frac{n \times m \times u}{\Delta t} $ $ = \frac{10 \times \frac{10}{1000} \times 800}{5} $ $ =\frac{80}{5} = 16 \, \text{N} $

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A stone is dropped from a height h. It hits the ground with a certain momentum p. If the same stone is dropped from a height 100% more than the previous height, the momentum, when it hits the ground, will change by:
questions.explanation: We know that, $$ \mathbf{v}=\sqrt{2gh} $$ and momentum, $$ p=m\mathbf{v} $$ $$ p\propto\sqrt{h} $$ $$ \Rightarrow $$ $$ \bullet \quad \bullet\ast $$ $$ \frac{p_{2}}{p_{1}}=\sqrt{\frac{h_{2}}{h_{1}}}=\sqrt{\frac{2h}{h}}=\sqrt{2} $$ $$ p_{2}=\sqrt{2}p_{1} $$ $$ \bullet \quad \bullet\bullet $$ $$ p_{2}=1.414p_{1} $$ $$ \%change=\frac{p_{2}-p_{1}}{p_{1}}\times100\%=41\% $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A body of mass M hits normally a rigid wall with velocity v and bounces back with the same velocity. The impulse experienced by the body is:
questions.explanation: Impulse = Change in linear momentum $ = M v - (-M v) = 2 M v $

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A body, under the action of a force $ \overrightarrow{F}=6\overrightarrow{i}-8\overrightarrow{j}+10\overrightarrow{k} $, acquires an acceleration of $ 1\ m s^{-2} $. The mass of this body must be:
questions.explanation: We know that, $$ Force=mass\times acceleration. $$ ∴ $$ \�d_{F}=6\stackrel{\wedge}{i-8}\stackrel{\wedge}{j+10}\stackrel{\wedge}{k} $$ $$ \begin{aligned}|\mathrm{F}|&=\sqrt{6^{2}+(8)^{2}+10^{2}}\\&=\sqrt{36+64+100}\\&=10\sqrt{2}\,\mathrm{N}\end{aligned} $$ and $$ a=1~\mathrm{m s}^{-2} $$ $$ m=\frac{10\sqrt{2}}{1}=10\sqrt{2}\ kg $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A particle of mass $m$ is projected with velocity $v$ making an angle of $45^{\circ}$ with the horizontal from level ground. When the particle lands on the level ground the magnitude of the change in its momentum will be:
questions.explanation:
The situation is shown in the figure,
Change in momentum $$ \begin{aligned}\overrightarrow{\Delta\mathrm{P}}&=\overrightarrow{\mathrm{P}}_{f}-\overrightarrow{\mathrm{P}}_{i}=m(\mathrm{v}_{f}-\mathrm{v}_{i})\\&=m[(\mathrm{v}\cos45^{\circ}\stackrel{\wedge}{i}-\mathrm{v}\sin45^{\circ}\stackrel{\wedge}{j})\\&\quad-(\mathrm{v}\cos45^{\circ}\stackrel{\wedge}{i}+\mathrm{v}\sin45^{\circ}\stackrel{\wedge}{j})]\\&=m\left[\left(\frac{\mathrm{v}}{\sqrt{2}}\stackrel{\wedge}{i}-\frac{\mathrm{v}}{\sqrt{2}}\stackrel{\wedge}{j}\right)-\left(\frac{\mathrm{v}}{\sqrt{2}}\stackrel{\wedge}{i}+\frac{\mathrm{v}}{\sqrt{2}}\stackrel{\wedge}{j}\right)\right]\\&=-\sqrt{2}m\mathrm{v}\stackrel{\wedge}{j}\end{aligned} $$ $$ \vert\overrightarrow{\Delta\mathbf{P}}\vert=-\sqrt{2}\;m\mathbf{v} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A 0.5 kg ball moving with a speed of 12 m/s strikes a hard wall at an angle of $30^{\circ}$ with the wall. It is reflected with the same speed at the same angle. If the ball is in contact with the wall for 0.25 seconds, the average force acting on the wall is:
questions.explanation:
