Components of momentum parallel to the wall are in the same direction and components of momentum perpendicular to the wall are opposite to each other. Therefore, change of momentum = 2mv sin $\theta$
$ F \times t = \text{Change in momentum} = 2mv \sin \theta $ ∴ $$ \begin{array}{l}\text{F}=\frac{2mv\sin\theta}{t}\\=\frac{2\times0.5\times12\times\sin30^{\circ}}{0.25}\\=48\times\frac{1}{2}=24\text{N}\end{array} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: An object of mass 3 kg is at rest. Now a force of $ \vec{F}=6t^{2}\hat{i}+4t\hat{j} $ is applied on the object then velocity of object at $ t=3 $ s is:
questions.explanation: Mass. $$ m=3\mathrm{~k g}, $$ force, $$ \mathrm{F}=6t^{2}\hat{i}+4t\hat{j} $$ ∴ acceleration. $$ a=\frac{F}{m}=\frac{6t^{2}\hat{i}+4t\hat{j}}{3} $$ $$ a=2t^{2}\hat{i}+\frac{4}{3}t\hat{j} $$ Now, $$ a=\frac{dv}{dt}=2t^{2}\hat{i}+\frac{4}{3}t\hat{j} $$ $$ d\mathbf{v}=\left(2t^{2}\hat{i}+\frac{4}{3}t\hat{j}\right)dt $$ $$ \begin{array}{c} \displaystyle \mathbf{v} =\int_{0}^{3} \left(2 t^{2} \hat{i} + \frac{4}{3} t \hat{j}\right) dt \\ \displaystyle = \left[ \frac{2}{3} t^{3} \hat{i} + \frac{4}{6} t^{2} \hat{j} \right]_{0}^{3} = 18 \hat{i} + 6 \hat{j}. \end{array} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A cricketer catches a ball of mass 150 g in 0.1 sec moving with speed 20 m/s, then he experiences force of:
questions.explanation: Impulse = Change in momentum $$ \mathrm{~F~}.\Delta t=m\,\mathbf{v}; $$ $$ \begin{aligned}F&=\frac{m.v}{\Delta t}\\&=\frac{150\times10^{-3}\times20}{0.1}=30N\end{aligned} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: If the force on a rocket, moving with a velocity of 300 m/s is 210 N, then the rate of combustion of the fuel is:
questions.explanation: Force = $ \frac{d}{dt} $ (momentum) = $ \frac{d}{dt} $ (mv) = v $ \left(\frac{dm}{dt}\right) $ $ \Rightarrow 210 = 300\left(\frac{dm}{dt}\right) $ $ \frac{dm}{dt} = rate of combustion $ $ \frac{210}{300} = 0.7 kg/s $

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A body of mass 3 kg hits a wall at an angle of $ 60^{\circ} $ and returns at the same angle. The impact time was 0.2 s. The force exerted on the wall is:
questions.explanation: Change in momentum = $ mv_2\sin\theta - (mv_1\sin\theta) $ = 2 $ mv\sin\theta $ = 2 $ \times $ 3 $ \times $ 10 $ \times $ $ \sin60^{\circ} $ = 60 $ \times $ $ \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} $ $$ \begin{array}{l}Force=\frac{Change~in~momentum}{Impact~time}\\ \quad=\frac{30\sqrt{3}}{0.2}\\ \quad=150\sqrt{3}\mathrm{N}\end{array} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A bullet is fired from a gun. The force on the bullet is given by $ F = 600 - (2 \times 10^{5}) $ t. Where, F is in newton and t in seconds. The force on the bullet becomes zero as soon as it leaves the barrel. What is the average impulse imparted to the bullet?
questions.explanation: When $$ F=600-2\times10^{5}t=0 $$ $$ t=\frac{600}{2\times10^{5}}=3\times10^{-3}s $$ Now, impulse, $$ \begin{aligned}\mathrm{I}&=\int\limits_{0}^{t}F d t=\int\limits_{0}^{t}\left(600-2\times10^{5}t\right)d t\\&=600\ t-2\times10^{5}\frac{t^{2}}{2}\\&=600\times3\times10^{-3}-10^{5}\times(3\times10^{-3})^{2}\\\mathrm{I}&=1.8-0.9=0.9\;\mathrm{Ns}\end{aligned} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A force of 6 N acts on a body at rest and of mass 1 kg. During this time, the body attains a velocity of 30 m/s. The time for which the force acts on the body is:
questions.explanation: and Final velocity (v) = 30 m/s Therefore, $$ acceleration,(a)=\frac{F}{m}=\frac{6}{1}=6\ m/s^{2} $$ and velocity, $ (v)=30=u+at=0+6\times t $ or $ t = 5 \text{ seconds} $.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A 10 N force is applied on a body produce in it an acceleration of 1 m/s². The mass of the body is:
questions.explanation: Force (F) = 10 N and acceleration (a) = 1 m/s $ ^{2} $. mass $ m = \frac{F}{a} = \frac{10}{1} = 10 \, kg. $

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A particle of mass m is moving with a uniform velocity v_{1}. It is given an impulse such that its velocity becomes v_{2}. The impulse is equal to:
questions.explanation: Impulse is a vector quantity and is equal to change in momentum of the body, thus, (same as $F \times t$, where $t$ is short) $$ \mathrm{Impulse}=mv_{2}-mv_{1}=m\left(v_{2}-v_{1}\right) $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Sand is being dropped on a conveyor belt at the rate of M kg/s. The force necessary to keep the belt moving with constant velocity of v m/s will be:
questions.explanation: Force required, $ F = \frac{d(mv)}{dt} $ $ = v\left(\frac{dm}{dt}\right) = vM $ as velocity v is constant Hence, $ \mathrm{F}=\mathrm{Mv} $

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A 5000 kg rocket is set for vertical firing. The exhaust speed is 800 ms^{-1}. To give an initial upward acceleration of 20 ms^{-2}, the amount of gas ejected per second to supply the needed thrust will be (g = 10 ms^{-2}):
questions.explanation: $$ \mathrm{Thrust}=\mathbf{M}(g+a)=u\frac{dm}{dt} $$ $$ \begin{array}{l}\frac{dm}{dt}=\frac{M(g+a)}{u}\\=\frac{5000(10+20)}{800}\\ =187.5\ kg/s \end{array} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: In a rocket, fuel burns at the rate of 1 kg/s. This fuel is ejected from the rocket with a velocity of 60 km/s. This exerts a force on the rocket equal to:
questions.explanation: Rate of burning of fuel, $ \left(\frac{dm}{dt}\right)=1\ kg/s $ and velocity of ejected fuel, (v) = 60 km/s = 60 × 10³ m/s Force = Rate of change of momentum = $ \frac{dp}{dt} = \frac{d(mv)}{dt} $ = v $ \frac{dm}{dt} $ =(60 × 10³) × 1 = 60000 N

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A satellite in free space sweeps stationary interplanetary dust at a rate of $ \frac{dM}{dt} = \alpha v $, where M is mass and v is the speed of satellite and $ \alpha $ is a constant. The acceleration of satellite is:
questions.explanation: Rate of change of mass, $$ =\frac{d\mathbf{M}}{dt}=\alpha\mathbf{v}. $$ Retarding force = Rate of change of momentum = Velocity $ \times $ Rate of change in mass $ = -v \times \frac{dM}{dt} $ $ = -v \times \alpha v = -\alpha v^{2} $. (Minus sign of v due to deceleration) Therefore, acceleration $ = -\frac{\alpha v^{2}}{M} $.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A 600 kg rocket is set for a vertical firing. If the exhaust speed is 1000 ms^{-1}, the mass of the gas ejected per second to supply the thrust needed to overcome the weight of rocket is:
questions.explanation: Thrust is the force with which the rocket moves upward given by, $$ \mathrm{F}=u\frac{dm}{dt} $$ Thus mass of the gas ejected per second to supply the thrust needed to overcome the weight of the rocket is $$ \frac{dm}{dt}=\frac{F}{u}=\frac{ma}{u} $$ or $$ \begin{array}{l}\frac{dm}{dt}=\frac{600\times10}{1000}\\ =6\mathrm{kg}\mathrm{s}^{-1}\end{array} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: An object flying in air with velocity $(20\hat{i}+25\hat{j}-12\hat{k})$ suddenly breaks in two pieces whose masses are in the ratio 1:5. The smaller mass flies off with a velocity $(100\hat{i}+25\hat{j}-8\hat{k})$. The velocity of the larger piece will be:
questions.explanation: Let $m$ be the mass of the object, which is flying with velocity $(20\hat{i}+25\hat{j}-12\hat{k})$ is in air. According to the question, the mass breaks into two pieces of ratio 1:5, that means the small piece is $ \frac{m}{6} $ and another piece is $ \frac{5m}{6} $. Using law of conservation of momentum, Initial momentum = Final momentum $$ \begin{array}{r} \begin{aligned}m\left(20\hat{i}+25\hat{j}-12\hat{k}\right)&=\frac{m}{6}\left(100\hat{i}+35\hat{j}-8\hat{k}\right)\\ &\quad+\frac{5}{6}m\vec{v}\end{aligned} \end{array} $$ $$ \begin{array}{l}\Rightarrow(120\hat{i}+150\hat{j}-72\hat{k})=(100\hat{i}+35\hat{j}+8\hat{k})+5\vec{v}\\\Rightarrow\quad\begin{array}{r}\vec{v}=\frac{1}{5}\left(20\hat{i}+115\hat{j}-80\hat{k}\right)\\ \vec{v}=4\hat{i}+23\hat{j}-16\hat{k}\end{array}\end{array} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A person holding a rifle (mass of person and rifle together is 100 kg) stands on a smooth surface and fires 10 shots horizontally, in 5 s. Each bullet has a mass of 10 g with a muzzle velocity of 800 ms⁻¹. The final velocity acquired by the person and the average force exerted on the person are:
questions.explanation: Using law of conservation of momentum,

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: An explosion breaks a rock into three parts in a horizontal plane. Two of them go off at right angles to each other. The first part of mass 1 kg moves with a speed of 12 ms^{-1} and the second part of mass 2 kg moves with 8 ms^{-1} speed. If the third part flies off with 4 ms^{-1} speed, then its mass is:
questions.explanation:
We know that momentum, P = mv
∴ Using law of conservation of momentum, $$ \begin{array}{c} 1\times12\hat{i}+2\times8\hat{j}+\overrightarrow{\mathrm{\bf p}_{3}}=0\end{array} $$ $$ \mathbf{p}_{3}=-\left(12\hat{i}+16\hat{j}\right) $$ $$ \begin{array}{l} \text{p3|} =\displaystyle {\sqrt{(12)^{2} + (16)^{2}}} \\ \qquad = \sqrt{144 + 256} \\ \qquad = 20 \text{ kg m/s} \end{array} $$ Now, $$ p_{3}=m_{3}\mathbf{v}_{3} $$ $$ m_{3}=\frac{\mathbf{p}_{3}}{\mathbf{v}_{3}}=\frac{20}{4}=5\mathrm{~kg} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A man of 50 kg mass is standing in a gravity free space at a height of 10 m above the floor. He throws a stone of 0.5 kg mass downwards with a speed of $ 2 \, m s^{-1} $. When the stone reaches the floor, the distance of the man above the floor will be:
questions.explanation: Time taken by stone to reach the ground is $$ t=\frac{s}{u_{1}}=\frac{10}{2}=5s $$ Using conservation of moment, $$ m_{1}u_{1}=m_{2}u_{2} $$ $$ \Rightarrow $$ $$ u_{2}=\frac{0.5\times2}{50}=\frac{1}{50}m/s $$ Distance travelled by man in 5 s $$ =\frac{1}{50}\times5=0.1m $$ Distance from the ground. = 10 + 0.1 = 10.1 m

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A man fires a bullet of mass 200 g at a speed of 5 m/s. The gun is of one kg mass. By what velocity the gun rebounds backward?
questions.explanation: Mass of bullet $ m_{1} $ = 200 g = 0.2 kg; $$ (\mathbf{v}_{1})=5\mathrm{~m/s} $$ and mass of gun $ (m_{2}) = 1 \, kg $. Before firing, total momentum is zero. After firing total momentum is = $ m_1v_1 + m_2v_2 $. From the law of conservation of momentum, $$ m_{1}\mathbf{v}_{1}+m_{2}\mathbf{v}_{2}=0 $$ or $$ \mathbf{v}_{2}=\frac{-m_{1}\mathbf{v}_{1}}{m_{2}}=\frac{-0.2\times5}{1}=-1\ m/s $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A body of mass 5 kg explodes at rest into three fragments with masses in the ratio 1:1:3. The fragments with equal masses fly in mutually perpendicular directions with speeds of 21 m/s. The velocity of heaviest fragment in m/s will be:
questions.explanation: Since, 5 kg body explodes into three fragments with masses in the ratio 1:1:3 thus, masses of fragments will be 1 kg, 1 kg and 3 kg respectively. The magnitude of resultant momentum of two fragments each of mass 1 kg, moving with velocity 21 m/s, in perpendicular directions is $$ \begin{aligned}&=\sqrt{\left(m_{1}\mathbf{v}_{1}\right)^{2}+\left(m_{2}\mathbf{v}_{2}\right)^{2}}\\&=\sqrt{\left(21\right)^{2}+\left(21\right)^{2}}=21\sqrt{2}\mathrm{~kg}m/s\end{aligned} $$ According to law of conservation of linear momentum, $$ m_{3}v_{3}=21\sqrt{2} $$ or $$ 3\mathbf{v}_{3}=21\sqrt{2} $$ or $$ \mathbf{v}_{3}=7\sqrt{2}\mathrm{~m/s} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Two blocks A and B of masses 3m and m respectively are connected by a massless and inextensible string. The whole system is suspended by a massless spring as shown in figure. The magnitudes of acceleration of A and B immediately after the string is cut, are respectively:
questions.explanation:
This problem has two cases,
Case (I): The system is in equilibrium with mass 3m (weight = 4mg) over the spring. Case (II): When the string is cut according to the figure then: (a) Free body diagram $ \underline{\text{of}} $ mass m.
Here, force on mass m = mg $ \therefore $ Acceleration = g (b) Free body diagram of mass 3m Net force, $$ \mathrm{F_{net}}=4mg-3mg $$ ： $$ 3m.a.=mg $$ $$ a=\frac{g}{3} $$
$\therefore$ Accelerations of blocks A and B are $\frac{g}{3}$ and $g$ respectively.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A horizontal force 10 N is applied to a block X as shown in figure. The mass of blocks X and Y are 2 kg and 3 kg, respectively. The blocks slide over a frictionless surface. The force exerted by block X on block Y is:
questions.explanation:
From the given diagram, we can clearly say that the bodies are connected. In connected motion, Common acceleration $ a=\frac{F}{M} $ where F is net force and M is the total mass, F = 10 N, M = 5 kg $$ a=\frac{10}{5}=2m/s^{2} $$ Now from FBD of 2 kg block and 3 kg block
Force exerted by block X on block Y, $$ F_{YX}=(3)a $$ $$ \mathrm{F}_{\mathrm{YX}}=3\times2=6\mathrm{N} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Calculate the maximum acceleration of a moving car so that a body lying on the floor of the car remains stationary. The coefficient of static friction between the body and the floor is 0.15: $ (g = 10 \, \text{ms}^{-2}) $.
questions.explanation: As retarding force = force of friction $$ \begin{array}{l}\therefore\quad\quad\quad ma=\mu mg\\\Rightarrow\quad\quad\quad a=\mu\times g\\\Rightarrow\quad\quad\quad a=0.15\times10\\\Rightarrow\quad\quad\quad a=1.5\ m/s^{2}\end{array} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A conveyor belt is moving at a constant speed of 2 m/s. A box is gently dropped on it. The coefficient of friction between them is $\mu = 0.5$. The distance that the box will move relative to belt before coming to rest on it (taking $g = 10 \, \text{ms}^{-2}$) is:
questions.explanation: Here, $$ \mathrm{F}=\mu m\mathrm{g} $$ Retardation of the block on the belt. $$ a=\frac{\mathrm{F}}{m}=\frac{\mu mg}{m}=\mu g $$ ∴ From third equation of motion. $$ \begin{aligned}\mathrm{v}^{2}&=u^{2}+2a s\\0&=(2)^{2}-2(\mu\mathrm{g})\mathrm{s}\\s&=\frac{4}{2\times0.5\times10}=0.4\mathrm{~m}\end{aligned} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Calculate the acceleration of the block and trolly system shown in the figure. The coefficient of kinetic friction between the trolly and the surface is 0.05. ($g = 10\ m/s^{2}$, mass of the string is negligible and no other friction exists).
questions.explanation:
The situation is shown in figure.
Here, $a$ = acceleration of the system. Here, from free body diagram. $$ T-\mu R=10a $$ $$ \therefore\mathrm{T}-0.05\times10\mathrm{g}=10a\quad\{\mu\mathrm{R}=0.05\times100=5\} $$ $$ T-5=10a $$ For 2 kg block, the eq. of motion, $$ 2g-T=2a $$ $$ 2\times10-T=2a $$ $$ 20-T=2a $$ From equation (i) and (ii), we get $$ T-5=10a $$ $$ 20-T=2a $$ $$ 20-5=12a $$ $$ 15=12a $$ $$ a=\frac{15}{12}=\frac{5}{4}=1.25\mathrm{~m s}^{-2}. $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A body of mass m is kept on a rough horizontal surface (coefficient of friction = \mu). Horizontal force is applied on the body, but it does not move. The resultant of normal reaction and the frictional force acting on the object is given as F, where F is:
questions.explanation:
Since, body does not move, hence it is in equilibrium. $f_{r}=$ Frictional force which is less than or equal friction. F' = Horizontal force applied on body.
Now. $$ N=mg $$ Hence, $$ \begin{array}{r}{\vec{\mathrm{~F~}}=\vec{\mathrm{~N~}}+\vec{f},}\end{array} $$ $$ \left|\overrightarrow{F}\right|^{2}\leq(mg)^{2}+(\mu mg)^{2} $$ $$ |\overrightarrow{\mathrm{F}}|\leq m g\sqrt{1+\mu^{2}} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Which one of the following statements is INCORRECT?
questions.optionA: Frictional force opposes the relative motion.
questions.optionB: Limiting value of static friction is directly proportional to normal reaction.
questions.optionC: Rolling friction is smaller than sliding friction.
questions.optionD: Coefficient of sliding friction has dimension of length.
questions.explanation: The coefficient of sliding friction is, $$ \mu_{s}=\frac{\mathrm{N}}{\mathrm{F_{s l i d i n g}}} $$ The dimension of N and F are same. So, $ \mu $ is a dimensionless quantity.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A plank with a box on its one end is gradually raised about the other end. As the angle of inclination with the horizontal reaches $ 30^{\circ} $, the box starts to slip and slides 4.0 m down the plank in 4.0 s. The coefficient of static and kinetic friction between the box and the plank will be respectively:
questions.explanation:
The situation is shown in the figure.
Here, coefficient of static friction, $$ \mu_{s}=\tan30^{\circ}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}=0.6 $$ And distance covered by plank, $$ s=u t+\frac{1}{2}a t^{2} $$ Here, $u = 0$ and $a = g$ ($\sin \theta - \mu_k \cos \theta$) $$ \circ\circ $$ $$ 4=\frac{1}{2}g(\sin30^{\circ}-\mu_{k}\cos30^{\circ}) $$ $$ 0.5=10\times\frac{1}{2}-\mu_{k}\times10\times\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} $$ $$ \begin{array}{l}\Rightarrow\\ \quad\Rightarrow\\ \quad\Rightarrow\end{array}\quad\begin{array}{l}5\sqrt{3}\ \mu_{k}=4.5\\ \mu_{k}=0.51\end{array} $$ Coefficient of kinetic friction is 0.51

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A block A of mass $m_{1}$ rests on a horizontal table. A light string connected to it passes over a frictionless pulley at the edge of table and from its other and, another block B of mass $m_{2}$ is suspended. The coefficient of kinetic friction between the block and the table is $\mu_{k}$. When the block A is sliding on the table, the tension $T$, in the string is: $$ \frac{(m_{2}+\mu_{k}m_{1})g}{(m_{1}+m_{2})} $$ $$ \frac{(m_{2}-\mu_{k}m_{1})g}{(m_{1}+m_{2})} $$
questions.explanation:
The situation is.
Let T be the tension in the string $a$ is acceleration of the block. Then from free body diagram of A and B is
From both the diagram, we have, $$ m_{2}g-T=m_{2}a $$ $$ \mathrm{T}-\mu_{k}m_{1}g=m_{1}a $$ From eq. (i) and (ii), $$ m_{2}g-\mu_{k}m_{1}g=(m_{1}+m_{2})a $$ $$ a=\frac{(m_{2}-\mu_{k}m_{1})g}{m_{1}+m_{2}} $$ Putting the value of $a$ in eq. (i), $$ \begin{aligned}\mathrm{T}&=m_{2}g-m_{2}a\\&=m_{2}g-m_{2}\left[\frac{(m_{2}-\mu_{k} m_{1})g}{m_{1}+m_{2}}\right]\\&=\ m_{2}g\left[1-\frac{(m_{2}-\mu_{k} m_{1})}{m_{1}+m_{2}}\right]\end{aligned} $$ $$ \mathrm{T}=\frac{m_{1}m_{2}(1+\mu_{k})g}{m_{1}+m_{2}} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Three blocks A, B and C of masses 4 kg. 2 kg and 1 kg respectively, are in contact on a frictionless surface, as shown. If a force of 14 N is applied on the 4 kg block, then the contact force between A and B is:
questions.explanation:
From the question, It is given that, $ M_{A} = 4\ kg $, $ M_{B} = 2\ kg $, $ M_{C} = 1\ kg $ $$ \begin{aligned}\mathbf{M}&=\mathbf{M}_{\mathbf{A}}+\mathbf{M}_{\mathbf{B}}+\mathbf{M}_{\mathbf{C}}\\&=(4+2+1)=7\mathrm{kg}\end{aligned} $$ We know, $$ \mathrm{F=M a} $$ $$ 14=7a $$
Let f be the contact force between A and B then net force, F - f = 4a $$ 14-f=4\times2 $$ $$ f=6\textbf{N} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A system consists of three masses $m_{1}, m_{2}$ and $m_{3}$ connected by a string passing over a pulley P. The $m_{1}$ hangs freely and $m_{2}$ and $m_{3}$ are on a rough horizontal table (the coefficient of friction = $\mu$). The pulley is frictionless and of negligible mass. The downward acceleration of mass $m_{1}$ is (Assume, $m_{1} = m_{2} = m_{3} = m_{1}m_{2} = m_{3}m_{1} = m_{2}m_{3} = m_{1}m_{2} = m_{3}m_{1} = m_{2}m_{3} = m_{1}m_{2} + m_{3}m_{1
questions.explanation:
Consider free body diagram for block $m_{1}$,
Here $$ m_{1}=m_{2}=m_{3} $$ $$ mg-T_{1}=ma $$ For block $ m_{2} $ and $ m_{3} $, $$ T_{1}-2\mu mg=2ma $$ Solving eq. (i) and (ii) $$ mg-T_{1}=ma $$ $$ T_{1}-2\mu mg=2\ ma $$ $$ mg-2\mu mg=3ma $$ $$ mg(1-2\mu)=3\mu a $$ ∴ $$ a=\frac{g(1-2\mu)}{3} $$ This is downward acceleration of mass.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: The upper half of an inclined plane of inclination $\theta$ is perfectly smooth while lower half is rough. A block starting from rest at the top of the plane will again come to rest at the bottom. The coefficient of friction between the block and lower half of the plane is given by:
questions.explanation: For first half, Acceleration = g sin $\theta$ $$ \mathbf{v}^{2}=2(g\sin\theta)l $$ For second half, Acceleration = g (sin $\theta - \mu_{k}$ cos $\theta$) $$ 0=\mathbf{v}^{2}+2g\left(\sin\theta-\mu_{\mathbf{k}}\cos\theta\right)l\ldots(\mathrm{i i}) $$ Putting eq. (i) in (ii), we get $$ \begin{aligned}{0=2\left(g\operatorname{s i n}\theta\right)l+\left(2g\operatorname{s i n}\theta\right)l}\\ {-\left(2g\;\mu_{\mathbf{k}}\operatorname{c o s}\theta\right)l}\\ \end{aligned} $$ $ \therefore \mu_{k} \cos \theta = 2 \sin \theta $

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: The mass of a lift is 2000 kg. When the tension in the supporting cable is 28000 N, then its acceleration is:
questions.explanation:
$$ \upmu_{\mathbf{k}}=2\tan\theta $$
When the lift is accelerating upward. Then, Apparent weight > actual weight Then the equation becomes, $$ R-mg=ma $$ $$ 28000-20000=2000a $$ $$ \begin{array}{r} \begin{aligned}a&=\frac{8000}{2000}\\ &=4\ m s^{-2}\end{aligned} \end{array} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A block of mass 5 kg is placed on horizontal surface and a pushing force 20 N is acting on block as shown in figure. If coefficient of friction between block and surface is 0.2, then calculate speed of block after 15 s. (Given $ g = 10 \, m/s^{2} $):
questions.explanation:
The different forces acting on the block are shown in the given figure.
Horizontal component = 20 cos 45° Vertical component = 20 sin 45° Now we can write. $$ 20\cos45^{\circ}-f=5a $$ Where $$ \begin{aligned}f&=frictional~force\\&=\mu N\end{aligned} $$ $$ =0.2\times\aleph $$ And $$ \begin{array}{l}\text{N}=5\text{g}+20\sin45^{\circ}\\=50+10\sqrt{2}\end{array} $$ Considering eq. (i) frictional force in eq. (i) $$ 10\sqrt{2}-0.2(50+10\sqrt{2}) $$ solving we get $$ a=0.2624 $$ ∴ speed of block after 15 sec. is $$ \begin{aligned}\mathbf{v}&=u+at\\&=0.2624\times15=3.936\mathrm{~ms}^{-1}\end{aligned} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A block B is pushed momentarily along a horizontal surface with an initial velocity v. If $\mu$ is the coefficient of sliding friction between B and the surface, block B will come to rest after a time.
questions.explanation: By using $$ \mathbf{v}=u+at $$ $$ 0=\mathbf{v}-\mu g t $$ $$ t=\frac{\mathbf{v}}{\mu g} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: The coefficient of static friction \mu_{s}, between block A of mass 2 kg and the table as shown in the figure is 0.2. What would be the maximum mass value of block B, so that the two blocks do not move? The string and the pulley are assumed to be smooth and massless. $ g = 10 \, m/s^{2} $:
questions.explanation:
According to the question, $$ \mathrm{T}=m_{\mathrm{B}}g=\mu_{S}\left(m_{\mathrm{A}}g\right) $$
$$ \begin{aligned}m_{\mathrm{B}}&=\mu_{s} m_{\mathrm{A}}=0.2\times2\\&=0.4\mathrm{kg}\end{aligned} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A block of mass 10 kg placed on the rough horizontal surface having coefficient of friction $\mu = 0.5$, if a horizontal force of 100 N acting on it then acceleration of the block will be:
questions.explanation: $$ \begin{aligned}&Here\quad&f_{max}&=\mu N=\mu mg\\&&\quad&=0.5\times10\times10=50N\\&\Rightarrow\quad&a&=\frac{net force}{mass}\\&&\quad&=\frac{100-50}{10}=5m/s^{2}\end{aligned} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A lift of mass 1000 kg which is moving with an acceleration of $ 1 \, m s^{-2} $ in the upward direction, then the tension developed in the string which is connected to lift is:
questions.explanation: Using the equation, $$ \begin{aligned}T&=m(g+a)=1000(9.8+1)\\&=10800N\end{aligned} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A block has been placed on an inclined plane with the slope angle $\theta$, block slides down the plane at constant speed. The coefficient of kinetic friction is equal to:
questions.explanation:
Angle of sliding is defined as the minimum angle of inclination of a plane with the horizontal, such that a body placed on the plane just begins to slide down. AB is an inclined plane such that a body placed on it just begins to slide down.
$$ \angle BAC=\theta=angle~of~repose $$ In equilibrium, and $$ f=m g\sin\theta $$ $$ \mathrm{R}=m g\cos\theta $$ $$ \frac{f}{\mathrm{R}}\;=\;\frac{m g\sin\theta}{m g\cos\theta}=\tan\theta $$ $$ \upmu=\tan\theta $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A heavy uniform chain lies on horizontal table top. If the coefficient of friction between the chain and the table surface is 0.25, then the maximum friction of the length of the chain that can hang over one edge of the table is:
questions.explanation: The force of friction should balance the weight of chain hanging. If M is the mass of whole chain of length L and x is the length of chain hanging to balance, then μ $$ \frac{\mathbf{M}}{\mathbf{L}}\left(\mathbf{L}-x\right)g=\frac{\mathbf{M}}{\mathbf{L}}x g $$ or $$ \mu\left(L-x\right)=x $$ or $$ x=\frac{\mu L}{\mu+1}=\frac{0.25L}{1.25}\quad(As,\mu=0.25) $$ ∴ $$ x=\frac{L}{5} $$ or $$ \frac{x}{L}=\frac{1}{5} $$ i.e., fraction of chain will be $$ =\frac{1}{5}\times100=20\% $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A bob is whirled in a horizontal circle by means of a string at an initial speed of 10 rpm. If the tension in the string is quadrupled while keeping the radius constant, the new speed is:
questions.explanation:
Initial speed = 10 rpm Tension of string before = T Tension of string after = 4T $$ \mathrm{F_{cp}=m\omega_{1}^{2}} $$ $$ \mathrm{T}=m\omega_{2}^{2} $$ New tension become 4T $$ 4\mathrm{T}=m r\omega_{r}^{2} $$
Substituting value of T in (ii) we get $$ \omega_{2}^{2}=\frac{4mr\omega_{1}^{2}}{mr} $$ $$ \bullet \quad \bullet\ast $$ $$ \omega_{2}^{2}=4\times(10)^{2}\qquad\quad(\because\omega_{1}=10\mathrm{~r p m}) $$ $$ \omega_{2}^{2}=400 $$ $$ \omega_{2}=20\mathrm{r p m} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A block of mass 10 kg is in contact against the inner wall of a hollow cylindrical drum of radius 1 m. The coefficient of friction between the block and the inner wall of the cylinder is 0.1. The minimum angular velocity needed for the cylinder to keep the block stationary when the cylinder is vertical and rotating about its axis, will be $ g = 10 \, m/s^{2} $:
questions.explanation: To keep the block stationary Frictional force $ \geq $ weight $$ \mu N\geq M g $$ Here. $$ \mathbf{N}=\mathbf{M}\omega^{2}r\left\{\mathrm{Hence},r=1\mathrm{~m},\mu=0.1\right\} $$ $$ \mu M\omega^{2}r=M g $$ $$ \begin{aligned}\omega&=\sqrt{\frac{g}{\mu r}}=\sqrt{\frac{10}{0.1\times1}}\\&=10\ rad s^{-1}\end{aligned} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: One end of the string of length $l$ is connected to a particle of mass $m$ and the other end is connected to a small peg on a smooth horizontal table. If the particle moves in circle with speed v, the net force on the particle (directed towards center) will be (T represents the tension in the string):
questions.explanation:
According to the question, string $l connected to a particle is shown below.
v = Speed of particle Since, the particle is in uniform circular motion, so net force is equal to centripetal force. ∴ Centripetal force = Net force $$ \frac{mv^{2}}{l}=T $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: In the given figure, $a = 15\ \mathrm{m/s^{2}}$ represents the total acceleration of a particle moving in the clockwise direction in a circle of radius $R = 0.2\ \mathrm{m}$ at a given instant of time. The speed of the particle is:
questions.explanation:
The particle is moving in circle of radius R. So acceleration will be:
$$ a_{c}=\frac{v^{2}}{R} $$ from figure $$ a_{c}=a\cos30^{\circ} $$ $$ \frac{v^{2}}{R}=a\cos30^{\circ} $$ $$ \begin{aligned}\mathbf{v}^{2}&=a\cos30^{\circ}\times\mathbf{R}\\&=15\times\cos30^{\circ}\times2.5\end{aligned} $$ $$ \mathbf{v}^{2}=15\times2.5\times\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} $$ $$ \mathbf{v}=5.7~\mathbf{m}/\mathbf{s} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A car is negotiating a curved road of radius R. The road is banked at an angle \theta. The coefficient of friction between the tyres of the car and the road is \mu_{s}. The maximum safe velocity on this road is:
questions.explanation: For vertical equilibrium on the road. $$ \mathrm{~N~c o s}\theta=m g+f\sin\theta $$ $$ mg=N\cos\theta-f\sin\theta $$ Centripetal force for safe turning, $$ \mathbf{N}\sin\theta+f\cos\theta=\frac{mv^{2}}{R} $$ From eqns. (i) and (ii), we get $$ \frac{v^{2}}{Rg}=\frac{N\sin\theta+f\cos\theta}{N\cos\theta-f\sin\theta} $$ U $$ \frac{v_{\mathrm{max}}^{2}}{Rg}=\frac{N\sin\theta+\mu_{s}N\cos\theta}{N\cos\theta-\mu_{s}N\sin\theta} $$ $$ \mathbf{v}_{\max}=\sqrt{\mathrm{R}g\left(\frac{\mu_{s}+\tan\theta}{1-\mu_{s}\tan\theta}\right)} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Two stones of masses $m$ and $2m$ are whirled in horizontal circles, the heavier one in a radius $\frac{r}{2}$ and the lighter one in radius $r$. The tangential speed of lighter stone is $n$ times that on the value of heavier stone when they experience same centripetal forces. The value of $n$ is:
questions.explanation: Let v be tangential speed of heavier stone. Then, centripetal force experienced by lighter stone is, $$ (F_{c})_{lighter}=\frac{m(nv)^{2}}{r} $$ and that of heavier stone is $$ (F_{c})_{\mathrm{heavier}}=\frac{2mv^{2}}{r/2} $$ But, $$ (F_{c})_{lighter}=(F_{c})_{heavier} $$ $$ \frac{m(n v)^{2}}{r}=\frac{2mv^{2}}{(r/2)} $$ or, $$ n^{2}\left(\frac{m\mathbf{v}^{2}}{r}\right)=4\left(\frac{m\mathbf{v}^{2}}{r}\right) $$ $$ n^{2}=4or n=2 $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A car is moving in a circular horizontal track of radius 10 m with a constant speed of 10 m/s. A bob is suspended from the roof of the car by a light wire of length 1.0 m. The angle made by the wire with the vertical is:
questions.explanation: Let $ \theta $ is the angle made by the wire with the vertical. $$ \theta=\frac{v^{2}}{rg} $$ Here, $$ v=10m/s,r=10m,g=10m/s^{2} $$ $$ \tan\theta=\frac{(10\mathrm{m/s})^{2}}{10\mathrm{m}(10\mathrm{m/s}^{2})}=1 $$ $$ \theta=\tan^{-1}\left(1\right)=\frac{\pi}{4} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A car of mass m is moving on a level circular track of radius R. If \mu_{s} represents the static friction between the road and tyres of the car, the maximum speed of the car in circular motion is given by:
questions.explanation: Force of friction provides the necessary centripetal force. $$ \frac{mv^{2}}{R}\leq\mu_{s}N; $$ $$ \mathbf{v}^{2}\leq\frac{\boldsymbol{\mu}_{s}\mathbf{R}\mathbf{N}}{m} $$ $$ \begin{aligned}\mathbf{v}^{2}&\leq\mu_{s}\mathbf{R}\mathbf{g}\\\mathbf{v}&\leq\sqrt{\mu_{s}\mathbf{R}\mathbf{g}}\end{aligned} $$ or ∴ The maximum speed of the car in circular motion is $$ \mathrm{v}_{\max}=\sqrt{\mu_{s}R g} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A car of mass 1000 kg negotiates a banked curve of radius 90 m on a frictionless road. If the banking angle is $45^{\circ}$, the speed of the car is:
questions.explanation: Given banking angle = $ 45^{\circ} $ Radius = 90 m and g = 10 m/s^{2} Now banking angle, $\tan\theta=\frac{v^{2}}{rg}$ $$ \tan45^{\circ}=\frac{v^{2}}{90\times10} $$ $$ \begin{aligned}v^{2}&=90\times10\times\tan45^{\circ}\\v&=\sqrt{90\times10\times\tan45^{\circ}}=30\end{aligned} $$ $$ \bullet \quad \bullet\bullet $$ Speed of car, v = 30 m/s

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A ball of mass 0.25 kg attached to the end of a string of length 1.96 m is moving in a horizontal circle. The string will break if the tension is more than 25 N. What is the maximum speed with which the ball can be moved?
questions.explanation:
For a ball to move in horizontal circle, the ball should satisfy the condition Tension in the string = Centripetal force $$ \mathrm{T}_{\max}=\frac{\mathbf{M}\mathbf{v}_{\max}^{2}}{\mathrm{R}} $$ $$ \mathbf{v}_{\mathbf{max}}\ =\sqrt{\frac{\mathbf{T}_{\mathbf{max}}\cdot\mathbf{R}}{\mathbf{M}}} $$ Making substitution, we obtain, $$ \mathrm{v}_{\max}=\sqrt{\frac{25\times1.96}{0.25}}=\sqrt{196}=14\mathrm{~m}/\mathrm{s} $$
##### Caution Students should know that in a vertical circle, the tension at the highest point is zero and at lowest point is maximum.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Two racing cars of masses $m$ and $4m$ are moving in circles of radii $r$ and $2r$ respectively. If their speeds are such that each makes a complete circle in the same time, then the ratio of the angular speeds of the first to the second car is:
questions.explanation: We have. $$ \omega=\frac{2\pi}{T} $$ Since, both cars take the same time to complete the circle. Therefore, ratio of angular speeds of the cars will be 1:1.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A box of mass 5 kg is pulled by a cord, up along a frictionless plane inclined at $30^{\circ}$, with the horizontal. The tension in the cord is 30 N. The acceleration of the box is (Take $g = 10\ \text{ms}^{-2}$):
questions.explanation: Mass of the box = 5 kg inclined Angle of Plane, $ \theta = 30^{\circ} $ Tension in cord, T = 30 N $$ ma=T-mg\sin\theta $$ $$ 5a=30-5\times10\left(\sin30^{\circ}\right) $$ $$ 5a=30-\left(\begin{array}{c}50\times\frac{1}{2}\\ \frac{24}{5}\end{array}\right) $$ $$ 5a=5 $$ $$ a=1\mathrm{~m/s^{2}} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A block of mass $m$ is placed on a smooth inclined wedge ABC of inclination $\theta$ as shown in the figure. The wedge is given an acceleration $a$ towards the right. The relation between $a$ and $\theta$ for the block to remain stationary on the wedge is:
questions.explanation: In non-inertial frame. N sin $\theta = ma$ N cos $\theta = mg$ From eq. (i) and (ii), we get $$ \tan\theta=\frac{a}{g} $$ $$ a=g\tan\theta $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A balloon with mass $m$ is descending down with an acceleration $a$ (where $a < g$). How much mass should be removed from it so that it starts moving up with an acceleration $a?$
questions.explanation:
Let F be the upthrust of the air. As the balloon is descending down with an acceleration a, $$ mg-F=ma $$
Let mass $m_{0}$ be removed from the balloon, so that it starts moving up with an acceleration $a$. Then. $$ \mathrm{F}-(m-m_{0})g=(m-m_{0})a $$ $$ \mathrm{F}-m g+m_{0}g=m a-m_{0}a $$ Adding eqn. (i) and eqn. (ii), we get $$ m_{0}g=2ma-m_{0}a; $$ $$ m_{0}g+m_{0}a=2m a $$ $$ m_{0}(g_{*}+a)=2m a $$ $$ m_{0}=\frac{2ma}{g+a} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Three blocks with mass m, 2m and 3m are connected by strings, as shown in the figure. After an upward force F is applied on block m, the masses move upward at constant speed v. What is the net force on the block of mass 2m? (g is the acceleration due to gravity)
questions.explanation: As all blocks are moving with constant speed, therefore, acceleration is zero. So, net force on each block is zero.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A person of mass 60 kg is inside a lift of mass 940 kg and presses the button on control panel. The lift starts moving upwards with an acceleration 1.0 m/s². If g = 10 ms⁻², the tension in the supporting cable is:
questions.explanation:
Here, Mass of a person, m = 60 kg Mass of lift, M = 940 kg. $$ a=1\ m/s^{2},g=10\ m/s^{2} $$
Let T be the tension in the supporting cable. $$ \begin{aligned}\therefore\mathrm{~T}-(\mathbf{M}+\boldsymbol{m})\boldsymbol{g}&=(\mathbf{M}+\boldsymbol{m})\boldsymbol{a}\\ \mathrm{T}&=(\mathbf{M}+\boldsymbol{m})(\boldsymbol{a}+\boldsymbol{g})\\ &=(940+60)(1+10)\\ &=11000\mathrm{~N}\end{aligned} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A block of mass m is placed on a smooth wedge of inclination \theta. The whole system is accelerated horizontally so that the block does not slip on the wedge. The force exerted by the wedge on the block will be (g is acceleration due to gravity):
questions.explanation:
The wedge is given an acceleration to the left. ∴ The block has a pseudo acceleration to the right, pressing against the wedge because of which the block is not moving.
$ \therefore mg\sin\theta=ma\cos\theta $ or $$ a=\frac{g\sin\theta}{\cos\theta} $$ Total reaction of the wedge on the block is, $$ \mathrm{N}=m g\cos\theta+m a\sin\theta $$ or $$ \mathrm{N}=m g\cos\theta+\frac{m g\sin\theta\sin\theta}{\cos\theta} $$ or $$ \mathbf{N}=\frac{mg\left(\cos^{2}\theta+\sin^{2}\theta\right)}{\cos\theta}=\frac{mg}{\cos\theta} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A monkey of mass 20 kg is holding a vertical rope. The rope will not break when a mass of 25 kg is suspended from it but will break if the mass exceeds 25 kg. What is the maximum acceleration with which the monkey can climb up along the rope? (g = 10 m/s²):
questions.explanation: Let T be the tension in the rope when monkey climbs up with an acceleration a. Then, $$ T-mg=ma $$ $$ 25g-20g=20a $$ $$ \begin{array}{l}a=\frac{5\times10}{20}\\ =2.5\ m/s^{2}\end{array} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A mass M is placed on a very smooth wedge resting on a surface without friction. Once the mass is released, the acceleration to be given to the wedge so that M remains at rest is a where
questions.optionA: $a$ is applied to the left and $a = g \tan\theta$
questions.optionB: $a$ is applied to the right and $a = g \tan\theta$
questions.optionC: $a$ is applied to the left and $a = g \sin\theta$
questions.optionD: $a$ is applied to the left and $a = g \cos\theta$
questions.explanation:
The pseudo acceleration for the body $a^{\prime}=a$ If the pseudo force, Ma cos $\theta = Mg \sin \theta$, then the body will be at rest.
$$ a=g\tan\theta $$ This horizontal acceleration should be applied to the wedge to the left.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A monkey is descending from the branch of a tree with constant acceleration. If the breaking strength of branch is 75% of the weight of the monkey, the minimum acceleration with which monkey can slide down without breaking the branch is:
questions.explanation: Let T be the tension in the branch of a tree when monkey is descending with acceleration a. Thus, $$ mg-T=ma $$ also, T = 75% of weight of monkey $$ \mathrm{T}=\left(\frac{75}{100}\right)mg=\frac{3}{4}mg $$ ∴ $$ ma=mg-\left(\frac{3}{4}\right)mg=\frac{1}{4}mg $$ or $$ a=\frac{g}{4} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A particle moves so that its position vector is given by $ r = \cos \omega x + \sin \omega y $, where $ \omega $ is a constant. Which of the following is true?
questions.optionA: Velocity is perpendicular to $r$ and acceleration is directed towards the origin.
questions.optionB: Velocity is perpendicular to $r$ and acceleration is directed away from the origin.
questions.optionC: Velocity and acceleration both are parallel to r
questions.optionD: Velocity and acceleration both are perpendicular to r
questions.explanation: Given, $ \vec{r} = \cos \omega t \, \hat{x} + \sin \omega t \, \hat{y} $ $ \therefore $ $ \vec{v} = \frac{d\vec{r}}{dt} = -\omega \sin \omega t \, \hat{x} + \omega \cos \omega t \, \hat{y} $ $ \vec{a} = \frac{d\vec{v}}{dt} = -\omega^2 \cos \omega t \, \hat{x} - \omega^2 \sin \omega t \, \hat{y} = -\omega^2 \vec{r} $ Since, position vector $ (\vec{r}) $ is directed away from the origin, so, acceleration $ (-\omega^2 \vec{r}) $ is directed towards the origin. Also, $ \vec{r} \cdot \vec{v} = (\cos \omega t \hat{x} + \sin \omega t \hat{y}) $. $ (-\omega \sin \omega t \hat{x} + \omega \cos \omega t \hat{y}) $ $ = -\omega \sin \omega t \cos \omega t + \omega \sin \omega t \cos \omega t = 0 $ $ \Rightarrow $ $ \vec{r} \perp \vec{v} $

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: If vectors $ \overrightarrow{A} = \cos\omega t + \sin\omega t $ and $ \overrightarrow{B} = \cos\frac{\omega t}{2}t + \sin\frac{\omega t}{2}t $ are functions of time, then the value of $ t $ at which they are orthogonal to each other is:
questions.explanation: Two vectors $\overrightarrow{A}$ and $\overrightarrow{B}$ are orthogonal to each other, if their scalar product is zero i.e. $\overrightarrow{A} \cdot \overrightarrow{B} = 0$ Here, $\overrightarrow{A} = \cos \omega t \overrightarrow{i} + \sin \omega t \overrightarrow{j}$ and $\overrightarrow{B} = \cos \frac{\omega t}{2} \overrightarrow{i} + \sin \frac{\omega t}{2} \overrightarrow{j}$ $\therefore \overrightarrow{A} \cdot \overrightarrow{B} = (\cos \omega t \overrightarrow{i} + \sin \omega t \overrightarrow{j}) \cdot \left( \cos \frac{\omega t}{2} \overrightarrow{i} + \sin \frac{\omega t}{2} \overrightarrow{j} \right)$ $$= \cos \omega t \cos \frac{\omega t}{2} + \sin \omega t \sin \frac{\omega t}{2}$$ $$= \cos \left( \omega t - \frac{\omega t}{2} \right)$$ But $ \overline{A} $ $ \cdot\overline{B}=0 $ (as $ \overline{A} $ and $ \overline{B} $ are orthogonal to each other) $ \therefore \cos\left(\omega t-\frac{\omega t}{2}\right)=0 $ $ \cos\left(\omega t-\frac{\omega t}{2}\right)=\cos\frac{\pi}{2} $ or $ \omega t - \frac{\omega t}{2} = \frac{\pi}{2} $ $ \frac{\omega t}{2} = \frac{\pi}{2} $ or $ t = \frac{\pi}{\omega} $

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: If a vector $ 2\hat{i} + 3\hat{j} + 8\hat{k} $ is perpendicular to the vector $ 4\hat{j} - 4\hat{i} + \alpha\hat{k} $, then the value of $ \alpha $ is:
questions.explanation: $ \vec{a} = 2\hat{i} + 3\hat{j} + 8\hat{k} $, $ \vec{b} = 4\hat{j} - 4\hat{i} + \alpha\hat{k} $ $ \vec{a} \cdot \vec{b} = 0 $ if $ \vec{a} \perp \vec{b} $ $ (2\hat{i} + 3\hat{j} + 8\hat{k}) $. $ (-4\hat{i} + 4\hat{j} + \alpha\hat{k}) = 0 $ $$ \begin{aligned}&or,&-8+12+8\alpha&=0\\&\Rightarrow&4+8\alpha&=0\\&\Rightarrow&\alpha&=-\frac{1}{2}\end{aligned} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: The vector sum of two forces is perpendicular to their vector differences. In that case, the forces:
questions.optionB: are equal to each other in magnitude
questions.optionC: are not equal to each other in magnitude
questions.explanation: Given: $ (\overrightarrow{\mathrm{F}_{1}} + \overrightarrow{\mathrm{F}_{2}}) \perp (\overrightarrow{\mathrm{F}_{1}} - \overrightarrow{\mathrm{F}_{2}}) $ $$ (\vec{\mathbf{F}}_{1}+\vec{\mathbf{F}}_{2}).(\vec{\mathbf{F}}_{1}-\vec{\mathbf{F}}_{2})=0 $$ $$ \overrightarrow{\mathbf{F}}_{1}^{2}-\overrightarrow{\mathbf{F}}_{2}^{2}-\overrightarrow{\mathbf{F}}_{1}.\overrightarrow{\mathbf{F}}_{2}+\overrightarrow{\mathbf{F}}_{2}.\overrightarrow{\mathbf{F}}_{1}=0 $$ $$ \overrightarrow{F}_{1}^{2}=+\overrightarrow{F}_{2}^{2}\quad(\because\overrightarrow{a},\overrightarrow{b}=\overrightarrow{b}.\overrightarrow{a}) $$ i.e., $F_{1}$, $F_{2}$ are equal to each other in magnitude.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: The position vector of a particle is $ \vec{r} = (a \cos \omega t) \hat{i} + (a \sin \omega t) \hat{j} $. The velocity of the particle is:
questions.explanation: Position vector of the particle, $$ \hat{r}=(a\cos\omega t)i+(a\sin\omega t)j $$ velocity vector $$ \begin{aligned}\overline{\mathbf{v}}=&\frac{\overline{d}\overline{r}}{dt}=(-\alpha\omega\sin\omega t)\hat{i}+(a\omega\cos\omega t)\hat{j}\\=&\omega\left[(-\alpha\sin\omega t)\hat{i}+(a\cos\omega t)\hat{j}\right]\end{aligned} $$ $$ \begin{aligned}\vec{\mathbf{v}}\cdot\vec{r}&=\omega a[-\sin\omega t\hat{i}+\cos\omega t\hat{j}].[a\cos\omega t\hat{i}+\\&\quad a\sin\omega t\hat{j}]\\&=\omega[-a^{2}\sin\omega t\cos\omega t+a^{2}\cos\omega t\sin\omega t]=0\end{aligned} $$ Therefore, velocity vector is perpendicular to the position vector.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.subtopic: Notions of Work and Kinetic Energy: The Work-Energy Theorem
questions.question: An object moving along horizontal x-direction with kinetic energy 10 J is displaced through $x = (3i)$ m by the force $\overrightarrow{F} = (-2i + 3\overrightarrow{j}) N$. The kinetic energy of the object at the end of the displacement $x$ is:
questions.explanation: K.E of an object $ (K_{i})=10\ J $ $$ \begin{aligned}\boldsymbol{x}&=(3\hat{i})\boldsymbol{m}\\\overrightarrow{\boldsymbol{F}}&=(-2\hat{i}+3\hat{j})\mathbf{N}\\\mathbf{W}&=\mathbf{K}_{f}-\mathbf{K}_{i}\\\mathbf{W}&=\mathbf{F}.\boldsymbol{s}\\&=(-2\hat{i}+3\hat{j})\times(3\hat{i})\\\mathbf{W}&=-6\mathbf{J}\\\mathbf{K}_{f}&=4\mathbf{J}\end{aligned} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.subtopic: Notions of Work and Kinetic Energy: The Work-Energy Theorem
questions.question: A particle of mass 5m at rest suddenly breaks on its own into three fragments. Two fragments of mass m each move along mutually perpendicular direction with each speed v. The energy released during the process is:
questions.explanation:
From law of conservation of linear momentum,
$$ \therefore0=m\hat{v}j+m\hat{v}i+3m\vec{v_{1}} $$ $$ \overrightarrow{\mathbf{v}_{1}}=-\frac{\mathbf{v}}{3}(\hat{i}+\hat{j}) $$ $$ \mathbf{v}_{1}=\frac{\sqrt{2}}{3}\mathbf{v} $$ $$ \therefore\mathrm{~K E}_{i}=0 $$ $$ \begin{aligned}\mathrm{KE}_{f}&=\frac{1}{2}mv^{2}+\frac{1}{2}mv^{2}+\frac{1}{2}(3m)\left(\frac{\sqrt{2}}{3}\right)^{2}v^{2}\\&=mv^{2}+\frac{mv^{2}}{3}=\frac{4}{3}mv^{2}\end{aligned} $$ $$ \Delta\mathrm{KE}=\mathrm{KE}_{f}-\mathrm{KE}_{i}=\frac{4}{3}mv^{2} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.subtopic: Notions of Work and Kinetic Energy: The Work-Energy Theorem
questions.question: Consider a drop of rain water having mass 1 g falling from a height of 1 km. It hits the ground with a speed of 50 m/s. Take g, constant with a value of 10 m/s². The work done by the (i) gravitational force and (ii) the resistive force of air is:
questions.explanation: Work done by gravitational force, $$ \begin{aligned} & \text{w} = mgh \\ \therefore & \quad \text{W} = 10^{-3} \times 10 \times 1 \times 10^{3} = 10\,\text{J} \\ & \text{From work energy theorem}, \\ & \text{Work done} = \text{change in KE} \\ & \quad \Delta K = W_{\text{gravity}} + W_{\text{air resistance}} \\ & \quad \frac{1}{2}mv^{2} - 0 = mgh + W_{\text{air resist}} \\ & \quad \frac{1}{2} \times 10^{-3} \times (50)^{2} = 10 + W_{\text{air resist}} \\ & \quad W_{\text{air resist}} = \frac{1}{2} \times 10^{-3} \times 50 \times 50 - 10 \\ & \quad = 1.25 - 10 \\ & \quad W_{\text{air resist}} = -8.75\,\text{J} \end{aligned} $$ $$ \mathbf{W}=mgh $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.subtopic: Notions of Work and Kinetic Energy: The Work-Energy Theorem
questions.question: A particle of mass 10 g moves along a circle of radius 6.4 cm with a constant tangential acceleration. What is the magnitude of this acceleration if the kinetic energy of the particle becomes equal to $ 8 \times 10^{-4} $ J by the end of the second revolution after the beginning of the motion?
questions.explanation: Here, $ m = 10 \, \text{g} = 10^{-2} \, \text{kg} $, $ R = 6.4 \, \text{cm} = 6.4 \times 10^{-2} \, \text{m} $, $ K_f = 8 \times 10^{-4} \, \text{J} $, $ K_i = 0 $ Using work energy theorem, Work done by all the forces = Change in KE $ W_{tangential force} + W_{centripetal force} = K_f - K_i $ $ \Rightarrow a_t = \frac{K_f}{4\pi R m} $ $ =\frac{8 \times 10^{-4}}{4 \times \frac{22}{7} \times 6.4 \times 10^{-2} \times 10^{-2}} $ $ = 0.099 = 0.1 \, \text{ms}^{-2} $

questions.subject: Physics
questions.subtopic: Notions of Work and Kinetic Energy: The Work-Energy Theorem
questions.question: A block of mass 10 kg moving in x-direction with a constant speed of 10 ms^{-1}, is subjected to a retarding force F = 0.1 x J/m during its travel from x = 20 m to 30 m. Its final KE will be:
questions.explanation: According to the question, Mass of block, $m = 10\ kg$ Speed of block, $v = 10\ ms^{-1}$ Retarding force, $f = 0.1\ x\ J/m$ Using work energy theorem, Work done = change in kinetic energy $\mathrm{W} = \mathrm{KE}_{f} - \mathrm{KE}_{i}$ $\therefore \quad \mathrm{KE}_{f} = \mathrm{W} + \mathrm{KE}_{i}$ $=\int_{20}^{30}-0.1\times xdx+\frac{1}{2}\times10\times10^{2}$ $=\left[-0.1\frac{x^{2}}{2}\right]_{20}^{30}+500$ $$ \begin{aligned}&=-0.1\times\frac{500}{2}+500\\&=-25+500=475J\end{aligned} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.subtopic: Notions of Work and Kinetic Energy: The Work-Energy Theorem
questions.question: A body of mass 10 kg, released from a wall of height 20 m, acquires a velocity of 10 ms^{-1} after falling through the distance 20 m then the work done by the push of the air on the body is:
questions.explanation: From work energy theorem. $$ \begin{aligned}Work~done~&=change~in~K.E.\\\therefore\quad W_{gravity}+W_{air}&=\Delta KE\\\Rightarrow\quad mgh+W_{air}&=\frac{1}{2}mv^{2}-\frac{1}{2}mu^{2}\end{aligned} $$ Putting the values in above equation. $$ 10\times10\times20+W_{air}=\frac{1}{2}(10)(10)^{2}-0 $$ $$ \mathbf{W}_{air}=-1500\mathrm{J} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.subtopic: Notions of Work and Kinetic Energy: The Work-Energy Theorem
questions.question: A bullet of mass 10 g leaves a rifle at an initial velocity of 1000 m/s and strikes the earth at the same level with a velocity of 500 m/s. The work done in joule to overcome the resistance of air will be:
questions.explanation: According to the work-energy theorem, work done by a force in displacing a body determines the change in kinetic energy of the body. or $$ \begin{aligned}\mathbf{W}&=\Delta\mathbf{K}\mathbf{E}\\&=\text{final}\mathbf{K}\mathbf{E}-\text{initial}\mathbf{K}\mathbf{E}\\&=\frac{1}{2}m\mathbf{v}^{2}-\frac{1}{2}m\mathbf{u}^{2}\end{aligned} $$ Given, $ v = 1000 \, \text{m/s}, m = 10 \, \text{g} $ Putting the values of $m$, $u$ and $v$, we get ： $$ \begin{aligned}\mathbf{W}&=\frac{1}{2}\times0.01[(1000)^{2}-(500)^{2}]\\&=3750\mathbf{J}\end{aligned} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A particle moves from a point $(-2\hat{i}+5\hat{j})$ to $(4\hat{j}+3\hat{k})$ when a force of $(4\hat{i}+3\hat{j})$ N is applied. How much work has been done by the force?
questions.explanation: Here $$ \begin{array}{l}\overrightarrow{r_{1}}=(-2\hat{i}+5\hat{j})\mathbf{m},\\\overrightarrow{r_{2}}=(4\hat{j}+3\hat{k})\mathbf{m}\\\overrightarrow{\mathrm{F}}=(4\hat{i}+3\hat{j})\mathrm{N},\end{array} $$ Work done by force F in moving from $ \overline{r_{1}} $ to $ \overline{r_{2}} $, $$ \mathbf{W}=\mathrm{\overrightarrow{F}}.(\mathrm{\overrightarrow{r_{2}}}-\mathrm{\overrightarrow{r_{1}}}) $$ $$ \begin{aligned}\mathbf{W}&=(4\widehat{i}+3\widehat{j}).(4\widehat{j}+3\widehat{k}+2\widehat{i}-5\widehat{j})\\&=(4\widehat{i}+3\widehat{j}).(2\widehat{i}-\widehat{j}+3\widehat{k})\end{aligned} $$ $$ \mathbf{W}=8+(-3)=5\mathbf{J} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A uniform force of $(3\hat{i}+\hat{j})$ N acts on a particle of mass 2 kg. Hence the particle is displaced from position $(2\hat{i}+\hat{k})$ m to position $(4\hat{i}+3\hat{j}-\hat{k})$ m. The work done by the force on the particle is:
questions.explanation: Work done, W = $ \overrightarrow{F} $.S From the question. $$ \begin{aligned}\vec{\mathrm{F}}&=(3\hat{i}+\hat{j})\mathrm{N}\\\bar{\mathrm{S}}&=\bar{r}_{2}-\bar{r}_{1}\\&\{r_{1}=2\hat{i}+\hat{k};r_{2}=4\hat{i}+3\hat{j}-\hat{k}\}\\&=4\hat{i}+3\hat{j}-\hat{k}-(2\hat{i}+\hat{k})\\&=(2\hat{i}+3\hat{j}-2\hat{k})\mathrm{m}\end{aligned} $$ $ \therefore $ $$ \begin{aligned}\mathbf{W}&=\overrightarrow{\mathbf{F}}\cdot\overrightarrow{\mathbf{S}}=(3\overrightarrow{i}+\overrightarrow{j})\cdot(2\overrightarrow{i}+3\overrightarrow{j}-2\overrightarrow{k})\\&=3\times2+3+0=9\mathbf{J}\end{aligned} $$ $$ \hat{i}\hat{j}=1,\hat{i}\hat{j}=0\hat{j}\hat{j}=1,\hat{j}\hat{k}=0 $$ $$ \hat{k}.\hat{k}=1,\hat{k}j=0\} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A body moves a distance of 10 m along a straight line under the action of a 5 N force. If the work done is 25 J, then angle between the force and direction of motion of the body is:
questions.explanation: Distance $(s)=10\ m$; Force (F)=5 N and work done, (W) = 25 J Work done (W) = Fs cosθ $$ \circlearrowleft $$ $$ 25=5\times10\cos\theta=50\cos\theta $$ or $$ \theta=\frac{25}{50}=\frac{1}{2} $$ or $$ \theta=60^{\circ} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A body, constrained to move in y-direction, is subjected to a force given by $ \overline{F}=(-2\dot{i}+15\dot{j}+6\dot{k}) $ N. The work done by this force in moving the body through a distance of 10 $ \hat{j} $ m along y-axis, is:
questions.explanation: Force. $$ \vec{\mathbf{F}}=(-2\hat{i}+15\hat{j}+6\hat{k})\mathbf{N}, $$ $$ d=10\hat{j} $$ $$ \begin{aligned}\mathbf{W}&=\overrightarrow{\mathbf{F}}\overrightarrow{d}=\left(-2\hat{i}+15\hat{j}+6\hat{k}\right).\left(10\hat{j}\right)\\&=150\text{Nm}=150\text{J}\end{aligned} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: When a body moves with a constant speed along a circle:
questions.optionA: no work is done on it
questions.optionB: no acceleration is produced in it
questions.explanation: As in circular motion, displacement by the object is zero. Hence, no work is done on it.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A body of mass (4m) is lying in xy plane at rest. It suddenly explodes into three pieces. Two pieces each of mass (m) move perpendicular to each other with equal speeds (v). The total kinetic energy generated due to explosion is:
questions.explanation: $\sqrt{2} m v = \text{Resultant momentum of two small masses}$ $\therefore \text{Using law of conversation of momentum, } \sqrt{2} m v = 2 m v'$ $$ \Rightarrow\quad\mathbf{v}^{\prime}=\frac{\mathbf{v}}{\sqrt{2}} $$ Now total kinetic energy by the explosion is, $$ \begin{aligned}\mathbf{K}.\mathbf{E}.&=\frac{1}{2}m\mathbf{v}^{2}+\frac{1}{2}m\mathbf{v}^{2}+\frac{1}{2}\left(2\ m\right)\mathbf{v}^{12}\\&=m\mathbf{v}^{2}+m\left(\frac{\mathbf{v}}{\sqrt{2}}\right)^{2}=m\mathbf{v}^{2}+\frac{m\mathbf{v}^{2}}{2}\\&=\frac{3}{2}m\mathbf{v}^{2}\\ \end{aligned} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: An engine pumps water continuously through a hose. Water leaves the hose with a velocity v and m is the mass per unit length of the water jet. What is the rate at which kinetic energy is imparted to water?
questions.explanation: Let, $ m = \text{mass per unit let} $ $ \therefore $ Rate of change in mass per sec = $ \frac{mx}{t} $ $ = m\frac{x}{t} = mv $ ∴ Rate of change in KE = $ \frac{1}{2}(mv)v^{2} $ $ =\frac{1}{2}mv^{3} $

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A bomb of mass 30 kg at rest explodes into two pieces of masses 18 kg and 12 kg. The velocity of 18 kg mass is $ 6 \, m s^{-1} $. The kinetic energy of the other mass is:
questions.explanation:
According to law of conservation of linear momentum,
$$ 30\times0=18\times6+12\times v $$ $$ \mathbf{v}=-9~\mathbf{m}/\mathrm{s} $$ Negative sign indicates that both fragments move in opposite direction. $$ \begin{array}{l} \text{K.E. of 12 kg} =\displaystyle {\frac{1}{2}} m v^{2} \\ \qquad = \frac{1}{2} \times 12 \times 81 \\ \qquad = 486 \text{ J} \end{array} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A ball of mass 2 kg and another of mass 4 kg are dropped together from a 60 feet tall building. After a fall of 30 feet each towards earth, their respective kinetic energies will be in the ratio of:
questions.explanation: Let $ E_{1} $ and $ E_{2} $ be the K.E. of two bodies. Then, $$ \mathbf{E}_{1}=\frac{1}{2}m_{1}\mathbf{v}_{1}^{2} $$ and $$ \mathbf{E}_{2}=\frac{1}{2}m_{2}\mathbf{v}_{2}^{2} $$ $$ \frac{\mathbf{E}_{1}}{\mathbf{E}_{2}}=\frac{\frac{1}{2}m_{1}\mathbf{v}_{1}^{2}}{\frac{1}{2}m_{2}\mathbf{v}_{2}^{2}}=\frac{m_{1}\mathbf{v}_{1}^{2}}{m_{2}\mathbf{v}_{2}^{2}} $$ The initial velocity of both the bodies are zero. $ \therefore v^{2}=2gh $ is same for both bodies. $$ \frac{\mathrm{E}_{1}}{\mathrm{E}_{2}}=\frac{m_{1}}{m_{2}}=\frac{2}{4}=\frac{1}{2} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A particle of mass $ m_{1} $ is moving with a velocity $ v_{1} $ and another particle of mass $ m_{2} $ is moving with a velocity $ v_{2} $. Both of them have the same momentum but their different kinetic energies are $ E_{1} $ and $ E_{2} $ respectively. If $ m_{1} > m_{2} $ then:

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A stationary particle explodes into two particles of masses m_{1} and m_{2} which move in opposite direction with velocities v_{1} and v_{2}. The ratio of their kinetic energies \frac{E_{1}}{E_{2}} is:
questions.explanation: According to law of conservation of linear momentum. $$ m_{1}\mathbf{v}_{1}=m_{2}\mathbf{v}_{2} $$ $$ \mathrm{E}_{1}=\frac{1}{2}m_{1}\mathbf{v}_{1}^{2},\mathrm{E}_{2}=\frac{1}{2}m_{2}\mathbf{v}_{2}^{2} $$ $$ \frac{\mathrm{E}_{1}}{\mathrm{E}_{2}}=\frac{\frac{1}{2}m_{1}\mathrm{v}_{1}^{2}}{\frac{1}{2}m_{2}\mathrm{v}_{2}^{2}}-\frac{(m_{1}\mathrm{v}_{1})^{2}}{(m_{2}\mathrm{v}_{2})^{2}}\times\frac{m_{2}}{m_{1}} $$ $$ \frac{\mathrm{E}_{1}}{\mathrm{E}_{2}}=\frac{m_{2}}{m_{1}} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: If kinetic energy of a body is increased by 300%, than percentage change in momentum will be:
questions.explanation: Here, $ p_1 = \sqrt{2mE_1} $ and $ p_2 = \sqrt{2mE_2} $ $$ \mathrm{E}_{2}=\mathrm{E}_{1}+\mathrm{E}_{1}\times\frac{300}{100} $$ $$ \begin{aligned}p_{2}&=\sqrt{2m\Big(\mathrm{E}_{1}+\frac{300}{100}\mathrm{E}_{1}\Big)}\\&=\sqrt{2m4\mathrm{E}_{1}}\\&=2p_{1}\end{aligned} $$ So, the momentum will change by 100%.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A particle is projected making an angle of $45^{\circ}$ with horizontal having kinetic energy K. The kinetic energy at highest point will be:
questions.explanation: Kinetic energy of ball = K and angle of projection (\theta)=45^{\circ} Velocity of the ball at the highest point $ = v \cos \theta $ $ = v \cos 45^{\circ} = \frac{v}{\sqrt{2}} $ Therefore, kinetic energy of the ball, $$ \frac{1}{2}m\times\left(\frac{v}{\sqrt{2}}\right)^{2}=\frac{1}{4}mv^{2}=\frac{K}{2} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: If the momentum of a body is increased by 50%, then the percentage increase in its kinetic energy is:
questions.explanation: Let $p_{1}, p_{2}$ be the initial momentum and inversed momentum respectively. So, $$ p_{2}=\frac{150}{100}p_{1} $$ i.e., $$ m\mathbf{v}_{2}=\frac{15}{10}m\mathbf{v}_{1} $$ or $$ (p_{1}=m\mathbf{v}_{1},p_{2}=m\mathbf{v}_{2}) $$ $$ \mathbf{v}_{2}=\frac{15}{10}\mathbf{v}_{1} $$ Now, $$ \begin{array}{l}\frac{\mathrm{E}_{2}}{\mathrm{E}_{1}}=\frac{\frac{1}{2}m\mathrm{v}_{2}^{2}}{\frac{1}{2}m\mathrm{v}_{1}^{2}}=\left(\frac{\mathrm{v}_{2}}{\mathrm{v}_{1}}\right)^{2}\\=\left(\frac{15}{10}\right)^{2}=\frac{225}{100}\end{array} $$ Clearly, $$ \mathrm{E}_{2}>\mathrm{E}_{1} $$ So, percentage increase in K.E. $$ \begin{aligned}&=\frac{(\mathrm{E}_{4}-\mathrm{E}_{1})}{\mathrm{E}_{1}}\times100\\&=\cdot\left(\frac{225}{100}-1\right)\times100=125\%\end{aligned} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: The kinetic energy [AIPMT 1995]
questions.explanation: $$ \mathbf{v}^{2}=u^{2}+2a s\text{or}\mathbf{v}^{2}-u^{2}=2a s $$ or $$ \mathbf{v}^{2}-(0)^{2}=2\times\frac{\mathbf{F}}{m}\times\mathbf{s} $$ or $$ \mathbf{v}^{2}=\frac{2\mathbf{F}\mathbf{s}}{m} $$ and $$ \mathbf{K.E.}=\frac{1}{2}m\mathbf{v}^{2}=\frac{1}{2}m\times\frac{2\mathbf{F}s}{m}=\mathbf{F}s. $$ Thus, K.E. is independent of m or directly proportional to $ m^{0} $.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Two masses of 1 g and 9 g are moving with equal kinetic energies. The ratio of the magnitudes of their respective linear momenta is:

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A particle of mass M is moving in a horizontal circle of radius R with uniform speed v. When it moves from one point to a diametrically opposite point, its:
questions.explanation: On the diametrically opposite points, the velocities have same magnitude but opposite directions. Therefore, change in momentum is $ mv - (-mv) = 2mv $

questions.subject: Physics
questions.subtopic: Work Done by a Variable Force
questions.question: A force $F = 20 + 10y$ acts on a particle in y-direction, where, F is in newton and y in meter. Work done by this force to move the particle from $y = 0$ to $y = 1$ m is:
questions.explanation: Work done by variable force is, $$ \mathbf{W}=\begin{array}{c}\displaystyle\int\limits_{y_{i}}^{y_{f}}\mathbf{F}d y\end{array} $$ Here, $ y_{i}=0 $ and $ y_{f}=1 $ m $$ \begin{aligned}\mathbf{W}&=\int\limits_{y_{i}}^{y_{f}}(20+10y)dy\\&=\left[20y+\frac{10y^{2}}{2}\right]_{0}^{1}=25\mathrm{~J}\end{aligned} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.subtopic: Work Done by a Variable Force
questions.question: An object of mass 500 g, initially at rest acted upon by a variable force whose $x$ components varies with $x$ in the manner shown. The velocities of the object a point $x=8\ \mathrm{m}$ and $x=12\ \mathrm{m}$, would be the respective values of (nearly):
questions.explanation: From work-energy theorem, $ \Delta K = \text{work} = \text{area under } F-x \text{ graph} $ from x = 0 to x = 8 m $$ \frac{1}{2}m\mathrm{v}^{2}=100+30 $$ $$ \frac{1}{2}\times\frac{1}{2}\times\mathrm{v}^{2}=130 $$ $$ \mathbf{v}^{2}=520 $$ $$ \mathrm{v~=~\sqrt{5}20~=22.8~m/s} $$ $$ x=0\text{ to}x=12m $$ from $$ \frac{1}{2}m\mathbf{v}^{2}=100+30-45+20 $$ $$ \begin{array}{l} \text{v } =\sqrt{420} \\ \text{v } = 20.5\ \text{m/s} \end{array} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.subtopic: Work Done by a Variable Force
questions.question: Force F on a particle moving in a straight line varies with distance as shown in the figure. The work done on the particle during its displacement of 12 m is:
questions.explanation: From question, Work done = Area under (F - d) graph $$ \begin{aligned}k\ done&=Area\ under\ (F-d)\ graph\\&=2\times(7-3)+\frac{1}{2}\times2\times(12-7)\\&=8+\frac{1}{2}\times10\\&=8+5=13\ J\end{aligned} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.subtopic: Work Done by a Variable Force
questions.question: A body of mass 3 kg is under a constant force which causes a displacement s (in metres) in it, given by the relation s=\frac{1}{3}t^{2}, where t is in seconds. Work done by the force in 2 seconds is:
questions.explanation: It is given that: $$ s=\frac{t^{2}}{3} $$ Differentiating w.r.t. $t$, we have, $$ \mathrm{U}=\frac{d s}{d t}=\frac{2t}{3} $$ Again differentiating w.r.t. $t$, we have, $$ a=\frac{d^{2}s}{dt^{2}}=\frac{2}{3} $$ Now, $$ \begin{array}{l}\text{W}=\displaystyle\int\text{F}d s=\displaystyle\int m a~d s\\ \quad=\displaystyle\int_{0}^{2}m\times\frac{2}{3}\times\frac{2t}{3}d t\\ \quad=\frac{4}{9}m\displaystyle\int_{0}^{2}t~d t\end{array} $$ $$ \begin{aligned}W&=\frac{4}{9}m\times\left[\frac{t^{2}}{2}\right]_{0}^{2}\\&=\frac{4}{9}\times3\times2=\frac{8}{3}J\end{aligned} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.subtopic: Work Done by a Variable Force
questions.question: A force F acting on an object varies with distance x as shown here. The force is in N and x in m. The work done by the force in moving the object x=0 to x=6 m is:
questions.explanation: Work done = Area under F - x graph. = area of trapezium = $ \frac{1}{2}\times(6+3)\times3=\frac{27}{2}=13.5 $ J

questions.subject: Physics
questions.subtopic: Work Done by a Variable Force
questions.question: A force acts on a 3.0 g particle in such a way that the position of the particle as a function of time is given by $ x = 3t - 4t^{2} + t^{3} $, where x is in metre and t in second. The work done during the first 4 s is:
questions.explanation: Given, $$ x=3t-4t^{2}+t^{3} $$ So, velocity $$ \mathbf{v}=\frac{dx}{dt}=3-8t+3t^{2} $$ At t = 0 s, $$ v_{1}=3-0+0=3m/s $$ At $ t = 4 \, s $, $$ \begin{aligned}V_{2}&=3-8\times4+3\times4^{2}\\&=3-32+48=19m/s\end{aligned} $$ Now, during $t=0$ to $t=4$ s $$ \begin{aligned}work~done&=gain~in~kinetic~energy\\&=\frac{1}{2}m v_{2}^{2}-\frac{1}{2}m v_{1}^{2}\\&=\frac{1}{2}m\left(v_{2}^{2}-v_{1}^{2}\right)\\&=\frac{1}{2}\times3\times10^{-3}\left[(19)^{2}-(3)^{2}\right]\\ \end{aligned} $$ $$ \begin{aligned}[\text{Using},a^{2}-b^{2}]&=(a+b)(a-b)]\\&=1.5\times10^{-3}\times[(19+3)(19-3)]\\&=1.5\times10^{-3}\times22\times16\\&=528\times10^{-3}\text{J}=528\text{mJ}\end{aligned} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.subtopic: Work Done by a Variable Force
questions.question: A position dependent force $ F = (7 - 2x + 3x^{2}) $ N, acts on a small body of mass 2 kg and displaces it from x = 0 to x = 5 m. Work done in joule is:
questions.explanation: Work done by a variable force F in displacement from $x = x_{1}$ to $x = x_{2}$ is given by, Given. $$ \mathbf{W}=\int_{x_{1}}^{x_{2}}\mathbf{F}dx $$ $$ x_{1}=0,x_{2}=5 $$ $$ \mathbf{F}=\left(7-2x+3x^{2}\right)\mathbf{N} $$ $$ \begin{aligned}\mathbf{W}&=\int_{0}^{5}\quad(7-2x+3x^{2})dx\\&=\left[7x-x^{2}+x^{3}\right]_{0}^{5}\\&=\left[7\times5-(5)^{2}+(5)^{3}\right]\\&=\left[35-25+125\right]=135J\end{aligned} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A particle is released from height S from the surface of the Earth. At a certain height its kinetic energy is three times its potential energy. The height from the surface of earth and the speed of the particle at that instant are respectively:
questions.explanation: Given, Height = S Arbitrary height = h where $$ \mathrm{KE}_{h}=3\ \mathrm{PE}_{h} $$ At height 'S', PE = mg S At arbitrary height $h$, $(h < S)$ $$ PE=mgh $$ $$ KE=\frac{1}{2}mv^{2} $$ where $$ \mathbf{v}^{2}=2\mathbf{g}\left(\mathbf{S}-h\right) $$ $$ \begin{aligned}KE&=\frac{1}{2}m2g(S-h)\\&=mg(S-h)\end{aligned} $$ Given. $$ KE_{h}=3PE_{h} $$ mg (S - h) = 3mgh $$ h=\frac{S}{4} $$ $$ \mathbf{v}^{2}=2g\left(\mathbf{S}-\frac{\mathbf{S}}{4}\right) $$ $$ \mathbf{v}^{2}=2g\frac{3S}{4} $$ $$ \bullet \quad \bullet\bullet $$ $$ \mathbf{v}=\sqrt{\frac{3g\mathbf{S}}{2}} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: What is the minimum velocity with which a body of mass $m$ must enter a vertical loop of radius $R$, so that it can complete the loop?
questions.explanation: When minimum speed of body is $ \sqrt{5gR} $ then no matter from where it enters the loop, it will complete full vertical loop.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: The potential energy of a particle in a force field is $ U = \frac{A}{r^2} - \frac{B}{r} $ where, A and B are positive constants and r is the distance of the particle from the centre of the field. For stable equilibrium, the distance of the particle is:
questions.explanation: Here, $$ \mathbf{U}=\frac{\mathbf{A}}{\mathbf{r}^{2}}-\frac{\mathbf{B}}{\mathbf{r}} $$ For equilibrium, $ \frac{dU}{dr}=0 $ ∴ $$ -\frac{2\mathbf{A}}{r^{3}}+\frac{\mathbf{B}}{r^{2}}=0 $$ or $$ \frac{2A}{r^{3}}=\frac{B}{r^{2}} $$ or $$ r=\frac{2\mathbf{A}}{\mathbf{B}} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: The potential energy of a system increases if work is done:
questions.optionA: upon the system by a non conservative force
questions.optionB: by the system against a conservative force
questions.optionC: by the system against a non conservative force
questions.optionD: upon the system by a conservative force.
questions.explanation: The potential energy of a system increases. If work is done by the system against a conservative force. then, $ W_{int} = -\Delta U $

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A chain of mass $m$ and length $L$, is placed, on a table in such a way that its $\frac{1}{n}^{\text{th}}$ part is hanging below the edge of table. The work done to pull the hanging part of chain is:
questions.explanation: Potential energy of chain at table level be $U_{f}$ $$ \mathbf{U}_{f}=0\mathrm{~a n d~}\mathbf{U}_{i}=-m g h $$ $$ \mathrm{U}_{i}=-\left(\frac{\mathbf{M}}{\mathbf{L}}\right)\left(\frac{\mathbf{L}}{n}g\right)\left(\frac{\mathbf{L}}{2n}\right)=-\frac{\mathbf{M}\mathbf{g}\mathbf{L}}{2n^{2}} $$ Work done = U_{f} - U_{i} =\left[0-\left(-\frac{MgL}{2n^{2}}\right)\right]=\frac{MgL}{2n^{2}}

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: 300 J of work is done in sliding a 2 kg block up an inclined plane of height 10 m. Work done against friction is: Taking, $g = 10\ m/s^{2}$,
questions.explanation: Potential energy, (C) Potential energy, P.E. = 2 × 10 × 10 = 200 J and work done, W = 300 J ∴ Work done against friction = 300 - 200 = 100 J.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A shell, in flight, explodes into four unequal parts. Which of the following is conserved?
questions.explanation: Momentum

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: The potential energy between two atoms, in a molecule, is given by $ U(x)=\frac{a}{x^{12}}-\frac{b}{x^{6}} $, where a and b are positive constants and x is the distance between the atoms. The atom is in stable equilibrium, when:
questions.explanation: $$ \mathrm{U}(x)=\frac{a}{x^{12}}-\frac{b}{x^{6}} $$ Atom is in stable equilibrium when P.E. is minimum hence, $$ -\frac{12a}{x^{13}}-\frac{-6b}{x^{7}}=0 $$ or $$ x^{6}=\frac{2a}{b} $$ Therefore, $$ x=\left(\frac{2a}{b}\right)^{1/6} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A car moving with a speed of 40 km/h can be stopped after 2 m by applying brakes. If the same car is moving with a speed of 80 km/h, what is the minimum stopping distance?
questions.explanation: According to conservation of energy, The kinetic energy of car = work done in stopping the car i.e., $$ \frac{1}{2}mv^{2}=Fs $$ where, F is the retarding force and s is the stopping distance. For same retarding force. $$ s\propto v^{2} $$ $$ \frac{s_{2}}{s_{1}}=\left(\frac{\mathbf{v}_{2}}{\mathbf{v}_{1}}\right)^{2}=\left(\frac{80}{40}\right)^{2}=4 $$ $$ s_{2}=4s_{1}=4\times2=8m $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.subtopic: The Potential Energy of a Spring
questions.question: The potential energy of a long spring when stretched by 2 cm is U. If the spring is stretched by 8 cm, potential energy stored in it will be:
questions.explanation: Given: $$ x=2cm $$ As we know, $$ \begin{aligned}\mathrm{U}&=\frac{1}{2}k x^{2}\\\mathrm{U}&=\frac{1}{2}k\left(2\right)^{2}=2k\end{aligned} $$ When spring is stretched by 8 cm, then potential energy is U' given by: $$ \begin{aligned}\mathrm{U}^{\prime}&=\frac{1}{2}k\left(8\right)^{2}=32k\\\frac{\mathrm{U}^{\prime}}{\mathrm{U}}&=\frac{32k}{2k}=16\\\mathrm{U}^{\prime}&=16\mathrm{U}\end{aligned} $$ Ratio, $ \Rightarrow $

questions.subject: Physics
questions.subtopic: The Potential Energy of a Spring
questions.question: Two similar springs P and Q have spring constant $ K_P $ and $ K_Q $, such that $ K_P > K_Q $. They are stretched, first by the same amount (case a), then by the same force (case b). The work done by the springs $ W_P $ and $ W_Q $ are related as, in case (a) and case (b), respectively:
questions.explanation: In case (a), elongation (x) is same for both the springs. So, work done by springs P and Q is respectively, $$ \mathbf{W}_{\mathrm{P}}=\frac{1}{2}\mathbf{K}_{\mathrm{P}}x^{2}\text{and}\mathbf{W}_{\mathrm{Q}}=\frac{1}{2}\mathbf{K}_{\mathrm{Q}}x^{2} $$ If, $$ \begin{aligned}&\mathbf{K}_{\mathbf{P}}>\mathbf{K}_{\mathbf{Q}}\\&\mathbf{W}_{\mathbf{P}}>\mathbf{W}_{\mathbf{Q}}\end{aligned} $$ $$ \Rightarrow\therefore\quad\mathbf{W}_{\mathbf{P}}>\mathbf{W}_{\mathbf{Q}} $$ In case (b), force of elongation (F) is same and so elongation is given by, $$ \begin{array}{l}x_{\mathrm{P}}=\frac{\mathrm{F}}{\mathrm{K}_{P}}\quad\text{and}\quad x_{\mathrm{Q}}=\frac{\mathrm{F}}{\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{Q}}}\\ \mathrm{W}_{\mathrm{P}}=\frac{1}{2}\mathrm{K}_{P}x_{\mathrm{P}}^{2}=\frac{1}{2}\frac{\mathrm{F}^{2}}{\mathrm{K}_{P}}\\ \mathrm{W}_{\mathrm{Q}}=\frac{1}{2}\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{Q}}x_{\mathrm{Q}}^{2}=\frac{1}{2}\frac{\mathrm{F}^{2}}{\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{Q}}}\end{array} $$ $$ \mathbf{W}_{\mathbf{P}}<\mathbf{W}_{\mathbf{Q}} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.subtopic: The Potential Energy of a Spring
questions.question: A block of mass M is attached to the lower end of a vertical spring. The spring is hung from a ceiling and has force constant k. The mass is released from rest with the spring initially unstretched. The maximum extension produced in the length of the spring will be:
questions.explanation: When the mass attached to a spring fixed at the other end is allowed to fall suddenly, it extends the spring by x. Potential energy lost by the mass is gained by the spring. $$ \begin{array}{c}Mgx=\frac{1}{2}kx^{2}\\x=\frac{2Mg}{k}\end{array} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.subtopic: The Potential Energy of a Spring
questions.question: A vertical spring with force constant k is fixed on a table. A ball of mass m at a height h above the free upper end of the spring falls vertically on the spring, so that the spring is compressed by a distance d. The net work done in the process is:
questions.explanation: Net work done = work done by gravitational force + work done by spring force $ = mg(h+d)-\frac{1}{2}kd^{2} $

questions.subject: Physics
questions.subtopic: The Potential Energy of a Spring
questions.question: A mass of 0.5 kg moving with a speed of 1.5 m/s on a horizontal smooth surface, collides with a nearly weightless spring of force constant $k = 50\ N/m$. The maximum compression of the spring would be:
questions.explanation: According to the question, $$ \begin{aligned}\frac{1}{2}mv^{2}&=\frac{1}{2}kx^{2}\\x&=\sqrt[v]{\frac{m}{k}}=1.5\sqrt{\frac{0.5}{50}}\\&=0.15m\end{aligned} $$ ⇔

questions.subject: Physics
questions.subtopic: The Potential Energy of a Spring
questions.question: Two springs of spring constant $k_{1}$ and $k_{2}$ are joined in series. The effective spring constant of the combination is given by:
questions.explanation: When the spring joined in series the total extension of spring is: $$ \begin{aligned}y&=y_{1}+y_{2}\\&=\frac{-F}{k_{1}}-\frac{F}{k_{2}}\end{aligned} $$ $$ y=-\mathrm{F}\left[\frac{1}{k_{1}}+\frac{1}{k_{2}}\right] $$ $\therefore$ Spring constant $k=\frac{k_{1}k_{2}}{k_{1}+k_{2}}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.subtopic: The Potential Energy of a Spring
questions.question: When a long spring is stretched by 2 cm, its potential energy is U. If the spring is stretched by 10 cm, the potential energy stored in it will be:
questions.explanation: $$ \mathrm{U}=\frac{1}{2}\mathrm{K}(2)^{2} $$ $$ \mathrm{U}^{\prime}=\frac{1}{2}\mathrm{K}(10)^{2} $$ From above equations (i) and (ii), $$ \mathrm{U^{\prime}=25U} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.subtopic: The Potential Energy of a Spring
questions.question: Two springs A and B having spring constant $ K_{A} $ and $ K_{B} $ ( $ K_{A} = 2K_{B} $) are stretched by applying force of equal magnitude. If energy stored in spring A is $ E_{A} $ then energy stored in B will be:
questions.explanation: $$ \mathrm{Energy}=\frac{1}{2}\mathrm{K}x^{2}=\frac{1}{2}\frac{\mathrm{F}^{2}}{\mathrm{K}} $$ $$ \frac{\mathbf{K}_{\mathbf{A}}}{\mathbf{K}_{\mathbf{B}}}=2; $$ $$ \frac{\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{A}}}{\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{B}}}=\frac{\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{B}}}{\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{A}}}\mathrm{~o r~}\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{B}}=2\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{A}} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: At any instant of time $t$, the displacement of any particle is given by $2t-1$ (SI unit) under the influence of force of 5 N. The value of instantaneous power is (in SI unit):
questions.explanation: $$ \begin{array}{c}s=2t-1\\\mathrm{v}=\frac{ds}{dt}=\frac{d}{dt}\ (2t-1)\\=2\,\mathrm{m/s}\end{array} $$ Instantaneous power. $$ \begin{aligned}\mathbf{P}&=\overrightarrow{\mathbf{F.v}}\\&=5\times2=10\mathbf{W}\end{aligned} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: The energy that will be ideally radiated by 100 kW transmitter in 1 hour is:
questions.explanation: Given, $$ \mathrm{P}=100~\mathrm{kW} $$ Time. $$ \begin{array}{c}\mathrm{E}=\mathrm{P}\times t=100\times1=100\mathrm{kWh}\\ \mathrm{E}=100\times3.6\times10^{6}\mathrm{J}=36\times10^{7}\mathrm{J}\\ (\because1\mathrm{kWh}=3.6\times10^{6}\mathrm{J})\end{array} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.topic: System of Particles and Rotational Motion
questions.question: Two objects of mass 10 kg and 20 kg respectively are connected to the two ends of a rigid rod of length 10 m with negligible mass. The distance of the center of mass of the system from the 10 kg mass is :
questions.explanation: Given. $$ \mathrm{M}_{1}=10\mathrm{~kg},\mathrm{M}_{2}=20\mathrm{~kg}, $$ Length of rod = 10 m Let $M_{1}$ is at origin, then $X_{1}$ and $X_{2}$ is 0 and 10 m respectively. Centre of mass, $ X = \frac{M_{1}X_{1} + M_{2}X_{2}}{M_{1} + M_{2}} $ Putting the values in above equation we get, $$ \begin{aligned}X&=\frac{10\times0+20\times10}{10+20}\\&=\frac{200}{30}=\frac{20}{3}\end{aligned} $$ Distance of the centre of mass of the system from the 10 kg mass is $ \frac{20}{3} $ m.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.topic: System of Particles and Rotational Motion
questions.question: Three identical spheres, each of mass M, are placed at the corners of a right angled triangle with the mutually perpendicular sides equal to 2 m (see figure). Taking the point of intersection of the two mutually perpendicular sides as the origin, the position vector of the centre of mass is:
questions.explanation: Given,

questions.subject: Physics
questions.topic: System of Particles and Rotational Motion
questions.question: Two particles of mass 5 kg and 10 kg respectively are attached to the two ends of a rigid rod of length 1 m with negligible mass. The centre of mass of the system from the 5 kg particle is nearly at a distance of:
questions.optionB: the relationship between L_{A} and L_{B} depends upon the slope of the line AB
questions.explanation:
In case of rolling, the velocity of the point of contact with the surface is zero. Also, the velocity of the point of the highest point of the rolling body is twice the velocity of COM of the body. So. point body P moves faster then point Q.
Since, $$ \mathbf{v}=\sqrt{\frac{2gh}{1+\frac{\mathbf{K}^{2}}{\mathbf{R}^{2}}}} $$ $$ \mathbf{v}^{2}=\frac{2gh}{1+\frac{1}{2}} $$ $$ \left[\frac{\mathbf{K}^{2}}{\mathbf{R}^{2}}=\frac{1}{2}\text{for solid cylinder}\right] $$ $$ 2gh=4^{2}\times\frac{3}{2} $$ $$ h=\frac{12}{10}=1.2m $$ Now, $$ x=\frac{h}{\sin30^{\circ}}=\frac{1.2}{\frac{1}{2}}=2.4m $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.topic: System of Particles and Rotational Motion
questions.question: Three masses are placed on the x-axis: 300 g at origin, 500 g at x = 40 cm and 400 g at x = 70 cm. The distance of the centre of mass from the origin is:
questions.explanation: Centre of mass. $$ \begin{array}{l}r_{\mathrm{c m}}=\frac{m_{1}x_{1}+m_{2}x_{2}+m_{3}x_{3}}{m_{1}+m_{2}+m_{3}}\\\quad=\frac{300\times0+500\times40+400\times70}{300+500+400},\\\quad=40\mathrm{~c m}\end{array} $$
