questions.question: Two persons of masses 55 kg and 65 kg respectively, are at the opposite ends of a boat. The length of the boat is 3.0 m and weighs 100 kg. The 55 kg man walks up to the 65 kg man and sits with him. If the boat is in still water, the centre of mass of the system shifts by:
questions.explanation: Since no external force applied on the system. And centre of mass (CM) be at rest. So answer is zero.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.topic: System of Particles and Rotational Motion
questions.question: Two bodies of mass 1 kg and 3 kg have position vectors $ \hat{i} + 2\hat{j} + \hat{k} $ and $ -3\hat{i} - 2\hat{j} + \hat{k} $ respectively. The center of mass of this system has a position vector:
questions.explanation: According to the question, position vector, $ \vec{r} = \frac{m_1 \vec{r}_1 + m_2 \vec{r}_2}{m_1 + m_2} $ $ \vec{r} = \frac{1 (\hat{i} + 2 \hat{j} + k) + 3 (-3 \hat{i} - 2 \hat{j} + \hat{k})}{1 + 3} $ $ \vec{r} = \frac{1}{4} (-8 \hat{i} - 4 \hat{j} + 4 \hat{k}) $ $ \vec{r} = -2 \hat{i} - \hat{j} + \hat{k} $ $$ C.M.=\frac{m_{1}x_{1}+m_{2}x_{2}}{m_{1}+m_{2}} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.topic: System of Particles and Rotational Motion
questions.question: Consider a system of two particles having masses $ m_{1} $ and $ m_{2} $. If the particle of mass $ m_{1} $ is pushed towards the mass centre of particles through a distance d, by what distance would the particle of mass $ m_{2} $ moves so as to keep the mass centre of particles at the original position?
questions.explanation: We know that, After changing a position of $m_{1}$ and to keep the position of C.M. same. $$ \mathrm{C.M.}=\frac{m_{1}(x_{1}-d)+m_{2}(x_{2}+d^{\prime})}{m_{1}+m_{2}} $$ From both eqn., $$ \begin{aligned}&\frac{m_{1}x_{1}+m_{2}x_{2}}{m_{1}+m_{2}}=\frac{m_{1}(x_{1}-d)+m_{2}(x_{2}+d^{\prime})}{m_{1}+m_{2}}\\ &-m_{1}d+m_{2}d^{\prime}=0\\ \Rightarrow\quad d^{\prime}=\frac{m_{1}}{m_{2}}d\\ \end{aligned} $$

questions.subject: Physics

questions.question: Three identical metal balls, each of radius $r$ are placed touching each other on horizontal surface such that an equilateral triangle is formed when centres of three balls joined. The centre of the mass of the system is located at:
questions.optionA: solid sphere reaches the bottom first
questions.optionB: solid sphere reaches the bottom last
questions.optionC: disc will reach the bottom first
questions.optionD: all reach the bottom at same time.
questions.explanation: Centre of mass of each ball lies on the centre. $ \Rightarrow $ Centre of mass of combined body will be at the centroid of equilateral triangle.

questions.question: The resultant of $\overrightarrow{A} \times 0$ will be equal to:
questions.explanation: $ \overrightarrow{A} \times 0 $ is a zero vector.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.topic: System of Particles and Rotational Motion
questions.question: Vectors, $\overrightarrow{A}$, $\overrightarrow{B}$ and $\overrightarrow{C}$ are such that $\overrightarrow{A} \cdot \overrightarrow{B} = 0$ and $\overrightarrow{A} \cdot \overrightarrow{C} = 0$. Then the vector parallel to $\overrightarrow{A}$ is:
questions.explanation: Vector triple product of three vectors $\overrightarrow{A}$, $\overrightarrow{D}$ and $C$ is: $\overrightarrow{A}\times(\overrightarrow{B}\times\overrightarrow{C})=(\overrightarrow{A}\cdot\overrightarrow{C})\overrightarrow{B}-(\overrightarrow{A}\cdot\overrightarrow{B})\overrightarrow{C}$ Given: $\overrightarrow{A} \cdot \overrightarrow{B} = 0$, $\overrightarrow{A} \cdot \overrightarrow{C} = 0$ $\therefore \overrightarrow{A} \times (\overrightarrow{B} \times \overrightarrow{C}) = 0$ Thus, the vector $\overrightarrow{A}$ is parallel to vector $\overrightarrow{B} \times \overrightarrow{C}$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.topic: System of Particles and Rotational Motion
questions.question: $\overrightarrow{A}$ and $\overrightarrow{B}$ are two vectors and $\theta$ is the angle between them, if $\overrightarrow{A} \times \overrightarrow{B} = \sqrt{3} (\overrightarrow{A} \cdot \overrightarrow{B})$, the value of $\theta$ is:
questions.explanation: $ \begin{array}{l} |\overrightarrow{A} \times \overrightarrow{B}| = \sqrt{3} (\overrightarrow{A} \cdot \overrightarrow{B}) \\ \therefore \quad AB \sin \theta = \sqrt{3} AB \cos \theta \\ \text{or,} \quad \tan \theta = \sqrt{3} \\ \text{or,} \quad \theta = \tan^{-1} \sqrt{3} = 60^{\circ}\end{array} $

questions.subject: Physics
questions.topic: System of Particles and Rotational Motion
questions.question: If the angle between the vectors $ \overrightarrow{A} $ and $ \overrightarrow{B} $ is $ \theta $, the value of the product $ (\overrightarrow{A} \times \overrightarrow{B}) $. $ \overrightarrow{A} $ is equal to:
questions.explanation: Let $ \overrightarrow{A} \times \overrightarrow{B} = C $ The cross product of $ \overrightarrow{B} $ and $ \overrightarrow{A} $ is perpendicular to the plane containing $ \overrightarrow{A} $ and $ \overrightarrow{B} $ i.e., perpendicular to $ \overrightarrow{A} $. Therefore, product of $ (\overrightarrow{B} \times \overrightarrow{A}) \cdot \overrightarrow{A} = (BA \sin \theta) A \cos 90^{\circ} = 0 $ $ \therefore \cos 90^{\circ} = 0 $

questions.subject: Physics
questions.topic: System of Particles and Rotational Motion
questions.question: If $ |\overrightarrow{A} \times \overrightarrow{B}| = \sqrt{3} \, \overrightarrow{A} \cdot \overrightarrow{B} $ then the value of $ |\overrightarrow{A} + \overrightarrow{B}| $ is:
questions.explanation: $ |\overrightarrow{A} \times \overrightarrow{B}| = \sqrt{3} \overrightarrow{A} \cdot \overrightarrow{B} $ $ |\overrightarrow{A} | \overrightarrow{B}| \sin \theta = \sqrt{3} |\overrightarrow{A} | \overrightarrow{B}| \cos \theta $ $ \tan \theta = \sqrt{3} $ $ \Rightarrow $ $ \theta = 60^\circ $ $ |\overrightarrow{A} + \overrightarrow{B}| = \sqrt{|\overrightarrow{A}|^2 + |\overrightarrow{B}|^2 + 2 |\overrightarrow{A}||\overrightarrow{B}| \cos \theta} $ $ = (A^2 + B^2 + AB)^{1/2} $

questions.question: What is the value of linear velocity, if $ \overrightarrow{r}=3\overrightarrow{i}-4\overrightarrow{j}+\overrightarrow{k} $ and $ \overrightarrow{\omega}=5\overrightarrow{i}-6\overrightarrow{j}+6\overrightarrow{k} $?
questions.explanation: $ \vec{v} \rightarrow \vec{\omega} \times \vec{r} \rightarrow \begin{vmatrix} \hat{i} & \hat{j} & \hat{k} \\ 5 & -6 & 6 \\ 3 & -4 & 1 \end{vmatrix} = 18\hat{i} + 13\hat{j} - 2\hat{k} $

questions.subject: Physics
questions.topic: System of Particles and Rotational Motion
questions.question: Find the torque about the origin, when a force of $3\hat{j}N$ acts on the particle whose position vector is $2\hat{k}m$.
questions.explanation: According to question, Position vector, $$ \hat{r}=2\hat{k}\mathbf{m} $$ Force $$ \overset{\rightarrow}{\mathbf{F}}=3\hat{j}\mathbf{N} $$ ∴ Torque, $$ \begin{array}{l}\rightarrow\\\tau=\stackrel{\rightarrow}{r}\times\stackrel{\rightarrow}{F}=2\hat{k}\times3\hat{j}\\=-6\hat{i}\\\\=-6\hat{i}\mathrm{N m}.\end{array} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.topic: System of Particles and Rotational Motion
questions.question: A force $ \overrightarrow{F} = \alpha\overrightarrow{i} + 3\overrightarrow{j} + 6\overrightarrow{k} $ is acting at a point $ \overrightarrow{r} = 2\overrightarrow{i} - 6\overrightarrow{j} - 12\overrightarrow{k} $. The value of $ \alpha $ for which angular momentum about origin is conserved is:
questions.explanation: From the question. Force. $$ \stackrel{\rightarrow}{\mathrm{\boldmath~F~}}=\alpha\hat{i}+3\hat{j}+6\hat{k} $$ and Position. $$ \overset{\rightarrow}{r}=2\hat{i}-6\hat{j}-12\hat{k} $$ Using law of conservation of angular moments, $$ \tau=c o n s t a n t $$ $$ \overset{\to}{r}\;\times\;\overset{\to}{\mathrm{F}}=0 $$ $$ \begin{aligned}&(2\hat{i}-6\hat{j}-12\hat{k})\times(\alpha\hat{i}+3\hat{j}+6\hat{k})=\begin{vmatrix}\hat{i}&\hat{j}&\hat{k}\\2&-6&-12\\\alpha&3&6\end{vmatrix}=0\\ &\Rightarrow(-36+36)\overset{\hat{i}-}(12+12\alpha)\overset{\hat{j}}{}+(6+6\alpha)\overset{\hat{k}}{=}0\\ &\Rightarrow-12(1+\alpha)\overset{\hat{j}+}6(1+\alpha)\overset{\hat{k}}{=}0\\ &\Rightarrow\alpha=-1\\ \end{aligned} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.topic: System of Particles and Rotational Motion
questions.question: When a mass is rotating in a plane about a fixed point, its angular momentum is directed along:
questions.optionA: a line perpendicular to the plane of rotation
questions.optionB: the line at angle of  $ 45^{\circ} $ to the plane of rotation
questions.explanation: The formula is $L = m \, (r \times v)$. This shows that when a mass is rotating in a plane about a fixed point, its angular momentum is directed along a line perpendicular to the plane of rotation.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.topic: System of Particles and Rotational Motion
questions.question: ABC is an equilateral triangle with O as its centre. $F_{1}$, $F_{2}$ and $F_{3}$ represent three forces acting along the sides AB, BC and AC respectively. If the total torque about O is zero then the magnitude of $F_{3}$ is:
questions.explanation:
The torques $F_{1}d$ and $F_{2}d$ of $F_{1}$ and $F_{2}$ respectively are counterclockwise. The torque $F_{3}d$ is clockwise.
$$ \begin{array}{r l r}&{\xrightarrow{}\tau_{\mathrm{N e t}}=0}&\\ &{\Longrightarrow\mathrm{~F}_{1}d+\mathrm{F}_{2}d-\mathrm{F}_{3}d=0}\end{array} $$ (counterclockwise direction as positive) $$ \mathbf{F}_{3}=\mathbf{F}_{1}+\mathbf{F}_{2} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.topic: System of Particles and Rotational Motion
questions.question: If $\overrightarrow{F}$ is the force acting on a particle having position vector $\overrightarrow{r}$ and $\overrightarrow{\tau}$ be the torque of this force about the origin, then:
questions.explanation: Torque is an axial vector i.e., its direction is always perpendicular to the plane containing vectors $ \overrightarrow{r} $ and $ \overrightarrow{F} $. Therefore, $$ \overrightarrow{\tau}~=~\overrightarrow{r}\times\overrightarrow{\mathrm{F}} $$ $ \because $ Torque is perpendicular to both $ \overrightarrow{r} $ and $ \overrightarrow{F} $. $$ \ddots $$ $$ \overset{\to}{\tau}\overset{\to}{.}\overset{\to}{r}_{}={0} $$ $$ \ddot{\theta} $$ $$ {\overrightarrow{\mathrm{\boldmath~F}}}\cdot{\overrightarrow{\tau}}=0 $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.topic: System of Particles and Rotational Motion
questions.question: A uniform rod of length 200 cm and mass 500 g is balanced on a wedge placed at 40 cm mark. A mass of 2 kg is suspended from the rod at 20 cm and another unknown mass ‘m’ is suspended from the rod at 160 cm mark as shown in the figure. Find the value of ‘m’ such that the rod is in equilibrium, (g = 10 m/s²):
questions.explanation: Given, $$ \mathrm{L H S}=2\times20 $$ $$ \mathrm{RHS}=0.5\times60+m\times120 $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.topic: System of Particles and Rotational Motion
questions.question: Which of the following statements are correct? (I) Centre of mass of a body always coincides with the centre of gravity of the body. (II) Centre of mass of a body is the point at which the total gravitational torque on the body is zero. (III) A couple on a body produces both translational and rotational motion in a body. (IV) Mechanical advantage greater than one means that small effort can be used to lift a large load. Options:
questions.explanation: Centre of mass may or may not coincide with centre of gravity. $$ 10=120\;m $$ $$ m=\frac{10}{120}=\frac{1}{12}kg $$ So $$ \mathbf{W}=\mathbf{N}_{1}+\mathbf{N}_{2} $$ For equilibrium. $$ \mathbf{N}_{1}x=\mathbf{N}_{2}\left(d-x\right) $$ From (i) $$ \mathbf{N}_{1}x=(\mathbf{W}-\mathbf{N}_{1})(d-x) $$ $$ \begin{array}{l}\mathrm{N}_{1}x=\mathrm{W}d-\mathrm{W}x-\mathrm{N}_{1}d+\mathrm{N}_{1}x\\\Rightarrow\quad\mathrm{N}_{1}d=\mathrm{W}(d-x)\\\Rightarrow\quad\mathrm{N}_{1}=\frac{\mathrm{W}(d-x)}{d}\end{array} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.topic: System of Particles and Rotational Motion
questions.question: A rod of weight W is supported by two parallel knife edges A and B and is in equilibrium in a horizontal position. The knives are at a distance d from each other. The centre of mass of the rod is at distance x from A. The normal reaction on A is:
questions.explanation: From question, weight of the rod is W and the weight W balances the normal reach. $$ 2\times20=30+120m $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.topic: System of Particles and Rotational Motion
questions.question: The radius of gyration of a solid sphere of mass 5 kg about XY is 5 m as shown in figure. The radius of the sphere is $ \frac{5x}{\sqrt{7}} $ m, then the value of x is:
questions.explanation: Mass = 5 kg radius of gyration, k = 5 m radius of solid sphere, $ R = \frac{5x}{\sqrt{7}}m $ Radius of gyration of a solid sphere about its diameter is $ k = \sqrt{\frac{7}{5}}R $ Put the value in eqn (i), $$ 5=\sqrt{\frac{7}{5}}\times\frac{5x}{\sqrt{7}} $$ On squaring both sides, $$ 25=\frac{7}{5}\times\frac{25x^{2}}{7} $$ $$ x=\sqrt{5} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.topic: System of Particles and Rotational Motion
questions.question: The moment of inertia of a thin rod about an axis passing through its mid point and perpendicular to the rod is $ 2400 \, \text{g} \, \text{cm}^2 $. The length of the 400 g rod is nearly:
questions.explanation: MOI of a thin rod = 2400 g cm $ ^{2} $ mass of the rod = 400 g length of the rod = ? $$ M.I of rod=\frac{ML^{2}}{12}=2400g cm^{2} $$ $$ \Rightarrow\quad\frac{400\mathrm{L}^{2}}{12}=2400 $$ $$ L^{2}=72 $$ $$ L=\sqrt{72}=8.5cm $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.topic: System of Particles and Rotational Motion
questions.question: The ratio of radius of gyration of a solid sphere of mass M and radius R about its own axis to the radius of gyration of the thin hollow sphere of same mass and radius about its axis is:
questions.explanation: Radius of gyration is $ K = \sqrt{\frac{I}{M}} $ i.e., $$ \frac{K_{solid}}{K_{hollow}}=\sqrt{\frac{2MR^{2}/5M}{2MR^{2}/3M}}=\sqrt{3}:\sqrt{5} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.topic: System of Particles and Rotational Motion
questions.question: The ratio of the radius of gyration of a thin uniform disc about an axis passing through its centre and normal to its plane to the radius of gyration of the disk about its diameter is:
questions.explanation:
The figure of a thin uniform disc and disc is shown below :
Thin uniform disc Disc The radius of gyration of a thin uniform disc, $$ k=\sqrt{\frac{1}{m}} $$ and moment of inertia of the disc about an axis through its centre and normal to plane, $$ 1=\frac{m R^{2}}{2} $$ Here, we have R as the radius, m is the mass. Now, on putting the value in eqn. (i) we have, The radius of gyration of the uniform disc about a axis through its centre and normal to plane, $$ k_{1}=\sqrt{\frac{mR^{2}}{\frac{2}{m}}} $$ $$ k_{1}=\sqrt{\frac{R^{2}}{2}} $$ and the radius of gyration of the uniform disc about its diameter, $$ k_{2}=\sqrt{\frac{m\mathrm{R}^{2}}{\frac{4}{m}}} $$ $$ k_{2}=\sqrt{\frac{\mathbf{R}^{2}}{4}} $$ Now, on dividing equation (ii) by equation (iii) we have; $$ \frac{k_{1}}{k_{2}}=\frac{\sqrt{\frac{R^{2}}{2}}}{\sqrt{\frac{R^{2}}{4}}} $$ $$ k_{1}:k_{2}=\sqrt{2}:1 $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.topic: System of Particles and Rotational Motion
questions.question: From a circular ring of mass ‘M’ and radius ‘R’, an arc corresponding to a $ 90^{\circ} $ sector is removed. The moment of inertia of the remaining part of the ring about an axis passing through the centre of the ring and perpendicular to the plane of the ring is ‘K’ times ‘MR^{2}’. Then the value of ‘K’ is:
questions.explanation:
Given,
Mass = M Radius = R $ \operatorname{arc}\theta = 90^{\circ} $ $$ I_{remaining}=KMR^{2} $$ $$ 1=r^{2}\int dm $$ $$ d\boldsymbol{\Lambda}=d m r^{2} $$ $$ l=r^{2}\int dm $$ ∴ Complete ring was having the mass M and now one-fourth part is removed. $$ \begin{aligned}Mass~of~the~remaining~ring&=~M-\frac{M}{4}=\frac{3M}{4}\\ \therefore\quad&1=~r^{2}\int dm\quad(\because~r=R)\\ &=R^{2}\times\frac{3M}{4}\end{aligned} $$ $$ \mathrm{I}=\frac{3}{4}\mathrm{M}\mathrm{R}^{2} $$ $$ \mathrm{K}=\frac{3}{4} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.topic: System of Particles and Rotational Motion
questions.question: A solid sphere of mass $m$ and radius $R$ is rotating about its diameter. A solid cylinder of the same mass and same radius is also rotating about its geometrical axis with an angular speed twice that of the sphere. The ratio of their kinetic energies of rotation $\left(\frac{E_{\text{sphere}}}{E_{\text{cylinder}}}\right)$ will be:
questions.explanation: We know that, KE of rotating rigid body, $$ \mathrm{KE}=\frac{1}{2}\mathrm{I}\omega^{2} $$ ∴ KE of sphere, $$ \begin{array}{l}\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{S}}=\frac{1}{2}\mathrm{I}_{s}\omega^{2}\\ \quad=\frac{1}{2}\times\frac{2}{5}\mathrm{M}\mathrm{R}^{2}\times\omega^{2}\end{array} $$ In KE of cylinder, $$ \begin{array}{l}\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{C}}=\frac{1}{2}\mathrm{I}_{\mathrm{C}}\omega^{2}\\ \\ =\frac{1}{2}\times\frac{\mathrm{MR}^{2}}{2}\times4\omega^{2}.\end{array} $$ From (i) and (ii), $$ \mathrm{\frac{K_{s}}{K_{c}}}={\frac{1}{5}} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.topic: System of Particles and Rotational Motion
questions.question: A light rod of length l has two masses m_{1} and m_{2} attached to its two ends. The moment of inertia of the system about an axis perpendicular to the rod and passing through the centre of mass is:
questions.explanation: centre of mass of $ m_{1} $ and $ m_{2} $ lies at, $$ r=\frac{m_{1}r_{1}+m_{2}r_{2}}{m_{1}+m_{2}} $$ $$ r_{1}=\frac{m_{2}l}{m_{1}+m_{2}},r_{2}=\frac{m_{1}l}{m_{1}+m_{2}} $$ $$ \begin{array}{l} \text{I}_{CM}=\ m_{1}r_{1}^{2}+m_{2}r_{2}^{2} \\ =\ m_{1}\Bigg[\frac{m_{2}l}{m_{1}+m_{2}}\Bigg]^{2}+m_{2}\Bigg[\frac{m_{1}l}{m_{1}+m_{2}}\Bigg]^{2} \\ =\frac{m_{1}m_{2}l^{2}}{\left(m_{1}+m_{2}\right)^{2}}\left(m_{1}+m_{2}\right) \\ =\frac{m_{1}m_{2}}{m_{1}+m_{2}}l^{2} \end{array} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.topic: System of Particles and Rotational Motion
questions.question: From a disc of radius R and mass M, a circular hole of diameter R, whose rim passes through the centre is cut. What is the moment of inertia of the remaining part of the disc about a perpendicular axis, passing through the centre?
questions.explanation:
The diagram is,
Moment of Inertia of disc, $$ \begin{aligned}\mathrm{I}&=\mathrm{I}_{\text{remain}}+\mathrm{I}_{\mathrm{R}/2}\\\mathrm{I}_{\text{remain}}&=\mathrm{I}-\mathrm{I}_{\mathrm{R}/2}\end{aligned} $$ $$ \bullet \quad \bullet\ast $$ $$ \begin{aligned}&=\frac{\mathbf{M}\mathbf{R}^{2}}{2}-\left[\frac{\mathbf{\frac{M}{4}}\left(\frac{\mathbf{R}}{2}\right)^{2}}{2}+\frac{\mathbf{M}}{4}\left(\frac{\mathbf{R}}{2}\right)^{2}\right]\\&=\frac{\mathbf{M}\mathbf{R}^{2}}{2}-\left[\frac{\mathbf{M}\mathbf{R}^{2}}{32}+\frac{\mathbf{M}\mathbf{R}^{2}}{10}\right]\\&=\frac{\mathbf{M}\mathbf{R}^{2}}{2}-\frac{3\mathbf{M}\mathbf{R}^{2}}{32}\end{aligned} $$ $$ I_{remain}=\frac{13MR^{2}}{32} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.topic: System of Particles and Rotational Motion
questions.question: Three identical spherical shells, each of mass $m$ and radius $r$ are placed as shown in figure. Consider an axis XX', which is touching to two shells and passing through diameter of third shell: Moment of inertia of the system consisting of these three spherical shells about XX' axis is:
questions.explanation: Let $I_{1}$, $I_{2}$ and $I_{3}$ be the Moment of Inertia of three spheres. So, Moment of Inertia of the spheres about the axis passing through XX' is, Here, $$ \begin{aligned}\mathrm{I}&=\mathrm{I}_{1}+\mathrm{I}_{2}+\mathrm{I}_{3}\\\mathrm{I}_{1}&=\frac{2}{3}mr^{2}\end{aligned} $$ $$ \mathrm{I}_{2}=\mathrm{I}_{3}=\frac{2}{3}m r^{2}+m r^{2} $$ (using parallel axis theorem) $$ \begin{aligned}\mathrm{I}_{3}=\mathrm{I}_{2}&=\frac{5}{3}mr^{2}\\\mathrm{I}&=\frac{2}{3}mr^{2}+2\times\frac{5}{3}mr^{2}\\&=\;mr^{2}\left(\frac{2}{3}+\frac{10}{3}\right)=4mr^{2}\end{aligned} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.topic: System of Particles and Rotational Motion
questions.question: The moment of inertia of a uniform circular disc is maximum about an axis perpendicular to the disc and passing through:
questions.explanation: According to the parallel axis theorem, $$ \mathrm{I}=\mathrm{I}_{\mathrm{cm}}+m d^{2} $$ d is maximum to B.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.topic: System of Particles and Rotational Motion
questions.question: The moment of inertia of a thin uniform rod of mass M and length L about an axis passing through its mid-point and perpendicular to its length is $ I_{0} $. Its moment of inertia about an axis passing through one of its ends and perpendicular to its length is:
questions.explanation: Moment of inertia about an axis passing through one end $$ \mathrm{I}=\mathrm{I}_{\mathrm{CM}}+md^{2} $$ $$ \mathrm{I}=\mathrm{I}_{0}+\mathrm{M}\left(\frac{\mathrm{L}}{2}\right)^{2}=\mathrm{I}_{0}+\frac{\mathrm{ML}^{2}}{4} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.topic: System of Particles and Rotational Motion
questions.question: From a circular disc of radius R and mass 9M, a small disc of mass M and radius \frac{R}{3} is removed concentrically. The moment of inertia of the remaining disc about an axis perpendicular to the plane of the disc and passing through its centre is:
questions.explanation: The Moment of Inertia of disc about an axis perpendicular to the plane and passing through the centre, $$ \begin{aligned}I&=I_{1}-I_{2}\\I&=\frac{9MR^{2}}{2}-\frac{MR^{2}}{18}\\I&=\frac{81MR^{2}-MR^{2}}{18}\\&\quad40MR^{2}\end{aligned} $$ $$ I=\frac{40MR^{2}}{9} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.topic: System of Particles and Rotational Motion
questions.question: Three identical rings of mass $m$ and radius $r$ are placed as shown in figure. Find moment of inertia of the system about axis $XX$?
questions.explanation:
Moment of Inertia of system about XX'
$$ \mathrm{I}=\mathrm{I}_{1}+\mathrm{I}_{2}+\mathrm{I}_{3} $$ Here, $ I_{1} = M.I. $ of ring about its diameter $ \frac{1}{2}mr^{2} $ $$ \mathrm{I}_{2}=\frac{1}{2}m r^{2}+m r^{2}=\frac{3}{2}m r^{2} $$ Similarly, $$ I_{3}=\frac{3}{2}mr^{2} $$ $$ \begin{aligned}I&=\frac{1}{2}mr^{2}+\frac{3}{2}mr^{2}+\frac{3}{2}mr^{2}\\ &=\frac{7}{2}mr^{2}\end{aligned} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.topic: System of Particles and Rotational Motion
questions.question: Four identical thin rods each of mass M and length $l$ form a square frame. Moment of inertia of this frame about an axis through the centre of the square and perpendicular to its plane is:
questions.explanation: From parallel axis theorem, Moment of Inertia, $ I = \frac{M l^{2}}{2} + M \left( \frac{l}{2} \right)^{2} = \frac{M l^{2}}{3} $ $\therefore$ Moment of Inertia of the system, $I = \frac{M l^{2}}{3} \times 4$ $=\frac{4}{3} M l^{2}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.topic: System of Particles and Rotational Motion
questions.question: A thin rod of length L and mass M is bent at its midpoint into two halves so that the angle between them is 90^{\circ}. The moment of inertia of the bent rod about an axis passing through the bending point and perpendicular to the plane defined by the two halves of the rod is:
questions.explanation: Moment of Inertia of the system, $$ \begin{array}{l}\mathrm{I}=\frac{\mathrm{M}(\mathrm{L}/2)^{2}}{3}+\frac{\mathrm{M}(\mathrm{L}/2)^{2}}{3}\\ \quad=\frac{\mathrm{ML}^{2}}{12}+\frac{\mathrm{ML}^{2}}{12}\\ \quad=\frac{\mathrm{ML}^{2}}{6}\end{array} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.topic: System of Particles and Rotational Motion
questions.question: The ratio of the radii of gyration of a circular disc to that of a circular ring, each of same mass and radius around their respective axes is:
questions.explanation: We know that, Radius of gyration is $$ \mathrm{K}=\sqrt{\frac{\mathrm{I}}{\mathrm{M}}} $$ Using this formula we have, $$ \mathbf{M}\mathbf{K}_{1}^{2}=\mathbf{M}\mathbf{R}^{2} $$ $$ \mathbf{K}_{1}=\mathbf{R} $$ and $$ \mathbf{M}\mathbf{K}_{2}^{2}=\frac{\mathbf{M}\mathbf{R}^{2}}{2} $$ $$ \mathbf{K}_{2}=\frac{\mathbf{R}}{\sqrt{2}} $$ $$ \frac{\mathbf{K}_{2}}{\mathbf{K}_{1}}=\frac{\mathbf{R}}{\sqrt{2}\mathbf{R}}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.topic: System of Particles and Rotational Motion
questions.question: The moment of inertia of a uniform circular disc of radius R and mass M about an axis passing from the edge of the disc and normal to the disc is:
questions.explanation: Moment of Inertia of disc about its normal = $ \frac{1}{2}MR^{2} $ Moment of Inertia about its one edge = $ MR^{2} + \frac{MR^{2}}{2} $ $ \therefore $ Moment of Inertia = $ \frac{3}{2}MR^{2} $

questions.subject: Physics
questions.topic: System of Particles and Rotational Motion
questions.question: The ratio of the radii of gyration of a circular disc about a tangential axis in the plane of the disc and of a circular ring of the same radius about a tangential axis in the plane of the ring is:
questions.explanation: The radius of gyration of disc about a tangential axis in the plane of disc is $k_{1}$, $$ k_{1}=\sqrt{\frac{5}{4}\frac{\mathrm{M R}^{2}}{\mathrm{M}}}=\frac{\sqrt{5}}{2}\mathrm{R} $$ (From parallel Axis Theorem) And radius of gyration of circular ring of same radius about tangential axis is given by: $$ k_{2}=\sqrt{\frac{3}{2}\frac{\mathrm{MR}^{2}}{\mathrm{M}}}=\frac{\sqrt{3}}{\sqrt{2}}\mathrm{R} $$ ： (From parallel Axis Theorem) $$ \begin{array}{l}\frac{k_{1}}{k_{2}}=\frac{\sqrt{5}}{2}\mathbf{R}\left/\frac{\sqrt{3}}{\sqrt{2}}\mathbf{R}\right.\\ =\frac{\sqrt{5}}{2}\times\frac{\sqrt{2}}{\sqrt{3}}\\ =\frac{\sqrt{5}}{\sqrt{6}}\end{array} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.topic: System of Particles and Rotational Motion
questions.question: Three particles, each of mass $m$ gram, are situated at the vertices of an equilateral triangle ABC side $l$ cm (as shown in the figure). The moment of inertia of the system about a line AX perpendicular to AB and in the plane of ABC, in gram $cm^{2}$ units will be:
questions.explanation: According to the question, $$ \begin{array}{l} \displaystyle \mathrm{I}_{A X} = m l^{2} + m \left(\frac{l}{2}\right)^{2} \\ \displaystyle \qquad = \frac{m l^{2} + \frac{m l^{2}}{4}}{5} \\ \displaystyle \qquad = \frac{4}{4} m l^{2} \end{array} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.topic: System of Particles and Rotational Motion
questions.question: A circular disc is to be made by using iron and aluminium, so that it acquire maximum moment of inertia about the geometrical axis. It is possible with:
questions.optionA: aluminium at interior and iron surrounding it
questions.optionB: iron at interior and aluminium surrounding it
questions.optionC: using iron and aluminium lives in an alternate order
questions.optionD: sheet of iron is used at the external surface and aluminium sheet as internal layers.
questions.explanation: Moment of inertia depends on distribution of mass about the axis of rotation. Density of iron is more than that of aluminium. Therefore, the moment of inertia to be maximum, the iron should be far away from the axis.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.topic: System of Particles and Rotational Motion
questions.question: The moment of inertia of a disc of mass M and radius R about an axis, which is tangential to the circumference of the disc and parallel to its diameter is:
questions.explanation:
Moment of inertia of a disc about its diameter, $$ \mathbf{l}_{d}=\frac{1}{4}\mathbf{M}\mathbf{R}^{2} $$
Now, according to perpendicular axis theorem, moment of inertia of disc about a tangent passing through rim in the plane of disc, $$ \begin{aligned}\mathrm{I}&=\mathrm{I}_{d}+\mathrm{MR}^{2}\\\mathrm{I}&=\frac{1}{4}\mathrm{MR}^{2}+\mathrm{MR}^{2}\\\mathrm{I}&=\frac{5}{4}\mathrm{MR}^{2}\end{aligned} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.topic: System of Particles and Rotational Motion
questions.subtopic: Kinematics of Rotational Motion about a Fixed Axis
questions.question: The angular speed of the wheel of a vehicle is increased from 360 rpm to 1200 rpm in 14 s. Its angular acceleration is:
questions.explanation: Initial angular velocity of wheel, $$ \begin{aligned}\omega_{i}&=2\pi f_{o}=2\pi\times\frac{360}{60}\mathrm{rad/s}\\&=12\pi\mathrm{rad/s}\end{aligned} $$ final angular velocity of wheel, $$ \begin{aligned}\omega_{f}&=2\pi f=2\pi\times\frac{1200}{60}\mathrm{rad/s}\\&=40\pi\mathrm{rad/s}\end{aligned} $$ $$ \Delta t=14\,\mathrm{s} $$ From equation of rotational motion $$ \omega_{f}=\omega_{i}+\alpha\Delta t $$ $$ \begin{array}{r}{\alpha=\frac{\omega_{f}-\omega_{i}}{\Delta t}}\\ {=2\pi\mathrm{r a d/s}}\end{array} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.topic: System of Particles and Rotational Motion
questions.subtopic: Kinematics of Rotational Motion about a Fixed Axis
questions.question: A wheel has angular acceleration of 3.0 rad/sec² and an initial angular speed of 2.00 rad/sec. In a time of 2 sec, it has rotated through an angle (in radian) of:
questions.explanation: Here, $$ \begin{array}{l}\theta=\omega_{0}t+\frac{1}{2}\alpha t^{2}\\ =(2)(2)+\frac{1}{2}(3)(2)^{2}\\ =4+6=10\text{rad}\end{array} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.topic: System of Particles and Rotational Motion
questions.subtopic: Dynamics of Rotational Motion about a Fixed Axis
questions.question: A bob is whirled in a horizontal plane by means of a string with an initial speed of $\omega$ rpm. The tension in the string is T. If speed becomes $2\omega$ while keeping the same radius, the tension in the string becomes:
questions.explanation:
Torque about A.
$$ \tau=m g\times\frac{l}{2}=\frac{m g l}{2} $$ Also, $ \tau = I\alpha $ ∴ Angular acceleration, $$ \alpha=\frac{\tau}{1}=\frac{mg\frac{l}{2}}{\frac{ml^{2}}{3}}=\frac{3g}{2l} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.topic: System of Particles and Rotational Motion
questions.subtopic: Dynamics of Rotational Motion about a Fixed Axis
questions.question: A solid cylinder of mass 2 kg and radius 4 cm is rotating about its axis at the rate of 3 rpm. The torque required to stop after $ 2\pi $ revolution is:
questions.explanation: Mass, M = 2 kg, Radius R = 4 cm. Initial angular speed, $ \omega_{0} = 3 \times \frac{2\pi}{60} \text{ rad/s} $ $ = \frac{\pi}{10} \text{ rad/s} $ We know that. $$ \omega^{2}=\omega_{0}^{2}+2\alpha\theta $$ $$ 0=\ \left(\frac{\pi}{10}\right)^{2}+2\alpha\times2\pi\times2\pi $$ ↓ $$ \alpha=-\mathrm{\small~\frac{1}{800}~}\mathrm{r a d/s^{2}} $$ Now moment of inertia of a solid cylinder, $$ \mathrm{I}=\frac{\mathrm{MR}^{2}}{2}=\frac{2\times\left(\frac{4}{100}\right)^{2}}{2}=\frac{16}{10^{4}} $$ Now we know that, Torque, $$ \begin{array}{c} \tau=\mathrm{I}\times\alpha=\frac{16}{10^{4}}\times\left(-\frac{1}{800}\right)\\ =-2\times10^{-6}\ (\mathrm{Nm}). \end{array} $$ Negative sign indicates that the applied torque is retarding in nature.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.topic: System of Particles and Rotational Motion
questions.subtopic: Dynamics of Rotational Motion about a Fixed Axis
questions.question: Three objects, A: (a solid sphere), B: (a thin circular disk) and C: (a circular ring), each have the same mass M and radius R. They all spin with the same angular speed \omega about their own symmetry axes. The amounts of work W required to bring them to rest, would satisfy the relation:
questions.explanation: Work done required to bring them rest, $$ \begin{aligned}\Delta\mathbf{W}&=\Delta\mathbf{K}\mathbf{E}\\\mathbf{W}_{\mathrm{A}}:\mathbf{W}_{\mathrm{B}}:\mathbf{W}_{\mathrm{C}}&=\frac{2}{5}\mathbf{M}\mathbf{R}^{2}:\frac{1}{2}\mathbf{M}\mathbf{R}^{2}:\mathbf{M}\mathbf{R}^{2}\\&=\frac{2}{5}:\frac{1}{2}:1\\&=4:5:10\\\mathbf{W}_{\mathrm{C}}&>\mathbf{W}_{\mathrm{B}}>\mathbf{W}_{\mathrm{A}}.\end{aligned} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.topic: System of Particles and Rotational Motion
questions.subtopic: Dynamics of Rotational Motion about a Fixed Axis
questions.question: A rope is wound around a hollow cylinder of mass 3 kg and radius 40 cm. What is the angular acceleration of the cylinder, if the rope is pulled with a force of 30 N?
questions.explanation:
Torque on the cylinder due to force F
and we know that, $ \tau = \mathrm{I}\alpha $ Where, I = Moment of Inertia of hollow cylinder about axis and $\alpha =$ angular acceleration on comparing both the equations, $$ \alpha=\frac{\tau}{I}=\frac{\mathrm{F R}}{\mathrm{M R}^{2}}=\frac{\mathrm{F}}{\mathrm{M R}} $$ $$ \alpha=\frac{30}{3\times40\times10^{-2}}=25rad/s^{2} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.topic: System of Particles and Rotational Motion
questions.subtopic: Dynamics of Rotational Motion about a Fixed Axis
questions.question: A uniform circular disc of radius 50 cm at rest is free to turn about an axis which is perpendicular to its plane and passes through its centre. It is subjected to a torque which produces a constant angular acceleration of 2.0 rad s^{-2}. Its net acceleration in ms^{-2} at end of 2.0 s is approximately:
questions.explanation: According to the question. Angular acceleration, $ \alpha = 2 \, rad s^{-2} $ Angular speed, $ \omega = \alpha t = 4 \, rad \, s^{-1} $ ∵ centripetal acceleration at the end of 2.0 sec $$ \begin{aligned}a_{t}&=\alpha r=2\times0.5=1\mathrm{~m/s^{2}}\\a_{c}&=\omega^{2}r\\&=4^{2}\times0.5\\&=8\mathrm{~m/s^{2}}\end{aligned} $$ $$ \begin{array}{l} \displaystyle \therefore \text{Net acceleration,} a = \sqrt{a_{c}^{2} + a_{t}^{2}} \\ \displaystyle \qquad = \sqrt{(8)^{2} + (1)^{2}} \\ \displaystyle \qquad = \sqrt{65} \approx 8\ \text{m/s}^2 \end{array} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.topic: System of Particles and Rotational Motion
questions.subtopic: Dynamics of Rotational Motion about a Fixed Axis
questions.question: Point masses $m_{1}$ and $m_{2}$ are placed at the opposite ends of a rigid rod of length L and negligible mass. The rod is to be set rotating about an axis perpendicular to it. The position of point P on this rod through which the axis should pass, so that the work required to set the rod rotating with angular velocity $\omega_{0}$ is minimum, is given by:
questions.explanation:
Here $ m_{1} $ and $ m_{2} $ masses are placed opposite end of the rigid rod whose length is L.
∴ Moment of Inerita of rod, $$ \begin{aligned}\mathrm{I}&=m_{1}x^{2}+m_{2}(\mathrm{L}-x)^{2}\\&=m_{1}x^{2}+m_{2}L^{2}+m_{2}x^{2}-2m_{2}\mathrm{L}x\end{aligned} $$ I $ _{a} $ is minima then, $$ \begin{aligned}\frac{d\mathrm{I}}{dx}&=2m_{1}x+0+2xm_{2}-2m_{2}\mathrm{L}\\&=0\end{aligned} $$ $$ \Rightarrow\quad x(2m_{1}+2m_{2})=2m_{2}L $$ $$ x=\frac{m_{2}L}{m_{1}+m_{2}} $$ $ \therefore $ Minimum work = Minimum rotational K.E. $\Rightarrow$ Maximum angular moments = Minimum moment of inertia ∴ Its rotation should be about CM $$ x=\frac{m_{2}L}{m_{1}+m_{2}} $$

questions.subtopic: Dynamics of Rotational Motion about a Fixed Axis
questions.question: An automobile moves on a road with a speed of 54 km h^{-1}. The radius of its wheels is 0.45 m and the moment of inertia of the wheel about its axis of rotation is 3 kg m^{2}. If the vehicle is brought to rest in 15 s, the magnitude to average torque transmitted by its brokes to the wheel is:
questions.explanation: According to question, Velocity of automobile vehicle is $$ v=54km/h=54\times\frac{5}{18}=15m/s $$ And angular velocity, $ \mathbf{v} = \omega_0 r $ $$ \Theta_{0}=\frac{v}{R}=\frac{15}{0.45}=\frac{100}{3}\text{rad/s} $$ and then angular acceleration is $$ \begin{array}{l}\alpha=\frac{\Delta\omega}{t}=\frac{\omega_{t}-\omega_{0}}{t}\\=\frac{0-\frac{100}{3}}{15}=-\frac{100}{45}\\=-\frac{100}{45}\ \mathrm{rad/s^{2}}\end{array} $$ ∴ Torque, $$ \begin{aligned}\tau&=I\alpha\\&=3\times\frac{100}{45}=6.66kg m^{2}/s^{2}\end{aligned} $$

questions.question: A solid cylinder of mass 50 kg and radius 0.5 m is free to rotate about the horizontal axis. A massless string is wound round the cylinder with one end attached to it and other hanging freely. Tension in the string required to produce an angular acceleration $ 2 \, \text{rev/s}^{2} $ is:
questions.explanation: According to the question, Mass of cylinder, M = 50 kg. Radius of the cylinder, R = 0.5 m Angular acceleration, $ \alpha = 2 \, \text{rev/s}^{2} $ We know that torque, $ \tau = I\alpha $ Now torque produced in the string is : $$ \tau=\mathrm{T}\times\mathrm{R}=\mathrm{T}\times0.5=\frac{\mathrm{T}}{2} $$ From eq. (i) and (ii) $$ \circ\circ $$ $$ \tau=\frac{T}{2} $$ $$ \ensuremath{\ddots} $$ $$ \frac{\mathrm{T}}{2}=\cdot\left(\frac{\mathrm{MR}^{2}}{2}\right)\times2\times2\pi $$ $$ I=\frac{MR^{2}}{2} $$ $$ \begin{aligned}\frac{T}{2}&=\frac{50\times(0.5)^{2}}{2}\times4\pi\\1&=50\times\frac{1}{4}\times4\pi=50\pi\\&=157N\end{aligned} $$

According to question.
Torque on the rod = Moment of the rod P $$ \tau=m g\frac{L}{2} $$ Moment of inertia of rod about P. $$ I=\frac{mL^{2}}{3} $$ From eq. (i) and (ii). {Since $ \tau = 1\alpha $ $$ \alpha=\frac{3g}{2L} $$

questions.question: The instantaneous angular position of a point on a rotating wheel is given by the equation, \theta(t)=2t^{3}-6t^{2}. The torque on the wheel becomes zero at:
questions.explanation: Given that, $ \theta(t) = 2t^{3} - 6t^{2} $ $$ \frac{d\Theta}{d t}=6t^{2}-12t $$ $$ \frac{d^{2}\Theta}{d t^{2}}=12t-12 $$ It is given that, torque $ \tau = 0 $ means $ \alpha = 0 $ and $$ \alpha=\frac{d^{2}\theta}{dt^{2}}=0 $$ $$ \begin{array}{r} 12t-12=0 \\ \Rightarrow \quad t=1\mathrm{s} \end{array} $$

questions.question: A solid cylinder of mass 2 kg and radius 50 cm rolls up an inclined plane of angle inclination $30^{\circ}$. The centre of mass of cylinder has speed of 4 m/s. The distance travelled by the cylinder on the inclined surface will be: (Take $g = 10\ m/s^{2}$)
questions.explanation: $$ \mathrm{I}=2\,\mathrm{k g}\,\mathrm{m}^{2}, $$ $$ \omega_{0}=\frac{60}{60}\times2\pi\mathrm{rad/s} $$ $$ t=60s $$ The torque required to stop the wheel's rotation is, $$ \begin{aligned}\tau&=1\alpha=1\left(\frac{\omega_{0}-\omega}{t}\right)\\\tau&=\frac{2\times2\pi\times60}{60\times60}\\&=\frac{\pi}{15}Nm\end{aligned} $$

questions.subtopic: Angular Momentum in Case of Rotation about a Fixed Axis
questions.question: A solid sphere is in rolling motion. In rolling motion, a body possesses translational kinetic energy $(K_{t})$ as well as rotational kinetic energy $(K_{r})$ simultaneously. The ratio $K_{t}:(K_{t}+K_{r})$ for the sphere is:
questions.explanation: Translational kinetic energy of rolling body, $$ \mathbf{K}_{t}=\frac{1}{2}m\mathbf{v}^{2} $$ ∴ Total kinetic energy = Rotational KE + Translational KE = $ K_{t}+K_{r} $ (Solid sphere, $$ \begin{aligned}\mathrm{I}&=\frac{2}{5}m\mathrm{R}^{2})\\&=\mathrm{K}_{t}+\mathrm{K}_{r}\\&=\frac{1}{2}\bigg(\frac{2}{5}m\mathrm{R}^{2}\bigg)\omega^{2}+\frac{1}{2}m\mathrm{v}^{2}\\&=\frac{1}{2}\bigg(\frac{2}{5}m\mathrm{R}^{2}\bigg)\bigg(\frac{\mathrm{v}}{\mathrm{R}}\bigg)^{2}+\frac{1}{2}m\mathrm{v}^{2}\\&=\frac{1}{5}m\mathrm{v}^{2}+\frac{1}{2}m\mathrm{v}^{2}=\frac{7}{10}m\mathrm{v}^{2}\end{aligned} $$ $$ \begin{array}{l}\text{Ratio}=\frac{\mathbf{K}_{t}}{\mathbf{K}_{t}+\mathbf{K}_{r}}=\frac{\frac{1}{2}m\mathbf{v}^{2}}{\frac{7}{10}m\mathbf{v}^{2}}\\=\frac{1}{2}\times\frac{10}{7}=\frac{5}{7}=5:7\end{array} $$

questions.subject: Physics

questions.subtopic: Angular Momentum in Case of Rotation about a Fixed Axis
questions.question: Two discs of same moment of inertia rotating about their regular axis passing through centre and perpendicular to the plane of disc with angular velocities $\omega_{1}$ and $\omega_{2}$. They are brought into contact face to face coinciding the axis of rotation. The expression for loss of energy during this process is:
questions.explanation: Using law of conservation of angular momentum. $$ \bullet \quad \bullet\bullet $$ $$ =1_{1}\omega_{1}+1_{2}\omega_{2} $$ $$ \begin{aligned}&=\mathrm{I}_{\mathrm{net}}(\boldsymbol{\omega}\\&=(\mathrm{I}_{1}+\mathrm{I}_{2})\boldsymbol{\omega}\end{aligned} $$ $$ \omega=\frac{\mathrm{I}_{1}\omega_{1}+\mathrm{I}_{2}\omega_{2}}{\mathrm{I}_{1}+\mathrm{I}_{2}} $$ $$ \omega=\frac{\mathrm{I}\omega_{1}+\mathrm{I}\omega_{2}}{2\mathrm{I}}=\frac{\omega_{1}+\omega_{2}}{2} $$ $$ \begin{array}{r l}{\therefore\quad\mathrm{L o s s~o f~e n e r g y}=\frac{1}{2}\mathrm{I}\omega_{1}^{2}+\frac{1}{2}\mathrm{I}\omega_{2}^{2}-\frac{1}{2}(2\mathrm{I})\omega^{2}}\\ {\quad}\\ {=\frac{1}{4}\mathrm{I}(\omega_{1}-\omega_{2})^{2}}\end{array} $$

questions.subject: Physics

questions.subtopic: Angular Momentum in Case of Rotation about a Fixed Axis
questions.question: The rotating bodies A and B of masses m and 2m with moments of inertia I_{A} and I_{B}(I_{B} > I_{A}) have equal kinetic energy of rotation. If L_{A} and L_{B} be their angular momenta respectively, then:
questions.explanation: The K.E. of rotating body is, $$ \mathrm{K E}=\frac{1}{2}\mathrm{I}\omega^{2}=\frac{1}{2}\frac{\mathrm{I}^{2}\omega^{2}}{\mathrm{I}}=\frac{\mathrm{L}^{2}}{2\mathrm{I}} $$ (Since, L = Iω) From question $$ \begin{aligned}&K_{\mathrm{A}}=K_{\mathrm{B}}\\&\overset{\Rightarrow}{\quad}\quad\frac{\mathrm{L}_{\mathrm{A}}^{2}}{2\mathrm{I}_{\mathrm{A}}}=\frac{\mathrm{L}_{\mathrm{B}}^{2}}{2\mathrm{I}_{\mathrm{B}}}\quad\\&\mathrm{As},\quad\mathrm{I}_{\mathrm{B}}>\mathrm{I}_{\mathrm{A}}\\&\mathrm{So},\quad\mathrm{L}_{\mathrm{A}}^{2}<\mathrm{L}_{\mathrm{B}}^{2}\\&\overset{\Rightarrow}{\quad}\quad\mathrm{L}_{\mathrm{A}}<\mathrm{L}_{\mathrm{B}}\end{aligned} $$

questions.subtopic: Angular Momentum in Case of Rotation about a Fixed Axis
questions.question: A disc and a sphere of same radius but different masses roll off on two inclined planes of the same altitude and length. Which one of the two objects gets to the bottom of the plane first?
questions.optionA: Both reach at the same time
questions.explanation: Time taken by the body to reach the bottom when it rolls down on an inclined plane without slipping is given by, $$ t=\sqrt{\frac{2l\left(1+\frac{k^{2}}{R^{2}}\right)}{g\sin\theta}} $$ Since $g$ is constant and $l$, $R$ and $\sin\theta$ are same for both, $$ \begin{aligned}\frac{t_{d}}{t_{s}}&=\frac{\sqrt{1+\frac{k_{d}^{2}}{\mathrm{R}^{2}}}}{\sqrt{1+\frac{k_{s}^{2}}{\mathrm{R}^{2}}}}=\sqrt{\frac{1+\frac{\mathrm{R}^{2}}{2\mathrm{R}^{2}}}{1+\frac{2\mathrm{R}^{2}}{5\mathrm{R}^{2}}}}\\&\quad\left(\therefore k_{d}=\frac{\mathrm{R}}{\sqrt{2}},k_{s}=\sqrt{\frac{2}{5}}\mathrm{R}\right)\\&=\sqrt{\frac{3}{2}\times\frac{5}{7}}=\sqrt{\frac{15}{14}}\\t_{d}&>t_{s}\end{aligned} $$ Hence, the sphere gets to the bottom first.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.topic: System of Particles and Rotational Motion
questions.subtopic: Angular Momentum in Case of Rotation about a Fixed Axis
questions.question: The ratio of the acceleration for a solid sphere (mass m and radius R) rolling down and incline of angle $ \theta $ without slipping and slipping down the incline without rolling is:
questions.explanation:
In this question there are two cases. First when a solid sphere rolling without slipping down an inclined plane and second a sphere slipping down in inclined plane. First case :
$$ \begin{aligned}a_{1}&=\frac{g\sin\theta}{1+\frac{k^{2}}{R^{2}}}\\&=\frac{g\sin\theta}{1+\frac{2}{5}}\end{aligned} $$ $$ a_{1}=\frac{5}{7}g\sin\theta $$ (Since, solid sphere $ k^{2} = \frac{2}{5} R^{2} $) Second case: $$ a_{2}=g\sin\theta $$ From (i) and (ii), $$ \begin{aligned}\frac{a_{1}}{a_{2}}&=\frac{\frac{5}{7}g\sin\theta}{g\sin\theta}=\frac{5}{7}\\\frac{a_{1}}{a_{2}}&=\frac{5}{7}\end{aligned} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.topic: System of Particles and Rotational Motion
questions.subtopic: Angular Momentum in Case of Rotation about a Fixed Axis
questions.question: Two discs are rotating about their axis, normal to the plane of the discs and passing through the centre of the discs. Disc $D_{1}$ has 2 kg mass and 0.2 m radius and initial angular velocity of 50 rad s$^{-1}$. Disc $D_{2}$ has 4 kg mass, 0.1 m radius and initial angular velocity of 200 rad s$^{-1}$. The two discs are brought in contact face to face, with their axes of rotation coincident. The final angular velocity (in rad s$^{-1}$) of the system is:
questions.explanation: Moment of Inertia of $ D_{1} $ $$ \mathrm{I}_{1}=\frac{\mathrm{MR}^{2}}{2}=\frac{2(0.2)^{2}}{2}=0.04\mathrm{kg}\cdot\mathrm{m}^{2} $$ Initial angular velocity of D_{1} is \omega_{1}=50\ \mathrm{rad}\ \mathrm{s}^{-1} M.I of $ D_{2} $ is $ I_{2} = \frac{4 \times (0.1)^{2}}{2} = 0.02\ kg\ m^{2} $ Initial angular velocity of $D_{2}$ is $\omega_{2}=200\ \text{rad s}^{-1}$. According to the law of conservation of angular momentum, $$ \begin{aligned}&L_{1}=L_{2}\\ \therefore\quad&I_{1}\omega_{1}+I_{2}\omega_{2}=(I_{1}+I_{2})\omega\\ \Rightarrow\quad&\omega=\frac{I_{1}\omega_{1}+I_{2}\omega_{2}}{I_{1}+I_{2}}\\ \therefore\quad&\omega=\frac{(0.04)^{2}\times50+(0.02)^{2}\times200}{0.04+0.02}\\ &=\frac{2+4}{0.06}=100rad s^{-1}\end{aligned} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.topic: System of Particles and Rotational Motion
questions.subtopic: Angular Momentum in Case of Rotation about a Fixed Axis
questions.question: A small object of uniform density rolls up a curved surface with an initial velocity 'v'. It reaches up to a maximum height of $ \frac{3v^{2}}{4g} $ with respect to the initial position. The object is:
questions.explanation:
The Kinetic energy of the rolling object is converted into potential energy at height h.
$$ h=\frac{3\mathrm{v}^{2}}{4g} $$ So by the law of conservation of mechanical energy, we have $$ \frac{1}{2}\mathbf{M}\mathbf{v}^{2}+\frac{1}{2}\mathbf{I}\boldsymbol{\omega}^{2}=\mathbf{M}\mathbf{g}h $$ $$ \frac{1}{2}\mathbf{M}\mathbf{v}^{2}+\frac{1}{2}\mathbf{I}\left(\frac{\mathbf{v}}{\mathbf{R}}\right)^{2}=\mathbf{M}\mathbf{g}\left(\frac{3\mathbf{v}^{2}}{4g}\right) $$ $$ \frac{1}{2}\mathbf{I}\frac{\mathbf{v}^{2}}{\mathbf{R}^{2}}=\frac{3}{4}\mathbf{M}\mathbf{v}^{2}-\frac{1}{2}\mathbf{M}\mathbf{v}^{2} $$ $$ \frac{1}{2}\mathrm{I}\frac{\mathbf{v}^{2}}{\mathbf{R}^{2}}=\frac{1}{4}\mathbf{M}\mathbf{v}^{2} $$ or $$ \mathrm{I}=\frac{1}{2}\mathbf{M}\mathbf{R}^{2} $$ Hence, the object is disc.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.topic: System of Particles and Rotational Motion
questions.subtopic: Angular Momentum in Case of Rotation about a Fixed Axis
questions.question: A solid cylinder of mass 3 kg is rolling on a horizontal surface with velocity $4\mathrm{~m s}^{-1}$. It collides with a horizontal spring of force constant 200 $\mathrm{N m}^{-1}$. The maximum compression produced in the spring will be:
questions.explanation: At maximum compression the solid cylinder will stop. According to law of conservation of mechanical energy Loss in kinetic energy of cylinder = Gain in potential of spring $$ \frac{1}{2}mv^{2}+\frac{1}{2}I\omega^{2}=\frac{1}{2}kx^{2}; $$ $$ \frac{1}{2}mv^{2}+\frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{mR^{2}}{2}\right)\left(\frac{v}{R}\right)^{2}=\frac{1}{2}kx^{2} $$ ( $ \because v = R\omega $ and for solid cylinder. $ I = \frac{1}{2}mR^{2} $) $$ \frac{1}{2}mv^{2}+\frac{1}{4}mv^{2}=\frac{1}{2}kx^{2}\text{or,} $$ $$ \frac{3}{4}m{\bf v}^{2}=\frac{1}{2}k x^{2}\mathrm{o r} $$ $$ x^{2}=\frac{3}{2}\frac{mv^{2}}{k} $$ Here, $m = 3\ \mathrm{kg}$, $v = 4\ \mathrm{ms}^{-1}$, $k = 200\ \mathrm{Nm}^{-1}$ Substituting the given values, we get $$ x^{2}=\frac{3\times3\times4\times4}{2\times200} $$ $$ x^{2}=\frac{36}{60}or x=0.6m $$

questions.question: The solid cylinder and a hollow cylinder both of the same mass and same external diameter are released from the same height at the same time on a inclined plane. Both roll down without slipping. Which one will reach the bottom first?
questions.optionA: Both together only when angle inclination of plane is  $ 45^{\circ} $
questions.explanation: $$ \mathrm{T}=\frac{1}{\sin\theta}\sqrt{\frac{2h}{g}\left(1+\frac{\mathbf{K}^{2}}{\mathbf{R}^{2}}\right)} $$ And hence, $$ \left(\frac{\mathbf{K}^{2}}{\mathbf{R}^{2}}\right)_{S}<\left(\frac{\mathbf{K}^{2}}{\mathbf{R}^{2}}\right)_{H} $$ Solid body will reach the bottom first with greater velocity.

questions.subject: Physics

questions.subtopic: Angular Momentum in Case of Rotation about a Fixed Axis
questions.question: A thin circular ring of mass M and radius R is rotating in a horizontal plane about an axis vertical to its plane with a constant angular velocity $ \omega $. If two objects each of mass m be attached gently to the opposite ends of a diameter of the ring, the ring will then rotate with an angular velocity:
questions.explanation: Applying law of conservation of angular momentum $$ \mathrm{I}_{1}\omega_{1}=\mathrm{I}_{2}\omega_{2} $$ It is given that : $$ \begin{aligned}\mathrm{I}&=\mathbf{M}\mathbf{R}^{2}\\\mathrm{I}_{2}&=\mathbf{M}\mathbf{R}^{2}+2m\mathbf{R}^{2}\\\omega_{2}&=\frac{\mathrm{I}_{1}}{\mathrm{I}_{2}}\omega\\&=\frac{\mathbf{M}}{\mathbf{M}+2m}\omega\end{aligned} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.topic: System of Particles and Rotational Motion
questions.subtopic: Angular Momentum in Case of Rotation about a Fixed Axis
questions.question: A circular disc of moment of inertia I_{t} is rotating in a horizontal plane, about its symmetry axis, with a constant angular speed \omega_{t}. Another disk of moment of inertia I_{b} is dropped coaxially onto the rotating disc. Initially the second disc has zero angular speed. Eventually both the discs rotates with a constant angular speed \omega_{f}. The energy lost by the initially rotating disc due to friction is:
questions.explanation: According to given problem $$ \mathbf{I}_{t}\boldsymbol{\omega}_{i}=(\mathbf{I}_{t}+\mathbf{I}_{b})\boldsymbol{\omega}_{f} $$ or $$ \boldsymbol{\omega}_{f}=\frac{\mathbf{I}_{t}\boldsymbol{\omega}_{i}}{(\mathbf{I}_{t}+\mathbf{I}_{b})},\mathrm{E}_{i}=\frac{1}{2}\mathbf{I}_{t}\boldsymbol{\omega}_{i}^{2} $$ $$ \mathbf{E}_{f}=\frac{1}{2}(\mathbf{I}_{t}+\mathbf{I}_{b})\boldsymbol{\omega}_{f}^{2}=\frac{1}{2}(\mathbf{I}_{t}+\mathbf{I}_{b})\quad\left(\frac{\mathbf{I}_{t}\boldsymbol{\omega}_{l}}{\mathbf{I}_{t}+\mathbf{I}_{b}}\right) $$ Loss of energy is $ \Delta E $ ∴ $$ \begin{aligned}\Delta\mathbf{E}&=\mathbf{E}_{i}-\mathbf{E}_{f}\\&=\frac{1}{2}\mathbf{I}_{t}\boldsymbol{\omega}_{i}^{2}-\frac{1}{2}\frac{\mathbf{I}_{t}^{2}\boldsymbol{\omega}_{i}^{2}}{(\mathbf{I}_{t}+\mathbf{I}_{b})}\\&=\frac{1}{2}\frac{\mathbf{I}_{b}\mathbf{I}_{t}\boldsymbol{\omega}_{i}^{2}}{(\mathbf{I}_{t}+\mathbf{I}_{b})}\end{aligned} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.topic: System of Particles and Rotational Motion
questions.subtopic: Angular Momentum in Case of Rotation about a Fixed Axis
questions.question: A roller coaster is designed such that riders experience "weightlessness" as they go round the top of a hill whose radius of curvature is 20 m. The speed of the car at the top of the hill is between:
questions.explanation: Balancing the force, $$ \mathbf{M}\mathbf{g}-\mathbf{N}=\frac{\mathbf{M}\mathbf{v}^{2}}{r} $$ When N = 0 for weightlessness $$ \frac{\mathbf{M}\mathbf{v}^{2}}{r}=\mathbf{M}\mathbf{g} $$ ∴ $$ \mathbf{v}^{2}=g r=20\times10=200 $$ $$ \mathrm{v}=\sqrt{200}=14.14\mathrm{~m/s} $$ The speed of the car at the top of the hill is between 14 m/s and 15 m/s.

questions.question: A round disc of moment of inertia $I_{2}$ about its axis perpendicular to its plane and passing through its centre is placed over another disc of moment of inertia $I_{1}$ rotating with an angular velocity $\omega$ about the same axis. The final angular velocity of the combination of discs is:
questions.explanation: From law of conservation of angular momentum we have : $$ \mathrm{I}_{1}\omega_{1}=(\mathrm{I}_{1}+\mathrm{I}_{2})\omega_{2}^{\prime} $$ $$ \omega_{2}^{\prime}=\frac{\mathrm{I}_{1}\omega_{1}}{(\mathrm{I}_{1}+\mathrm{I}_{2})} $$

questions.question: A thin circular ring of Mass M and radius r is rotating about its axis with a constant angular velocity $ \omega $. Four objects each of mass m, are kept gently to the opposite ends of two perpendicular diameters of the ring. The angular velocity of the ring will be:
questions.explanation: According to law of conservation of angular momentum, $$ \mathbf{M}r^{2}\boldsymbol{\omega}=(\mathbf{M}r^{2}+4m r^{2})\boldsymbol{\omega}^{\prime} $$ $$ \omega^{\prime}=\frac{\mathbf{M}\omega}{\mathbf{M}+4m} $$

questions.question: A solid cylinder of mass M and radius R rolls without slipping an inclined plane of length L and height h. What is the speed of its centre of mass when the cylinder reaches its bottom?
questions.explanation: Here, K.E. of centre of mass when cylinder reached the bottom, $$ \mathrm{K.E.}=\frac{1}{2}m v^{2}+\frac{1}{2}\mathrm{I}\omega^{2} $$ Here. $$ \mathrm{I}=\frac{1}{2}\mathrm{M}\mathrm{K}^{2} $$ and $$ \omega=\frac{\mathbf{v}}{\mathbf{R}} $$ ∴ $$ \begin{array}{l}\mathrm{KE}=\frac{1}{2}m\mathrm{v}^{2}+\frac{1}{2}m\mathrm{K}^{2}.\frac{\mathrm{v}^{2}}{r^{2}}\\=\frac{1}{2}m\mathrm{v}^{2}\left[1+\frac{\mathrm{K}^{2}}{\mathrm{R}^{2}}\right]\end{array} $$ But for solid cylinder, $K = \frac{R}{\sqrt{2}}$ or $$ \frac{\mathbf{K}^{2}}{\mathbf{R}^{2}}=\frac{1}{2} $$ $$ \mathrm{KE}=\frac{3}{4}mv^{2} $$ $$ \boldsymbol{\ddot{\theta}} $$ PE of the solid cylinder at height h is $$ PE=mgh $$ From eq. (ii) and (iii), $$ mgh=\frac{3}{4}mv^{2} $$ $$ \mathbf{v}=\sqrt{\frac{3}{4}gh} $$ $ \Rightarrow $

questions.subject: Physics
questions.topic: System of Particles and Rotational Motion
questions.subtopic: Angular Momentum in Case of Rotation about a Fixed Axis
questions.question: A ball rolls without slipping. The radius of gyration of the ball about an axis passing through its centre of mass is K. If radius of the ball be R, then the fraction of total energy associated with its rotational energy will be:
questions.explanation: $$ \begin{aligned}Total~energy&=\frac{1}{2}I\omega^{2}+\frac{1}{2}mv^{2}\\&=\frac{1}{2}mv^{2}\left(1+\frac{\mathbf{K}^{2}}{\mathbf{R}^{2}}\right)\end{aligned} $$ Rotational energy = $ \frac{1}{2}I\omega^{2} = \frac{1}{2}mv^{2}\frac{K^{2}}{R^{2}} $ $$ \frac{\mathrm{Rotational~Energy}}{\mathrm{Total~Energy}}=\frac{\mathrm{K}^{2}/\mathrm{R}^{2}}{1+\frac{\mathrm{K}^{2}}{\mathrm{R}^{2}}} $$ $$ \begin{array}{l}\text{Required fraction}=\frac{\mathrm{K}^{2}/\mathrm{R}^{2}}{1+\mathrm{K}^{2}/\mathrm{R}^{2}}\\=\frac{\mathrm{K}^{2}}{\mathrm{R}^{2}+\mathrm{K}^{2}}\end{array} $$

questions.question: Consider a point P at the contact point of a wheel on the ground which roll on the ground without slipping, then the value of the displacement of point P when wheel completes half of the rotation (if radius of wheel is 1 m) is:
questions.explanation: \[\begin{array}{l} \text{Displacement PQ} =\displaystyle {\lim_{\text{(PM) } k \to 0}} \quad ( \sqrt { ( \text{PM}) ^ { 2 } + ( \text{QM}) ^ { 2 } } ) \\ \qquad = \displaystyle {\lim_{\text{v } d \to 8}} \left( \text{let} \text{let } x \to 2 \text{or} \text{let } y \to 8 \text{or} \text{let } u \to 8 \text{or} \text{let } z \to 8} \\ \qquad = 2 \text{or} \displaystyle {\lim_{\text{f } d \to 8}} 4 \text{or} \displaystyle {\lim_{\text{y } a \to 8}} 4 \text{or} \displaystyle {\lim_{\text{y } h \to 8}} 6 \text{or} \displaystyle {\lim_{\text{y } o \to 8}} 4 \text{or} \text{lim } x \to 8 \text{or} \displaystyle {\lim_{\text{y } t \to 8}} 4 \text{or} \text{lim } o \to 8 \text{or} \displaystyle {\lim_{\text{y } t \to 8}} 4 \text{or} \text{lim } w \to 8 \text{or} \displaystyle {\lim_{\text{y } o \to 8}} 4 \text{or} \text{lim } t \to 8 \text{or} \displaystyle {\lim_{\text{y } n \to 8}} 4 \text{or}

questions.subject: Physics
questions.topic: System of Particles and Rotational Motion
questions.subtopic: Angular Momentum in Case of Rotation about a Fixed Axis
questions.question: A disc is rotating with angular speed $ \omega $. If a child sits on it, what is conserved?
questions.explanation: $$ \begin{aligned}\tau&=\frac{d\mathrm{L}}{dt},\mathrm{so}\tau=0\\\tau&=\frac{d\mathrm{L}}{dt}=\mathrm{constant}\end{aligned} $$ Law of conservation of angular momentum.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.topic: System of Particles and Rotational Motion
questions.subtopic: Angular Momentum in Case of Rotation about a Fixed Axis
questions.question: A disc is rolling, the velocity of its centre of mass is $ v_{cm} $. Which one will be correct?
questions.optionA: The velocity of highest point is  $ 2v_{cm} $ and at point of contact is zero.
questions.optionB: The velocity of highest point is  $ v_{cm} $ and at point of contact is  $ v_{cm} $.
questions.optionC: The velocity of highest point is  $ 2v_{cm} $ and point of contact is  $ v_{cm} $.
questions.optionD: The velocity of highest point is $2v_{cm}$ and point of contact is $2v_{cm}$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.topic: System of Particles and Rotational Motion
questions.subtopic: Angular Momentum in Case of Rotation about a Fixed Axis
questions.question: The speed of a homogenous solid sphere after rolling down an inclined plane of vertical height $h$ from rest without sliding is:
questions.explanation: $$ \mathrm{P.E.}=\mathrm{t o t a l~K.E.} $$ $$ m g h=\frac{7}{10}m v^{2},v=\sqrt{\frac{10g h}{7}} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.topic: System of Particles and Rotational Motion
questions.subtopic: Angular Momentum in Case of Rotation about a Fixed Axis
questions.question: At any instant, a rolling body may be considered to be in pure rotation about an axis through the point of contact. This axis is translating forward with speed:
questions.explanation: Since, the instantaneous axis of rotation is always below the centre of mass in this case. This is only possible if the point of contact moves at the same speed as the centre of mass.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.topic: System of Particles and Rotational Motion
questions.subtopic: Angular Momentum in Case of Rotation about a Fixed Axis
questions.question: A solid homogenous sphere of mass M and radius is moving on a rough horizontal surface, partly rolling and partly sliding. During this kind of motion of the sphere:
questions.optionA: the total kinetic energy is conserved.
questions.optionB: the angular momentum of the sphere about the point of contact with the plane is conserved.
questions.optionC: only the rotational kinetic energy about the centre of mass is conserved.
questions.optionD: angular momentum about the centre of mass is conserved.
questions.explanation: Angular momentum about the point of contact with the surface includes the angular momentum about the centre. Because of friction, linear momentum will not be conserved.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: The kinetic energies of a planet in an elliptical orbit about the Sun, at positions A, B and C are $K_{A}$, $K_{B}$ and $K_{C}$ respectively, AC is the major axis and SB is perpendicular to AC at the position of the Sun S as shown in the figure. Then:

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A planet moving along an elliptical orbit is closest to the sun at a distance $ r_{1} $ and farthest away at a distance of $ r_{2} $. If $ v_{1} $ and $ v_{2} $ are the linear velocities at these points respectively, then the ratio $ \frac{v_{1}}{v_{2}} $ is:
questions.explanation: Using law of conservation of angular momentum $ L_1 = L_2 $ $ mr_1v_1 = mr_2v_2 $ $ r_1v_1 = r_2v_2 $ $ \frac{v_1}{v_2} = \frac{r_2}{r_1} $

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: The figure shows elliptical orbit of a planet $m$ about the sun S. The shaded area SCD is twice the shaded area SAB, if $t_{1}$ is the time for the planet to move from C to D and $t_{2}$ is the time to move from A to B, then:
questions.explanation: Applying Kepler's law of planetary motion. $$\frac{d\mathbf{A}}{dt} = \mathrm{constant}$$ $$\Rightarrow \quad \frac{\mathbf{A}_{1}}{t_{1}} = \frac{\mathbf{A}_{2}}{t_{2}}$$ $$\Rightarrow \quad t_{1} = \frac{\mathbf{A}_{1}}{\mathbf{A}_{2}}t_{2}$$ Since, $A_{1} = 2A_{2}$, Hence, $t_{1} = 2t_{2}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: The period of revolution of the planet A around the sun is 8 times that of B. The distance of A from the sun is how many times greater than that of B from the sun?
questions.explanation: According to Kepler's third law, $$ \mathrm{T}^{2}\propto r^{3} $$ where, T = Time period of revolution r = Semi-major axis $\therefore \frac{T_{A}^{2}}{T_{B}^{2}} = \frac{r_{A}^{3}}{r_{B}^{3}}$ $\therefore \frac{r_{A}}{r_{B}} = \left(\frac{T_{A}}{T_{B}}\right)^{2/3}$ $=(8)^{\frac{2}{3}}$ $= 2^{3 \times \frac{2}{3}}$ =4 or $r_{A}=4r_{B}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: The distance of two planets from the sun are $ 10^{13} $ m and $ 10^{12} $ m respectively. The ratio of time periods of these two planets is:
questions.explanation: According to Kepler's third law (or law of periods) i.e., $ T^{2} \propto r^{3} $ Here, $ r_{1} = 10^{13} $ m, $ r_{2} = 10^{12} $ m (C) According to Kepler's third law (or law i.e., $ T^{2} \propto r^{3} $ Here, $ r_{1}=10^{13} $ m, $ r_{2}=10^{12} $ m $ \therefore\frac{T_{1}^{2}}{T_{2}^{2}}=\frac{r_{1}^{3}}{r_{2}^{3}}=\frac{\left(10^{13}\right)^{3}}{\left(10^{12}\right)^{3}} $ or $ \frac{T_{1}^{2}}{T_{2}^{2}}=\frac{10^{39}}{10^{36}}=10^{3} $ or $ \frac{T_{1}}{T_{2}}=10\sqrt{10} $

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A planet is moving in an elliptical orbit around the Sun. If T, V, E and L stand respectively for its kinetic energy, gravitational potential energy, total energy and magnitude of angular momentum about the centre of force, which of the following is correct?
questions.optionD: L is conserved but direction of vector L changes continuously.
questions.explanation: In a circular or elliptical orbital motion of a planet, the angular momentum is conserved. In attractive field, potential energy and the total energy is negative. Kinetic energy increases with increase in velocity. If the motion is in a plane, the direction of L does not change.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: The largest and the shortest distance of the Earth from the sun are $ r_{1} $ and $ r_{2} $. Its distance from the sun when it is at perpendicular to the major axis of the orbit drawn from the sun is:
questions.explanation: Applying the properties of ellipse. we have. $$ \frac{2}{R}=\frac{1}{r_{1}}+\frac{1}{r_{2}}=\frac{r_{1}+r_{2}}{r_{1}r_{2}} $$ $$ \mathrm{R}=\frac{2r_{1}r_{2}}{r_{1}+r_{2}} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Two astronauts are floating in gravitational free space after having lost contact with their spaceship. The two will:
questions.optionA: keep floating at the same distance between them
questions.explanation: Both the astronauts are in the condition of weightlessness. Gravitational force between them pulls towards each other.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Kepler's third law states that square of period of revolution (T) of a planet around the sun, is proportional to third power of average distance $r$ between the sun and planet is $T^{2} = Kr^{3}$, here K is constant. If the masses of the sun and planet are M and $m$ respectively, then as per Newton's law of gravitation force of attraction between them is: $F = \frac{GMm}{r^{2}}$, here G is gravitational constant. The relation between G and K is described as:
questions.explanation: Let, mass of Sun = M and mass of planet = m Then the gravitational force between them, $$ \mathrm{F}=\frac{\mathrm{GM}m}{r^{2}} $$ where, r = distance between sun and planet. The above force provided the centripetal force, $$ \begin{array}{l} \Rightarrow \quad \frac{GMm}{r^2}=\frac{mv^2}{r} \\ \Rightarrow \quad \sqrt{\frac{GM}{r}}=v \end{array} $$ We know that time period of planet is, $$ \begin{array}{c} \mathrm{T}=\frac{2\pi r}{\mathrm{v}}\\ \Rightarrow\quad\mathrm{T}=\frac{2\pi r}{\sqrt{\frac{\mathrm{G M}}{r}}}\\ \Rightarrow\quad\mathrm{T}^{2}=\frac{4\pi^{2}r^{2}}{\underline{\mathrm{G M}}r}=\frac{4\pi^{2}r^{3}}{\mathrm{G M}} \end{array} $$ According to Kepler's third law, $$ \begin{aligned}&T^{2}\propto r^{3}\\&T^{2}=kr^{3}\\ \end{aligned} $$ From eq. (i) and (ii), $$ \frac{4\pi^{2}r^{3}}{GM}=kr^{3} $$ $$ G M K=4\pi^{2} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Two spherical bodies of masses M and 5M and radii R and 2R are released in free space with initial separation between their centres equal to 12R. If they attract each other due to gravitational force only, then the distance covered by the smaller body before collision is:
questions.explanation: Mass of one spherical body = M $$ Its~radius=R $$ Mass of second body = 5M, $$ \mathrm{Its~radius}=2\mathrm{R} $$ Then we have. $$ \begin{aligned}\mathrm{M}x&=5\mathrm{M}\left(9\mathrm{R}-x\right)\\x&=45\mathrm{R}-5x\\x&=\frac{45\mathrm{R}}{6}=7.5\mathrm{~R}\end{aligned} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A body projected vertically from the Earth reaches a height equal Earth's radius before returning to the Earth. The power exerted by the gravitational force is greatest:
questions.optionA: at the instant just before the body hits the Earth
questions.optionC: at the instant just after the body is projected
questions.optionD: at the height position of the body
questions.explanation: We know that, $$ {\bf\nabla}_{\bf P}\mathrm{\;=\;F.v\;=\;F v\;}\cos\theta $$ Power will be maximum, when velocity and $\cos\theta$ will be maximum.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Two spheres of masses m and M are situated in air and the gravitational force between them is F. The space around the masses is now filled with a liquid of specific gravity 3. The gravitational force will now be:
questions.explanation: The gravitational force does not depend upon the medium in which objects are placed.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Two particles of equal mass m go around a circle of radius R under the action of their mutual gravitational attraction. The speed v of each particle is:
questions.explanation: Force between the two masses, $$ \mathrm{F}=\frac{\mathrm{Gm}^{2}}{4\mathrm{R}^{2}} $$ This force will provide the necessary centripetal force for the masses to go around a circle, then, $$ \frac{G m^{2}}{4R^{2}}=\frac{mv^{2}}{R} $$ $$ ↳\Downarrow $$ $$ \mathbf{v}^{2}=\frac{G m}{4R} $$ $$ \mathbf{v}=\frac{1}{2}\sqrt{\frac{G m}{R}} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: The Earth (mass = $6 \times 10^{24}$ kg) revolves around the sun with an angular velocity of $2 \times 10^{-7}$ rad/s in a circular orbit of radius $1.5 \times 10^{8}$ km. The force exerted by the sun on the Earth, in Newton is:
questions.explanation: Given: $$ mass(m)=6\times10^{24}kg; $$ angular velocity ( $ \omega $) = $ 2 \times 10^{-7} $ rad/s and $$ \begin{aligned}radius\left(r\right)&=1.5\times10^{8}km\\&=1.5\times10^{11}m\end{aligned} $$ $$ \begin{aligned}Force~exerted~on~the~Earth,~F&=mR\omega^{2}\\&=(6\times10^{24})\times(1.5\times10^{11})\\&=36\times10^{21}N\end{aligned}\times(2\times10^{-7})^{2} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: If the gravitational force between two objects were proportional to $\frac{1}{R}$ (and not as $\frac{1}{R^{2}}$), where R is the distance between them, then a particle in a circular path (under such a force) would have its orbital speed $v$, proportional to:
questions.explanation: Centripetal force (F) = $ \frac{mv^{2}}{R} $ $$ \mathrm{F}=\frac{\mathrm{GM}m}{\mathrm{R}} $$ $$ \mathrm{F}=\frac{\mathrm{G M m}}{\mathrm{R}},(\mathrm{w h e r e}\mathrm{R}^{2}\rightarrow\mathrm{R}). $$ Since, $$ \frac{mv^{2}}{R}=\frac{GMm}{R} $$ therefore. $$ \mathbf{v}=\sqrt{\mathbf{G}\mathbf{M}}. $$ Thus velocity v is independent of R. Hence, particle in circular orbit under such a force would have its orbital speed v perportional to $ R^{\circ} $.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.subtopic: Acceleration Due to Gravity of the Earth
questions.question: The mass of a planet is $\frac{1}{10}^{th}$ that of the earth and its diameter is half that of the earth. The acceleration due to gravity on that planet is:
questions.explanation: As $$ g=\frac{G M}{R^{2}}=9.8m/s^{2} $$ $$ (g=9.8\;\mathrm{m/s^{2}}) $$ Now for planet. $$ \mathbf{M}=\frac{\mathbf{M}}{10} $$ [Given: $ \frac{1}{10} $ m of Earth] $$ \mathbf{D}^{\prime}=\frac{\mathbf{D}}{2} $$ [Given: half of the Earth] $$ \mathbf{R}^{\prime}=\frac{\mathbf{R}}{2} $$ Now $$ \begin{aligned}\mathbf{g}^{\prime}&=\frac{\mathrm{GM}^{\prime}}{(\mathrm{R}^{\prime})^{2}}=\frac{\mathrm{GM}\times4}{10\times\mathrm{R}^{2}}\\&=\frac{4}{10}\times\frac{\mathrm{GM}}{\mathrm{R}^{2}}\end{aligned} $$ Using eqn (i), we get $$ g^{\prime}=0.4\times9.8\mathrm{~m/s^{2}}=3.92\mathrm{~m/s^{2}}. $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.subtopic: Acceleration Due to Gravity of the Earth
questions.question: If the mass of the Sun were ten times smaller and the universal gravitational constant were ten times larger in magnitude, which of the following is NOT correct?
questions.optionA: Time period of a simple pendulum on the Earth would decreases
questions.optionB: Walking on the ground would become more difficult
questions.optionD: 'g' on the Earth will not change
questions.explanation: Let: $ M_{e} = \text{Original mass of Earth} $ G = Gravitational constant Then from question, ∴ New gravitational constant $$ \mathrm{G}^{\prime}=10\mathrm{G} $$ We know that, Acceleration due to gravity, $$ g=\frac{GM_{e}}{R^{2}} $$ $$ M_{e}=mass of Earth $$ New acceleration due to gravity, $$ \begin{array}{l}\mathrm{g}^{\prime}=\frac{\mathrm{G}\mathrm{M}_{e}}{\mathrm{R}^{2}}\\=\frac{10\mathrm{M}_{e}\mathrm{G}}{\mathrm{R}^{2}}\end{array} $$ $$ g^{\prime}=10g $$ $$ \Rightarrow $$ We also know that time period of simple pendulum is, $$ \mathrm{T}=2\pi\sqrt{\frac{l}{g}} $$ $$ \mathrm{T}\propto\sqrt{\frac{l}{g}} $$ or This confirms that time period of pendulum also decreases with the increase in g.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.subtopic: Acceleration Due to Gravity of the Earth
questions.question: A spherical planet has a mass M_{P} and diameter D_{P}. A particle of mass m falling freely near the surface of this planet will experience an acceleration due to gravity is:
questions.explanation: We know that, Gravitational force, $$ \mathrm{F}=\frac{\mathrm{GM}_{e}m}{\mathrm{R}^{2}} $$ and $$ \begin{aligned}F&=\frac{\mathrm{G}\mathrm{M}_{\mathrm{p}}m}{\left(\mathrm{D}_{\mathrm{p}}/2\right)^{2}}\\&=\frac{4\mathrm{G}\mathrm{M}_{\mathrm{p}}m}{\mathrm{D}_{\mathrm{p}}^{2}}\end{aligned} $$ And $$ \mathbf{F}=ma $$ $$ a=\frac{F}{m} $$ $$ \begin{array}{l}a=\displaystyle\frac{4\mathrm{GM_{p}}m}{(\mathrm{D_{p}^{2}}.m)}\\ =\displaystyle\frac{4\mathrm{GM_{p}}}{\mathrm{D_{p}^{2}}}\end{array} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.subtopic: Acceleration Due to Gravity of the Earth
questions.question: Imagine a new planet having the same density as that of Earth but it is 3 times bigger than the Earth in size. If the acceleration due to gravity on the surface of Earth is g and that on the surface of the new planet is g' then:
questions.explanation: $$ \begin{array}{l}g=\frac{\mathrm{G}\mathbf{M}}{\mathbf{R}^{2}}=\frac{\mathrm{G}\frac{4}{3}\pi\mathbf{R}^{3}\rho}{\mathbf{R}^{2}}\\ =\frac{4}{3}\mathbf{G}\pi\mathbf{R}\rho\end{array} $$ $$ \begin{aligned}g^{\prime}&=\frac{4}{3}\mathbf{G}\pi\left(3\mathbf{R}\right)\boldsymbol{\rho}\\&=3g\end{aligned} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.subtopic: Acceleration Due to Gravity of the Earth
questions.question: The density of a newly discovered planet is twice that of Earth. The acceleration due to gravity at the surface of the planet is equal to that at the surface of the Earth. If the radius of the Earth R, the radius of the planet would be:
questions.explanation: We know that: $$ \frac{\mathbf{G}\mathbf{M}_{p}}{\mathbf{R}_{p}^{2}}\;=\;\frac{\mathbf{G}\mathbf{M}_{e}}{\mathbf{R}_{e}^{2}} $$ $$ \frac{\mathbf{G}\times\frac{4}{3}\boldsymbol{\pi}\mathbf{R}_{p}^{3}\boldsymbol{\rho}_{p}}{\mathbf{R}_{p}^{2}}=\frac{\mathbf{G}\times\frac{4}{3}\boldsymbol{\pi}\mathbf{R}_{e}^{3}\boldsymbol{\rho}_{e}}{\mathbf{R}_{e}^{2}} $$ we get, $$ \mathrm{R}_{p}\uprho_{p}=\mathrm{R}_{e}\uprho_{e} $$ Given. $$ \uprho_{p}=2\uprho_{e} $$ then $$ \mathrm{\bf~R}_{p}\times2\uprho_{\mathrm{e}}=\mathrm{\bf~R}_{e}\uprho_{e} $$ $$ \mathbf{R}_{p}=\frac{\mathbf{R}_{r}}{2}=\frac{\mathbf{R}}{2} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.subtopic: Acceleration Due to Gravity of the Earth
questions.question: The acceleration due to gravity on the planet A is 9 times the acceleration due to gravity on planet B. A man jumps to a height of 2 m on the surface of A. What is the height of jump by the same person on the plane B?
questions.explanation: Height of jump on the planet B $$= \frac{g_{\mathrm{A}}}{g_{\mathrm{B}}} \times \text{height of jump on the planet A}$$ $$= 2 \times 9 = 18 \, \mathrm{m}$$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.subtopic: Acceleration Due to Gravity of the Earth
questions.question: What will be the formula of mass of the Earth in terms of g, R and G?
questions.explanation: As we know, $$ \mathrm{F}=\frac{\mathrm{GMm}}{\mathrm{R}^{2}} $$ $$ mg=\frac{GMm}{R^{2}} $$ $$ g=\frac{\mathrm{G M}}{\mathrm{R}^{2}} $$ So, $$ \mathbf{M}=\frac{g\mathbf{R}^{2}}{G} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.subtopic: Acceleration Due to Gravity of the Earth
questions.question: The acceleration due to gravity g and mean density of the Earth $ \rho $ are related by which of the following relations? (where G is the gravitational constant and R is the radius of the Earth.)
questions.explanation: Acceleration due to gravity $$ \begin{array}{l}g=\mathrm{G}\times\frac{\mathbf{M}}{\mathbf{R}^{2}}\\=\mathrm{G}\frac{(4/3)\pi\mathbf{R}^{3}\times\rho}{\mathbf{R}^{2}}\\ \cdot=\mathrm{G}\times\frac{4}{3}\pi\mathbf{R}\times\rho\end{array} $$ $$ \rho=\frac{3g}{4\pi GR} $$ or

questions.subject: Physics
questions.subtopic: Acceleration Due to Gravity of the Earth
questions.question: The radius of Earth is about 6400 km and that of Mars is 3200 km. The mass of the Earth is about 10 times mass of Mars. An object weights 200 N on the surface of Earth. Its weight on the surface of Mars will be:
questions.explanation: Given, Radius of Earth $ R_{e} $ = 6400 km; Radius of Mars $ R_{m} $ = 3200 km; Mass of Earth $ M_{e} $ = 10 M_{m} and Weight of the object on Earth $ W_{e} $ = 200 N. $$ \begin{aligned}\frac{\mathrm{W}_{m}}{\mathrm{W}_{e}}&=\frac{m g_{m}}{m g_{e}}=\frac{\mathrm{M}_{m}}{\mathrm{M}_{e}}\times\left(\frac{\mathrm{R}_{e}}{\mathrm{R}_{m}}\right)^{2}\\&=\frac{1}{10}\times(2)^{2}=\frac{2}{5}\end{aligned} $$ or $$ \mathbf{W}_{m}=\mathbf{W}_{e}\times\frac{2}{5}=200\times0.4=80\mathrm{~N} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.subtopic: Acceleration Due to Gravity Below and Above the Surface of Earth
questions.question: An object of mass 100 kg falls from point X to Y as shown in figure. The change in its weight, corrected to the nearest integer is $(R_{E})$ is the radius of the earth):
questions.explanation: The acceleration due to gravity at height $h$ above the Earth's surface is given by, $$ g=\frac{\mathrm{G M}}{(\mathrm{R}_{e}+h)^{2}} $$ At point x, $$ g_{1}=\frac{\mathrm{G M}}{\left(3\mathrm{R}_{e}\right)^{2}} $$ At point $y$, $$ g_{2}=\frac{\mathrm{G M}}{\left(\frac{5\mathrm{R}_{e}}{2}\right)^{2}} $$ Change in weight, $$ \begin{array}{l} \displaystyle \Delta \mathbf{W} = m g_{2} - m g_{1} \\ \displaystyle \qquad = m \left[ \frac{4}{25} - \frac{1}{9} \right] \frac{\mathbf{G}\mathbf{M}}{\mathbf{R}_{o}^{2}} \\ \displaystyle \qquad = \frac{11 m g}{225} \\ \displaystyle \qquad = \frac{11}{225} \times 100 \times 10 \\ \displaystyle \qquad = 48.89 \simeq 49 \text{N} \end{array} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.subtopic: Acceleration Due to Gravity Below and Above the Surface of Earth
questions.question: What is the depth at which the value of acceleration due to gravity becomes $ \frac{1}{n} $ times the value at the surface of Earth (Radius of Earth = R)?
questions.explanation: Radius of Earth = R Let at depth $d$, gravitational acceleration become $\frac{g}{n}$. $$ g_{d}=g_{o}\left(1-\frac{d}{\mathbf{R}}\right) $$ $$ \frac{g_{o}}{n}=g_{o}\left(1-\frac{d}{R}\right) $$ $$ \frac{1}{n}=1-\frac{d}{\mathbf{R}} $$ $$ \mathrm{\frac{d}{R}}=1-\frac{1}{n} $$ $$ \mathrm{\frac{d}{R}}=\left(\mathrm{\frac{n-1}{n}}\right) $$ $$ d=\left(\frac{n-1}{n}\right)R. $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.subtopic: Acceleration Due to Gravity Below and Above the Surface of Earth
questions.question: A body weights 72 N on the surface of the Earth. What is the gravitational force on it, at a height equal to half of radius of the Earth?
questions.explanation: Given, W = mg = 72 N on surface of the Earth. At height h, equal to half of radius of Earth. $$ h=\frac{\mathbf{R}}{2} $$ ∴ Acceleration due to gravity, $$ \begin{aligned}g^{\prime}&=g\left(\frac{\mathbf{R}}{\mathbf{R}+h}\right)^{2}\\&=\mathbf{g}\left(\frac{\mathbf{R}}{\mathbf{R}+\frac{\mathbf{R}}{2}}\right)^{2}\\&=g\left(\frac{4\mathbf{R}^{2}}{9\mathbf{R}^{2}}\right)\\ \end{aligned} $$ $$ g^{\prime}=g\times\frac{4}{9} $$ $$ \begin{array}{l} \text{mg}' = m g \times \frac{4}{9} \\ \qquad = \frac{4}{9} \times 72 \end{array} $$ $$ mg^{\prime}=32\mathrm{N} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.subtopic: Acceleration Due to Gravity Below and Above the Surface of Earth
questions.question: A body weights 200 N on the surface of the Earth. How much will it weight half way down to the centre of the Earth?
questions.explanation:
Below the surface of Earth, acceleration due to gravity is: $$ g^{\prime}=g\left(1-\frac{d}{R}\right) $$
Where, d = depth, R = radius of Earth $g = \text{acceleration due to gravity}$ Multiply m in both sides of equations. Given, $$ m g^{\prime}=m g\left(1-\frac{d}{R}\right) $$ Given. $$ \mathrm{W}=m\mathrm{g}=200 $$ $$ d=\frac{\mathrm{R}}{2} $$ Putting these values in eq. (ii), we get $$ \begin{aligned}mg)&=200\left(1-\frac{R}{\frac{2}{R}}\right)=200\left(1-\frac{1}{2}\right)\\&=200\times\frac{1}{2}\\&=100\text{N}\\ \end{aligned} $$ ∴ The body will weight 100 N half way down to the centre of Earth.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.subtopic: Acceleration Due to Gravity Below and Above the Surface of Earth
questions.question: The acceleration due to gravity at a height 1 km above the Earth is the same as at a depth d below the surface of Earth. Then:
questions.explanation: Given, Acceleration due to gravity at depth $d$ is, $$ g^{\prime}=g\left(1-\frac{d}{\mathbf{R}}\right) $$ Where, R = radius of Earth And Acceleration due to gravity at height $h$ is, $$ g^{\prime \prime}=g\left(1-\frac{2h}{\mathrm{R}}\right) $$ According to question, $$ g^{\prime}=g\mathrm{\,a n d\,\,}h=1\mathrm{\,k m} $$ $$ \circ\circ $$ $$ g\left(1-\frac{d}{R}\right)=g\left(1-\frac{2h}{R}\right) $$ (putting h = 1) $$ g\left(1-\frac{d}{\mathrm{R}}\right)=g\left(1-\frac{2}{\mathrm{R}}\right) $$ $$ \frac{d}{R}=\frac{2}{R} $$ $$ d=2\mathrm{k m} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.subtopic: Acceleration Due to Gravity Below and Above the Surface of Earth
questions.question: The height at which the weight of a body becomes $ \frac{1}{16} $ its weight on the surface of Earth (radius R), is:
questions.explanation: According to the question, $$ \frac{GMm}{\left(R+h\right)^{2}}=\frac{1}{16}\frac{GMm}{R^{2}} $$ Where, R = Radius of Earth $$ \frac{1}{\left(R+h\right)^{2}}=\frac{1}{16R^{2}} $$ $$ \frac{\mathrm{R}}{\mathrm{R}+h}=\frac{1}{4} $$ $$ \frac{\mathbf{R}+h}{\mathbf{R}}=4 $$ $$ h=3R $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A body of mass 60 g experiences a gravitational force of 3.0 N, when placed at a particular point. The magnitude of the gravitational field intensity at that point is:
questions.explanation: Given: Mass of the body, m = 60 g = 0.06 kg Gravitational force, F = 3.0 N The gravitational field intensity is written as; $$ I_{g}=\frac{F}{m} $$ Now, On putting all the given values in equation (i) we have, $$ I_{g}=\frac{3.0}{0.06} $$ $$ \mathrm{I}_{g}=50\mathrm{N/k g} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A particle of mass M is situated at the centre of a spherical shell of same mass and radius a. The magnitude of the gravitational potential at a point situated at $ \frac{a}{2} $ distance from the centre, will be:
