##### Related Theory As we move from Earth's surface to deep mine, the acceleration due to gravity decreases and hence the time period of simple pendulum increases. $ \text{H}_{2}\text{O}_{2} $ $ \text{NaOH} + \text{H}_{2}\text{SO}_{4} \rightarrow \text{Na}_{2}\text{SO}_{4} + 2\text{H}_{2}\text{O} + \text{NaOH} + \text{H}_{2}\text{SO}_{4} + 2\text{NaOH} + \text{H}_{2}\text{O} $ $ \text{NaOH} + \text{H}_{2}\text{SO}_{4} \rightarrow \text{Na}_{2}\text{SO}_{4} + 2\text{H}_{2}\text{O} + 2\text{NaOH} + \text{H}_{2}\text{SO}_{4} + 2\text{NaOH} + \text{H}_{2}\text{O} $ $ \text{NaOH} + \text{H}_{2}\text{SO}_{4} \rightarrow \text{Na}_{2}\text{SO}_{4} + 2\text{H}_{2}\text{O} + 2\text{NaOH} + \text{H}_{2}\text{SO}_{4} + 2\text{NaOH} + \text{H}_{2}\text{O} $ $$ \begin{array}{c} \left[\begin{array}{cc} \frac{2}{3} \times \frac{2}{2} \\ 46.75 \end{array} \right] \mp \left[\begin{array}{cc} 2 & 10 \\ 2 & 200000000.76 \end{array} \right] \\ \left[\begin{array}{cc} 51.475 & -10 \\ 4 & -12.51^{4} & 4 \end{array} \right] \pm 5.1 \end{array} \right] \\ \succeq 0 \end{array} $$ \(\begin{array}{l}\pi r^2 + 2\pi(2R-r^2)\leq2r^2-2r^2-2r^2-2r^2- $ \textcircled{p} \frac{3}{4} = 1.3 \times 7 = 7.3 $ $$ \textcircled{2}A\quad\textcircled{2}A_{0} $$ $$ \textcircled{N}x=\frac{1}{2}x\operatorname{l o g}(1+\frac{1}{4}x^{2}) $$ $ \textcircled{p} \frac{3}{4} \times 620 \times 4y - 86.77 \div 2 = 1 \div 33 - 100 = 100 $ $$ \textcircled{6}\frac{1}{x}\quad\textcircled{6}\frac{1}{x-x_{0}} $$ $$ ↳\textcircled{s}t $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Which one of the following statements is true?
questions.optionA: Both light and sound waves can travel in vacuum.
questions.optionB: Both light and sound waves in air are transverse.
questions.optionC: The sound waves in air are longitudinal while the light waves are transverse.
questions.optionD: Both light and sound waves in air are longitudinal.
questions.explanation: The sound waves in air, are longitudinal while the light waves are transverse.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.subtopic: Displacement Relation in a Progressive Wave
questions.question: The displacement of a travelling wave is $ y = C \sin \frac{2\pi}{\lambda} $ (at - x) where t is time, x is distance and $ \lambda $ is the wavelength, all in S.I. units. Then the frequency of the wave is:
questions.explanation: Given, $y = C \sin \left( \frac{2\pi}{\lambda}(at - x) \right)$ we can write it as $y = C \sin \left( \frac{2\pi}{\lambda} at - \frac{2\pi x}{\lambda} \right)$ $\omega = \frac{2\pi a}{\lambda}$ The frequency $f$ is given by $f = \frac{\omega}{2\pi}$ $f = \frac{\frac{2\pi a}{\lambda}}{2\pi} = \frac{a}{\lambda}$ (A) According to the question, $ A = 1 \, m $ $ \therefore \quad y = A \sin(kx - \omega t) $ $ =\sin\left(\frac{2\pi}{2\pi}x-2\pi\times\frac{1}{\pi}t\right) $ $ =\sin(x-2t) $

questions.subject: Physics
questions.subtopic: Displacement Relation in a Progressive Wave
questions.question: A wave travelling in the positive x-direction having displacement along y-direction as 1 m, wavelength $ 2\pi m $ and frequency of $ \frac{1}{\pi} $ Hz is represented by:

questions.subject: Physics
questions.subtopic: Displacement Relation in a Progressive Wave
questions.question: Two waves are represented by the equations $ y_{1} = A \sin(\omega t + kx + 0.57) \text{ m and } y_{2} = A \cos(\omega t + kx) \text{ m} $, where, x in metre and t in second. The phase difference between them is:
questions.explanation: It is given that, $$ \begin{array}{l l l}{}&{y_{1}=\textup{A}\operatorname{s i n}\left(\omega t+k x+0.57\right)\textup{m}}&{\dots(\textup{i})}\\ {\textup{a n d}\quad}&{y_{2}=\textup{A}\operatorname{c o s}\left(\omega t+k x\right)\textup{m}}\\ {\Rightarrow\quad}&{y_{2}=\textup{A}\operatorname{s i n}\left(\frac{\pi}{2}+\omega t+k x\right)\textup{m}}&{\dots(\textup{i i})}\\ \end{array} $$ Thus from these two equations, $ \Delta\phi = \phi_{2} - \phi_{1} $ $ =\frac{\pi}{2}-0.57 $ $ =1.57-0.57=1\ rad $

questions.subject: Physics
questions.subtopic: Displacement Relation in a Progressive Wave
questions.question: A wave in a string has an amplitude of 2 cm. The wave travels in the +x direction with a speed of 128 ms^{-1} and it is noted that 5 complete wave fit in 4 m length of the string. The equation describing the wave is:
questions.explanation: According to the question, A = 2 cm and Direction = + x direction v = 128 ms^{-1} $ \therefore $ \quad $ k = \frac{2\pi}{\lambda} = \frac{2\pi \times 5}{4} = 7.85 \, \text{rad} \, \text{m}^{-1} $ And \quad $ \mathbf{v} = \frac{\omega}{k} = 128 \, \text{ms}^{-1} $ $ \therefore $ \quad $ \omega = \mathbf{v} \times k = 128 \times 7.85 = 1005 \, \text{rad} \, \text{s}^{-1} $ $ \therefore $ \quad $ y = A \sin (kx - \omega t) $ becomes \quad $ y = 2 \sin (7.85x - 1005 \, t) $ $ = (0.02) \sin (7.85x - 1005 \, t) $

questions.subject: Physics
questions.subtopic: Displacement Relation in a Progressive Wave
questions.question: The wave described by $y=0.25\sin(10\pi x-2\pi t)$, where $x$ and $y$ are in metre and $t$ is in second, is a wave travelling along the:
questions.optionC: +ve $x$ - direction with frequency 1 Hz and wavelength $\lambda=0.2\mathrm{~m}$
questions.optionD: +ve $x$ - direction with amplitude 0.25 m and wavelength $\lambda=0.2\mathrm{~m}$
questions.explanation: Given, $y = 0.25 \sin (10 \pi x - 2\pi t)$ Comparing with equation of wave, $y = A \sin (kx - \omega t)$ $A = 0.25, k = 10 \pi, \omega = 2\pi$ $\frac{2\pi}{\lambda} = 10\pi,$ $2\pi f = 2\pi$ $f = 1 \text{ Hz}$ $\therefore \quad \lambda = \frac{1}{5} = 0.2 \text{ m}$ When sign of coefficient of $t$ and $x$ are opposite, it means, $\frac{dx}{dt} = v > 0$ i.e., wave is propagating in the direction of increasing $x$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.subtopic: Displacement Relation in a Progressive Wave
questions.question: A wave travelling in the positive x-direction with amplitude A = 0.2 m, velocity v = 360 ms⁻¹ and wavelength λ = 60 m, then the correct expression for the wave is:
questions.explanation: The general equation of wave travelling in x-direction is given as, $$ y=A\sin\left[\frac{2\pi}{\lambda}(\mathrm{v}t-x)\right] $$ Here, A = 0.2 m, v = 360 ms^{-1}, $ \lambda = 60 $ m ∴ The equation becomes, $$ y=0.2\sin\left[\frac{2\pi}{60}(360t-x)\right] $$ $$ y=0.2\sin\left[2\pi\left(6t-\frac{x}{60}\right)\right] $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.subtopic: The Speed of a Travelling Wave
questions.question: If the initial tension on a stretched string is doubled then the ratio of the initial and final speeds of a transverse wave along the string is:
questions.explanation: We know that, velocity of a transverse wave in a stretched string is, $$ \mathbf{v}=\sqrt{\frac{\mathbf{T}}{\mu}} $$ Given: Tension, $ T' = 2T $ Now, speed of the wave in the string will be, $$ \mathbf{v}^{\prime}=\sqrt{\frac{2\mathrm{T}}{\mu}} $$ from eq. (i) and (ii), we get $$ \frac{\mathbf{v}}{\mathbf{v}^{\prime}}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.subtopic: The Speed of a Travelling Wave
questions.question: A uniform rope of length L and mass m_{1} hangs vertically from a rigid support. A block of mass m_{2} is attached to the free end of the rope. A transverse pulse wavelength \lambda_{1} is produced at the lower end of the rope. The wavelength of the pulse when it reaches the top of the rope is \lambda_{2}. The ratio \frac{\lambda_{2}}{\lambda_{1}} is:
questions.explanation: From the question: Wavelength of transverse pulse, $$ \lambda=\frac{\mathbf{v}}{n} $$ Also, we know that, $$ \mathbf{v}=\sqrt{\frac{\mathrm{T}}{\mu}} $$ v = velocity of the wave, n = frequency of wave T = tension in the string $ \mu $ = mass per unit length of rope From eq. (i) and (ii), we get, $$ \lambda=\frac{1}{n}\sqrt{\frac{T}{\mu}} $$ $$ \lambda\propto\sqrt{T} $$ ∴ for different cases, $$ \frac{\lambda_{2}}{\lambda_{1}}=\sqrt{\frac{T_{2}}{T_{1}}}=\sqrt{\frac{m_{1}+m_{2}}{m_{2}}} $$ $$ y=3\sin\frac{\pi}{2}(50t-x) $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.subtopic: The Speed of a Travelling Wave
questions.question: The equation of a simple harmonic wave is given by: $ y = 3 \sin \frac{\pi}{2}(50t - x) $, where x and y are in metres and t is in seconds. The ratio of maximum particle velocity to the wave velocity is:
questions.explanation: The given wave equation is $$ y=3\sin\left(25\pi t-\frac{\pi}{2}x\right) $$ The standard wave equation is $$ y=\mathbf{A}\sin\left(\omega t-kx\right) $$ Comparing (i) and (ii), we get $$ \omega=25\pi,k=\frac{\pi}{2} $$ Wave velocity $$ \begin{array}{l}\text{v}=\displaystyle\frac{\omega}{k}\\ \quad=\displaystyle\frac{25\pi}{\pi/2}=50\mathrm{~m s}^{-1}\end{array} $$ Particle velocity, $$ \begin{array}{l}\text{v}_{p}=\displaystyle\frac{d y}{d t}\\ \qquad=\displaystyle\frac{d}{d t}\bigg(3\sin\bigg(25\pi t-\frac{\pi}{2}x\bigg)\bigg)\\ \qquad=\displaystyle 75\pi\cos\bigg(25\pi t-\frac{\pi}{2}x\bigg)\end{array} $$ Maximum particle velocity, $ (v_{p})_{\text{max}} = 75\pi \, \text{ms}^{-1} $ $$ \frac{(\mathbf{v}_{p})_{\max}}{\mathbf{v}}=\frac{75\pi}{50}=\frac{3}{2}\pi $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.subtopic: The Speed of a Travelling Wave
questions.question: Sound waves travel at 350 m/s through a warm air and at 3500 m/s through brass. The wavelength of a 700 Hz acoustic wave as it enters brass from warm air:
questions.explanation: Given, Velocity of wave in warm air, $ v_{1}=350\ m/s $ Velocity of wave in brass, $ v_{2}=3500\ m/s $ We know that, Velocity of sound, v = n $ \lambda $ $$ \frac{\mathbf{v}_{1}}{\mathbf{v}_{2}}=\frac{n_{1}\lambda_{1}}{n_{2}\lambda_{2}} $$ Since $$ n_{1}=n_{2} $$ $$ \frac{\mathbf{v}_{1}}{\mathbf{v}_{2}}=\frac{\lambda_{1}}{\lambda_{2}} $$ $$ \begin{aligned}\lambda_{2}&=\lambda_{1}\frac{\mathbf{v}_{2}}{\mathbf{v}_{1}}\\&=\lambda_{1}\times\frac{3500}{350}\\&=\lambda_{1}\times10\\\lambda_{2}&=10\lambda_{1}\end{aligned} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.subtopic: The Speed of a Travelling Wave
questions.question: A transverse wave is represented by y = A \sin(\omega t - kx). For what value of the wavelength is the wave velocity equal to the maximum particle velocity?
questions.explanation: Wave velocity, $ v = \frac{\omega}{K} = \omega A $ $ \Rightarrow $ $ \frac{\lambda}{2\pi} = A $ $ \Rightarrow $ $ \lambda = 2\pi A $

questions.subject: Physics
questions.subtopic: The Speed of a Travelling Wave
questions.question: Two points are located at a distance 10 m and 15 m from the source of oscillation. The period of oscillation is 0.05 s and the velocity of the wave is 300 m/s. What is the phase difference between the oscillation of two points?
questions.explanation: We know that, Phase difference, $ \phi = \frac{2\pi}{\lambda} \times \text{path difference} $ Here, path difference, $$ \Delta x=15-10=5\mathrm{~m} $$ Now frequency. $$ \mathrm{v}=\frac{1}{\mathrm{T}}=\frac{1}{0.05}=20\mathrm{~Hz} $$ velocity, v =300 m/s $$ \bullet \quad \bullet\bullet $$ $$ \lambda=\frac{v}{v}=\frac{300}{20}=15m $$ ∴ Phase difference. $$ \begin{aligned}\Delta\phi&=\frac{2\pi}{\lambda}\times\Delta x\\&=\frac{2\pi}{15}\times5=\frac{2\pi}{3}\end{aligned} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.subtopic: The Speed of a Travelling Wave
questions.question: A transverse wave propagating along x-axis is represented by y(x,t)=8.0\sin(0.5\pi x-4\pi t-\frac{\pi}{4}), where, x is in metres and t is in seconds. The speed of the wave is:
questions.explanation: It is given that, $$ y(x,t)=8.0\sin\left[0.5\pi x-4\pi t-\frac{\pi}{4}\right] $$ Compare this equation with standard equation. $$ y=A\sin\left(\frac{2\pi x}{\lambda}-\frac{2\pi t}{T}+\phi\right) $$ we have. $$ \begin{aligned}\frac{2\pi}{\lambda}&=0.5\pi\\\lambda&=\frac{2\pi}{0.5\pi}=4m\\\frac{2\pi}{T}&=4\pi\\ T&=\frac{1}{2}s\\ v=\frac{\lambda}{T}&=\frac{4}{1/2}=8m/s\end{aligned} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.subtopic: The Speed of a Travelling Wave
questions.question: A point source emits sound equally in all directions in a non-absorbing medium. Two points P and Q are at distances of 2 m and 3 m respectively from the source. The ratio of the intensities of the wave at P and Q is:
questions.explanation: Given: $$ \mathrm{Intensity}\propto\frac{1}{(\mathrm{distance})^{2}} $$ $$ I_{1}\propto\frac{1}{2^{2}} $$ and $$ I_{2}\propto\frac{1}{3^{2}} $$ $$ \frac{I_{1}}{I_{2}}=\frac{9}{4}=9:4 $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.subtopic: The Speed of a Travelling Wave
questions.question: The equation of a wave is given by $y = A \sin \left(100t - \frac{x}{10}\right)$, where $x$ and $y$ are in metre and $t$ is in second, then velocity of wave is:
questions.explanation: We have, $$ y=A\sin\left(100t-\frac{x}{10}\right) $$ On comparing with standard wave equation, We get $$ \omega=100,k=\frac{1}{10} $$ Velocity of the wave, $$ \begin{aligned}\mathbf{v}&=\frac{\omega}{k}=\frac{100}{1/10}\\&=100\times10=1000\mathrm{~m/s}\end{aligned} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.subtopic: The Principle of Superposition of Waves
questions.question: Two waves of same frequency and intensity superimpose on each other in opposite phases. After the superposition, the intensity and frequency of waves will:
questions.explanation: Sound and light are both altered by interference. In sound when the resultant intensity is maximum (in phase), the interference is said to be constructive and when the resultant intensity is minimum or zero, it is said to be destructive (in opposite phase).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: The ratio of frequencies of fundamental harmonic produced by an open pipe to that of closed pipe having the same length is:
questions.explanation: Fundamental frequency for open organ pipe, $$ f_{o}=\frac{\mathbf{v}}{2\mathbf{L}_{o}} $$ Fundamental frequency for closed organ pipe, $$ \begin{aligned}&f_{c}=\frac{\mathbf{v}}{4\mathrm{L}_{c}}\\ &\frac{f_{o}}{f_{c}}=\frac{\mathbf{v}}{2\mathrm{L}_{o}}\times\frac{4\mathrm{L}_{c}}{\mathbf{v}}\\ &\begin{aligned}\\ &\frac{f_{o}}{f_{c}}=\frac{2}{1}&&\left(\because\mathrm{L}_{o}=\mathrm{L}_{c}\right)\\ &\end{aligned}\\ \end{aligned} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: The length of the string of a musical instrument is 90 cm and has a fundamental frequency of 120 Hz. Where should it be pressed to produce fundamental frequency of 180 Hz?
questions.explanation: Length of string of musical instrument $$ \begin{aligned}B)&Length~of~string~of~musical~instrument\\&l=90\mathrm{~cm}=0.9\mathrm{~m}\\&fundamental~frequency,\\&f_{1}=120\mathrm{~Hz},f_{2}=180\mathrm{~Hz}.\\&We~know~that:\\&\quad f\propto\frac{1}{l}\\&\Rightarrow\quad\frac{f_{1}}{f_{2}}=\frac{l_{2}}{l_{1}}\\&\Rightarrow\quad l_{2}=\frac{f_{1}}{f_{2}}\times l_{1}\\&\quad l_{2}=\frac{120}{180}\times0.9\\&\quad l_{2}=\frac{2}{3}\times0.9=0.6\mathrm{~m}\\&\quad l_{r}=60\mathrm{~cm}.\end{aligned} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: The fundamental frequency in an open organ pipe is equal to the third harmonic of a closed organ pipe. If the length of the closed organ pipe is 20 cm, the length of the open organ pipe is:
questions.explanation: For closed organ pipe, Third harmonics, $$ f=\frac{3\mathbf{v}}{4I} $$ where, l is the length of closed organ pipe. For open organ pipe, fundamental frequency, $$ f=\frac{\mathbf{v}}{2l}, $$ where, $ l = \text{length of the open organ pipe} $. Equating equation (i) and (ii) $$ \begin{aligned}\frac{3\mathrm{v}}{4l}&=\frac{\mathrm{v}}{2l^{\prime}}\\l^{\prime}&=\frac{4l}{3\times2}=\frac{2l}{3}\\\bar{l}&=\frac{2}{3}\times20=\frac{40}{3}\\&\quad[\because\text{Since},l=20\text{cm given}]\\&=13.33\text{cm}\end{aligned} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A tuning fork is used to produce resonance in a glass tube. The length of the air column in this tube can be adjusted by a variable piston. At room temperature of $ 27^{\circ} $C, two successive resonances are produced at 20 cm and 73 cm of column length. If the frequency of the tuning fork is 320 Hz, the velocity of sound in air at $ 27^{\circ} $C is:
questions.explanation: For first resonance, $ l_{1} = \frac{\lambda}{4} $ and second resonance, $ l_{2} = \frac{3\lambda}{4} $ $ \Rightarrow $ $ l_{2} - l_{1} = \frac{3\lambda}{4} - \frac{\lambda}{4} = \frac{\lambda}{2} $ $ \lambda = 2(l_{2} - l_{1}) $ Velocity of sound wave, $$ \begin{aligned}&\mathbf{v}=\mathbf{v}\lambda\qquad\quad(Where,v=frequency)\\&\mathbf{v}=\mathbf{v}\left(2\right)\left(l_{2}-l_{1}\right)\\&\mathbf{v}=2[320(0.73-0.20)]\\&\mathbf{v}=2\times320\times0.53=339.2ms^{-1}\\&\mathbf{v}\approx339ms^{-1}\\ \end{aligned} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: The second overtone of an open organ pipe has the same frequency as the first overtone of a closed pipe L metre long. The length of the open pipe will be:
questions.explanation: For second overtone $(3^{rd}$ harmonics) in open organ pipe, $3\lambda$ $$ \frac{3\lambda}{2}=l_{o} $$ $$ \lambda=\frac{2l_{p}}{3} $$ for first overtone $(2^{nd}$ harmonics)$ in closed organ pipe, $$ \begin{aligned}&\frac{3\lambda}{4}=l_{c}\\&\Rightarrow\quad\lambda=\frac{4l_{c}}{3}=\frac{4L}{3}\\&So,\quad\frac{2l_{o}}{3}=\frac{4L}{3}\\&\Rightarrow\quad l_{0}=2L\end{aligned} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: An air column, closed at one end and open at the other, resonates with a tuning fork when the smallest length of the column at 50 cm. The next larger length of the column resonating with the same tuning fork is:
questions.explanation: The situation is shown in the given diagram, $$ \begin{array}{l}\displaystyle\bigcup_{\mathrm{L}_{\min}}=\frac{\lambda}{4}\\ \vdots\\ \displaystyle\mathrm{L}_{\min}=\frac{\lambda}{4}\\ \Rightarrow\quad50=\frac{\lambda}{4}\\ \Rightarrow\quad\lambda=200\mathrm{~cm}\end{array} $$ Next higher length of an column, $$ \begin{aligned}L&=\frac{\lambda}{4}+\frac{\lambda}{2}=\frac{3\lambda}{4}\\&=\frac{3}{4}\times200=150cm\end{aligned} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A string is stretched between fixed points separated by 75.0 cm. It is observed to have resonant frequencies of 420 Hz and 315 Hz. There are no other resonant frequencies between these two. The lowest resonant frequency for this string is:
questions.explanation: Two consecutive resonant frequencies for a string fixed at both ends will be, $$ \begin{array}{c}\frac{n\mathbf{v}}{2l}\ and\ \frac{(n+1)}{2l}\mathbf{v}\\\Rightarrow\frac{(n+1)\mathbf{v}}{2l}-\frac{n\mathbf{v}}{2l}\ =420-315\\\Rightarrow\quad\frac{\mathbf{v}}{2l}\ =105\ Hz\end{array} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: The fundamental frequency of a closed organ pipe of length 20 cm is equal to the second overtone of an organ pipe open at both the ends. The length of organ pipe open at both the ends is:
questions.explanation: For closed organ pipe, frequency of vibration $f_{c}$ is given, $$ f_{c}=\frac{\mathbf{v}}{4l} $$ l = length of closed organ pipe and for open organ pipe frequency of vibration is $ f_{0} $ $$ f_{0}=\frac{\mathbf{v}}{2l^{\prime}} $$ $ l' = \text{length of open organ pipe} $ According to question, $$ f_{c}=3f_{o} $$ $$ \mathrm{\frac{v}{4l}}=\mathrm{\frac{3v}{2l^{\prime}}} $$ ↓ $$ l^{\prime}=6l $$ $$ l^{\prime}=6\times20=120cm $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: If n_{1}, n_{2} and n_{3} are the fundamental frequencies of three segments into which a string is divided, then the original fundamental frequency n of the string is given by:
questions.explanation:
String is divided into three points,
$ l_{1} $ has frequency $ n_{1} $ $ l_{2} $ has frequency $ n_{2} $ and $l_{3}$ has frequency $n_{3}$ Using, $$ \mathbf{v}=f\boldsymbol{\lambda} $$ $$ \lambda=\frac{\mathbf{v}}{f} $$ $$ l_{1}=\frac{\mathbf{v}}{n_{1}},l_{2}=\frac{\mathbf{v}}{n_{2}},l_{3}=\frac{\mathbf{v}}{n_{3}} $$ $$ l=l_{1}+l_{2}+l_{3} $$ $$ \frac{\mathbf{v}}{n}=\frac{\mathbf{v}}{n_{1}}+\frac{\mathbf{v}}{n_{2}}+\frac{\mathbf{v}}{n_{3}} $$ where, v is same in all case, $$ \frac{1}{n}=\frac{1}{n_{1}}+\frac{1}{n_{2}}+\frac{1}{n_{3}} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: The number of possible natural oscillations of air column in a pipe closed at one end of length 85 cm, whose frequencies lie below 1250 Hz are: (velocity of sound = 340 ms^{-1})
questions.explanation:
For pipe closed at one end,
$$ \begin{aligned}f_{n}&=(2n-1)\left(\frac{\mathbf{v}}{4l}\right)\\&=(2n-1)\times\frac{340}{4\times85\times10^{-2}}\end{aligned} $$ $$ =(2n-1)100 $$ $$ f_{1}=100\mathrm{~Hz},f_{2}=300\mathrm{~Hz} $$ $$ f_{3}=500Hz,f_{4}=700Hz $$ $$ f_{5}=900\mathrm{~Hz},f_{6}=1100\mathrm{~Hz} $$ This confirms that number of possible natural oscillations could be 6.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: If we study the vibration of a pipe open at both ends, then which of the following statement is NOT true?
questions.optionB: Odd harmonics of the fundamental frequency will be generated.
questions.optionC: All harmonics of the fundamental frequency will be generated.
questions.optionD: Pressure change will be maximum at both ends.
questions.explanation: In the study of the vibration of a pipe, open at both the ends, the displacement node is pressure antinode. So, the pressure change will be maximum at the length $ \frac{l}{2} $ and the pressure variation will be minimum at both the ends. The odd and even harmonics will be present in the vibration.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Standing waves are produced in a 10 m long stretched string. If the string vibrates in 5 segments and the wave velocity is 20 m/s, the frequency is:
questions.explanation: For standing wave, the length of one segment will be $\frac{\lambda}{2}$. Since, there are 5 segments and total length of the string is 10 m. $\therefore\quad5\times\frac{\lambda}{2}=10$ $\Rightarrow\lambda=4\ m$ Thus, frequency Thus, frequency, $$ f=\frac{v}{\lambda}=\frac{20}{4}=5Hz\quad(\because v=20m/s) $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: In a guitar, two strings A and B made of same material are slightly out of tune and produce beats of frequency 6 Hz. When tension in B is slightly decreased, the beat frequency increases to 7 Hz. if the frequency of A is 530 Hz, the original frequency of B will be:
questions.explanation: In case of beats formation, Unknown frequency, $$ \mathbf{v}_{B}=\mathbf{v}_{A}\pm\mathrm{b e a t s} $$ Where $$ v_{A}=530Hz,beats=6Hz $$ $$ \begin{array}{l}\bullet\\ \bullet\end{array} $$ $$ \mathrm{v_{B}=530\pm6=536,o r524H z} $$ From question, when tension of B is slightly decreased, then beats frequency increases to 7 Hz. Thus, the original frequency B is 524 Hz.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Three sound waves of equal amplitudes have frequencies $(n-1), n, (n+1)$. They superimpose to give beats. The number of beats produced per second will be:
questions.explanation: Now divide 1 second into 1, 1, 2, equal divisions $$ \frac{1}{1},\frac{1}{1},\frac{2}{1} $$ By eliminating common time instants, total maxima in one second is 2.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A source of unknown frequency gives 4 beats/s when sounded with a source of known frequency 250 Hz. The second harmonic of the source of unknown frequency gives 5 beats per second when sounded with source of frequency 513 Hz. Unknown frequency is:
questions.explanation: Frequency of unknown source = 246 Hz or 254 Hz second harmonic of this source = 492 Hz or 508 Hz when gives 5 beats per second when sounded with a source frequency 513 Hz. Therefore, unknown frequency = 254 Hz.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Two identical piano wires kept under the same tension T have a fundamental frequency of 600 Hz. The fractional increase in the tension of one of the wires which will lead to occurrence of 6 beat/s when both the wires oscillate together would be:
questions.explanation: According to the question, Frequency of the string varies directly as the square root of its tension. $$ \begin{aligned}&\Rightarrow\quad f\propto\sqrt{\mathrm{T}}\\ &\quad\quad\quad\quad\frac{\Delta f}{f}=\frac{1}{2},\frac{\Delta\mathrm{T}}{\mathrm{T}}\\ &\Rightarrow\quad\quad\quad\quad\frac{\Delta\mathrm{T}}{\mathrm{T}}=2,\frac{\Delta f}{f}\\ &、\quad\quad\quad\quad=2\times\frac{6}{600}=0.02\\ \end{aligned} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A tuning fork of frequency 512 Hz makes 4 beats/s with the vibrating string of a beats. The beat frequency decreases to 2 beats when the tension in the piano string is slightly increased. The frequency of the piano string before increasing the tension was:
questions.explanation: We have, $$ n_{p}\propto\sqrt{\mathrm{T}} $$ $$ n_{f}=frequency of tuning~fork=512Hz $$ $$ x=Beat~frequency=4~beats/s $$ Hence, $ n_{p} - n_{f} = x \downarrow $ (wrong) $$ n_{f}-n_{p}=x\downarrow(correct) $$ $$ n_{p}=n_{f}-x=512-4=508\mathrm{~Hz} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Each of the two strings of length 51.6 cm and 49.1 cm are tensioned separately by 20 N force. Mass per unit length of both the strings is same and equal to 1 g/m. When both the strings vibrate simultaneously the number of beats is:
questions.explanation: According to the question, Frequency of first string, $$ \begin{aligned}f_{1}&=\frac{1}{2l}\sqrt{\frac{T}{m}}\\&=\frac{1}{2\times51.6\times10^{-2}}\sqrt{\frac{20}{10^{-3}}}\\&=137.03Hz\end{aligned} $$ Frequency of second string, $$ \begin{aligned}f_{2}&=\frac{1}{2\times49.1\times10^{-2}}\sqrt{\frac{20}{10^{-3}}}\\&=144.01\mathrm{~Hz}\end{aligned} $$ ∴ Number of beats, $$ f_{2}-f_{1}=144-137=7\mathrm{b e a t s} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Two sound waves with wavelengths 5.0m and 5.5 m respectively, each propagate in a gas with velocity 330 m/s. We expect the following number of beats per second:
questions.explanation: $$ Frequency=\frac{Velocity}{Wavelength} $$ $$ \mathrm{v}_{1}=\frac{\mathrm{v}}{\lambda_{1}}=\frac{330}{5}=66\mathrm{~Hz} $$ and $$ \mathrm{v}_{2}=\frac{\mathrm{v}}{\lambda_{2}}=\frac{330}{5\cdot5}=60\mathrm{~Hz} $$ ∴ Number of beats per second $$ \begin{aligned}&={\bf v}_{1}-{\bf v}_{2}\\&=66-60=6.\end{aligned} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Two vibrating tuning forks produce progressive waves given by $ y_{1} = 4 \sin 500\pi t $ and $ y_{2} = 2 \sin 506\pi t $. Number of beats produced per minute is:
questions.explanation: $$ y_{1}=4\sin500\pi t $$ Here, $$ y_{2}=2\sin506\pi t $$ $$ \omega_{1}=500\pi,\omega_{2}=506\pi $$ $$ \mathrm{v}_{1}=\frac{\omega_{1}}{2\pi}=\frac{500\pi}{2\pi}=250\mathrm{Hz} $$ $$ \mathrm{v}_{2}=\frac{\omega_{2}}{2\pi}=\frac{506\pi}{2\pi}=253\mathrm{~Hz} $$ $$ \begin{array}{l}v=v_{2}-v_{1}=253-250\\=3\mathrm{beats/s}=180\mathrm{beats/min}\end{array} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Two strings A and B have lengths $ l_{A} $ and $ l_{B} $ and carry masses $ M_{A} $ and $ M_{B} $ at their lower ends, the upper ends being supported by rigid supports, If, $ n_{A} $ and $ n_{B} $ are the frequencies of their vibrations and $ n_{A}=2n_{B} $, then:
questions.explanation: We have, the frequency of vibrations of string, $$ n=\frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{g}{l}} $$ $$ \begin{aligned}&Given,&n_{A}=2n_{B}\\&\therefore&\quad\frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{g}{l_{A}}}=2.\frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{g}{l_{B}}}\\&\Rightarrow&\quad\frac{1}{l_{A}}=\frac{4}{l_{B}}\\&\Rightarrow&l_{B}=4l_{A}\\&It~is~clear~from~eq.(i),~the~frequ\\&string~does~not~decoord.\end{aligned} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A wave has SHM (simple harmonic motion), whose period is 4s while another wave which also possesses SHM has its period 3s. If both are combined, then the resultant wave will have the period equal to:
questions.explanation: Beats are produced when both waves are combined. Frequency of beats will be, $$ \mathbf{v}_{1}-\mathbf{v}_{2}=\frac{1}{\mathrm{T}_{1}}-\frac{1}{\mathrm{T}_{2}}=\frac{1}{3}-\frac{1}{4}=\frac{1}{12} $$ Hence, time period = 12 s

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Two point charges A and B having charges + Q and -Q respectively, are placed at certain distance apart and force acting between them is F. If 25% charge of A is transferred to B, then force between the charges becomes:

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Suppose the charge of a proton and an electron differ slightly. One of them is $e$ and the other is $(e + \Delta e)$. If the net electrostatic force and gravitational force between two hydrogen atoms placed at a distance $d$ (much greater than atomic size) apart is zero, then $\Delta e$ is of the order of [Given: mass of hydrogen, $m_{h} = 1.67 \times 10^{-27}$ kg]:
questions.explanation: Here, gravitational attractive force between two H-atoms is: $$ \mathrm{F_{G}}=\frac{\mathrm{G}m^{2}}{d^{2}} $$ and electrostatic repulsive force between two H-atom is, $$ \mathrm{F}_{e}=\frac{\mathrm{K}q^{2}}{d^{2}}=\frac{\mathrm{K}(\Delta e)^{2}}{d^{2}} $$ $\because$ Net charge on H-atom $=(e+\Delta e)-e=\Delta e$ Then we have, $$ \frac{\mathrm{K}(\Delta e)^{2}}{d^{2}}=\frac{\mathrm{G}m^{2}}{d^{2}} $$ $$ \begin{array}{r} \left[\begin{array}{c} 9\times10^{9}\left(\Delta e\right)^{2}=6.67\times10^{-11}\times1.67\times10^{-27}\times1.67 \\ \times10^{-27} \end{array} \right] \\ \times \left[\begin{array}{cc} 3 & 16 \end{array} \right] \\ \times \left[\begin{array}{cc} 15 & 16 \end{array} \right] \\ \succeq 0 \end{array} $$ $$ (\Delta e)^{2}=\frac{6.67\times1.67\times1.67}{9}\times10^{-74} $$ $$ \bullet \quad \bullet\bullet $$ $$ \Delta e=1.437\times10^{-37}C $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Two identical charged spheres suspended from a common point by two massless strings of lengths $l$ are initially at a distance $d$ ($d < l$) apart because of their mutual repulsion. The charges begin to leak from both the spheres at a constant rate. As a result, the spheres approach each other with a velocity $v$. Then, $v$ varies as a function of the distance $x$ between the spheres, as:
questions.explanation:
From the diagram, $$ \tan\theta=\frac{F_{e}}{mg}=\theta $$ ### 1.7. Electric Field 10. An electron falls from rest through a vertical distance $h$ in a uniform and vertically upward directed electric field E. The direction of electric field is now reversed, keeping its magnitude the same. A proton is allowed to fall from rest in it through the same vertical distance $h$. The time of fall of the electron, in comparison to the time of fall of the proton is: (A) 10 times greater (B) 5 times greater (C) smaller (D) equal [NEET 2018] 11. The electric field in a certain region is acting radially outward and is given by, E = Ar. A charge contained in a sphere of radius 'a' centered at the origin of the field will be given by: (A) $ 4\pi\varepsilon_{0}Aa^{2} $ (B) $ A\varepsilon_{0}a^{2} $ (C) $ 4\pi\varepsilon_{0}Aa^{3} $ (D) $ \varepsilon_{0}Aa^{3} $ 12. The electric field at a distance $ \frac{3R}{2} $ from the centre of a charged conducting spherical shell of radius R is E. The electric field at a distance $ \frac{R}{2} $ from the centre of the sphere is: (A) zero (B) E (C) $ \frac{E}{2} $ (D) $ \frac{E}{2} $ [AIPMT Mains 2010] 13. A particle of mass $m$ and charge $q$ is placed at rest in a uniform electric field E and then released. The kinetic energy attained by the particle after moving a distance $y$ is: (A) $qEy^{2}$ (B) $qE^{2}y$ (C) $qEy$ (D) $q^{2}Ey$ [AIPMT 1998] ### 1.9. Electric Flux 14. A square surface of side L metre in the plane of the paper is placed in a uniform electric field E (volt/m) acting along the same plane at an angle $\theta$ with the horizontal side of the square as shown in figure. The electric flux linked to the surface in unit of V·m, is:
(A) EL^{2} (C) $ EL^{2}\sin\theta $ (B) $ EL^{2}\cos\theta $ (D) 0 [AIPMT Screening 2010] 15. A square surface of side L metres is in the plane of the paper. A uniform electric field E (volt/m), also in the plane of the paper, is limited only to the lower half of the square surface (see figure). The electric flux in SI units associated with the surface is:
(B) $ \frac{EL^{2}}{2\varepsilon_{0}} $ $$ EL^{2} $$ (C) $ \frac{EL^{2}}{2} $ (D) zero [AIPMT 2006] ### 1.10. Electric Dipole 16. Two point charges $ -q $ and $ +q $ are placed at a distance of $ L $, as shown in the figure. The magnitude of electric field intensity at a distance $ R $ ( $ R \gg L $) varies as: (A) $ \frac{1}{R^{2}} $ (B) $ \frac{1}{R^{3}} $ (C) $ \frac{1}{R^{4}} $ (D) $ \frac{1}{R^{6}} $ [NEET 2022] 17. Polar molecules are the molecules: (A) having zero dipole moment. (B) acquire a dipole moment only in the presence of electric field due to displacement of charges. (C) acquire a dipole moment only when magnetic field is absent. (D) having a permanent field (D) having a permanent electric dipole moment. [NEET 2021] 18. The electric field at a point on the equatorial plane at a distance r from the centre of a dipole having dipole moment P is given by $(r >> \text{separation of two charges forming the dipole}, \varepsilon_{0} = \text{permittivity of free space})$: (A) $\frac{-\mathrm{P}}{4\pi\varepsilon_{0}r^{3}}$ (B) $\frac{2\mathrm{P}}{4\pi\varepsilon_{0}r^{3}}$ (C) $-\frac{\mathrm{P}}{4\pi\varepsilon_{0}r^{2}}$ (D) $\frac{\mathrm{P}}{4\pi\varepsilon_{0}r^{2}}$ [NEET 2020] 19. Three point charges +q, -2q and +q are placed at points $ (x = 0, y = a, z = 0) $, $ (x = 0, y = 0, z = 0) $ and $ (x = a, y = 0, z = 0) $ respectively. The magnitude and direction of the electric dipole moment vector of this charge assembly are: (A) $ \sqrt{2}qa $ along the line joining points $ (x=0,y=0,z=0) $ and $ (x=a,y=a,z=0) $ (B) qa along the line joining points $ (x=0,y=0,z=0) $ and $ (x=a,y=a,z=0) $ (C) $ \sqrt{2}qa $ along +x-direction (D) $ \sqrt{2}qa $ along +y-direction ∴ $$ \frac{Kq^{2}}{x^{2}mg}=\frac{x}{2l} $$ or $$ x^{3}\propto q^{2} $$ or $$ x^{3/2}\propto q $$ Differentiate eq. (i) w.r.t. to time, we get $$ 3x^{2}\frac{dx}{dt}\propto2q\frac{dq}{dt} $$ but $ \frac{dq}{dt} $ is constant. So. $$ x^{2}(v)\propto q $$ Now, replace q from eq. (ii), we get $$ x^{2}\left(\mathrm{v}\right)\propto x^{3/2} $$ $$ \Downarrow: $$ $$ \textbf{v}\propto x^{\frac{-1}{2}} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Two pith balls carrying equal charges are suspended from a common point by strings of equal length, the equilibrium separation between them is r. Now, the strings are rigidly clamped at half the height. The equilibrium separation between the balls now becomes, The topic number and names correspond to the exact topics of the NCERT Class XII textbook, so it's easy for you to find in NCERT.
questions.explanation: $$ \begin{aligned}&Here,\quad&\tan\theta&=\frac{\mathrm{F}}{mg}\\&\Rightarrow&\frac{\frac{r}{2}}{y}&=\frac{kq^{2}}{r^{2}mg}\\&\Rightarrow&y&\propto r^{3}\\&Therefore,\left(\frac{r^{\prime}}{r}\right)^{3}&=\frac{y/2}{y}\\&\Rightarrow&r^{\prime}&=r\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{1/3}\end{aligned} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A charge 'q' is placed at the centre of the line joining two equal charges 'Q'. The system of three charges will be in equilibrium, if 'q' is equal to:
questions.explanation: According to the question, $$ \begin{array}{l}\mathrm{Q}\xrightarrow[\mathrm{A}]\quad\frac{q}{r}\\\longleftarrow\quad\mathrm{C}\\\longrightarrow r\end{array}\begin{array}{l}\mathrm{Q}\\\mathrm{B}\end{array} $$ for equilibrium, net force on charge Q=0, $$ \therefore\frac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_{0}}\frac{Qq}{\left(\frac{r}{2}\right)^{2}}+\frac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_{0}}\cdot\frac{Q^{2}}{r^{2}}=0 $$ $$ \frac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_{0}}\frac{Q^{2}}{r^{2}}=-\frac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_{0}}\frac{4Qq}{r^{2}} $$ $$ \mathbf{Q}=-4\mathbf{q} $$ $$ q=-\frac{Q}{4} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Two positive ions, each carrying a charge q, are separated by a distance d. If F is the force of repulsion between the ions, the number of electrons missing from each ion will be (e be the charge of an electron):
questions.explanation: The force of repulsion between them is, $$ \mathrm{F}=k\frac{q q}{d^{2}}=\frac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_{0}}\frac{q q}{d^{2}} $$ Here $$ q=ne $$ $$ \mathrm{F}=\frac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_{0}}\frac{(n e)(n e)}{d^{2}} $$ $$ n=\sqrt{\frac{4\pi\varepsilon_{0}F d^{2}}{e^{2}}} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: The unit of permittivity of free space $ \varepsilon_{0} $ is:
questions.explanation: We know that, $ F = \frac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_{0}}\frac{q^{2}}{r^{2}} $ $$ \Rightarrow $$ $$ \varepsilon_{0}=\frac{1}{4\pi}\frac{q^{2}}{Fr^{2}}=\frac{C^{2}}{N-m^{2}} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: An electron is moving around the nucleus of a hydrogen atom in a circular orbit of radius r. The coulomb force $ \rightarrow $ @THE_RDX_07 F between the two is $ \left( \text{where}, \mathrm{K} = \frac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_{0}} \right) $:
questions.explanation: The charge on hydrogen nucleus, $q_{1}=+e$ ∴ Coulomb's force, $$ \begin{array}{l}\text{F}=\text{K}\frac{\text{QQ}}{r^{2}}\hat{r}\\ =\text{K}\frac{(+e)(-e)}{r^{2}}\hat{r}\\ \quad=-\frac{\text{K}e^{2}}{r^{2}}\hat{r}\end{array} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: When air is replaced by a dielectric medium of constant K, the maximum force of attraction between two charges, separated by a distance:
questions.explanation: According to Coulomb's law, force between two charges is directly proportional to the product of charges and inversely proportional to the square of distance between them. $$ \mathrm{F}=\frac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_{0}}\frac{q_{1}q_{2}}{r^{2}} $$ where, $ \frac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_{0}} = \text{proportionality constant} $. If a dielectric medium of constant K is placed between them, then new force between the charges will be, $$ \mathrm{F}^{\prime}=\frac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_{0}\mathrm{K}}\frac{q_{1}q_{2}}{r^{2}} $$ Dividing eq. (ii) by (i), we get, $$ \frac{\mathbf{F}^{\prime}}{\mathbf{F}}=\frac{1}{\mathbf{K}} $$ Or $$ \mathrm{F}^{\prime}=\frac{\mathrm{F}}{\mathrm{K}} $$ Thus, new force decreases K times.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: The formation of dipole is due to two equal and dissimilar point charges placed at a:
questions.explanation: A pair of equal and opposite point charges separated by a small distance form an electric dipole. Electric dipoles are atoms or molecules of ammonia, water, alcohol, carbon dioxide, HCl and other compounds in which the centres of positive and negative charge distributions are separated by a small distance. 21. C) On the axial line of an electric dipole, the field intensity is given by, $$ \mathrm{E}=\frac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_{0}}\cdot\frac{2p}{r^{3}} $$ Electric field at an equatorial position, $$ \mathrm{E}=\frac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_{0}}\cdot\frac{p}{r^{3}} $$ where, $p$ is electric dipole moment. From eq. (i) and (ii), we get, $$ E\propto\frac{1}{r^{3}} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Intensity of an electric field (E) depends on distance r due to a dipole, is related as:

questions.subject: Physics
questions.subtopic: Dipole in a Uniform External Field
questions.question: An electric dipole is placed at an angle of $30^{\circ}$ with an electric field of intensity $2 \times 10^{5} \mathrm{NC}^{-1}$. It experiences a torque equal to 4 N·m. Calculate the magnitude of charge on the dipole, if the dipole length is 2 cm.
questions.explanation:
Given: Angle, $ \theta = 30^{\circ} $ Electric Field intensity, $$ E=2\times10^{5}NC^{-1} $$ Dipole length, = 2 cm = 0.02 m $ \tau = pE \sin \theta $ $ = (ql) E \sin \theta $ $ \therefore $ $$ \begin{array}{l}q=\displaystyle \frac{\tau}{\text{El}\sin\theta} \\ \qquad = \frac{4}{2 \times 10^{5} \times 0.02 \times \frac{1}{2}} \\ \qquad = 2 \text{ mC} \end{array} $$
##### Related Theory When a dipole is placed in a uniform external electric field, then net force $ (+qE\text{ and }-qE) $ on it, is zero, but it experiences a torque.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A metal cube of side 5 cm is charged with 6 μC. The surface charge density on the cube is:
questions.explanation: Total charge on cube $q = 6\ \mu\mathrm{C}$ Side of cube, $a = 0.05\ m$ As cube has 6 square forces, Surface area = $6a^{2}$ Surface charge density $=\frac{\text{Total Charge}}{\text{Surface area}}$ $=\frac{q}{6a^{2}}$ $=\frac{6\times10^{-6}\mathrm{C}}{6\times(0.05)^{2}}$ $=0.4\times10^{-3}\mathrm{C}/\mathrm{m}^{2}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A spherical conductor of radius 10 cm has a charge of $ 3.2 \times 10^{-7} $ C distributed uniformly. What is the magnitude of electric field at a point 15 cm from the centre of the sphere? $ \left(\frac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_{0}} = 9 \times 10^{9} \text{Nm}^{2}/\text{C}^{2}\right) $
questions.explanation: Given: $ r = 10 \, \text{cm} = 10 \times 10^{-2} \, \text{m} $ Charge, $ q = 3.2 \times 10^{-7} \, C $ ∴ Electric field at a point (x = 15 cm) from centre of sphere is: $$ \begin{aligned}E&=\frac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_{0}}\frac{q}{x^{2}}\\&=9\times10^{9}\times\frac{3.2\times10^{-7}}{225\times10^{-4}}\\&=1.28\times10^{5}N/C\end{aligned} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A thin conducting ring of radius R is given a charge + Q. The electric field at the centre O of the ring due to the charge on the part AKB of the ring is E. The electric field at the centre due to the charge on the part ACDB of the ring is:
questions.explanation: Electric field due to the given charged ring is zero at centre O. So, electric field due to AKB is equal and opposite to electric field due to ACBD, from the principle of superposition. Since, E is electric field strength, due to AKB of O along $ \overrightarrow{KO} $. So, electric field strength due to ACDB along $ \overrightarrow{OK} $ and it is equal to $ \overrightarrow{E} $.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: The electric field strength in air at NTP is $ 3 \times 10^{6} $ V/m. The maximum charge that can be given to a spherical conductor of radius 3 m is:
questions.explanation: Given, $ E_{\text{max}} = 3 \times 10^6 \, \text{V/m} $ and $ R = 3 \, \text{m} $ We have, $$ \mathrm{E}=\frac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_{0}}\times\frac{\mathrm{Q}}{\mathrm{R}^{2}} $$ $$ \begin{array}{c c}{\Rightarrow}&{\mathrm{Q_{m a x}}=4\pi\varepsilon_{0}\mathrm{R}^{2}\mathrm{E_{m a x}}}\\ {}&{=\frac{3\times3\times3\times10^{6}}{9\times10^{9}}}\\ {}&{=3\times10^{-3}\mathrm{C}}\\ \end{array} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Two spherical conductors A and B of radii 1 mm and 2 mm are separated by a distance of 5 cm and are uniformly charged. If the spheres are connected by a conducting wire, then in equilibrium condition, the ratio of the magnitude of the electric fields at the surfaces of spheres A and B is:
questions.explanation: After connecting the spheres by a conducting wire, they will be at same potential in equilibrium condition. ⇔ $$ \begin{aligned}\mathbf{V}_{1}&=\mathbf{V}_{2}\\\mathbf{K}\frac{q_{1}}{r_{1}}&=\mathbf{K}\frac{q_{2}}{r_{2}}\\\frac{q_{1}}{q_{2}}&=\frac{r_{1}}{r_{2}}\end{aligned} $$ The ratio of the magnitude of electric fields, $$ \begin{array}{r l r}&{\frac{\mathrm{\bf~E}_{1}}{\mathrm{\bf~E}_{2}}\;=\;\frac{\frac{\mathrm{\bf~K}q_{1}}{r_{1}^{2}}}{\frac{\mathrm{\bf~K}q_{2}}{r_{2}^{2}}}}\\ &{\Rightarrow\quad}&{\frac{\mathrm{\bf~E}_{1}}{\mathrm{\bf~E}_{2}}\;=\;\frac{q_{1}}{q_{2}}\bigg(\frac{r_{2}}{r_{1}}\bigg)^{2}}\\ &{\Rightarrow\quad}&{\frac{\mathrm{\bf~E}_{1}}{\mathrm{\bf~E}_{2}}\;=\;\frac{r_{1}}{r_{2}}\bigg(\frac{r_{2}}{r_{1}}\bigg)^{2}=\frac{r_{2}}{r_{1}}}\\ &{\Rightarrow\quad}&{\frac{\mathrm{\bf~E}_{1}}{\mathrm{\bf~E}_{2}}\;=\;\frac{2}{1}}\end{array} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: If $ \oint_S \overrightarrow{E} \cdot d\overrightarrow{S} = 0 $ over a surface, then:
questions.optionA: all the charges must necessarily be inside the surface.
questions.optionB: the electric field inside the surface is necessarily uniform.
questions.optionC: the number of flux lines entering the surface must be equal to the number of flux lines leaving it.
questions.optionD: the magnitude of electric field on the surface is constant.
questions.explanation: If $\oint_{s} \overrightarrow{E} \cdot ds = 0$, then over a surface, the number of flux lines entering the surface must be equal to the number of flux lines exiting from the surface.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A sphere enclose an electric dipole with charge \pm 3 \times 10^{-6} C. What is the total electric flux across the sphere?
questions.explanation: From the figure, it is clear that the sphere encloses a charge $ q = \pm 3 \times 10^{-6} $ C, which means it is a dipole. We know that, $$ \mathrm{Flux},\phi=\frac{(\mathrm{Charge})_{\mathrm{inside}}}{\varepsilon_{0}}=\frac{q_{in}}{\varepsilon_{0}} $$ Here, total charge inside the sphere is zero. $$ \phi=\frac{0}{\varepsilon_{0}}=0 $$ $$ \bullet \quad \bullet\bullet $$ ∴ Total electric flux is zero.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A charge Q is enclosed by a Gaussian spherical surface of radius R. If the radius is doubled, then the outward electric flux will:
questions.explanation: $$ \phi_{\mathrm{net}}=\frac{\Sigma q}{\varepsilon_{0}} $$ ∴ Net flux does not depend on size of Gaussian surface. ⇒ So, flux remain unchanged.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A hollow cylinder has a charge $q$ coulomb within it. If $\phi$ is the electric flux in units of $V_{m}$ associated with the curved surface B, the flux linked with the plane surface A in units of volt will be:
questions.explanation: Let $\phi_{A}$, $\phi_{B}$ and $\phi_{C}$ be the electric flux linked with A, B and C. ∴ According to Gauss theorem, $$ \phi_{\mathrm{A}}+\phi_{\mathrm{B}}+\phi_{\mathrm{C}}=\frac{q}{\varepsilon_{0}} $$ since $$ \phi_{\mathbf{A}}=\phi_{\mathbf{C}} $$ $$ \circlearrowleft $$ $$ 2\phi_{\mathrm{A}}+\phi_{\mathrm{B}}=\frac{q}{\varepsilon_{0}} $$ or $$ 2\phi_{\mathrm{A}}=\frac{q}{\varepsilon_{0}}-\phi_{\mathrm{B}} $$ or $$ 2\phi_{\mathrm{A}}={\frac{q}{\varepsilon_{0}}}-\phi $$ {since, $\phi_{B}=\phi_{1}$} $$ \phi_{\mathrm{A}}=\frac{1}{2}\bigg[\frac{q}{\varepsilon_{0}}-\phi\bigg] $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A charge q is located at the centre of a cube. The electric flux through any face is:
questions.explanation: Electric flux through any face, $$ \Phi_{face}=\frac{q}{6\varepsilon_{0}}=\frac{4\pi q}{6(4\pi\varepsilon_{0})} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A charge q is placed at the corner of a cube of side a. The electric flux through the cube is:
questions.explanation: According to Gauss law, the electric flux through a closed surface is equal to $ \frac{1}{\varepsilon_{0}} $ times the net charge enclosed by the surface. Since, q is the charge enclosed by the surface, then electric flux, \phi=\frac{q}{\varepsilon_{0}} If charge q is placed in one of the cube's corners, it will be divided into eight similar cubes. Therefore, electric flux through the cube,

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A point charge + q is placed at mid-point of a cube of side L. The electric flux emerging from the cube is: (a) $ \frac{q}{\varepsilon_{0}} $ (B) $ \frac{6qL^{2}}{\varepsilon_{0}} $
questions.explanation: According to Gauss's theorem, the total electric flux over a closed surface is $ \frac{1}{E_{0}} $ times the total charge contained inside surface. Total electric flux = $ \frac{\text{Total charge inside cube}}{\varepsilon_{0}} $

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Two parallel infinite line charges with linear charge densities + $ \lambda $ C/m and - $ \lambda $ C/m are placed at a distance of 2R in free space. What is the electric field mid-way between the two line charges?
questions.explanation:
$$ \phi=\frac{q}{\varepsilon_{0}} $$
Electric field due to line charge (1), $$ \overrightarrow{E}_{1}=\frac{\lambda}{2\pi\varepsilon_{0}R}\quad i\ N/C $$ Electric field due to line charge (2), $$ \overset{\rightarrow}{\mathrm{E}_{2}}=\frac{\lambda}{2\pi\varepsilon_{0}\mathrm{R}}\hat{i}\mathrm{N/C} $$ $$ \begin{align*}\mathrm{E}_{net}^{\rightarrow}&=\mathrm{E}_{1}^{\rightarrow}\mathrm{E}_{2}^{\rightarrow}\\&=\frac{\lambda}{2\pi\varepsilon_{0}\mathrm{R}}\hat{i}+\frac{\lambda}{2\pi\varepsilon_{0}\mathrm{R}}\hat{i}\\&=\frac{\lambda}{\pi\varepsilon_{0}\mathrm{R}}\hat{i}\quad\mathrm{N/C}.\end{align*} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A hollow metal sphere of radius R is uniformly charged. The electric field due to the sphere at a distance r from the centre:
questions.optionA: is zero as r increases for  $ r < R $ decreases as r increases for r > R
questions.optionB: is zero as r increases for  $ r < R $ increases as r increases for  $ r > R $
questions.optionC: decreases as r increases for  $ r < R $ and for  $ r > R $
questions.optionD: increases as r increases for  $ r < R $ and for  $ r > R $
questions.explanation:
Charge Q will be distributed over the surface of hollow metal sphere.
(i) For $ r < R $ (inside), By gauss law, $$ \begin{array}{c}\oint\limits_{\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{in}}}\rightarrow\rightarrow\rightarrow\rightarrow d\mathrm{s}=\frac{q_{e n}}{\varepsilon_{0}}\\\Rightarrow\quad\mathrm{E}_{i n}=0\end{array} $$ $$ (\because q_{en}=0) $$ (ii) For $ r > R $ (outside)
$$ \oint\overrightarrow{E_{\mathrm{o u t}}}\cdot\overrightarrow{d s}=\frac{q_{e n}}{e_{0}} $$ Here $$ q_{en}=Q $$ $$ \because q_{en}=Q) $$ $$ \mathrm{For}r>\mathrm{R},\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{out}}\xrightarrow{\rightarrow}=\frac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_{0}}\frac{\mathrm{Q}}{\left|\vec{r}^{2}\right|}\hat{r} $$ $$ \mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{out}}\propto\frac{1}{r^{2}} $$ $ \Rightarrow $ $ E_{out} $ decreases. ##### △! Caution While applying the Gauss law over a Gaussian surface, students should not think that the calculated electric field is only due to the charges inside the Gaussian surface. It is the resultant electric field of all the charges, present there.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: In a certain region of space with volume 0.2 m³, the electric potential is found to be 5 V throughout. The magnitude of electric field in this region is:
questions.explanation: Given Volume, $ V = 0.2 \, \text{m}^3 $ Electric potential = 5 V (constant) Electric field = ? Electric field, $ E = -\frac{dV}{dr} $ Here $ V = 5 \, \text{V} $ and constant $ \therefore $ $ E = -\frac{d(5)}{dr} $ $ = \frac{d(\text{constant})}{dr} = 0 $

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A bullet of mass 2 g is having a charge of 2 μC. Through what potential difference must it be accelerated, starting from rest, to acquire a speed of 10 m/s?
questions.explanation: According to question, a bullet of mass 2 g is having a charge of $ 2\ \mu\text{C} $. Here we apply, $$ \frac{1}{2}m\mathbf{v}^{2}=q\mathbf{V} $$ $$ \begin{aligned}\mathrm{V}&=\frac{1}{2}\times\frac{2\times10^{-3}\times10\times10}{2\times10^{-6}}\\&=50\mathrm{kV}\end{aligned} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.subtopic: Potential due to a Point Charge
questions.question: The value of electric potential at a distance of 9 cm from the point charge $ 4 \times 10^{-7} $ C is: $ \left(\text{Given that}\frac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_{0}}=9\times10^{9}\,\text{Nm}^{2}\,\text{C}^{-2}\right) $
questions.explanation: As we know $ V = \frac{kq}{r} $ Given $ q = 4 \times 10^{-7} C $ $ r = 9 \, cm = 0.09 \, m $ $ V = \frac{9 \times 10^{9} \times 4 \times 10^{-7}}{0.09} = 4 \times 10^{4} V $

questions.subject: Physics
questions.subtopic: Potential due to a Point Charge
questions.question: The electric potential at a point in free space due to charge Q coulomb is $ Q \times 10^{11} $ V. The electric field at that point is:
questions.explanation: Since $$ \begin{array}{r l}{\mathrm{)~I s i n c e~}}&{{}}\\ {{\mathrm{~G i v e n~}}}&{{}}\\ {{\Rightarrow}}&{{}}\\ {{\mathrm{~N o w~}}}&{{}}\\ {{\Rightarrow}}&{{}}\\ \end{array}\quad\begin{array}{l}{\mathrm{V}=\frac{\mathrm{Q}}{4\pi\varepsilon_{0}r}}\\ {\mathrm{V}=\mathrm{Q}\times10^{11}\mathrm{~V}}\\ {r=\frac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_{0}\times10^{11}}}\\ {\mathrm{E}=\frac{\mathrm{V}}{r}}\\ {\mathrm{E}=\mathrm{Q}\times10^{11}\times4\pi\varepsilon_{0}\times10^{11}}\\ {=4\pi\varepsilon_{0}\mathrm{~Q}\times10^{22}\mathrm{~V}/\mathrm{m}}\end{array} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.subtopic: Potential due to a Point Charge
questions.question: As per the diagram, a point charge +q is placed at the origin O. Work done in taking another point charge -Q from the point A [coordinate $ (0,a) $] to another point B [coordinates $ (a,0) $] along the straight path AB is:
questions.explanation:
Work done is equal to zero because the potential of A and B are the same, i.e., $ \frac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_{0}}\frac{q}{a} $
No work is done, if a particle does not change its potential energy. i.e., initial potential energy = final potential energy.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.subtopic: Potential due to an Electric Dipole
questions.question: Given below are two statements: one is labelled as Assertion A and the other is labelled as Reason R. Assertion (A): The potential (V) at any axial point, at 2 m distance (r) from the centre O of the dipole of dipole moment vector P of magnitude, $ 4 \times 10^{-6} $ Cm, is $ \pm 9 \times 10^{3} $ V. $ \left(\mathrm{Take}\frac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_{0}}=9\times10^{9}\mathrm{SI units}\right) $ Reason (R): $ \mathrm{V} = \pm \frac{2\mathrm{P}}{4\pi\varepsilon_{0}r^{2}} $, where $ r $ is the distance at any axial point, situated at 2 m from the centre of the dipole. In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below:@THE_RDX_07
questions.optionA: Both A and R are true and R is NOT the correct explanation of A.
questions.optionB: A is true but R is false.
questions.optionC: A is false but R is true.
questions.optionD: Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A.
questions.explanation: Formula of potential is given by $$ \mathbf{V}=\frac{k\varphi\cos\theta}{r^{2}} $$ For axial point, $ \theta = 0^{\circ} $ or $ 180^{\circ} $ $$ \begin{array}{l}\text{V}=\pm\frac{k p}{r^{2}}=\pm\frac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_{0}}\frac{p}{r^{2}}\\ =\pm\frac{9\times10^{9}\times4\times10^{-6}}{4}\\ =9\times10^{3}\text{V}\end{array} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.subtopic: Potential due to an Electric Dipole
questions.question: A short electric dipole has a dipole moment of $ 16 \times 10^{-9} $ Cm. The electric potential due to the dipole at a point at a distance of 0.6 m from the centre of the dipole, situated on a line making an angle of $ 60^{\circ} $ with the dipole axis is: $ \left(\frac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_{0}}=9\times10^{9}\mathrm{Nm}^{2}/\mathrm{C}^{2}\right) $
questions.explanation: Given $$ p=16\times10^{-9}C m $$ Distance, r = 0.6 m, Angle, $ \theta = 60^{\circ} $ $$ 60^{\circ}=\frac{1}{2} $$ The electric potential at a point at a distance r at some angle $\theta$ from electric dipole is V. and $$ \begin{aligned}\mathbf{V}&=\frac{p\cos\theta}{4\pi\varepsilon_{0}r^{2}}\\&=9\times10^{9}\times\frac{16\times10^{-9}\times\frac{1}{2}}{(0.6)^{2}}\\&=2\times10^{2}=200\end{aligned} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.subtopic: Potential due to a System of Charges
questions.question: A thin spherical shell is charged by some source. The potential difference between the two points C and P (in V) shown in the figure is: $$ \left(\mathrm{Take}\frac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_{0}}=9\times10^{9}\mathrm{SI units}\right) $$
questions.explanation: For a spherical shell $$ \mathbf{V}_{\mathrm{c e n t r e}}=\mathbf{V}_{\mathrm{s u r f a c e}} $$ Difference in potential is zero. because the electric field intensity inside the shell is zero. This means that the work done in moving a point charge inside the shell is also zero.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.subtopic: Potential due to a System of Charges
questions.question: Two hollow conducting spheres of radii $ R_{1} $ and $ R_{2} $ ( $ R_{1} \gg R_{2} $) have equal charges. The potential would be:
questions.optionD: dependent on the material property of the sphere
questions.explanation: For a hollow sphere, electric potential is, $$ \mathbf{V}=\frac{k q}{\mathbf{R}} $$ For sphere $$ \mathbf{V}_{1}=\frac{k q}{\mathbf{R}_{1}} $$ For sphere 2, $ V_{2}=\frac{kq}{R_{2}} $ $$ \begin{array}{l}\mathrm{As}\;\mathrm{R}_{1}>>\mathrm{R}_{2},\\\therefore\quad\mathrm{V}_{1}\ll\mathrm{V}_{2}.\end{array} $$ So, potential is more on smaller sphere.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.subtopic: Potential due to a System of Charges
questions.question: Two charged spherical conductors of radius $ R_{1} $ and $ R_{2} $ are connected by a wire. Then the ratio of surface charge densities of the spheres $ \frac{\sigma_{1}}{\sigma_{2}} $ is:
questions.explanation: Given, Radius = R_{1} and R_{2} Surface charge densities are $ \sigma_{1} $ and $ \sigma_{2} $ Since, the spheres are connected by wire. Hence, the potential on them will be equal. $$ \begin{aligned}\mathrm{V}_{1}&=\mathrm{V}_{2}\\\frac{k q_{1}}{\mathrm{R}_{1}}&=\frac{k q_{2}}{\mathrm{R}_{2}}\\\frac{q_{1}}{q_{2}}&=\frac{\mathrm{R}_{1}}{\mathrm{R}_{2}}\\q_{1}&=\sigma_{1}\mathrm{A}_{1},q_{2}=\sigma_{2}\mathrm{A}_{2}\\q_{1}&=\sigma_{1}4\pi\mathrm{R}_{1}^{2}\\q_{2}&=\sigma_{2}4\pi\mathrm{R}_{2}^{2}\\\frac{\sigma_{1}}{\sigma_{2}}&=\frac{\frac{q_{1}}{4\pi\mathrm{R}_{1}^{2}}}{\frac{q_{2}}{4\pi\mathrm{R}_{2}^{2}}}\\\frac{\sigma_{1}}{\sigma_{2}}&=\frac{\mathrm{R}_{2}^{2}}{\mathrm{R}_{1}^{2}}\times\frac{\mathrm{R}_{1}}{\mathrm{R}_{2}}=\frac{\mathrm{R}_{2}}{\mathrm{R}_{1}}\end{aligned} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.subtopic: Potential due to a System of Charges
questions.question: Twenty seven drops of same size are charged at 220 V each. They combine to form a bigger drop. The potential of the bigger drop is:
questions.explanation: Given, [Using (i)] Number of drops = 27 Radius of smaller sphere = r Voltage (V) = 220 V Total radius of bigger sphere = R Bigger sphere volume, $$ \mathbf{V}_{\mathbf{B}}=\frac{4}{3}\pi\mathbf{R}^{3} $$ Smaller sphere volume. $$ \mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{s}}=\frac{4}{3}\pi r^{3} $$ 27 $$ \mathrm{\frac{4}{3}}\pi r^{3}=\mathrm{\frac{4}{3}}\pi R^{3} $$ $$ 27\;r^{3}=\mathrm{R}^{3} $$ ∴ $$ \boldsymbol{R}=3\boldsymbol{r} $$ Now, $$ \mathrm{V}_{0}=\frac{q_{1}}{4\pi\varepsilon_{0}r} $$ Charge $q_{1}$ on smaller sphere, $$ q_{1}={\bf V}_{0}\:4\pi\epsilon_{0}r $$ Charge $q_{2}$ on bigger sphere, $$ \begin{aligned}q_{2}&=27q_{1}\\ V&=\frac{q_{2}}{4\pi\varepsilon_{0}R}\\&=\frac{27q_{1}}{4\pi\varepsilon_{0}3r}\\&=9V_{0}\\ V&=9\times220\\&=1980V\end{aligned} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.subtopic: Potential due to a System of Charges
questions.question: Two metal spheres, one of radius R and the other of radius 2R respectively have the same surface charge density $ \sigma $. They are brought in contact and separated. The new charge densities on them will be:
questions.explanation:
We know that surface charge density, $$ \begin{aligned}\boldsymbol{\sigma}&=\frac{q}{A}=\frac{q}{4\pi R^{2}}\\q&=\sigma.4\pi R^{2}\end{aligned} $$ $\Rightarrow$ Before contact of both the spheres,
$$ \begin{aligned}&q_{1}=\sigma.4\pi R^{2}\\&q_{2}=\sigma.4\pi(2R)^{2}\\&q_{2}=4q_{1}\\ \end{aligned} $$ After contact,
Here, $$ \sigma_{1}=\frac{q_{1}^{\prime}}{4\pi R^{2}} \text{ and} $$ $$ \sigma_{2}=\frac{q_{2}^{\prime}}{4\pi(2R)^{2}} $$ Again we know that, $$ \begin{aligned}\mathbf{V}&=\frac{k q}{\mathbf{R}}\\\mathbf{V}&=\frac{k q_{1}^{\prime}}{\mathbf{R}}=\frac{k q_{2}^{\prime}}{2\mathbf{R}}\\q_{2}^{\prime}&=2q_{1}^{\prime}\end{aligned} $$ So So, after contact we can say, $$ q_{1}^{\prime}+q_{2}^{\prime}=q_{1}+q_{2}=q_{1}+4q_{1}=5q_{1} $$ $$ \begin{array}{c} \therefore\quad q^{\prime}_{1}+2q^{\prime}_{1}=5\times\sigma\ 4\pi R^{2}\\ \Rightarrow\quad3q^{\prime}_{1}=5\times\sigma\times4\pi R^{2}\\ \end{array} $$ and $$ q_{2}^{\prime}=2q_{1}^{\prime}=\frac{10}{3}\times\sigma4\pi R^{2} $$ Putting the value of $q_{1}^{\prime}$ and $q_{2}^{\prime}$ in eqns (ii) & (iii), we get $$ \sigma_{1}=\frac{5}{3}\sigma\text{and}\sigma_{2}=\frac{5}{6}\sigma $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.subtopic: Potential due to a System of Charges
questions.question: Four point charges -Q,-q,2q and 2Q are placed, one at each corner of the square. The relation between Q and q for which the potential at the centre of the square is zero, is:
questions.explanation: According to the question, When potential at centre is zero, then

questions.subject: Physics
questions.subtopic: Potential due to a System of Charges
questions.question: Three charges, each + q, are placed at the corners of an isosceles triangle ABC of sides BC and AC, 2a. D and E are the mid point of BC and CA. The work done in taking a charge Q from D to E is:
questions.explanation:
The situation is shown in the diagram,
From the figure. $$ AC=BC(given) $$ D and E are midpoints of BC and AC. ∴ Using geometry it can be proved that BE = DA. $$ \mathrm{V_{D}=V_{E}=V} $$ $$ \begin{aligned}\mathbf{W}&=\mathbf{Q}(\mathbf{V}_{\mathrm{E}}-\mathbf{V}_{\mathrm{D}})\\&=\mathbf{Q}(\mathbf{V}-\mathbf{V})\\\mathbf{W}&=0\end{aligned} $$
$$ \begin{array}{l}\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{A}}=\frac{k q}{\mathrm{L}}+\frac{k q}{\mathrm{L}}-\frac{k q}{\sqrt{5}\mathrm{L}}-\frac{k q}{\sqrt{5}\mathrm{L}}\\ =\frac{2k q}{\mathrm{L}}\left(1-\frac{1}{\sqrt{5}}\right)\\ =\frac{2q}{\mathrm{L}}\times\frac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_{0}}\left(1-\frac{1}{\sqrt{5}}\right)\end{array} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.subtopic: Potential due to a System of Charges
questions.question: Four electric charges + q, + q, - q and - q are placed at the corners of a square of side 2L (see figure). The electric potential at point A mid-way between the two charges + q and q, is:
questions.explanation: $$ \begin{aligned}&\mathrm{V}_{1}+\mathrm{V}_{2}+\mathrm{V}_{3}+\mathrm{V}_{4}=0\\-\frac{\mathrm{KQ}}{\frac{r}{2}}-\frac{\mathrm{Kq}}{\frac{r}{2}}+\frac{\mathrm{K}(2\mathrm{Q})}{\frac{r}{2}}+\frac{\mathrm{K}2q}{\frac{r}{2}}=0\\\Rightarrow\quad&\quad-\mathrm{Q}-q+2q+2\mathrm{Q}=0\\\Rightarrow\quad&\quad\mathrm{Q}=-q\end{aligned} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.subtopic: Potential due to a System of Charges
questions.question: Three concentric spherical shells have radii $ a $, $ b $ and $ c $ ( $ a < b < c $) and have surface charge densities $ \sigma $, $ -\sigma $ and $ \sigma $ respectively. If $ V_A $, $ V_B $ and $ V_C $ denote the potentials of the three shells, then for $ c = a + b $, we have:
questions.explanation:
$$ \mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{A}}=\frac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_{0}}\left\{\frac{q_{\mathrm{A}}}{a}+\frac{q_{\mathrm{B}}}{b}+\frac{q_{\mathrm{C}}}{c}\right\} $$
$$ \mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{A}}=\frac{1}{\varepsilon_{0}}\left\{\frac{a^{2}\sigma}{a}-\frac{b^{2}\sigma}{b}+\frac{c^{2}\sigma}{c}\right\} $$ $$ \mathbf{V_{B}}=\frac{1}{\varepsilon_{0}}\left\{\frac{a^{2}\sigma}{b}-\frac{b^{2}\sigma}{b}+\frac{c^{2}\sigma}{c}\right\} $$ and $$ \mathbf{V}_{\mathrm{C}}=\frac{1}{\varepsilon_{0}}\left\{\frac{a^{2}\boldsymbol{\sigma}}{c}-\frac{b^{2}\boldsymbol{\sigma}}{c}+\frac{c^{2}\boldsymbol{\sigma}}{c}\right\} $$ Using, c = a + b, $$ \mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{A}}=\frac{2a\sigma}{\varepsilon_{0}} $$ $$ \mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{B}}=\frac{\sigma}{\varepsilon_{0}}\left(\frac{a^{2}}{b}+a\right)=\frac{\sigma}{\varepsilon_{0}}\frac{ac}{b} $$ $$ \begin{aligned}\mathbf{V_{C}}&=\frac{\boldsymbol{\sigma}}{\varepsilon_{0}}\Bigg[\frac{a^{2}-b^{2}+c^{2}}{c}\Bigg]\\&=\frac{\boldsymbol{\sigma}}{\varepsilon_{0}}\Bigg[\frac{a^{2}-b^{2}+(a+b)^{2}}{(a+b)}\Bigg]\quad\ldots(\mathrm{iii})\\&=\frac{\boldsymbol{\sigma}}{\varepsilon_{0}}\Bigg[\frac{(a-b)(a+b)+(a+b)(a+b)}{(a+b)}\Bigg]\\&=\frac{\boldsymbol{\sigma}}{\varepsilon_{0}}\times2a\quad\{\text{since}c=a+b\}\end{aligned} $$ So, from eqns (i), (ii) and (iii), we get $$ \mathbf{V}_{\mathbf{A}}=\mathbf{V}_{C}\neq\mathbf{V}_{\mathbf{B}} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.subtopic: Potential due to a System of Charges
questions.question: Two concentric spheres of radii R and r have similar charges with equal surface charge densities (\sigma). What is the electric potential at their common centre?
questions.explanation: Let the charges on the spheres be Q and q respectively. The potential at common center, $$ \begin{array}{c} \mathbf{V}=\frac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_{0}}\left(\frac{\mathbf{Q}}{\mathbf{R}}\right)\;+\;\frac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_{0}}\left(\frac{\mathbf{q}}{r}\right)\\ =\frac{1}{\varepsilon_{0}}\left[\frac{\mathbf{Q}}{4\pi\mathbf{R}^{2}}\times\mathbf{R}+\frac{\mathbf{q}}{4\pi r^{2}}\times\mathbf{r}\right] \end{array} $$ But, $$ \frac{Q}{4\pi R^{2}}=\frac{q}{4\pi r^{2}}=\sigma $$ $$ \begin{aligned}\mathbf{V}&=\frac{1}{\varepsilon_{0}}\left[\boldsymbol{\sigma}\mathbf{R}+\boldsymbol{\sigma}\mathbf{r}\right]\\&=\frac{\boldsymbol{\sigma}}{\varepsilon_{0}}\left(\mathbf{R}+\mathbf{r}\right)\end{aligned} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: The angle between the electric lines of force and the equipotential surface is:
questions.explanation: The angle between the electric lines of force and the equipotential surface is $ 90^{\circ} $.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: The diagrams below show regions of equipotentials: A positive charge is moved from A to B in each diagram.
questions.optionA: Maximum work is required to move q in figure (iii).
questions.optionB: In all the four cases, the work done is the same.
questions.optionC: Minimum work is required to move q in figure (i).
questions.optionD: Maximum work is required to move q in figure (ii).
questions.explanation:
Work done, $ W = q \Delta V $ ∆V is same in all the cases, so work done is same in all the cases.
##### Related Theory An equipotential surface is that at every point of which electric potential is the same and electric field lines of force are always perpendicular to an equipotential surface.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: If potential (in volts) in a region is expressed as $ \mathrm{V}(x, y, z) = 6xy - y + 2yz $, the electric field in (N/C) at point (1, 1, 0) is:
questions.explanation: From question, Potential in a region, $$ \mathrm{V}_{(x,y,z)}=6x y-y+2y z $$ ∴ Electric field. $$ \begin{aligned}\vec{E}&=-\frac{\partial\mathbf{V}}{\partial x}\hat{i}-\frac{\partial\mathbf{V}}{\partial y}\hat{j}-\frac{\partial\mathbf{V}}{\partial z}\hat{k}\\&=[-6y\hat{i}-(6x-1+2z)\hat{j}-2y\hat{k}\end{aligned} $$ $$ \begin{aligned}\therefore\quad\overrightarrow{\mathrm{E}}_{(1,1,0)}&=-1(6\times1)\hat{i}-(6\times1-1+2\times0)\hat{j}-(2\times1)\hat{k}\\&=-(6\hat{i}+5\hat{j}+2\hat{k})\mathbf{N}/\mathrm{C}\end{aligned} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: In a region, the potential is represented by $ V(x, y, z) = 6x - 8xy - 8y + 6yz $, where V is in volts and x, y, z are in metres. The electric force experienced by a charge of 2 coulomb situated at point $ (1, 1, 1) $ is:
questions.explanation: It is given, $$ V=6x-8xy-8y+6yz $$ We know that, $$ \mathrm{E}=-\frac{d\mathbf{V}}{d r} $$ $$ \mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{X}}=-\frac{d\mathbf{V}}{d x}=-6+8y $$ $$ \mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{Y}}=-\frac{d\mathbf{V}}{d y}=+8x+8-6z $$ $$ E_{z}=-\frac{d V}{d z}=-6y $$ The values of $ E_X $, $ E_Y $ and $ E_Z $ at $ (1, 1, 1) $ are, $$ \mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{X}}=-6+8\times1=+2 $$ $$ \mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{Y}}=8\times1+8-6\times1=+10 $$ $$ \mathrm{E_{Z}=-6~\times~1=-6} $$ $$ \begin{aligned}\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{net}}&=\sqrt{(2)^{2}+(10)^{2}+(6)^{2}}\\&=\sqrt{4+100+36}=\sqrt{140}\\&=\sqrt{35\times4}=2\sqrt{35}\mathrm{~N/C}\end{aligned} $$ $$ \begin{aligned}\mathrm{F}&=q,\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{net}}\\&=2\times(2\sqrt{35})\\&=4\sqrt{35}\mathrm{N}\end{aligned} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: The electric potential V at any points $(x, y, z)$, all in metres in space is given by $V = 4x^{2}$ volt. The electric field at the point $(1, 0, 2)$ in volt/metre is:
questions.explanation: It is given that, $ V = 4x^{2} $ then we have. $$ \begin{aligned}\mathrm{E}&=-\left[\hat{i}\frac{\partial\mathrm{V}}{\partial x}+\hat{j}\frac{\partial\mathrm{V}}{\partial y}+\hat{k}\frac{\partial\mathrm{V}}{\partial z}\right]\\&=-\hat{i}\frac{\partial}{\partial x}(4x^{2})\end{aligned} $$ $$ =-\mathbf{8}\mathbf{x}\hat{i}~\mathbf{V}\mathbf{m}^{-2} $$ $$ \begin{array}{l}\text{E}_{(1,0,2)}=-8\times1\text{i}\\ =-\hat{8i}.\text{Vm}^{-1}\end{array} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: The electric potential at a point (x, y, z) is given by, $ V = -x^2y - xz^3 + 4 $ The electric field $ \vec{E} $ at that point is:
questions.explanation: We know that. $$ \begin{aligned}\overset{\rightarrow}{\mathbf{E}}&=-\frac{d\mathbf{N}}{d r}=\left[-\frac{\partial\mathbf{V}}{\partial x}\hat{i}-\frac{\partial\mathbf{V}}{\partial y}\hat{j}-\frac{\partial\mathbf{V}}{\partial z}\hat{k}\right]\\&=\left[(2xy+z^{3})\hat{i}+x^{2}\hat{j}+3xz^{2}\hat{k}\right]\end{aligned} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.subtopic: Potential Energy of a System of Charges
questions.question: Charges +q and -q are placed at points A and B respectively which are a distance 2L apart, C is the midpoint between A and B. The work done in moving a charge +Q along the semicircle CRD is:
questions.explanation: $$ \mathbf{W}=\Delta\mathbf{U}=\mathbf{Q}\left(\mathbf{V}_{\mathrm{D}}-\mathbf{V}_{\mathrm{C}}\right) $$ where, $$ \mathbf{V_{C}}=0 $$ $$ \begin{aligned}\mathbf{W}&=\mathbf{Q}\left[\frac{q}{4\pi\varepsilon_{0}(3\mathrm{L})}-\frac{q}{4\pi\varepsilon_{0}(\mathrm{L})}\right]\\&=-\frac{\mathbf{Q}q}{6\pi\varepsilon_{0}\mathrm{L}}\end{aligned} $$ Potential at C is zero because the charges are equal and opposite and the distances are the same. Potential at D due to -q is greater than due to +q, because D is closer to B. ∴ It is negative.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.subtopic: Potential Energy of a System of Charges
questions.question: Two charges $q_{1}$ and $q_{2}$ are placed 30 cm apart, as shown in the figure. A third charge $q_{3}$ is moved along the arc of a circle of radius 40 cm from C to D. The change in the potential energy of the system is $\frac{q_{3}}{4\pi E}k$, where $k$ is: $$ 8q_{1} $$
questions.explanation:
$$ \mathrm{U}_{i}=\frac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_{0}}\left[\frac{q_{1}q_{3}}{(0.4)}+\frac{q_{1}q_{2}}{(0.3)}+\frac{q_{2}q_{3}}{(0.5)}\right] $$ $$ \mathbf{U}_{f}=\frac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_{0}}\left[\frac{q_{1}q_{3}}{(0.4)}+\frac{q_{1}q_{2}}{(0.3)}+\frac{q_{2}q_{3}}{(0.1)}\right] $$
$$ \begin{array}{l}\Delta\mathrm{U}=\mathrm{U}_{f}-\mathrm{U}_{i}\\ =~\frac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_{0}}~q_{2}q_{3}~\left(\frac{1}{0.1}-\frac{1}{0.5}\right)\\ =~\frac{q_{2}q_{3}}{4\pi\varepsilon_{0}}(8)\\ =~\frac{q_{3}}{4\pi\varepsilon_{0}}(8q_{2}) \end{array} $$ On comparing with the equation given in question, we get $$ k=8q_{2} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.subtopic: Potential Energy of a System of Charges
questions.question: Identical charges $(-q)$ are placed at each corner of a cube of side $b'$ then the electrical potential energy of charge $(+q)$ which is placed at the centre of cube will be:
questions.explanation: Distance between the charge -q and +q is, $$ r=\sqrt{\frac{(\sqrt{2}b)^{2}+(b)^{2}}{2}}=\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}b $$ Electric potential energy of charge + q is, $$ \begin{aligned}\mathbf{V}&=8\times\frac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_{0}}\frac{q(-q)}{r}\\&=8\times\frac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_{0}}\frac{q(-q)}{\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}b}\end{aligned} $$ $$ \mathrm{U}=\frac{-4q^{2}}{\sqrt{3}\pi\varepsilon_{0}b} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.subtopic: Potential Energy of a System of Charges
questions.question: In bringing an electron towards another electron, the electrostatic potential energy of the system:
questions.explanation: The electron is charged negatively. When an electron passes nearby another electron, the same negative charges produce a repulsive force between them. As a result, work is done against this repulsive force in order to bring them closer together. Electrostatic potential energy is used to store the work. As a result, the system's electrostatic potential energy increases. Since, Electrostatic potential energy of system of two electrons, $$ \mathrm{U}=\frac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_{0}}\frac{(-e)(-e)}{r}=\frac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_{0}}\frac{e^{2}}{r} $$ Clearly, r decreases, potential energy U increases.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.subtopic: Potential Energy in an External Field
questions.question: A dipole is placed in an electric field as shown. In which direction will it move?
questions.optionA: Towards the left as its potential energy will increase.
questions.optionB: Towards the right as its potential energy will decrease.
questions.optionC: Towards the left as its potential energy will decrease.
questions.optionD: Towards the right as its potential energy will increase.
questions.explanation: Since, the electric field is growing against the dipole direction. Hence, the dipole will move towards the left due to decrease in its potential energy.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.subtopic: Potential Energy in an External Field
questions.question: An electric dipole of dipole moment $p$ is aligned parallel to a uniform electric field E. The energy required to rotate the dipole by $90^{\circ}$ is:
questions.explanation: Potential energy of dipole $$ \mathrm{U}=-p\mathrm{E}\left(\cos\theta_{2}-\cos\theta_{1}\right) $$ Here $$ \begin{aligned}\theta_{2}&=0^{\circ}\\&=-p\mathrm{E}\left(\cos90^{\circ}-\cos0^{\circ}\right)\\\mathrm{U}&=p\mathrm{E}\end{aligned} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.subtopic: Potential Energy in an External Field
questions.question: An electric dipole of dipole moment p is lying along a uniform electric field E. The work done in rotating the dipole is:
questions.explanation: Work done, $$ \mathbf{W}=\underset{0}{\overset{\theta}{\int}}p\mathbf{E}\sin\theta\,d\theta $$ Since, $$ =p\mathrm{E}\left(1-\cos\theta\right) $$ $$ \theta=90^{\circ} $$ $$ \bullet \quad \bullet\ast $$ $$ \mathrm{W}=p\mathrm{E}(1-\cos90^{\circ}) $$ $$ \mathbf{W}=p\mathbf{E} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.subtopic: Potential Energy in an External Field
questions.question: A electric dipole has the magnitude of its charge as $q$ and its dipole moment is $p$. It is placed in a uniform electric field $E$. If its dipole moment is along the direction of the field, the force on it and its potential energy are respectively:
questions.explanation: Force on each charge of dipole Charge of dipole $ F = qE $ \therefore Total force = zero, they are in opposite direction \therefore \overrightarrow{p} and \overrightarrow{E} are parallel to each other \overrightarrow{11} = -\overrightarrow{p} \cdot \overrightarrow{E} is minimum.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Some charge is being given to a conductor. Then, its potential:
questions.optionD: is maximum somewhere between the surface and the centre
questions.explanation: We know that inside the conductor, E = 0. So, potential remains same.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: If the plates of a parallel plate capacitor connected to a battery are moved close to each other, then: (I) the charge stored in it, increases. (II) the energy stored in it, decreases. (III) its capacitance increases. (IV) the ratio of charge to its potential remains the same. (V) the product of charge and voltage increases. Choose the most appropriate answer from the options given below:
questions.explanation: On keeping the battery connected voltage remains same $$ \mathrm{C}=\frac{\varepsilon\mathrm{A}}{d},\mathrm{If}d\mathrm{~is~reduced}, $$ ∴ capacitance increases. From $$ Q=CV $$ If C is increased, the product of capacitance and voltage also increases, so charge also increases. E = $ \frac{1}{2} $ CV $ ^{2} $, As C increases, energy also increases. So, statement (I), (III) and (V) are correct.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: The electrostatic force between the metal plates of an isolated parallel plate capacitance C having charge Q and area A, is:
questions.optionA: proportional to the square root of the distance between the plates.
questions.optionB: linearly proportional to the distance between the plates.
questions.optionC: independent of the distance between the plates.
questions.optionD: inversely proportional to the distance between the plates.
questions.explanation: We know that, For isolated capacitor, Q = constant $$ \mathrm{F_{plate}}=\frac{\mathrm{Q}^{2}}{2A\varepsilon_{0}} $$ So. F is independent of the distance between the plates.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A parallel plate air capacitor has capacity C, distance of separation between plates is d and potential difference V is applied between the plates. Force of attraction between the plates of the parallel plate air capacitor is:
questions.explanation: According to question, $$ \mathbf{F}=\frac{\mathbf{Q}^{2}}{2\varepsilon_{0}\mathbf{A}} $$ $$ \mathrm{Q}=\mathrm{CV}~\mathrm{and}~\mathrm{C}=\frac{\varepsilon_{0}\mathrm{A}}{d} $$ $$ \varepsilon_{0}\mathbf{A}=\mathbf{C}d $$ From eqn. (i), (ii) and (iii), we get $$ \bullet \quad \bullet\ast $$ $$ \mathrm{F}=\frac{\mathbf{C}^{2}\mathbf{V}^{2}}{2\mathrm{Cd}}=\frac{\mathbf{C}\mathbf{V}^{2}}{2d} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A parallel plate air capacitor is charged to a potential difference of V volts. After disconnecting the charging battery, the distance between the plates of the capacitor is increased using an insulating handle. As a result the potential difference between the plates:
questions.explanation: According to the question q = Constant and decrease in C means increase in V. $$ \mathrm{C}=\frac{\varepsilon_{0}\mathrm{A}}{d} $$ $$ Capacitance=\frac{Charge}{Potential difference} $$ $$ \Rightarrow\mathrm{Potential}\ \mathrm{difference}=\frac{\mathrm{Charge}}{\mathrm{Capacitance}}. $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: The capacitance of a capacitor with charge $q$ and a potential difference $V$ depends on:
questions.explanation: The capacitance (C) of a capacitor depends on the geometry and the dielectric medium between the plates. It is defined by the relation. $$ C=\frac{A\varepsilon_{o}}{d} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A parallel plate capacitor having cross-sectional area A and separation $d$ has air in between the plates. Now an insulating slab of same area but thickness $\frac{d}{2}$ is inserted between the plates as shown in figure, having dielectric constant $(K=4)$. The ratio of new capacitance to its original capacitance will be:
questions.explanation: Capacitance of parallel plate capacitor when medium is air, $$ C_{0}=\frac{\varepsilon_{0}A}{d} $$ From $2^{\mathrm{nd}}$ condition, $$ \mathbf{A}^{\prime}=\mathbf{A},\,t=\frac{d}{2}\,,\,\mathbf{K}=4 $$ $$ \mathbf{C}=\frac{\varepsilon_{0}\mathbf{A}}{(d-t)+\frac{t}{\mathbf{K}}}=\frac{\varepsilon_{0}\mathbf{A}}{\left(d-\frac{d}{2}\right)+\frac{d/2}{4}} $$ $$ =\frac{\varepsilon_{0}\mathrm{A}}{\frac{d}{2}+\frac{d}{8}}=\frac{8}{5}.\frac{\varepsilon_{0}\mathrm{A}}{d} $$ $$ \frac{\mathrm{C}}{\mathrm{C}_{0}}=\frac{\frac{8\varepsilon_{0}\mathrm{A}}{5\mathrm{~d}}}{\frac{\varepsilon_{0}\mathrm{A}}{\mathrm{d}}} $$ $$ \frac{C}{C_{0}}=\frac{8}{5} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: The capacitance of a parallel plate capacitor with air as medium is 6 μF. With the introduction of a dielectric medium, the capacitance becomes 30 μF. The permittivity of the medium is: $ \varepsilon_0 = 8.85 \times 10^{-12} \, \text{C}^2\text{N}^{-1} \, \text{m}^{-2} $
questions.explanation: Given, $$ \mathbf{C}_{0}=6\mu\mathbf{F} $$ $$ \mathbf{C_{M}}=30~\mu\mathbf{F} $$ $\therefore$ Dielectric, $K=\frac{C_{M}}{C_{0}}=\frac{30}{6}=5$ Permittivity of medium, $$ \begin{aligned}\varepsilon_{m}&=\mathbf{K}\times\varepsilon_{\mathbf{o}}=5\times\varepsilon_{\mathbf{o}}\\&=5\times8.854\times10^{-12}\\&=0.44\times10^{-10}\mathbf{C}^{2}\mathbf{N}^{-1}\mathbf{\Phi m}^{-2}.\end{aligned} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Two parallel metal plates having charge + Q and -Q face each other at a certain distance between them. If the plates are now dipped in kerosene oil tank, the electric field between the plates will:
questions.explanation:
Electric field in volume, $ E_{0} = \frac{\sigma}{2\varepsilon_{0}} $ When plates are dipped in kerosene oil electric field,
