$$ \mathrm{E}=\frac{\sigma}{2\varepsilon_{0}\mathrm{K}} $$ Here, K > 1 $ \therefore $ $ E < E_{0} $ So, electric field between the plates will decrease.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A parallel plate condenser with oil (dielectric constant 2) between the plates has capacitance C. If oil is removed, the capacitance of capacitor becomes:
questions.explanation: We have, the capacitance of a parallel plate capacitor with dielectric (oil) between its plates is $$ C=\frac{\mathbf{K}\varepsilon_{0}\mathbf{A}}{d} $$ where, $ \varepsilon_{0} = $ electric permittivity of free space K = dielectric constant of oil A = area of each plate of capacitor d = distance between two plates When the dielectric (oil) is removed, then the capacitor's capacitance will be, $$ C_{0}=\frac{\varepsilon_{0}\mathbf{A}}{d} $$ On comparing Eqs. (i) and (ii), we get $$ C=K C_{0} $$ $$ \implies $$ $$ C_{0}=\frac{C}{K}=\frac{C}{2} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: If the following circuit, the equivalent capacitance between terminal X and terminal Y is:
questions.explanation:
This is a balanced Wheatstone bridge. The equivalent circuit is
On Solving the circuit, As $ C_{1} $ and $ C_{2} $ are in series so, $$ \frac{1}{C_{1}}+\frac{1}{C_{2}}=\frac{1}{C} $$ $$ C^{\prime}=1~\upmu F $$ As $ C_{3} $ and $ C_{4} $ are in series $$ \frac{1}{C_{3}}+\frac{1}{C_{4}}=\frac{1}{C} $$ SO. $$ C^{n}=1~\upmu F $$ Now C'and C" are in parallel so, $ \text{C} = \text{C}' + \text{C}'' = (1 + 1)\mu\text{F} = 2\mu\text{F} $

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: The equivalent capacitance of the system shown in the following circuit is:
questions.explanation: In parallel combination, $$ C_{p}=3+3=6\mu\mathrm{F} $$ $$ \begin{aligned}C_{eq}&=\frac{C_{P}\times3}{3+C_{P}}=\frac{3\times6}{6+3}\\&=2\mu F\end{aligned} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: The equivalent capacitance of the combination shown in the figure is:
questions.explanation: Since, the capacitor (3) is shorted out by the parallel wire, therefore the net capacitance is in parallel. $$ C_{P}=C+C=2C $$ $$ \begin{array}{l}\bullet\\ \bullet\end{array} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A parallel-plate capacitor of area A, plate separation d and capacitance C is filled with four dielectric materials having dielectric constants $ k_{1} $, $ k_{2} $, $ k_{3} $ and $ k_{4} $ as shown in the figure below. If a single dielectric material is to be used to have the same capacitance C in this capacitor, then its dielectric constant k is given by:
questions.explanation:
Here the capacitance, $ C = \frac{A\pi r_{0}}{d} $ The above three capacitors $ C_{1}, C_{2}, C_{3} $ are in parallel and then it is in series with $ C_{4} $. Here, $$ C_{1}=\frac{\left(\frac{A}{3}\right)k_{1}\varepsilon_{0}}{\frac{d}{2}}=\frac{2k_{1}}{3k}C $$ $$ C_{2}=\frac{\left(\frac{\mathbf{A}}{3}\right)k_{2}\varepsilon_{0}}{\frac{d}{2}}=\frac{2k_{2}}{3k}C $$ $$ C_{3}=\frac{\left(\frac{\mathbf{A}}{3}\right)k_{3}\varepsilon_{0}}{\frac{d}{2}}=\frac{2k_{3}}{3k}C $$ $$ C_{4}=\frac{A k_{4}\varepsilon_{0}}{\frac{d}{2}}=\frac{2k_{4}}{k}C $$ Now, the equivalent capacitance for the combination of four capacitors is, $$ \frac{1}{C_{eq}}=\frac{1}{C_{1}+C_{2}+C_{3}}+\frac{1}{C_{4}} $$ or $$ \frac{1}{C}=\frac{3k}{2C}\left[\frac{1}{k_{1}+k_{2}+k_{3}}\right]+\frac{k}{2k_{4}C} $$ or $$ \mathrm{(as~C_{eq}=C)} $$ $$ \frac{2}{k}=\frac{3}{k_{1}+k_{2}+k_{3}}+\frac{1}{k_{4}} $$
##### Caution Students may think that, the first three dielectrics appear to be connected in series so capacitor are also in series, but here since capacitor plates are connected together, so capacitance are connected in parallel.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Three capacitors each of capacitance C and of breakdown voltage V are joined in series. The capacitance and breakdown voltage of the combination will be:
questions.explanation: Here, $$ \mathbf{V}=3\mathbf{V} $$ $$ \frac{1}{C_{eq}}=\frac{1}{C_{1}}+\frac{1}{C_{2}}+\frac{1}{C_{3}} $$ $$ C_{eq}=\frac{C}{3} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A network of four capacitors capacity equal $ C_{1} = C $, $ C_{2} = 2C $, $ C_{3} = 3C $ and $ C_{4} = 4C $ are conducted to a battery as shown in the figure. The ratio of the charge on $ C_{2} $ and $ C_{4} $ is:
questions.explanation:
Here, $ C_{1} $, $ C_{2} $ and $ C_{3} $ are in series.
$$ \frac{1}{C^{\prime}}=\frac{1}{C}+\frac{1}{2C}+\frac{1}{3C} $$ $$ \frac{1}{C^{\prime}}=\frac{6+3+2}{6C}=\frac{11}{6C} $$ $$ C^{\prime}=\frac{6C}{11} $$ All the capacitors in branch number 1 is in series, so, the charge on each number capacitor is: $$ Q^{\prime}=\frac{6}{11}CV $$ Also charge on capacitor $ C_{4} $ is, Q = 4 CV $$ \begin{aligned}Ratio}&=\frac{Q^{\prime}}{Q}=\frac{6CV}{11\times4CV}\\&=\frac{3}{22}\end{aligned} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Three capacitors each of capacity $4\mu F$ are to be connected in such a way that the effective capacitance is $6\mu F$. This can be done by:
questions.optionA: connecting all of them in series.
questions.optionC: connecting two in series and one in parallel.
questions.optionD: connecting two in parallel and one in series.
questions.explanation:
The given situation can be shown as follows.
$$ \begin{aligned}\mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{AB}}&=(2+4)\mu\mathrm{F}\\&=6\mu\mathrm{F}\end{aligned} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A capacitor of capacitance $ C_{1} $ charged up to V volt and then connected to an uncharged capacitor $ C_{2} $. Then, the final potential difference across each capacitor will be:
questions.explanation: $$ \begin{array}{l} \text{I)V}=\frac{C_{1}V_{1}+C_{2}V_{2}}{C_{1}+C_{2}}\\\Rightarrow\quad V^{\prime}=\frac{C_{1}V_{1}}{C_{1}+C_{2}}\quad(\because V_{2}=0)\\ \Rightarrow\quad V^{\prime}=\frac{C_{1}V}{C_{1}+C_{2}}\quad\{\because V_{1}=V\}\end{array} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A 12 pF capacitor is connected to a 50 V battery, the electrostatic energy stored in the capacitor in nJ is:
questions.explanation: Given C = 12 pF = 2 $ \times $ 10 $ ^{-12} $ F $$ \begin{aligned}&\mathrm{pF}=2\times10^{-12}\mathrm{F}\\&\mathrm{V}=50\mathrm{~V}\\&\mathrm{U}=\frac{1}{2}\times12\times10^{-12}\times(50)^{2}\\&\mathrm{U}=\frac{1}{2}\times12\times10^{-12}\times2500\\&\mathrm{U}=1.5\times10^{-8}\mathrm{J}\\&\mathrm{U}=15\mathrm{nJ}\\ \end{aligned} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A capacitor of capacitance, C = 900 pF is charged fully by 100 V battery B as shown in figure (a). Then it is disconnected from the battery and connected to another uncharged capacitor of capacitance C = 900 pF as shown in fig (b). The electrostatic energy stored by the system (b) is:
questions.explanation: Given: $$ \begin{array}{l}C_{1}=C_{2}=900\ pF\\ V_{1}=100\ V,V_{2}=0\ V\end{array} $$ When $ C_{1} $ and $ C_{2} $ are joined, $$ \begin{aligned}\mathbf{V}&=\frac{\mathbf{C}_{1}\mathbf{V}_{1}+\mathbf{C}_{2}\mathbf{V}_{2}}{\mathbf{C}_{1}+\mathbf{C}_{2}}\\&=\frac{900\times100+0}{1800}=50\mathrm{~V}\end{aligned} $$ Energy, $$ \begin{aligned}\mathrm{U}&=\frac{1}{2}(\mathrm{C}_{1}+\mathrm{C}_{2})\mathrm{V}^{2}\\&=\frac{1}{2}\times1800\times10^{-12}\times50\times50\\&=2.25\times10^{-6}\mathrm{J}\end{aligned} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A parallel plate capacitor has a uniform electric field $ \vec{E} $ in the space between the plates. If the distance between the plates is ‘ $ d' $’ and the area of each plate is ‘ $ A' $, the energy stored in the capacitor is: $ (\varepsilon_{0} = \text{permittivity of free space}) $
questions.explanation: Given, Electric Field = E Distance between plates = d Area of plates = A Energy = ? We know, $\frac{\mathrm{Energy}}{\mathrm{Volume}} = \frac{1}{2} \varepsilon_{0} E^{2}$ $$ \mathrm{Energy}=\frac{1}{2}\varepsilon_{0}\mathrm{E}^{2}\times\mathrm{Volume} $$ $$ \mathrm{E n e r g y}=\mathrm{~\frac{1}{2}~}\varepsilon_{0}\mathrm{E}^{2}\mathrm{A}d $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Two identical capacitors $C_{1}$ and $C_{2}$ of equal capacitance are connected as shown in the circuit. Terminals $a$ and $b$ of the key $k$ are connected to charged capacitor $C_{1}$ using battery of emf V volt. Now, disconnecting $a$ and $b$, the terminal $b$ and $c$ are connected. Due to this, what will be the percentage loss of energy?
questions.explanation:
We know from question,
$$ \mathbf{V}_{\mathrm{i}}=\frac{1}{2}\mathbf{C}\mathbf{V}^{2} $$ On switching key at point C, $$ \begin{array}{l}\mathrm{V}_{f}=\frac{1}{2}\frac{\mathrm{C}^{2}}{2\mathrm{C}}\mathrm{V}^{2}\\=\frac{1}{4}\mathrm{CV}^{2}\end{array} $$ $$ \begin{array}{l} \text{Loss} =\frac{\mathbf{V}_{i}-\mathbf{V}_{f}}{\mathbf{V}_{i}}\times100 \\ =\frac{\left(\frac{1}{2}-\frac{1}{4}\right)\mathbf{C}\mathbf{V}^{2}}{\frac{1}{2}\mathbf{C}\mathbf{V}^{2}}\times100=50\% \end{array} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A capacitor is charged by a battery. The battery is removed and another identical uncharged capacitor is connected in parallel. The total electrostatic energy of resulting system:
questions.explanation:
The given scenario can be represented as follows
Charge on capacitor. $$ \boldsymbol{q}=\boldsymbol{C}\boldsymbol{V} $$ When it is connected with another uncharged Capacitor, $$ \mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{C}}=\frac{q_{1}+q_{2}}{\mathrm{C}_{1}+\mathrm{C}_{2}}=\frac{q+0}{\mathrm{C}+\mathrm{C}}=\frac{\mathrm{V}}{2} $$ Initial energy, $$ \mathbf{U}_{i}=\frac{1}{2}c\mathbf{v}^{2} $$ ∴ Final energy. $$ \begin{array}{l}\mathrm{U}_{f}=2\times\frac{1}{2}\mathrm{C}\left(\frac{\mathrm{V}}{2}\right)^{2}\\=\frac{\mathrm{CV}^{2}}{4}\end{array} $$ $\therefore$ Loss of energy, $U_{i}-U_{f}=\frac{CV^{2}}{4}$ i.e., decreases by a factor 2.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A capacitor of 2 μF is charged as shown in the figure. When the switch S is turned to position 2, the percentage of its stored energy dissipated is:
questions.explanation:
First, when switch S is connected to point 1. Then,
Initial energy stored in capacitor 2 $ \mu $F is, $$ \mathbf{U}_{i}=\frac{1}{2}(2\mu\mathbf{F})\mathbf{V}^{2}\left[\mathbf{E}=\frac{1}{2}\mathbf{C}\mathbf{V}^{2}\right] $$ Second, when switch S is connected to point 2, then $$ \mathrm{V}_{f}=\frac{\mathrm{C}_{1}\mathrm{V}_{1}}{\mathrm{C}_{1}+\mathrm{C}_{2}}=\frac{2\mathrm{V}}{10}=0.2\mathrm{~V} $$ ∴ Final energy in both the capacitors, $$ \begin{aligned}\mathrm{U}_{f}&=\frac{1}{2}(\mathrm{C}_{1}+\mathrm{C}_{2})\mathrm{V}_{f}^{2}\\&=\frac{1}{2}\times10\left(\frac{2\mathrm{V}}{10}\right)^{2}=0.2\mathrm{~V}^{2}\end{aligned} $$ So, energy dissipated $$ \frac{V^{2}-0.2V^{2}}{V^{2}}\times100=80\% $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A parallel plate air capacitor of capacitance C is connected to a cell of emf V and then disconnected from it. A dielectric slab of electric constant K, which can just fill the air gap of the capacitor, is now inserted in it. Which of the following is INCORRECT?
questions.optionA: The potential difference between the plates decrease K times.
questions.optionB: The energy stored in the capacitor decreases K times.
questions.optionC: The change in energy stored is  $ \frac{1}{2}CV^{2}\left(\frac{1}{K}-1\right) $
questions.optionD: The charge on the capacitor is not conserved.
questions.explanation: Let charge q on the capacitor (C) when connected to cell of emf (V) is given by, $$ \mathbf{V}=\frac{q}{C} $$ Energy stored is, $ U = \frac{1}{2}CV^{2} = \frac{q^{2}}{2C} $ When dielectric slab of dielectric constant K is inserted, then capacitance $ C' $ will be $$ \mathbf{V}^{\prime}=\frac{\boldsymbol{q}}{C^{\prime}}=\frac{\boldsymbol{q}}{C\mathbf{K}} $$ And energy stored is, $ U' = \frac{q^{2}}{2CK} $ $$ \begin{aligned}\Delta\mathbf{U}&=\mathbf{U}^{\prime}-\mathbf{U}\\&=\frac{q^{2}}{2C}\bigg(\frac{1}{\mathbf{K}}-1\bigg)\\&=\frac{1}{2}\mathbf{C}\mathbf{V}^{2}\bigg(\frac{1}{\mathbf{K}}-1\bigg)\end{aligned} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: The energy required to charge a parallel plate condenser of plate separation d and plate area of cross-section A such that the uniform electric field between the plates is E, is:
questions.explanation: The energy given to the cell, $$ E=C\mathbf{V}^{2} $$ where, $$ C=c a p a c i t a n c e o f c o n d u c t o r=\frac{A\varepsilon_{0}}{d} $$ V = potential difference across the plate $ \therefore $ $$ \begin{array}{c}{\mathrm{E}=\left(\frac{\mathrm{A}\varepsilon_{0}}{d}\right)(\mathrm{E}d)^{2}}\\ {\mathrm{=A}\varepsilon_{0}\mathrm{E}^{2}d}\end{array} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Two condensers, one of capacity C and other of capacity $ \frac{C}{2} $ are connected to a V-volt battery, as shown. The work done in charging fully both the condensers is:
questions.explanation: Work done in charging fully both the condensers, $$ \begin{aligned}\mathbf{W}=\mathbf{U}&=\frac{1}{2}\mathbf{C}\mathbf{V}^{2}+\frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{\mathbf{C}}{2}\right)\mathbf{V}^{2}\\&=\frac{3}{4}\mathbf{C}\mathbf{V}^{2}\end{aligned} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A capacitor is charged by connecting a battery across its plates. It stores energy U. Now the battery is disconnected and another identical capacitor is connected across it, then the energy stored by both capacitors of the system will be:
questions.explanation: The initial energy held in a capacitor when it is charged by connecting a battery across its plates. $$ \mathrm{U}=\frac{q^{2}}{2\mathrm{C}} $$ The charge remains constant when the battery is disconnected, i.e. $q = \text{constant}$. Now, another identical capacitor is connected across it, forming a parallel connection. Thus, the equivalent capacitance $$ \begin{aligned}C_{eq}&=C_{1}+C_{2}\\&=C+C=2\ C\end{aligned} $$ Final energy stored by the system of capacitors, $$ \begin{aligned}&U^{\prime}=\frac{q^{2}}{2C_{eq}}\\&U^{\prime}=\frac{q^{2}}{2(2C)}\\&U^{\prime}=\frac{1}{2}U\\ \end{aligned} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: If the potential of a capacitor having capacity 6 μF is increased from 10 V to 20 V, then increase in its energy will be:
questions.explanation: The energy stored in the capacitor is determined by $ U = \frac{1}{2} C V^{2} $ If the initial and the final potential are $ V_{1} $ and $ V_{2} $ respectively, Then increase in energy ( $ \Delta U $), $$ \begin{aligned}\Delta U&=\frac{1}{2}C(V_{2}^{2}-V_{1}^{2})\\&=\frac{1}{2}\times(6\times10^{-6})\times[(20)^{2}-(10)^{2}]\\&=\frac{1}{2}(6\times10^{-6})\times300=9\times10^{-4}J\end{aligned} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: In an electrical circuit, the voltage is measured as $ V = (200 \pm 4) \, \Omega $ volt and the current is measured as $ I = (20 \pm 0.2) \, A $. The value of the resistance is:
questions.explanation: Given, Voltage, $ V=(200\pm4)V $ Current, $ I=(20\pm0.2)A $ $$ \mathrm{R}=\frac{\mathbf{V}}{\mathrm{I}}=\frac{200}{20}=10\ \Omega $$ $$ \left(\frac{\Delta\mathbf{R}}{\mathbf{R}}\right)^{2}=\left(\frac{\Delta\mathbf{V}}{\mathbf{V}}\right)^{2}+\left(\frac{\Delta\mathbf{I}}{\mathbf{I}}\right)^{2} $$ $$ \frac{\Delta\mathbf{V}}{\mathbf{V}}=\frac{4}{200}=0.02 $$ $$ \left(\frac{\Delta\mathrm{R}}{\mathrm{R}}\right)^{2}=(0.02)^{2}+(0.01)^{2} $$ $$ \frac{\Delta R}{R}=\sqrt{0.0005} $$ $$ \Delta\mathbf{R}=\mathbf{R}\times0.0224 $$ $$ \Delta R=0.224 $$ $$ \therefore R=10\pm0.224\Omega=(10\pm0.3)\Omega $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Two solid conductors are made up of same material and have same length and same resistance. One of them has a circular cross-section of area $A_{1}$ and the other one has a square cross section of area $A_{2}$. The ratio $\frac{A_{1}}{A_{2}}$ is:
questions.explanation:
From question, $ R_{1}=R_{2} $ and $ l_{1}=l_{2} $ Resistance, $$ \mathbf{R}=\boldsymbol{\rho}\ \frac{\boldsymbol{l}}{\mathbf{A}} $$ $$ ↳\quad\downarrow\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad $$ $$ \begin{aligned}&\frac{\mathbf{R}_{1}}{\mathbf{R}_{2}}=\frac{l_{1}}{l_{2}}.\frac{\mathbf{A}_{2}}{\mathbf{A}_{1}}\\&\frac{\mathbf{R}_{1}}{\mathbf{R}_{1}}=\frac{l_{1}}{l_{1}}.\frac{\mathbf{A}_{2}}{\mathbf{A}_{1}}\\&\frac{\mathbf{A}_{2}}{\mathbf{A}_{1}}=1\\ \end{aligned} $$ [Using (i)]
##### Mnemonics ←Just REPLAY the video. $$ Resistance=\rho\left(\frac{Length}{Area}\right) $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: The resistance of a wire is R ohm. If it is melted and stretched to n times its original length, its new resistance will be:
questions.explanation: In both the cases, Volume of material is same, $$ \circlearrowleft $$ $$ \mathbf{A}_{1}l_{1}=\mathbf{A}_{2}l_{2} $$ It is also given, $l_{2}=nl_{1}$ $$ \mathbf{A}_{1}l_{1}=\mathbf{A}_{2}n l_{1} $$ $$ \mathbf{A}_{2}=\frac{\mathbf{A}_{1}}{n} $$ Now, when the wire is stretched, then resistance $$ \mathrm{R}_{1}=\mathrm{p}\frac{l_{2}}{\mathrm{A}_{2}}=\mathrm{p}\cdot\frac{n l_{1}}{\mathrm{A}_{1}} $$ $$ \mathbf{R}_{1}=\mathbf{R}\mathbf{n}^{2} \quad \left[\because\mathbf{R}=\left(\rho\frac{l_{1}}{\mathbf{A}_{1}}\right)\right] $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A wire of resistance $ 4 \Omega $ is stretched to twice its original length. The resistance of stretched wire would be:
questions.explanation: $$ \mathrm{R}=\frac{\rho l}{\mathrm{A}}=4\ \Omega $$ New length, l = 2l On stretching, volume remain constant. ∴ Resistance of stretched wire is, $$ \begin{aligned}\mathrm{R}^{\prime}&=\frac{\rho I^{\prime}}{\mathrm{A}^{\prime}}=\rho\frac{(2l)}{\left(\mathrm{A}/2\right)}=\frac{4\rho l}{\mathrm{A}}\\&=4\times4=16\Omega\end{aligned} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A wire of a certain material is stretched slowly by ten percent. Its new resistance and specific resistance become respectively:
questions.explanation: After stretching, Specific resistance ( $ \rho $) will remain same. Original resistance of the wire $$ \mathrm{R}=\mathrm{p}\frac{l}{\mathrm{A}} $$ or $$ \mathsf{R}\propto\frac{l}{\mathsf{A}} $$ or $$ \mathrm{R}\propto\frac{l^{2}}{\mathrm{V}} $$ $$ \left(\mathrm{as}\mathrm{V}=\mathrm{Al}\right) $$ and $$ \mathbf{R}^{\prime}\propto\frac{(l+10\%\times l)^{2}}{\mathrm{V}} $$ Therefore $$ \frac{\mathbf{R}^{\prime}}{\mathbf{R}}\ =\frac{\left(l+\frac{10}{100}l\right)^{2}}{l^{2}} $$ $$ \mathrm{\frac{r^{\prime}}{r}}={\frac{\left({\frac{11}{10}}l\right)^{2}}{l^{2}}}={\frac{121}{100}} $$ $$ \mathbf{R}^{\prime}=1.21\mathrm{~R~} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: The electric resistance of a certain wire of iron is R. If its length and radius are both doubled, then:
questions.optionA: the resistance will be doubled and the specific resistance will be halved.
questions.optionB: the resistance will be halved and the specific resistance will remain unchanged.
questions.optionC: the resistance will be halved and the specific resistance will be doubled.
questions.optionD: the resistance and the specific resistance, both will remain unchanged.
questions.explanation: Resistance of wire, $ R = \rho \frac{l}{A} $ $$ \mathrm{R}\propto\frac{l}{\mathrm{A}}=\frac{l}{\pi r^{2}} $$ According to the question, when l and r are doubled, $$ \mathrm{R}_{1}\propto\frac{2l}{\pi(2r)^{2}} $$ $$ \mathbf{R}_{1}\propto\frac{1}{2}\mathbf{R} $$ Here, specific resistance of wire is independent of geometry wire, so it only depends on the material of wire.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A 6 volt battery is connected to the terminal of a three metre long wire of uniform thickness and resistance of 100 ohm. The difference of potential between two points on the wire separated by a distance of 50 cm will be:
questions.explanation: $ \therefore $ Voltage on 50 cm = $ \frac{6}{300} \times 50 $ = 1 volt

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: There are three copper wires of length and cross-sectional area (L,A), $ \left(2L,\frac{A}{2}\right) $, $ \left(\frac{L}{2},2A\right) $. In which case is the resistance minimum?
questions.optionA: It is the same in all three cases
questions.explanation: The relation between length and cross-section area of a wire is given by, $$ \mathrm{R}=\frac{\rho l}{\mathrm{A}} $$ where, $\rho$ is the specific resistance. It is the proportionality constant and depends on nature of material. (1) Length = L, cross-sectional area = A Putting in (i), we get $$ \mathbf{R}=\frac{\rho L}{\mathbf{A}} $$ (2) Length = 2L, cross-sectional area = $ \frac{A}{2} $ Putting in (i), we get $$ R=\rho\frac{2L}{A/2}=\frac{4\rho L}{A} $$ $$ Length=\frac{L}{2}, $$ cross-sectional area = 2 A Putting in Eq. (i), we get $$ \mathrm{R}=\frac{\rho(\mathrm{L}/2)}{2\mathrm{~A}}=\frac{\rho\mathrm{L}}{4\mathrm{~A}} $$ It is clear from above, resistance is minimum only in option (B).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: If a negligibly small current is passed through a wire of length 15 m and of resistance 5 Ω having uniform cross-section of $ 6 \times 10^{-7} \, \text{m}^2 $, then coefficient of resistivity of material, is:
questions.explanation: Given, We have, $$ \begin{aligned}l&=15\mathrm{~m}\\ A&=6\times10^{-7}\mathrm{~m}^{2}\\\mathrm{R}&=5\Omega,\rho=\?\\\rho&=\frac{\mathrm{RA}}{l}\\&=\frac{5\times6\times10^{-7}}{15}\\&=2\times10^{-7}\mathrm{\Omega}\mathrm{-m}\end{aligned} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.subtopic: Drift of Electrons and The Origin of Resistivity
questions.question: A uniform wire of diameter $d$ carries a current of 100 mA when the mean drift velocity of electrons in the wire is $v$. For a wire of diameter $\frac{d}{2}$ of the same material to carry a current of 200 mA, the mean drift velocity of electrons in the wire is:
questions.explanation: For the original wire with diameter $d$, the current is $I_{1}=100\ m\ A=0.1\ A$, drift velocity $v_{d}=v$ $A=\pi\left(\frac{d}{2}\right)^{2}=\frac{\pi d^{2}}{4}$ The current in original wire is $I_{1}=n\left(\frac{\pi d^{2}}{4}\right)ev$ For the new wire with diameter $=\frac{d}{2}$ $A'=n\left(\frac{\frac{d}{2}}{2}\right)^{2}=\pi\left(\frac{d}{4}\right)^{2}=\frac{\pi d^{2}}{16}$ As $I=neAv_{d}$ $\frac{I_{2}}{I_{1}}=\frac{n\left(\frac{\pi d^{2}}{16}\right)ev'}{n\left(\frac{\pi d^{2}}{4}\right)ev}$ $\frac{0.2}{0.1}=\frac{1}{4}\cdot\frac{v'}{v}$ $\Rightarrow4\times2=\frac{v'}{v}$ $\therefore v'=8v$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.subtopic: Drift of Electrons and The Origin of Resistivity
questions.question: A copper wire of length 10 m and radius $ \left(\frac{10^{-2}}{\sqrt{\pi}}\right) $ m has electrical resistance of 10 $ \Omega $. The current density in the wire for an electric field strength of 10 (V/m) is:
questions.explanation: Given, $$ R=10\ \Omega,E=10\ V/m,l=10\ m $$ Radius, $$ r=\frac{10^{-2}}{\sqrt{\pi}}m $$ We know, $$ \begin{aligned}\rho&=\frac{RA}{l}=\frac{10\times\pi\times\frac{10^{-4}}{\pi}}{10}\\&=10^{-4}\Omega m\end{aligned} $$ Current density, $$ \begin{aligned}\mathrm{J}&=\mathrm{E}\ \sigma=\frac{\mathrm{E}}{\rho}=\frac{10}{10^{-4}}\\&=10^{5}\mathrm{~A}/\mathrm{m}^{2}\end{aligned} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.subtopic: Drift of Electrons and The Origin of Resistivity
questions.question: Column-I gives certain physical terms associated with flow of current through a metallic conductor. Column-II gives some mathematical relations involving electrical quantities. Match Column-I and Column - II with appropriate relation.
questions.explanation: Drift velocity, $ v_d = \frac{eL}{m}\tau $ Current density, $ J = \frac{I}{A} = \frac{neAv_d}{A} = nev_d $ Resistivity, $ \rho = \frac{m}{ne^2\tau} $ $ \Rightarrow $ $ \tau = \frac{m}{ne^2\rho} $ Resistance, $ R = \frac{V}{I} $ $ \Rightarrow $ $ \rho \frac{l}{A} = \frac{EI}{I} $ $ \rho = \frac{E}{L} $

questions.subject: Physics
questions.subtopic: Drift of Electrons and The Origin of Resistivity
questions.question: Across a metallic conductor of non-uniform cross-section, a constant potential difference is applied. The quantity which remains constant along the conductor is:
questions.explanation: We know that, $$ \mathbf{J}=\frac{1}{\mathbf{A}} $$ Drift velocity, $$ \mathbf{v}_{\mathrm{d}}=\frac{\mathrm{I}}{n A e}=\frac{\mathrm{J}}{n e} $$ Electric field, $ E = \rho J $ From eq. (i), (ii) and (iii), we confirm that current density, drift velocity and electric field depend on area of conductor. Even if the area of cross-section of conductor is non-uniform, the number of electrons are flowing.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: The resistance of a discharge tube is:
questions.explanation: In discharge tube, the flow of positive ions and electrons causes the current. Moreover, secondary electron emission is possible. As a result of the non-linearity of the V-I curve, its resistance will be non-ohmic.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: From the graph between current I and voltage V shown in figure, identify the portion corresponding to negative resistance.
questions.explanation:
From Ohm's law, $ R = \frac{\Delta V}{\Delta I} $ If current decreases as voltage or temperature rises, then resistance is said to be negative. In V-I graph, the current drops with rising voltage in graph segment CD. Thus, The negative resistance is represented by portion CD.
##### Related Theory The variation between Potential difference (V) and Current (I) through a conductor is represented by a straight line passing through the origin and having a constant slope, the reciprocal of slope of line gives resistance.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: The resistance of platinum wire at $0^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ is 2 Ω and 6.8 Ω at $80^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$. The temperature coefficient of resistance of the wire is:
questions.explanation: Given: $$ \mathrm{R}_{0}=2\ \Omega\ \mathrm{at}\ \mathrm{T}_{1}=0^{\circ}\mathrm{C} $$ $$ \mathrm{R}=6.8\ \Omega\ \mathrm{at}\ \mathrm{T}_{2}=80^{\circ}\mathrm{C} $$ As, $$ \mathbf{R}=\mathbf{R}_{0}\left[1+\alpha\left(\mathbf{T}_{2}-\mathbf{T}_{1}\right)\right] $$ $$ 6.8=2\left[1+\alpha\left(80-0\right)\right] $$ $$ 6.8=2+160\alpha $$ $$ \alpha=0.03or3\times10^{-2}\circ C^{-1} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: As the temperature increases, the electrical resistance:
questions.optionA: increase for both conductors and semiconductors.
questions.optionB: decrease for both conductors and semiconductors.
questions.optionC: increase for conductors but decrease for semiconductors
questions.optionD: decrease for conductors but increase for semiconductors
questions.explanation: As the temperature increases, the resistance of conductor increases and for semiconductor decreases, because temperature coefficient of resistance ($\alpha$) is positive for conductors and negative for insulators. ##### △! Caution Student may think that all the materials have identical dependence of resistivity on temperature but this is not true, as different materials have different dependence on temperature.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: The solids which have the negative temperature coefficient of resistance are:
questions.explanation:
The negative temperature coefficient of the resistance is only present in the insulators or the semiconductors. In these, the resistance decreases with increase in temperature.
##### Related Theory Temperature Coefficient of resistivity is positive for metals (Copper) and is negative for Semiconductor (Silicon).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Specific resistance of a conductor increase with:
questions.optionC: increase in the cross-section and decrease in the length
questions.explanation: Specific resistance of a (conductor) increases with increase in the temperature.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Two heaters X and Y have power rating of 1 kW and 2 kW, respectively. Those two are first connected in series and then in parallel to a fixed power source. The ratio of power outputs of these two cases is:
questions.explanation: $ P_{X} = 1 \, kW $, $ P_{Y} = 2 \, kW $ For parallel combination of heaters. $ P_{parallel} = P_X + P_Y = 1 + 2 = 3 \, kW $ For series combination of heaters $$ \frac{1}{\mathbf{P}_{\mathrm{series}}}=\frac{1}{\mathbf{P}_{\mathrm{A}}}+\frac{1}{\mathbf{P}_{\mathrm{B}}}=\frac{1}{1}+\frac{1}{2} $$ $$ \mathrm{P_{s e r i e s}}=\frac{2}{3}\mathrm{~k W} $$ $$ \frac{\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{parallel}}}{\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{series}}}=\frac{3}{2}\times3=\frac{9}{2}, $$ $$ \mathrm{\frac{P_{series}}{P_{parallel}}}=\frac{2}{9} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Two resistors of resistance, 100 Ω and 200 Ω are connected in parallel in an electrical circuit. The ratio of the thermal energy developed in 100 Ω to that in 200 Ω in a given time is:
questions.explanation: We know, $$ \mathrm{H}=\frac{\mathrm{V}^{2}}{\mathrm{R}}t $$ so, $$ \mathrm{H}_{1}=\frac{\mathrm{V}^{2}}{100}t $$ $$ \mathrm{H}_{2}=\frac{\mathrm{V}^{2}}{200}t $$ $$ \frac{H_{1}}{H_{2}}=\frac{200}{100}=2:1 $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Which of the following acts as a circuit protection device?
questions.explanation: Protective device are necessary to protect electrical appliances or equipment. From the given devices, fuse is used in electric circuit as a protective device, it helps in preventing excessive amount of current to flow in the circuit or from short circuiting.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A filament bulb (500 W, 100 V) is to be used in a 230 V main supply. When a resistance R is connected in series, it works perfectly and the bulb consumes 500 W. The value of R is:
questions.explanation: Current through bulb, $$ I=\frac{P}{V}=\frac{500W}{100V}=5A $$ $$ R=\frac{130V}{5A}=26\Omega $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Two cities are 150 km apart. Electric power is sent from one city to another city through copper wires. The fall of potential per km is 8 V and the average resistance per km is 0.5 Ω. The power loss in the wire is:
questions.explanation: Given, Total fall of potential between two cities $$ V=8\times150=1200V $$ Total resistance = 0.5 × 150 = 75 Ω $ \therefore $ Power loss, $ P = \frac{V^{2}}{R} = \frac{1200 \times 1200}{75} $ = 19200 W = 19.2 kW

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: The power dissipated in the circuit shown in the figure is 30 Watt. The value of R is:
questions.explanation: According to the question, $$ \begin{aligned}&\mathbf{R}_{1}=\mathbf{R},\mathbf{R}_{2}=5\Omega,\mathbf{V}=10\mathbf{V},\mathbf{P}=30\mathbf{W}\\&\therefore\quad\mathbf{P}=\frac{\mathbf{V}^{2}}{\mathbf{R}_{1}}+\frac{\mathbf{V}^{2}}{\mathbf{R}_{2}}\\&\quad\frac{10^{2}}{\mathbf{R}}=30-\frac{10^{2}}{5}\\&\Rightarrow\quad\frac{100}{\mathbf{R}}=30-20\\&\quad\mathbf{R}=10\mathbf{O}\end{aligned} $$ $$ \mathrm{R=10\Omega} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: If voltage across a bulb rated 220 V, 100 W drops by 2.5% to its rated value, the percentage at the rated value by which the power would decrease is:
questions.explanation: We know power, $ P = \frac{V^{2}}{R} $ $$ \begin{aligned}\frac{\Delta\mathrm{P}}{\mathrm{P}}\times100\%&=\frac{2\Delta\mathrm{V}}{\mathrm{V}}\times100\%\\&=2\times2.5=5\%\end{aligned} $$ Power.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A current of 3 A flows through the 2 $ \Omega $ resistor shown in the circuit. The power dissipated in the 5 $ \Omega $ is:
questions.explanation:
Voltage across all three branches are same.
Voltage across $ 2\ \Omega $ resistance, $$ \mathbf{V}=2\times3=6\mathbf{V} $$ So voltage across lowest arm, $$ \mathbf{V}_{l}=6\mathbf{V} $$ Current across 5 $ \Omega $ is, $$ I=\frac{6}{1+5}=1\ A $$ ∴ Power across 5 $ \Omega $ is, $$ \mathrm{P}=\mathrm{I}^{2}\mathrm{R}=(1)^{2}\times5=5\mathrm{~W} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: In the circuit shown, the current through the 4 Ω resistor is 1 A when the point P and M are connected to a DC voltage source. The potential difference between the points M and N is:
questions.explanation:
$ i_{1}=1\ \text{amp}=\text{current through}4\ \Omega $ $ i_{2}=\text{current through}3\ \Omega $ $$ \begin{aligned}&\therefore\quad\frac{i_{2}}{i_{1}}=\frac{4}{3}\\&\Rightarrow\quad i_{2}=\frac{4}{3}\text{A}\\&\quad i_{3}=i_{1}+i_{2}=1+\frac{4}{3}\\&\quad=\frac{7}{3}\text{A}\\&and\quad\frac{i_{3}}{i_{4}}=\frac{5/4}{12/7}\\&\Rightarrow\quad\frac{7}{3}\times\frac{12}{7}=i_{4}\times\frac{5}{4}\end{aligned} $$
$$ i_{4}=\frac{16}{5}=3.2A $$ $ V_{NM} = P.D. $ across M and N $ = i_{4} \times 1 $ $ = 3.2 \times 1 = 3.2 \, Volt $

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: The total power dissipated in watt in the circuit shown here is:
questions.explanation: Total power dissipated, $ P = \frac{V^{2}}{R} $ $ = \frac{18 \times 18}{6} = 54 \, \text{W} $

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Power dissipated across the 8 $ \Omega $ resistor in the circuit shown here is 2 watt. The power dissipated in watt units across the 3 $ \Omega $ resistor is:
questions.explanation: 1 $ \Omega $ and 3 $ \Omega $ are in series. So, $$ \mathbf{R_{s}}=\mathbf{R}_{1}+\mathbf{R}_{3} $$ $$ \mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{s}}=3+1=4\Omega $$ and $$ \mathrm{R}_{2}=8\ \Omega $$ i = net current in the circuit Current through $ R_{1} $ is, $$ i_{1}=\frac{i\times\mathrm{R}_{2}}{\mathrm{R}_{s}+\mathrm{R}_{2}}=\frac{i\times8}{12}=\frac{2i}{3} $$ Current through $ R_{2} $ is, $$ i_{2}=\frac{i\times\mathrm{R}_{s}}{\mathrm{R}_{s}+\mathrm{R}_{2}}=\frac{i\times4}{12}=\frac{i}{3} $$ Power dissipated in 3 $ \Omega $ resistance is, $$ \mathbf{P}_{1}=i_{1}^{2}\times3 $$ Power dissipated in 8 $ \Omega $ resistance is: $$ \mathbf{P}_{2}=i_{2}^{2}\times8 $$ From eqns (i), (ii), (iii) & (iv), we get $$ \mathrm{\frac{p_{1}}{p_{2}}}={\frac{i_{1}^{2}\times3}{i_{2}^{2}\times8}} $$ $$ \frac{\mathrm{P}_{1}}{\mathrm{P}_{2}}=\frac{\left(\frac{2i}{3}\right)^{2}\times3}{\left(\frac{i}{3}\right)^{2}\times8}=\frac{3}{2} $$ $$ \mathrm{P}_{1}=\frac{3}{2}\times\mathrm{P}_{2}=\frac{3}{2}\times2=3\mathrm{~W} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A 5-ampere fuse wire can withstand a maximum power of 1 watt in the circuit. The resistance of the fuse wire is:
questions.explanation: $$ \begin{aligned}&\mathrm{P}=\mathrm{I}^{2}\mathrm{~R}\\&\mathrm{P}=25\times\mathrm{R}\\&\mathrm{R}=\frac{1}{25}=0.04\Omega\\ \end{aligned} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: When three identical bulbs of 60 watt, 200 volt rating are connected in series to a 200 volt supply, the power drawn by them will be:
questions.explanation: The resistance of each bulb is, $ \frac{V^{2}}{P} = \frac{(200)^{2}}{60}\Omega $ When three bulbs are connected in series, Then equivalent resistance is = $ \frac{3 \times (200)^{2}}{60} $. $$ \because $$ $$ \mathrm{P}=\frac{\mathrm{V}^{2}}{\mathrm{R}_{eq}}=20\mathrm{W}. $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: In India, electricity is supplied for domestic use at 220 V. It is supplied at 110 V in USA. If the resistance of a 60 W bulb for use in India is R, the resistance of a 60 W bulb for use in USA will be:
questions.explanation: In India, $$ \mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{I}}=\frac{(220)^{2}}{\mathrm{R}} $$ $$ \mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{V}}=\frac{(110)^{2}}{\mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{v}}} $$ As $$ \mathbf{P}_{\mathrm{I}}=\mathbf{P}_{\mathbf{V}} $$ $$ \frac{(220)^{2}}{R}=\frac{(110)^{2}}{R_{V}} $$ $$ \mathrm{R_{v}}=\frac{110\times110}{220\times220}\mathrm{R} $$ $$ \mathbf{R}_{\mathbf{v}}=\frac{\mathbf{R}}{4} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Fuse wire is a wire of:
questions.optionA: high resistance and high melting point.
questions.optionB: high resistance and low melting point.
questions.optionC: low resistance and low melting point.
questions.optionD: low resistance and high melting point. [AIPMT2
questions.explanation: Fuse wire must have high resistance (per unit length) and low melting point.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Two 220 volt, 100 watt bulbs are connected first in series and then in parallel. Each time combination is connected to a 220 volt AC supply line. The power drawn by the combination in each case respectively will be:
questions.explanation: $$ R=\frac{V^{2}}{P}=\frac{220\times220}{100}=484\ \Omega $$ In series. $$ \mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{s}}=(484+484)~\Omega=968~\Omega $$ $$ \begin{aligned}P_{s}&=\frac{V^{2}}{R_{s}}=\frac{V^{2}}{968}\\&=\frac{220\times220}{968}=50W\end{aligned} $$ In parallel, total resistance, $$ R_{P}=\frac{484}{2}=242\ \Omega $$ ∴ Power in parallel combination. $$ \begin{array}{l}\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{p}}=\frac{\mathrm{V}^{2}}{\mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{p}}}=\frac{(220)^{2}}{242}\\ =200\mathrm{~W}\end{array} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Two bulbs 25 W, 220 V and 100 W, 220 V are given. Which has higher resistance?
questions.optionC: Both bulbs will have equal resistance
questions.optionD: Resistance of bulbs cannot be compared
questions.explanation: We have. power of electric bulb, $ P = \frac{V^{2}}{R} $ $$ \mathbf{R}=\frac{\mathbf{V}^{2}}{\mathbf{P}} $$ For same potential difference V, $$ \mathsf{R}\propto\frac{1}{\mathsf{P}} $$ As a result, We can see that when power is low, resistance is high, and vice versa. Given. $$ \mathrm{P}_{1}=25\mathrm{~W},\mathrm{P}_{2}=100\mathrm{~W}, $$ $$ \mathrm{V}_{1}=\mathrm{V}_{2}=220\mathrm{~V} $$ Hence, resistance of 25 W bulb is higher than 100 W bulb.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A heating coil is labelled 100 W, 220 V. The coil is cut in two equal halves and the two pieces are joined in parallel to the same source. The energy now liberated per second is:
questions.explanation: When a heating coil is cut into two equal halves and then joined in parallel, the coil's resistance is decreased to one-fourth of its prior value. $$ \left(\mathrm{H}\propto\frac{1}{\mathrm{R}}\right) $$ $$ i.e.,\quad4\times100=400\;{\mathrm{J}}. $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Three resistances each of 4 Ω are connected to form a triangle. The resistance between any two terminal is:
questions.explanation: Here, the two resistances are connected in series and the resultant is connected in parallel with the third resistance. Therefore, equivalent resistance will be, $$ \frac{1}{\mathbf{R}_{BC}}=\frac{1}{4}+\frac{1}{8} $$ $$ \frac{1}{R_{BC}}=\frac{2+1}{8} $$ $$ \mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{BC}}=\frac{8}{3}\Omega $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A current of 2 A, passing through a conductor produces 80 J of heat in 10 s. The resistance of the conductor in ohm is:
questions.explanation: The work done in carrying a charge $q$ from one end to the other end of a conductor with the potential difference $V$ equals the amount of heat created in that conductor. $$ \mathbf{H}=\mathbf{W}=\mathbf{V}\mathbf{q}=\mathbf{V}\mathbf{I}t=\mathbf{I}^{2}\mathbf{\Omega}\mathbf{R}t $$ $$ \begin{array}{r l}{\bullet}&{{}}\\ {\bullet}&{{}}\end{array} $$ $$ \mathbf{H}=\mathbf{I}^{2}\mathbf{R}t $$ $$ \implies $$ $$ [\mathrm{S i n c e},\mathrm{V}=\mathrm{I R}] $$ $$ \mathbf{R}=\frac{\mathbf{H}}{(\mathbf{I}^{2}t)} $$ Given, H = 80 J, I = 2 A, t = 10 s, So $$ \mathbf{R}=\frac{80}{(2)^{2}\times10}=2\ \Omega $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Six similar bulbs are connected as shown in the figure with a DC source of emf E and zero internal resistance. The ratio of power consumption by the bulbs when (i) all are glowing and (ii) in the situation when two from section A and one from section B are glowing will be:
questions.explanation:
(i) When all bulbs are glowing, then,
Here, $$ \mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{eq}}=\frac{\mathrm{R}}{3}+\frac{\mathrm{R}}{3}=\frac{2\mathrm{R}}{3} $$ $$ \because $$ $$ \mathrm{P}_{i}=\frac{\mathrm{E}^{2}}{\mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{eq}}}=\frac{3\mathrm{E}^{2}}{2\mathrm{R}} $$ (ii) Two from section A and one from section B are glowing,
Then $$ \begin{array}{l}\mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{eq}}=\frac{\mathrm{R}}{2}+\mathrm{R}\\=\frac{3\mathrm{R}}{2}\end{array} $$ $$ \therefore\text{Power,}P_{f}=\frac{2E^{2}}{3R} $$ ∴ From (i) and (ii), we get $$ \frac{\mathrm{P}_{i}}{\mathrm{P}_{f}}=\frac{3\mathrm{E}^{2}.3\mathrm{R}}{2\mathrm{R}.2\mathrm{E}^{2}}=9:4. $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: The internal resistance of 2.1 V cell which gives a current of 0.2 A through a resistance of 10 Ω is:
questions.explanation: The given situation can be represented as follows: $$ \begin{array}{c}0.2\mathrm{~A}\\2.1\mathrm{~V}\\\mathrm{I}=\frac{\mathrm{E}}{\mathrm{r}+\mathrm{R}}\\0.2=\frac{2.1}{\mathrm{r}+10}\\\mathrm{r}=0.5\Omega\end{array} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: For a cell, terminal potential difference is 2.2 V when circuit is open. If it reduces to 1.8 V when the cell is connected to a resistance of 5 Ω, the internal resistance of cell (r) is then:
questions.explanation: Terminal potential difference, $$ \mathbf{V}=\mathbf{E}-\mathbf{I}r $$ $$ \bullet \quad \bullet\ast $$ $$ \mathbf{V}=\mathbf{E}-\left[\frac{\mathbf{E}}{\mathbf{R}+r}\right]r=\frac{\mathbf{E}\mathbf{R}}{\mathbf{R}+r} $$ From given condition, $$ \mathrm{E}=2.2\mathrm{V} $$ and where, R = 5 Ω then potential, V = 1.8 V Therefore, $$ 1.8=\frac{2.2\times5}{5+r} $$ $$ r=\frac{10}{9}\Omega $$

questions.question: The magnitude and direction of the current in the following circuit is:
questions.optionA: $ \frac{5}{9} $ A from A to B through E
questions.optionB: 1.5 A from B to A through E
questions.optionC: 0.2 A from B to A through E
questions.optionD: 0.5 A from A to B through E
questions.explanation: Net emf of the circuit is, $$ \mathrm{E}=10-5=5\mathrm{~V} $$ New resistance, $ R = 1 + 2 + 7 = 10\ \Omega $ Therefore, current, $ I = \frac{E}{R} $ When $n$ equal resistors of resistance $R$ connected in series, then Putting value of E and R in eqn. (i), we get current, Current will be directed from A to B through E.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.subtopic: Cells in Series and in Parallel
questions.question: A set of 'n' equal resistor, of value 'R' each, are connected in series to a battery of emf 'E' and internal resistance 'R'. The current drawn is I. Now, the 'n' resistors are connected in parallel to the same battery. Then, the current drawn from battery becomes 10 I. The value of 'n' is:
questions.explanation:
According to question, $$ I=\frac{5}{10}=0.5\ A $$ $$ \mathrm{I}=\frac{\mathrm{E}}{n\mathrm{R}+r} $$ Current, where, $r = \text{internal resistance of battery}$ $nR = \text{equivalent resistance of } n$ resistors in series In question, $$ r=R $$ $$ \therefore $$ $$ I=\frac{E}{R(n+1)} $$ Similarly, when $n$ equal resistors of resistance $R$ are connected in parallel, then Current, $$ \Gamma=\frac{\mathrm{E}}{\frac{\mathbf{R}}{n}+\mathbf{R}} $$ where, $ \frac{R}{n} $ = equivalent resistance of n resistors in parallel It is given that, $$ 10I=\frac{E}{\frac{R}{n}+R}=\frac{nE}{(n+1)R} $$ From (ii) and (iii), we get $$ \begin{array}{c}10\left(\frac{\mathrm{E}}{\mathrm{R}(n+1)}\right)=\frac{n\mathrm{E}}{\mathrm{R}(n+1)}\\\Rightarrow\quad n=10\end{array} $$
##### Related Theory From given question, $$ I=\frac{E}{nR+R} $$ $$ 10I=\frac{R}{\frac{R}{n}+R} $$ Divide (ii) by (i), we get $$ 10=\frac{(n+1)R}{\left(\frac{1}{n}+1\right)R} $$ $$ n=10 $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.subtopic: Cells in Series and in Parallel
questions.question: Two cells, having the same emf are connected in series through an external resistance R. Cells have internal resistances $ r_1 $ and $ r_2 $ ( $ r_1 > r_2 $) respectively. When the circuit is closed, the potential difference across the first cell is zero. The value of R is:
questions.explanation: According to the question, $$ \mathrm{E}-\mathrm{Ir}_{1}=0\mathrm{~and~I}=\frac{\mathrm{E}+\mathrm{E}}{r_{1}+r_{2}+\mathrm{R}} $$ $$ \begin{aligned}\frac{\mathrm{E}}{r_{1}}&=\frac{2\mathrm{E}}{r_{1}+r_{2}+\mathrm{R}}\\\therefore\quad r_{1}+r_{2}+\mathrm{R}&=2r_{1}\\\Rightarrow\quad\mathrm{R}&=r_{1}-r_{2}\end{aligned} $$

questions.question: Two batteries, one of emf 18 volts and internal resistance 2 Ω and the other of emf 12 volts and internal resistance 1 Ω, are connected as shown. The voltmeter V will record a reading of:

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: In the circuit shown, the cells A and B have negligible resistances. For $ V_{A}=12\ V $, $ R_{1}=500\ \Omega $ and $ R=100\ \Omega $, the galvanometer (G) shows no deflection. The value of $ V_{B} $ is:

The situation is shown in the given figure. then we use Kirchhoff's law
$$ 500\mathrm{I}+100\mathrm{I}=12 $$ $$ I=\frac{12\times10^{-2}}{6} $$ $$ =2\times10^{-2}A $$ $$ V_{B}=100\left(2\times10^{-2}\right)=2V $$

questions.optionA: conservation of energy and electric charge respectively.
questions.optionC: conservation of electric charge and energy respectively.
questions.explanation: Kirchhoff's first and second law of electrical circuits are consequence of conservation of charge and energy respectively.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Kirchhoff's first law of electricity follows:
questions.optionA: only law of conservation of energy
questions.optionC: law of conservation of both energy and charge
questions.optionD: sometimes law of conservation of energy and some other times law of conservation of charge
questions.explanation: Kirchhoff's first law applies to currents at a junction in a circuit. It states that at a junction in an electrical circuit, the sum of currents flowing into the junction is equal to the sum of currents flowing out of the junction. Kirchhoff's first law is in favour of the law of charge conservation. This is due to the fact that a point in a circuit cannot be both a source and a sink of charge.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A wire carrying a current I along the positive x-axis has length L. It is kept in a magnetic field $ \overrightarrow{B}=(2\hat{i}+3\hat{j}-4\hat{k}) $ T. The magnitude of the magnetic force acting on the wire is:
questions.explanation: Here: $$ \begin{aligned}&\mathrm{Here}:\overline{\mathbf{B}}=(2\hat{i}+3\hat{j}-4\hat{k})\mathrm{T}\\&\quad\overline{\mathbf{F}}=\overrightarrow{\mathrm{IL}}\times\overline{\mathbf{B}}\\&\quad\overline{\mathbf{F}}=\mathrm{I}\hat{i}\mathrm{~L}\times(2\hat{i}+3\hat{j}-4\hat{k})\\&\quad\overline{\mathbf{F}}=3\mathrm{IL}\hat{k}+4\mathrm{IL}\hat{j}\\&\quad|\overline{\mathbf{F}}|=\sqrt{(3\mathrm{IL})^{2}+(4\mathrm{IL})^{2}}\\&\quad|\overline{\mathbf{F}}|=5\mathrm{IL}\mathrm{T e s l a}\end{aligned} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: An infinitely long straight conductor carries a current of 5 A as shown. An electron is moving with a speed of 10^{5} m/s parallel to the conductor. The perpendicular distance between the electron and the conductor is 20 cm at an instant. Calculate the magnitude of the force experienced by the electron at that instant. (Electron, $ v = 10^{5} $ m/s)
questions.explanation: Given, $$ \mathbf{v}_{e}=10^{5}\,\mathrm{m/s} $$ $$ r=20\ cm=0.2\ m $$ We know that due to magnetic field generated by the wire, the electron will experience a force, $$ \overset{\rightarrow}{\mathbf{F}}=\boldsymbol{q}\begin{pmatrix}\rightarrow&\rightarrow\\ \mathbf{v}\times\overrightarrow{\mathbf{B}}\end{pmatrix} $$ Since, the angle is $ 90^{\circ} $. Hence, $$ \begin{aligned}&\mathrm{F}=\mathrm{qv_{e}B}\\&\mathrm{B}=\frac{\mu_{0}\mathrm{I}}{2\pi\mathrm{r}}=2\ \frac{\mu_{0}}{4\pi}\frac{\mathrm{I}}{\mathrm{r}}\\&\quad=2\times10^{-7}\times\frac{5}{0.2}\\\mathrm{B}&=\frac{2\times50}{2}\times10^{-7}\\&\quad=5\times10^{-6}\end{aligned} $$ From eqn (i), we get $$ \begin{aligned}F&=1.6\times10^{-19}\times10^{5}\times5\times10^{-6}\\&=8\times10^{-20}N\end{aligned} $$

questions.question: A metallic rod of mass per unit length 0.5 kg m^{-1} is lying horizontally on a smooth inclined plane which makes an angle of $ 30^{\circ} $ with the horizontal. The rod is not allowed to slide down by flowing a current through it when a magnetic field of induction 0.25 T is acting on it in the vertical direction. The current flowing in the rod to keep it stationary is:
questions.explanation:
According to the question, For equilibrium,
mg sin 30^{\circ} = ILB cos 30^{\circ} I = $ \frac{mg}{LB} $ tan $ 30^{\circ} $ = $ \frac{0.5\times9.8}{0.25\times\sqrt{3}} $ $ \{\because B=0.25T\} $ = 11.32 A

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: When a proton is released from rest in a room, it starts with an initial acceleration $ a_{0} $ towards west. When it is projected towards north with a speed $ v_{0} $, it moves with an initial acceleration $ 3a_{0} $ towards west. The electric and magnetic field in the room are:
questions.explanation: Acceleration of charge particle, $$ \vec{a}=\frac{\dot{q}}{m}\left(\vec{E}+\vec{v}\times\vec{B}\right) $$ Released from rest $$ ↳\Downarrow $$ $$ a=\frac{qE}{m}(west) $$ $$ \mathrm{E}=\frac{m a_{0}}{e}\text{(west)} $$ When it is projected towards north, acceleration due to magnetic force = $ 2a_{0} $ \therefore Magnetic field =\frac{2ma_{0}}{ev_{0}} (down)

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A long straight wire carries a certain current and produces a magnetic field $ 2 \times 10^{-4} \frac{\text{Weber}}{\text{m}^{2}} $ at a perpendicular distance of 5 cm from the wire. An electron situated at 5 cm from the wire moves with a velocity $ 10^{7} \, \text{m/s} $ towards the wire along perpendicular to it. The force experienced by the electron will be (charge on electron $ 1.6 \times 10^{-19} \, \text{C} $):
questions.explanation:
From the question, Velocity of electron, $ v = 10^{7} $ m/s Magnetic field, $$ \mathbf{B}=2\times10^{-4}\mathbf{Wb}/\mathbf{m}^{2} $$
Then magnitude of force experienced by the electron, i.e. $ F = \text{ev} B \sin\theta $ $$ \begin{aligned}\{\because\mathbf{v}and\mathbf{B}are~perpendicular~to~each~other\}\\ =ev\mathbf{B}\sin90^{\circ}\\ =1.6\times10^{-19}\times10^{7}\times2\times10^{-4}\times1\\ =3.2\times10^{-16}N\end{aligned} $$

questions.question: A uniform electric field and a uniform magnetic field are acting along the same direction in a certain region. If an electron is projected in the region such that its velocity is pointed along the direction of fields, then the electron:

questions.optionD: with turn towards right of direction of motion
questions.explanation: According to the question, it is clear that field B is not applying any force. Field E will apply a force opposite to velocity of the electron and its speed will decrease.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: The magnetic force acting on a charged particle of charge -2 μC in a magnetic field of 2 T acting in y-direction, when the particle velocity is $(2\hat{i}+2\hat{j})\times10^{6}~\mathrm{m s}^{-1}$ is:
questions.explanation: Magnetic Lorentz force, $$ \begin{array}{l}\vec{\mathrm{F}}=q(\stackrel{\rightarrow}{\mathrm{v}}\times\stackrel{\rightarrow}{\mathrm{B}})\\\vec{\mathrm{F}}=-2\times10^{-6}(2\hat{i}+3\hat{j})\times2\hat{j}\times10^{6}\\\vec{\mathrm{F}}=-2\times10^{-6}(2\hat{i}\times2\hat{j})\times10^{6}\\\vec{\mathrm{F}}=-8\hat{k}\mathrm{~N}\end{array} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: When a charged particle moving with velocity v is subjected to a magnetic field of induction B, the force on it is non-zero. This implies that:
questions.optionA: angle between is either zero or $180^{\circ}$.
questions.optionC: angle between can have any value other than $90^{\circ}$.
questions.optionD: angle between can have any value other than zero and $180^{\circ}$.
questions.explanation: $$ \begin{array}{l}\vec{\mathrm{F}}=q(\stackrel{\rightarrow}{\mathrm{v}}\times\stackrel{\rightarrow}{\mathrm{B}})\\\\=q\,\mathrm{v}\,\mathrm{B}\sin\theta\;\\\mathrm{n}\,\theta=0^{\circ}\,\mathrm{o r}\,\theta=180^{\circ}\end{array} $$ Therefore, when $$ \mathbf{F}=\mathbf{0} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A very long straight wire carries a current I. At the instant when a charge + Q at point P has velocity $ \overrightarrow{v} $, as shown, the force on the charge is:
questions.explanation: According to Fleming's left hand rule, the direction of force is along y-axis which is perpendicular to wire, $$ \overset{\rightarrow}{\mathbf{F}}=q\overset{\rightarrow}{(\mathbf{v}\times\overset{\rightarrow}{\mathbf{B}})} $$ B due to I acting inwards is into the paper, v is along or: $$ \mathbf{F}=\boldsymbol{q}\left|\begin{array}{l l l}{\hat{i}}&{\hat{j}}&{\hat{k}}\\ {\mathbf{v}}&{0}&{0}\\ {0}&{0}&{-\mathbf{B}}\end{array}\right| $$ $$ \mathbf{F}=q[-\hat{j}(-\mathbf{v}\mathbf{B})+0] $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A charge q' moves in a region where the electric field and the magnetic field both exist, then the force on it is:
questions.explanation: Lorentz force, $ \vec{F}_v = \vec{F}_e + \vec{F}_m $ $ = q\vec{E} + q(\vec{v} \times \vec{B}) $

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Tesla is the unit of:
questions.explanation: SI unit of magnetic induction is tesla (T). If a charged particle of one coulomb moving at right angle to a magnetic field with a velocity of 1 ms $ ^{-1} $ experiences a force of 1 N at that point, the magnetic field induction is said to be one tesla. $$ 1\mathrm{~T}=1\mathrm{~N A}^{-1}\mathrm{~m}^{-1} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Ionised hydrogen atoms and $\alpha$-particles with same momenta enters perpendicular to a constant magnetic field, B. The ratio of their radii of their paths $r_{H}:r_{\alpha}$ will be:
questions.explanation: From question: $$ q_{\mathrm{H}}=e\mathrm{and}q_{\alpha}=2e $$ For circular motion, $$ \begin{array}{c}\frac{mv^{2}}{r}=Bqv\\r=\frac{mv}{Bq}\end{array} $$ $$ r\propto\frac{1}{q} $$ $$ \frac{r_{\mathrm{H}}}{r_{a}}=\frac{q_{a}}{q_{\mathrm{H}}}=\frac{2e}{e}=\frac{2}{1}=2:1 $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: An electron is moving in a circular path under the influence of a transverse magnetic field of $ 3.57 \times 10^{-2} $ T. If the value of $ \frac{e}{m} $ is $ 1.76 \times 10^{11} $ C/kg, the frequency of revolution of the electron is:
questions.explanation: Radius of charged particle in magnetic field is, $$ r=\frac{m\mathbf{v}}{q\mathbf{B}} $$ And time taken to complete the circle, $$ \begin{aligned}T&=\frac{2\pi r}{v}\\\frac{T}{2\pi}&=\frac{m}{qB}\\\omega&=\frac{2\pi}{T}=\frac{qB}{m}\end{aligned} $$ {using (i)} Here, $ q = e $, $ \frac{e}{m} = 1.76 \times 10^{11} $ C/kg and $$ \frac{2\pi}{T}=\frac{e\mathbf{B}}{m} $$ $$ \begin{aligned}f&=\frac{1}{2\pi}\frac{e}{m}\mathbf{B}\\&=\frac{1}{2\pi}\times1.76\times10^{11}\times3.57\times10^{-2}\\&=1.0\times10^{9}\mathrm{Hz}\\&=1\mathrm{GHz}\\ \end{aligned} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A proton and an alpha particle both enter a region of uniform magnetic field B, moving at right angles to the field B. If the radius of circular orbits for both the particles is equal and the kinetic energy acquired by proton is 1 MeV, the energy acquired by the alpha particle will be:
questions.explanation: We know that radius in magnetic field of circular orbit is R $$ \mathrm{R}=\frac{m\mathrm{v}}{q\mathrm{B}} $$ In circular orbit, total energy of moving particle, $$ \mathrm{E}=\frac{q^{2}\mathrm{B}^{2}\mathrm{R}^{2}}{2m} $$ When proton enter in the region of magnetic field then, $$ E_{p}=\frac{e^{2}\times B^{2}\times R^{2}}{2m_{p}} $$ where, $ m_{P} = \text{mass of proton} $ Again when $ \alpha $-particle enter in the region of magnetic field then, $$ \mathrm{E}_{\alpha}=\frac{(2e)^{2}\mathrm{B}^{2}\mathrm{R}^{2}}{2(4m_{\mathrm{p}})}\qquad\ldots(\mathrm{ii})\\ {\{\because\mathrm{since}m_{\alpha}=4m_{p}(\mathrm{given})\}} $$ Now from eq. (i) and (ii), we get $$ \begin{array}{r l r}&{\frac{\mathrm{E}_{\alpha}}{\mathrm{E_{P}}}=\frac{(2e)^{2}\times\mathrm{B}^{2}\times\mathrm{R}^{2}}{2\times4m_{\mathrm{P}}}\times\frac{2\times m_{\mathrm{P}}}{e^{2}\times\mathrm{B}^{2}\times\mathrm{R}^{2}}}\\ {\Rightarrow\quad}&{\frac{\mathrm{E}_{\alpha}}{\mathrm{E_{P}}}=1}\\ {\Rightarrow\quad}&{\mathrm{E}_{\alpha}=\mathrm{E_{P}}=1\mathrm{M e V}}\end{array} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: An electron moving in a circular orbit of radius r makes n rotations per second. The magnetic field produced at the centre has magnitude:
questions.explanation: We know that, $$ I=\frac{q}{t} $$ If an electron is moving in a circular orbit of radius r then $$ \begin{aligned}I&=\frac{e}{T}=\frac{e}{\frac{2\pi}{\omega}}\\&=\frac{\omega e}{2\pi}=\frac{2\pi n e}{2\pi}=n e\end{aligned} $$ ∴ Magnetic field produced at the centre is $$ \mathrm{B}=\mathrm{\frac{\mu_{0}I}{2R}}=\frac{\mu_{0}ne}{2r} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A particle having a mass of 10 kg carries a velocity of $ 5 \times 10^{-8} $ C. The particle is given an initial horizontal velocity of $ 10^{5} $ m s $ ^{-1} $ in the presence of electrical field E and magnetic field B. To keep the particle moving in a horizontal direction, it is necessary that: (I) \vec{\mathrm{B}} should be perpendicular to the direction of velocity, \vec{\mathrm{E}} should be along the direction of velocity. (II) Both \vec{\mathrm{B}} and \vec{\mathrm{E}} should be along the direction of velocity. (III) Both \vec{\mathrm{B}} and \vec{\mathrm{E}} are mutually perpendicular and perpendicular to the direction of velocity. (IV) \vec{\mathrm{B}} should be along the direction of velocity and \vec{\mathrm{E}} should be perpendicular to the direction of velocity. Which one of the following pairs of statements is possible?
questions.explanation: The Lorentz force acting on a charged particle is given by, $$ \overrightarrow{\mathrm{F}}=q(\overrightarrow{\mathrm{E}}+\overrightarrow{\mathrm{v}}\times\overrightarrow{\mathrm{B}}) $$ When $ \vec{B} \parallel \vec{E} \parallel \vec{v} $ $$ \begin{aligned}\overrightarrow{v}\times\overrightarrow{B}&=0\\F&=q\overrightarrow{E}\end{aligned} $$ Therefore, the particle will be moving in a horizontal direction. There may be another possibility when both $ \bar{B} $ and $ \bar{E} $ are mutually perpendicular and perpendicular to the velocity direction, the particle moves without any deflection.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A beam of cathode rays is subjected to crossed electric field (E) and magnetic field (B). The fields are adjusted such that the beam is not deflected. The specific charge of the cathode rays is given by:
questions.explanation: According to the law of conservation of energy, $$ \begin{array}{c}e\mathbf{V}=\frac{1}{2}m\mathbf{v}^{2}\\\mathbf{v}=\sqrt{\frac{2e\mathbf{V}}{m}}\end{array} $$ The condition is, electron beam is not deflected then $$ \begin{aligned}\mathbf{F}_{m}&=\mathbf{F}_{e}\\\mathbf{B}_{e}\mathbf{v}&=\mathbf{E}_{e}\\\mathbf{v}&=\frac{\mathbf{E}}{\mathbf{B}}\end{aligned} $$ From eq. (i) and (ii), we get $$ \mathrm{\frac{E}{B}}=\sqrt{\frac{2\;e V}{m}} $$ $$ \frac{E^{2}}{B^{2}}=\frac{2eV}{m} $$ $$ \frac{e}{m}=\frac{E^{2}}{2VB^{2}} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Under the influence of a uniform magnetic field, a charged particle moves with constant speed v in a circle of radius R. The time period of rotation of the particle:
questions.optionA: depends on v and not on R
questions.optionB: depends on R and not on v
questions.optionC: is independent of both v and R
questions.optionD: depends on both v and R
questions.explanation:
Time period of circular motion of charged particle is, $$ \mathrm{T}=\frac{2\pi r}{\mathrm{v}}=\frac{2\pi}{\mathrm{v}}\times\frac{m\mathrm{v}}{\mathrm{B}q} $$ $$ \mathrm{T}=\frac{2\pi m}{\mathrm{B}q} $$ which is independent of both v and R.
#### Related Theory If a charged particle is moving perpendicular to the direction of $ \overrightarrow{B} $, it experiences maximum force, which is perpendicular to $ \overrightarrow{B} $ as well as $ \overrightarrow{v} $.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A particle mass m, charge Q and kinetic energy T enters a transverse uniform magnetic field of induction \vec{\mathrm{B}}. After 3\mathrm{s}, the kinetic energy of the particle will be:
questions.explanation: After passing through a magnetic field, the magnitude of its mass and velocity of the particle remain same. So its energy does not change, i.e., K.E. will remain T.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A beam of electron passes undeflected through mutually perpendicular electric and magnetic fields. If the electric field is switched off, and the same magnetic field is maintained, the electrons move:
questions.explanation: In a magnetic field, a charged particle moves in a circular orbit.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: In a mass spectrometer for measuring the masses of ions, the ions are initially accelerated by an electric potential V and then made to describe semicircular paths of radius R using a magnetic field B. If V and B are kept constant, the ratio $ \left(\frac{\text{charge on the ion}}{\text{mass of the ion}}\right) $ will be proportional to:
questions.explanation: Consider the mass and charge of the ion be m and q. Energy of the ion is increased due to the electric field. $$ \begin{aligned}q V&=\frac{1}{2}m v^{2}\\\mathbf{v}&=\sqrt{\frac{q}{m}}\sqrt{2\mathbf{V}}\end{aligned} $$ The magnetic field provides centripetal force for motion in circle: $$ \begin{array}{c}\Rightarrow\\\hline\end{array}\frac{mv^{2}}{R}=qvB\\\Rightarrow\begin{array}{c}v=\frac{qBR}{m}\\\end{array}\\\Rightarrow\begin{array}{c}\frac{1}{BR}\sqrt{2V}=\sqrt{\frac{q}{m}}\end{array} $$ $$ \begin{array}{l} \Rightarrow \quad \frac{q}{m}=\frac{2\mathrm{V}}{\mathrm{B}^{2}\mathrm{R}^{2}} \\ \Rightarrow \quad \frac{q}{m}\propto\frac{1}{\mathrm{R}^{2}} \end{array} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A charged particle moves through a magnetic field in a direction perpendicular to it. Then the:
questions.optionA: speed of the particle remains unchanged
questions.optionB: direction of the particle remains unchanged
questions.explanation: A charged particle moves through a magnetic field in a direction perpendicular to it. Then the speed of the particle remains unchanged.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A charged particle of charge $q$ and mass $m$ enters perpendicularly in a magnetic field B. Kinetic energy of the particle is E, then frequency of rotation is:
questions.explanation:
We have, Magnetic force = Centripetal force $$ i.e $$ $$ qvB=\frac{mv^{2}}{r} $$ or $$ qv\mathbf{B}=\mathbf{m}\boldsymbol{r}\boldsymbol{\omega}^{2} $$ or $$ \omega^{2}=\frac{qvB}{mr}=\frac{q(r\omega)B}{mr} $$ Angular frequency, $\omega = \frac{qB}{m}$ If v is the frequency of rotation, then $$ \omega=2\pi\mathbf{v} $$ $$ \mathbf{v}=\frac{\omega}{2\pi} $$ $$ \mathrm{v}=\frac{\mathrm{qB}}{2\pi\mathrm{m}} $$
##### Related Theory Since, the resultant expression $ \frac{q}{m} $ is known as specific charge. It is sometimes denoted by $ \alpha $. So, $ \omega = B\alpha $ and $ \nu = \frac{B\alpha}{2\pi} $

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Current i is flowing in a coil of area A and number of turns N, then magnetic moment of the coil, M is equal to:
questions.explanation: If a coil contains N turns, i is the current running through it and A is the coil's area, then magnetic dipole moment or simply the magnetic moment of the coil, $$ M=NiA $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A 10 eV electron is circulating in a plane at right angle to a uniform field of magnetic induction $10^{-4} \text{Wb/m}^{2}$ (=1.0 Gauss). The orbital radius of the electron is:
questions.explanation:
A maximum force acting perpendicular to the direction of B as well as v is experienced by a charged particle moving perpendicular to the direction of B. As a result of this force, the charged particle will travel a circular path in the magnetic field of radius r, which is given by, $$ \frac{mv^{2}}{r}=qvB $$ Now, KE of electron = 10 eV $$ \begin{aligned}&\Rightarrow\quad\frac{1}{2}mv^{2}=10eV\\&\therefore\frac{1}{2}\times(9.1\times10^{-31})v^{2}=10\times1.6\times10^{-19}\\&\Rightarrow\quad v^{2}=\frac{2\times10\times1.6\times10^{-19}}{9.1\times10^{-31}}\\&\Rightarrow\quad v^{2}=3.52\times10^{12}\\&or\quad v=1.88\times10^{6}m/s\end{aligned} $$ Now radius of circular path, $$ \begin{array}{l}r=\frac{m\mathrm{v}}{q\mathrm{B}}\\ =\frac{9.1\times10^{-31}\times1.88\times10^{6}}{1.6\times10^{-19}\times10^{-4}}\\ =11\mathrm{~cm}\end{array} $$
##### Related Theory When a charged particle is projected perpendicular to the magnetic field, its path is circular in a plane perpendicular to the plane of magnetic field and direction of motion of the charged particle.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A uniform magnetic field acts at right angle to the direction of motion of electron. As a result, the electron moves in a circular path of radius 2 cm. If the speed of electrons is doubled, then the radius of the circular path will be:
questions.explanation: The centripetal force $ \left(F=\frac{mv^{2}}{r}\right) $ required for motion along a circular path of radius r is generated by the external magnetic field's force F on the charged particle. So $$ \begin{array}{c} qv\mathbf{B} = \displaystyle \frac{mv^{2}}{r} \\ r = \frac{mv}{q\mathbf{B}} \\ r \propto \mathbf{v} \end{array} $$ or $$ \left[\frac{m}{q\mathrm{B}}=\mathrm{constant}\right] $$ When, v is doubled, the radius is also doubled, Hence, radius, $ r = 2 \times 2 = 4 $ cm.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.subtopic: Magnetic Field due to a Current Element, Biot-Savart Law
questions.question: Given below are two statements: Statement I: Biot-Savart's law gives us the expression for the magnetic field strength of an infinitesimal current element (Idl) of a current-carrying conductor only. Statement II: Biot-Savart's law is analogous to Coulomb's inverse square law of charge q, with the former being related to the field produced by a scalar source, Id/while the latter being produced by a vector source, q. In light of above statements choose the most appropriate answer from the options given below:
questions.optionA: Both Statement I and Statement II are correct.
questions.optionB: Both Statement I and Statement II are incorrect.
questions.optionC: Statement I is correct and Statement II is incorrect.
questions.optionD: Statement I is incorrect and Statement II is correct.
questions.explanation: According to Biot - Savart's law, $$ \overrightarrow{\mathrm{dB}}=\frac{\mu_{0}\begin{pmatrix}\rightarrow&\rightarrow\\ \mathrm{Idl}\times\mathrm{r}\end{pmatrix}}{4\pi r^{3}} $$ Here, current element Idl is a vector quantity and the coulomb's force is produced by scalar source $q$. Hence statement - I is correct, but statement - II is incorrect.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.subtopic: Magnetic Field on the Axis of a Circular Current Loop
questions.question: A tightly wound 100 turns coil of radius 10 cm carries a current of 7 A. The magnitude of the magnetic field at the centre of the coil is (Take permeability of free space as $ 4\pi \times 10^{-7} $ SI units):
questions.explanation: Number of turns in coil N = 100 Radius of coil r = 0.1 m Current flowing in coil I = 7 A Magnetic field at the centre of coil B = $ \frac{\mu_{0}I N}{2r} $ $$ \begin{aligned}\mathrm{B}&=\frac{4\pi\times10^{-7}\times7\times100}{2\times0.1}\\&=43.96\times10^{-4}\mathrm{~T}\approx4.4\mathrm{~mT}\end{aligned} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.subtopic: Magnetic Field on the Axis of a Circular Current Loop
questions.question: A very long conducting wire is bent in a semi-circular shape from A to B as shown in figure. The magnetic field at point P for steady current configuration is given by:
questions.optionD: $ \frac{\mu_0 i}{4R} $ pointed away from the page
questions.explanation: As, $ \mathrm{B} = \frac{\mu_0 i}{4 \pi R} (\pi) - \frac{\mu_0}{4 \pi} \frac{2 i}{R} $ $ = \frac{\mu_0 i}{4 R} \left[ 1 - \frac{2}{\pi} \right] $ outward

questions.subject: Physics
questions.subtopic: Magnetic Field on the Axis of a Circular Current Loop
questions.question: A long wire carrying a steady current, is bent into a circular loop of one turn. The magnetic field at the centre of the loop is B. It is then bent into a circular coil of n turns. The magnetic field at the centre of the coil of n turns will be:
questions.explanation: Since, $ l = 2\pi R = n(2\pi r) $ $ \Rightarrow r = \frac{R}{n} $ for one turn, $ B = \frac{\mu_0 I}{2R} $ $$ \begin{array}{l} \text{for } n \text{ turn,} \\ \qquad \Rightarrow \end{array} $$ $$ \begin{aligned}&\mathbf{B}^{\prime}=\frac{\mu_{0}n\mathbf{I}}{2r}\\&\mathbf{B}^{\prime}=\frac{\mu_{0}n^{2}\mathbf{I}}{2\mathbf{R}}=n^{2}\left(\frac{\mu_{0}\mathbf{I}}{2\mathbf{R}}\right)\\&\mathbf{B}^{\prime}=n^{2}\mathbf{B}\\ \end{aligned} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.subtopic: Magnetic Field on the Axis of a Circular Current Loop
questions.question: A wire carrying current I has the shape as shown in adjoining figure. Linear parts of the wire are very long and parallel to X-axis while semicircular portion of radius R is lying in Y-Z plane. Magnetic field at point O is:
questions.explanation: Magnetic field due to wire parallel to the X-axis is, $$ \vec{B}_{I}=\frac{\mu_{0}}{4\pi}\frac{I}{R}(-\hat{k}) $$ Magnetic field due to another wire parallel to x-axis is, $ \vec{B}_{\mathrm{III}} = \frac{\mu_{0}}{4\pi} \frac{\mathrm{I}}{\mathrm{R}} (-\hat{k}) $ Magnetic field due to half semi-circular wire is, $$ \stackrel{\rightarrow}{\mathrm{\mathbf{B}_{II}}}=-\frac{\mu_{0}\mathrm{\mathbf{I}}}{4\mathrm{R}}\stackrel{\wedge}{(i)} $$ ∴ Magnetic field at the centre of wire is, $$ \overset{\rightarrow}{\mathbf{B}}=\overset{\rightarrow}{\mathbf{B}}_{\mathrm{I}}+\overset{\longrightarrow}{\mathbf{B}}_{\mathrm{II}}+\overset{\longrightarrow}{\mathbf{B}}_{\mathrm{III}} $$ $$ \vec{\mathrm{B}}_{\mathrm{c}}=-\frac{\mu_{0}\mathrm{I}}{4\mathrm{R}}\left(\stackrel{\wedge}{i}+\frac{2\stackrel{\wedge}{k}}{\pi}\right)=-\frac{\mu_{0}\mathrm{I}}{4\pi\mathrm{R}}(\stackrel{\wedge}{p}\stackrel{\wedge}{i}+2\stackrel{\wedge}{k}) $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.subtopic: Magnetic Field on the Axis of a Circular Current Loop
questions.question: A current loop consists of two identical semicircular parts each of radius R, one lying in the x-y plane and the other in x-z plane. If the current in the loop is i, the resultant magnetic field due to the two semicircular parts at their common centre is:
questions.explanation: Radius of semi-circular part = R Then magnetic field, $$ \mathbf{B}_{1}=\mathbf{B}_{2}=\frac{\mu_{0}i}{4R} $$ Then magnetic field at their common centre will be: $$ \begin{array}{l}\vec{\mathbf{B}}=\vec{\mathbf{B}}_{1}+\vec{\mathbf{B}}_{2}\\|\vec{\mathbf{B}}|=\sqrt{\left(\frac{\mu_{0}i}{4\mathbf{R}}\right)^{2}+\left(\frac{\mu_{0}i}{4\mathbf{R}}\right)^{2}}\\\mathbf{B}=\frac{\mu_{0}i}{2\sqrt{2}\mathbf{R}}\end{array} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.subtopic: Magnetic Field on the Axis of a Circular Current Loop
questions.question: Two circular coils 1 and 2 are made from the same wire but the radius of the $1^{st}$ coil is twice that of the $2^{nd}$ coil. In what ratio potential difference should be applied across them so that the magnetic field at their centres is the same?
questions.explanation: Let $ r_{1} $ and $ r_{2} $ be the radius of coil 1 and coil 2. If $ B_{1} $ and $ B_{2} $ are magnetic induction at their centre, then $$ \begin{aligned}\mathrm{B}_{1}&=\frac{\mu_{0}\mathrm{I}_{1}}{2r_{1}}\text{and}\mathrm{B}_{2}=\frac{\mu_{0}\mathrm{I}_{2}}{2r_{2}}\\\mathrm{B}_{1}&=\mathrm{B}_{2}\text{and}r_{1}=2r_{2}\\\mathrm{I}_{1}&=2\mathrm{I}_{2}\end{aligned} $$ then Again, if $R_{1}$ and $R_{2}$ are resistance of coils 1 and 2, then $R_{1}=2R_{2}$ (as $R \propto$ length) and if $V_{1}$ and $V_{2}$ are the potential difference across them respectively, then $$ \begin{array}{l}\frac{\mathbf{V}_{1}}{\mathbf{V}_{2}}=\frac{\mathbf{I}_{1}\mathbf{R}_{1}}{\mathbf{I}_{2}\mathbf{R}_{2}}\\=\frac{(2\mathbf{I}_{2})(2\mathbf{R}_{2})}{\mathbf{I}_{2}\mathbf{R}_{2}}=4\end{array} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.subtopic: Magnetic Field on the Axis of a Circular Current Loop
questions.question: An electron moves in a circular orbit with a uniform speed v. It produces a magnetic field B at the centre of the circle. The radius of the circle is proportional to:
questions.explanation:
The magnetic field produced by moving electron in circular path,
$$ \mathbf{B}=\frac{\mu_{0}\mathbf{I}}{2r} $$ where, $$ \mathbf{I}=\frac{\boldsymbol{q}}{t}=\frac{\boldsymbol{q}}{2\pi r}\times\mathbf{v} $$ $$ \mathbf{B}=\frac{\mu_{0}q\mathbf{v}}{4\pi r^{2}} $$ $$ r\propto\sqrt{\frac{\mathbf{v}}{\mathbf{B}}} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.subtopic: Magnetic Field on the Axis of a Circular Current Loop
questions.question: The magnetic field of a given length of wire for a single turn coil at the centre is 'B', then its value for two turns coil for the same wire is:
questions.explanation: Here $$ \mathbf{B}_{1}=\mathbf{B}=\frac{\mu_{0}\mathbf{I}}{2\mathbf{R}} $$ $$ \mathbf{B}_{2}=\frac{\mu_{0}(2\mathbf{I})}{2r} $$ $$ 2\times2\pi r=2\pi R $$ $$ r=\frac{\mathtt{R}}{2} $$ Now, Putting the value of $r$ in eqn (ii), we get $$ \begin{aligned}\mathrm{B}_{2}&=\frac{\mu_{0}2\mathrm{I}}{\frac{2\mathrm{R}}{2}}\\&=\frac{4\mu_{0}\mathrm{I}}{2\mathrm{R}}\\&=4\mathrm{B}_{1}\end{aligned} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: From Ampere's circuital law for a long straight wire of circular cross-section carrying a steady current, the variation of magnetic field in inside and outside region of the wire is:
questions.optionA: uniform and remains constant for both the regions
questions.optionB: a linearly increasing function of distance upto the boundary of the wire and then linearly decreasing for the outside region
questions.optionC: a linearly increasing function of distance $r$ upto the boundary of the wire and then decreasing one with $\frac{1}{r}$ dependence for the outside
questions.optionD: a linearly decreasing function of distance up to the boundary of the wire and then a linearly increasing one for the outside region.
questions.explanation: According to Ampere's circuital law, $$ \mathrm{B}_{\mathrm{in}}=\frac{\mu_{0}\mathrm{Ir}}{2\pi a^{2}}\text{or}\mathrm{B}_{\mathrm{in}}\propto r $$ $$ \mathrm{B}_{\mathrm{out}}=\frac{\mu_{0}\mathrm{I}}{2\pi r}\text{or}\mathrm{B}_{\mathrm{out}}\propto\frac{1}{r} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A long straight wire of radius $a$ carries a steady current I. The current is uniformly distributed over its cross-section. The ratio of the magnetic field B and B' at radial distance $\frac{a}{2}$ and $2a$ respectively, from the axis of the wire is:
questions.explanation: Here, two Amperian loops of radius $ \frac{a}{2} $ and 2a are shown from Ampere's circuital law, $ \oint \mathbf{B} \cdot dl = \mu_0 I_{enclosed} $ ∴ for small loop, $$ \mathbf{B}\times2\pi\frac{a}{2}=\mu_{0}\times\frac{\mathbf{I}}{\pi a^{2}}\times\pi\left(\frac{a}{2}\right)^{2} $$ $$ \mathbf{B}=\frac{\mu_{0}\mathbf{I}}{4\pi a} $$ Similarly for bigger loop, $$ \mathbf{B}^{\prime}\times2\pi(2a)=\mu_{0}\mathbf{I} $$ ： $$ \mathbf{B}^{\prime}=\frac{\mu_{0}\mathbf{I}}{4\pi a} $$ $$ \ddot{\cdot}\ddot{\cdot} $$ $$ \frac{\mathbf{B}}{\mathbf{B}^{\prime}}=\frac{\mu_{0}\mathbf{I}}{4\pi a}\times\frac{4\pi a}{\mu_{0}\mathbf{I}}=1 $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Two identical long conducting wires AOB and COD are placed at right angle wires to each other with one above other such that O is their common point for the two. The wires carry I_{1} and I_{2} currents respectively. Point P is lying at distance d from O along a direction perpendicular to the plane containing the wires. The magnetic field at the point P will be :
questions.explanation:
According to the question, Two wires AOB and COD are placed at right angle. O is the common point. $I_{1}$ and $I_{2}$ are current flowing in the wires.
Point P is lying at distance d along the axis. So,
∴ $$ |\mathbf{B}_{1}|=\frac{\mu_{0}}{2\pi}\frac{\mathbf{I}_{1}}{d} $$ $$ |\mathbf{B}_{2}|=\frac{\mu_{0}}{2\pi}\frac{\mathbf{I}_{2}}{d} $$ $$ \begin{aligned}\mathbf{B_{net}}&=\sqrt{\mathbf{B}_{1}^{2}+\mathbf{B}_{2}^{2}}\\&=\sqrt{\left(\frac{\mu_{0}}{2\pi}\times\frac{\mathbf{I}_{1}}{d}\right)^{2}+\left(\frac{\mu_{0}}{2\pi}\times\frac{\mathbf{I}_{2}}{d}\right)^{2}}\\&=\frac{\mu_{0}}{2\pi}\frac{(\mathbf{I}_{1}^{2}+\mathbf{I}_{2}^{2})^{\frac{1}{2}}}{d}\\ \end{aligned} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: If a long hollow copper pipe carries a current, then magnetic field is produced:
questions.optionC: both inside and outside the pipe
questions.explanation: According to Ampere's circuital law. $$ \int\mathbf{B}.d l=\mu_{0}\mathbf{I}_{e n c l o s e d} $$ $$ \mathrm{so},\quad\mathbf{B}\left(2\pi r\right)=\mu_{0}\times0 $$ [lenclosed = 0] Since, there is no current inside the Amperian loop in a hollow metallic (copper) pipe, Hence, the magnetic field is zero. For external points, however, the entire current behaves as if it were concentrated along the axis. So, outside, $$ \mathbf{B}_{0}=\frac{\mu_{0}\mathbf{I}}{2\pi\mathbf{r}} $$ Thus, the magnetic field is only created outside the pipe.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A coil of one turn is made of a wire of certain length and then from the same length a coil of two turns is made. If the same current is passed in both the cases, then the ratio of the magnetic induction at their centres will be:
questions.explanation: Magnetic induction at the centre of a current-carrying coil with N turns is equal to, $$ \mathbf{B}=\frac{\mu_{0}\mathbf{N}\mathbf{I}}{2r} $$ Suppose, length of the wire be L. Case 1: For coil of one turn, let radius be $ r_{1} $. $$ \circlearrowleft $$ $$ \mathrm{L}=2\pi r_{1}\times\mathrm{N} $$ $$ or $$ $$ r_{1}=\frac{L}{2\pi\times N}=\frac{L}{2\pi} $$ $$ (\because\mathbf{N}=1) $$ Case 2: For coil of two turns, let radius be $ r_{2} $. $$ \bullet \quad \bullet\ast $$ $$ L=2\pi r_{2}\times N $$ or $$ \mathrm{r}_{2}=\frac{\mathrm{L}}{2\pi\times\mathrm{N}}=\frac{\mathrm{L}}{2\pi\times2}\quad(\because\mathrm{N}=2) $$ or $$ r_{2}=\frac{r_{1}}{2} $$ By comparing two different cases from eq. (i), we get, $$ \frac{\mathbf{B}_{1}}{\mathbf{B}_{2}}=\frac{\mathbf{N}_{1}}{r_{1}}\times\frac{r_{2}}{\mathbf{N}_{2}} $$ or $$ \frac{\mathbf{B}_{1}}{\mathbf{B}_{2}}=\frac{1\times\frac{r_{1}}{2}}{r_{1}\times2} $$ $$ \bullet \quad \bullet\bullet $$ $$ \frac{\mathbf{B}_{1}}{\mathbf{B}_{2}}=\frac{1}{4} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Two equal electric currents are flowing perpendicular to each other as shown in the figure. AB and CD are perpendicular to each other and symmetrically placed w.r.t the currents, where do we expect the resultant magnetic field to be zero?
questions.explanation: Using the right hand grip rule and considering AB, the magnetic field corresponding to one current is upwards and the magnetic field due to the other is downward. Both magnetic fields cancel each other out, and the resultant magnetic field is zero. When considering CD and applying the right hand grip rule to the two currents, the magnetic field direction is the same in both cases, resulting in a non-zero resultant.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A straight wire of diameter 0.5 mm carrying a current of 1 A is replaced by another wire of 1 mm diameter carrying same current. The strength of magnetic field far away is:
questions.explanation: Magnetic field due to straight wire, $$ \mathrm{B}=\frac{\mu_{0}}{4\pi}\frac{2\mathrm{I}}{r} $$ The magnetic field due to a current-carrying conductor does not depend on the diameter of the wire; it only depends on the distance of the wire from the point and the current in the wire, therefore the magnetic field for both wires remains the same.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A long solenoid of radius 1 mm has 100 turns per mm. If 1 A current flows in the solenoid, the magnetic field strength at the centre of the solenoid is:
questions.explanation: Given: $ I = 1 \, A $, $ n = \frac{100}{1 \, mm} = 100 \times 10^{3} \, m^{-1} $ Magnetic field strength at the centre of the solenoid, B = μ₀nI = 4π × 10⁻⁷ × 10⁵ × 1 = 12.56 × 10⁻² T

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A long solenoid of 50 cm length having 100 turns carries a current of 2.5 A. The magnetic field at the centre of solenoid is (Take, $ \mu_{0} = 4\pi \times 10^{-7} \text{ T m A}^{-1} $):
