questions.explanation: From the question, l = 50 cm = 0.5 m, N = 100 turns and I = 2.5 A ∴ Magnetic field at the centre of solenoid is $$ \begin{aligned}\mathbf{B}&=\mu_{0}n\mathbf{I}=\mu_{0}\left(\frac{\mathbf{N}}{l}\right)\mathbf{I}\\&=4\pi\times10^{-7}\times\frac{100}{0.5}\times2.5\\&=6.28\times10^{-4}\mathrm{~T}\end{aligned} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A long solenoid of diameter 0.1 m has $2\times10^{4}$ turns per metre. At the centre of the solenoid, a coil of 100 turns and radius 0.01 m is placed with its axis coinciding with the solenoid axis. The current in the solenoid reduces at a constant rate of 0 A from 4 A in 0.05 s. If the resistance of the coil is $10\ \pi^{2}\Omega$, the total charge flowing through the coil during this time is:
questions.explanation: From the question, Resistance of the solenoid, $ R = 10\pi^{2}\Omega $ Radius of second coil, $ r = 10^{-2} $ m $ \Delta t = 0.05 $ s, $ \Delta I = 4 - 0 = 4 $ A Then we know that, charge flowing through the coil is, $$ \begin{aligned}\Delta q&=\left(\frac{\Delta\phi}{\Delta t}\right)\frac{1}{\mathrm{R}}\times(\Delta t)&\left(\because\phi=\mathrm{MI}\right)\\ &=\mu_{0}\mathrm{N}_{1}\mathrm{N}_{2}\pi r^{2}\left(\frac{\Delta\mathrm{I}}{\Delta t}\right)\frac{1}{\mathrm{R}}\Delta t\\ &=4\pi\times10^{-7}\times2\times10^{4}\times100\times\pi\\ &\quad\times(10^{-2})^{2}\quad\times\left(\frac{4}{0.05}\right)\times\frac{1}{10\pi^{2}}\times0.05\\ &=32\times10^{-6}\mathrm{C}=32\mu\mathrm{C}\\ \end{aligned} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: An arrangement of three parallel straight wires placed perpendicular to plane of paper carrying same current I along the same direction is shown in figure. Magnitude of force per unit length on the middle wire B is given by:
questions.explanation:
Force between BC and AB will be same in magnitude.
$$ \mathrm{F}_{\mathrm{BC}}=\mathrm{F}_{\mathrm{BA}}=\frac{\mu_{0}\mathrm{I}^{2}}{2\pi d} $$ and resultant force on B, $$ \mathrm{F}=\sqrt{\mathrm{F}_{1}^{2}+\mathrm{F}_{2}^{2}} $$ $$ \mathrm{F}=\sqrt{2}\mathrm{F}_{\mathrm{BC}}=\sqrt{2}\frac{\mu_{0}\mathrm{I}^{2}}{2\pi d} $$ $$ \mathrm{F}=\frac{\mu_{0}\mathrm{I}^{2}}{\sqrt{2}\pi d} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A square loop ABCD carrying a current i, is placed near and coplanar with a straight conductor XY carrying a current I, the net force on the loop will be: $$ \frac{\mu_{0}I i}{2\pi} $$
questions.explanation: $$ \mathrm{F}_{\mathrm{A B}}=\mathrm{I}/\mathrm{B}\mathrm{(a t t r a c t i o n)} $$ $$ \mathrm{F}_{\mathrm{AB}}=\frac{\mu_{0}\mathrm{I}i(\mathrm{L})}{2\pi\left(\frac{\mathrm{L}}{2}\right)}\left(\because\mathrm{B}_{\mathrm{AB}}=\frac{\mu_{0}\mathrm{I}}{2\pi\left(\frac{\mathrm{L}}{2}\right)}\right) $$ $ F_{BC}(\uparrow) $ and $ F_{AD}(\downarrow) \Rightarrow $ Cancels each other, $$ \mathrm{F_{CD}=I/B~(r e p u l s i o n)} $$ $$ \mathrm{F}_{\mathrm{C D}}=\frac{\mu_{0}\mathrm{I}i(\mathrm{L})}{2\pi\left(3\mathrm{L}/2\right)}\quad\left(\because\mathrm{B}_{\mathrm{C D}}=\frac{\mu_{0}\mathrm{I}}{2\pi\left(\frac{3\mathrm{L}}{2}\right)}\right) $$ $$ \mathrm{F}_{\mathrm{net}}=\frac{\mu_{0}\mathrm{I}i\mathrm{L}}{2\pi}\left(\frac{1}{\left(\frac{\mathrm{L}}{2}\right)}-\frac{1}{\left(\frac{3\mathrm{L}}{2}\right)}\right)=\frac{2\mu_{0}\mathrm{I}i}{3\pi} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A square loop carrying a steady current I is placed in a horizontal plane near a long straight conductor carrying a steady current $ I_{1} $ at a distance d from the conductor as shown in figure. The loop will experience:
questions.optionA: a net repulsive force away from the conductor.
questions.optionB: a net torque acting upward perpendicular to the horizontal plane.
questions.optionC: a net torque acting downward normal to the horizontal plane.
questions.optionD: a net attractive force towards the conductor.
questions.explanation:
The situation is shown in the diagram,
The relation between $ F_{2} $ and $ F_{4} $ is, $$ \mathbf{F}_{2}=-\mathbf{F}_{4} $$ $$ \mathrm{F}_{1}=\frac{\upmu_{0}\mathbf{I}_{1}\mathbf{u}}{2\uppi d} $$ $$ \mathrm{F}_{3}=\frac{\mu_{0}\mathrm{I}_{1}\mathrm{I}}{2\pi(d+l)} $$ $$ \begin{array}{l}\Rightarrow\quad\mathrm{F}_{1}>\mathrm{F}_{3}\\\Rightarrow\quad\mathrm{F}_{\text{net}}=\mathrm{F}_{1}-\mathrm{F}_{3}\end{array} $$ So, the wire attract loop.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A long solenoid carrying a current produces a magnetic field B along its axis. If the current is doubled and the number of turns per cm is halved, the new value of the magnetic field is:
questions.explanation: Magnetic field induction at point inside the solenoid, having $n$ turns per unit length carrying current I, is given by $$ \mathbf{B}=\boldsymbol{\mu}_{0}n\mathbf{I} $$ If I is doubled and n is halved, then B remains same.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.subtopic: Torque on Current Loop, Magnetic Dipole
questions.question: A uniform conducting wire of length 12a and resistance ‘R’ is wound up as a current carrying coil in the shape of: (i) an equilateral triangle of side ‘a’ (ii) a square of side ‘a’ The magnetic dipole moments of the coil in each case respectively are:
questions.explanation: Given, Length of wire, l = 12 a Resistance = R Current = I (i) Equilateral triangle of side $a'$ we know, $m = NIA$ $$ \mathbf{A}=\frac{\sqrt{3}}{4}a^{2} $$ Perimeter = 3a $$ \boldsymbol{N}=4 $$ $$ \begin{aligned}m&=4.\ I.\ \frac{\sqrt{3}}{4}a^{2}\\&=\sqrt{3}\ I a^{2}\end{aligned} $$ (ii) Area of Square of side 'a' Square, $ A = a^{2} $ perimeter = 4a $ \therefore $ N = 3 m = 3.1 a^{2}

questions.subject: Physics
questions.subtopic: Torque on Current Loop, Magnetic Dipole
questions.question: A wire of length L carrying a current of I ampere is bent in the form of a circle. Its magnetic moment is:
questions.explanation: When wire of length L is bent in the form of a circle of radius R then $$ \mathrm{L}=2\pi\mathrm{R} $$ $$ \displaystyle\ \ \bullet $$ $$ \mathrm{R}=\frac{\mathrm{L}}{2\pi} $$ ∴ Magnetic moment, $$ \begin{aligned}\mu&=I A\\&=I(\pi R^{2})\\&=I.\pi.\left(\frac{L}{2\pi}\right)^{2}\\&=\frac{I\pi L^{2}}{4\pi^{2}}=\frac{I L^{2}}{4\pi}\end{aligned} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.subtopic: Torque on Current Loop, Magnetic Dipole
questions.question: A 250-turn rectangular coil of length 2.1 cm and width 1.25 cm carries a current of 85 μA and subjected to a magnetic field of strength 0.85 T. Work done for rotating the coil by 180° against the torque is:
questions.explanation: Work done in rotating the coil, $$ \begin{aligned}\mathrm{W}&=\mathrm{MB}\left(\cos\theta_{1}-\cos\theta_{2}\right)\\\therefore\quad\mathrm{W}&=\mathrm{MB}\left(\cos0^{\circ}-\cos180^{\circ}\right)\\&=2\mathrm{MB}=2\times\mathrm{NIA}\times\mathrm{B}\\&=2\times250\times85\times10^{-6}\times2.1\times\\&\quad1.25\times10^{-4}\times0.85\\&=9.48\times10^{-6}\mathrm{J}\\&=9.5\mu\mathrm{J}\end{aligned} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.subtopic: Torque on Current Loop, Magnetic Dipole
questions.question: A current loop in magnetic field:
questions.optionA: experiences a torque whether the field is uniform or non-uniform in all orientation
questions.optionB: can be in equilibrium in one orientation
questions.optionC: can be in equilibrium in two orientations
questions.optionD: can be in equilibrium in two orientations one stable while the other is unstable
questions.explanation: For parallel, it is stable and for antiparallel, it is unstable.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.subtopic: Torque on Current Loop, Magnetic Dipole
questions.question: A circular coil ABCD carrying a current 'i' is placed in a uniform magnetic field. If the magnetic force on the segment AB is $ \vec{F} $, the force on the remaining segment BCDA is:
questions.explanation:
The net magnetic force on a current loop in a uniform magnetic field is always zero.
$$ \vec{F}_{AB}+\vec{F}_{BCDA}=0 $$ $$ \vec{\mathbf{F}}_{\mathrm{B C D A}}=-\vec{\mathbf{F}}_{\mathrm{A B}}=-\vec{\mathbf{F}} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.subtopic: Torque on Current Loop, Magnetic Dipole
questions.question: A square current carrying loop is suspended in a uniform magnetic field acting in the plane of the loop. If the force on one arm of the loop is $\overrightarrow{F}$, what will be the net force on the remaining three arms of the loop?
questions.explanation: If magnetic field B is uniform, then force on closed loop = 0 One force is F, then the total force should be zero. This confirms that, $\overrightarrow{F} = -\overrightarrow{F}$ for zero net force.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.subtopic: Torque on Current Loop, Magnetic Dipole
questions.question: A closed loop PQRS carrying a current is placed in a uniform magnetic field. If the magnetic forces on segments PS, SR and RQ are $F_{1}$, $F_{2}$ and $F_{3}$ respectively and are in the plane of the paper and along the directions shown, the force on the segment QP is:
questions.explanation: If placed in uniform magnetic field, total force on the current carrying closed loop should be zero. $$ \mathrm{F}_{\mathrm{horizontal}}=\mathrm{F}_{3}-\mathrm{F}_{1} $$ $$ \mathrm{F_{vertical}}=\mathrm{F_{2}} $$ Resultant of $ \vec{F}_1 $, $ \vec{F}_2 $ and $ \vec{F}_3 $ is $ \vec{F} $, Where $$ \overrightarrow{\mathrm{F}}=\sqrt{\left(\mathrm{F}_{3}-\mathrm{F}_{1}\right)^{2}+\mathrm{F}_{2}^{2}} $$ since, total force = 0, Hence, force on QP is equal to $ \overrightarrow{F} $ in magnitude but opposite direction. $$ \mathrm{F_{QP}}=\sqrt{\left(\mathrm{F_{3}}-\mathrm{F_{1}}\right)^{2}+\mathrm{F_{2}^{2}}} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.subtopic: Torque on Current Loop, Magnetic Dipole
questions.question: A charged particle (charge q) is moving in a circle of radius R with uniform speed v. The associated magnetic moment $\mu$ is given by:
questions.explanation: Magnetic moment, $ \mu = IA $ Since, $$ \mathrm{T}=\frac{2\pi\mathrm{R}}{\mathrm{v}} $$ $$ I=\frac{q}{T}=\frac{q v}{2\pi R} $$ also, $$ \begin{aligned}\mu&=\left(\frac{q\mathbf{v}}{2\pi\mathbf{R}}\right)(\pi\mathbf{R}^{2})\\&=\frac{q\mathbf{v}\mathbf{R}}{2}\end{aligned} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.subtopic: Torque on Current Loop, Magnetic Dipole
questions.question: A current carrying coil is subjected to a uniform magnetic field. The coil will orient so that its plane becomes:
questions.optionA: inclined at $45^{\circ}$ to the magnetic field
questions.optionB: inclined at any arbitrary angle to the magnetic field
questions.explanation: The coil must be oriented in such a way that its magnetic moment coincides with the field. So that the coil's magnetic force is zero. Since, the direction of magnetic moment is perpendicular of coil's plane which means plane has to be perpendicular to magnetic field.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: If the galvanometer G does not show any deflection in the circuit shown, the value of R is given by:
questions.explanation: Since, galvanometer shows no deflection, then voltage across R is 2 V. Now voltage across R is given by, $$ =\frac{10\mathrm{R}}{400+\mathrm{R}} $$ or $$ 2=\frac{10\mathrm{R}}{400+\mathrm{R}} $$ $$ \mathrm{R}=100\Omega $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Current sensitivity of a moving coil galvanometer is 5 div/mA and its voltage sensitivity (angular deflection per unit voltage applied) is 20 div/V. The resistance of the galvanometer is:
questions.explanation: Current sensitivity (I_{S}) for moving coil galvanometer is: $$ \mathrm{I}_{\mathrm{S}}=\frac{\theta}{\mathrm{I}}=\frac{\mathrm{NAB}}{\mathrm{C}} $$ where, N = Number of turns in the coil A = Area of each turn of the coil B = Magnetic field C = Restoring torque per unit twist of the strip Now, voltage sensitivity $ V_{S} $ is given by, $$ \mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{S}}=\frac{\theta}{\mathrm{V}}=\frac{\mathrm{NAB}}{\mathrm{CR}_{\mathrm{G}}} $$ $ R_{G} $ = resistance of galvanometer ∴ Resistance of galvanometer is, $$ \mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{G}}=\frac{\mathrm{I}_{\mathrm{S}}}{\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{S}}}=\frac{5\times1}{20\times10^{-3}}=\frac{5000}{20}=250\Omega $$ $$ \mathrm{R_{G}=250\Omega} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: In the ammeter, 0.2% of main current passes through the galvanometer. If resistance of galvanometer is G, the resistance of ammeter will be:
questions.explanation:
The situation of the question is given below.
∴ For ammeter. $$ \mathrm{G}\times0.002\mathrm{I}=0.998\mathrm{I}\times r_{g} $$ $$ r_{g}=\frac{0.002}{0.998}G=\frac{1}{499}G $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A millivoltmeter of 25 mV range is to be converted into an ammeter of 25 A range. The value (in Ohm) of necessary shunt will be:
questions.explanation:
Deflection current, $ I_{g} = \frac{25mV}{G} $ Ampere Where, G = resistance of galvanometer The value of shunt will be, $$ \mathrm{S}=\frac{\mathrm{I}_{\mathrm{g}}\mathrm{G}}{\mathrm{I}-\mathrm{I}_{\mathrm{g}}}=\frac{25\mathrm{mV}}{25}=0.001\ \Omega $$ 67.( ) According to the question, If resistance is same, then current is unchanged.
$$ \mathbf{G}=\frac{\mathbf{G}\mathbf{S}}{\mathbf{G}+\mathbf{S}}+\mathbf{R} $$ $$ \begin{aligned}R&=G-\frac{GS}{G+S}\\&=\frac{G^{2}}{G+S}\end{aligned} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A galvanometer of resistance, G is shunted by a resistance S ohm. To keep the main current in the circuit unchanged, the resistance to be put in series with the galvanometer is:

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A galvanometer has a coil of resistance 100 Ω and gives a full scale deflection for 30 mA current. If it is to work as a voltmeter of 30 V range, the resistance required to be added will be:
questions.explanation: Required resistance $$ \begin{aligned}\mathbf{R}&=\frac{\mathbf{V}}{1_{g}}-\mathbf{G}\\&=\frac{30}{30\times10^{-3}}-100=900\Omega\end{aligned} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A galvanometer having a coil resistance of 60 Ω shows full scale deflection when a current of 1.0 A passes through it. It can be converted into an ammeter to read currents up to 5.0 A by:
questions.optionA: putting in parallel a resistance of 240 Ω
questions.optionB: putting in series a resistance of 15 Ω
questions.optionC: putting in series a resistance of 240 Ω
questions.optionD: putting in parallel a resistance of 15 Ω
questions.explanation: From the question, Given, $$ G=60\Omega,I_{g}=1.0A,I=5A $$ $$ \begin{aligned}\mathrm{I}_{g}\mathrm{G}&=(\mathrm{I}-\mathrm{I}_{g})\mathrm{S}\\ \mathrm{S}&=\frac{\mathrm{I}_{g}\mathrm{G}}{\mathrm{I}-\mathrm{I}_{g}}=\frac{1}{5-1}\times60=15\Omega\end{aligned} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A galvanometer of resistance 50 Ω connected to a battery of 3 V along with a resistance of 2950 Ω in series. A full scale deflection of 30 divisions is obtained in the galvanometer. In order to reduce this deflection to 20 divisions, the resistance in series should be:
questions.explanation: Current through galvanometer, $$ \mathrm{I}=\frac{3}{50+2950}=10^{-3}\mathrm{A} $$ Current for 30 division = $ 10^{-3} $ A $$ \begin{aligned}Current~for~20~divisions&=\frac{20}{30}\times10^{-3}\\&=\frac{2}{3}\times10^{-3}A=\frac{1}{1500}A\end{aligned} $$ Required current = $ \frac{3}{50 + R} $ $$ \frac{1}{1500}=\frac{3}{50+R} $$ → $$ 50+R=4500 $$ $$ \mathrm{R}=4500-50 $$ $$ \mathrm{R}=4450\Omega $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: The resistance of an ammeter is 13 Ω and its scale is graduated for a current upto 100 amps. After an additional shunt has been connected to this ammeter, it becomes possible to measure currents upto 750 amperes by this meter. The value of shunt-resistance is:
questions.explanation: Let, S = shunt resistance Given that, $$ 1=750A $$ $$ \mathbf{I}_{\mathbf{g}}=100\mathrm{~A},\mathbf{R}_{\mathrm{G}}=13\Omega $$ $$ \mathrm{I_{g}R_{G}=(I-I_{g})S} $$ $$ \begin{aligned}&\Rightarrow\text{□}100\times13=[750-100]S\\ &\Rightarrow\text{□}S=2\Omega\\ \end{aligned} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A galvanometer acting as a voltmeter will have:
questions.optionA: a high resistance in parallel with its coil
questions.optionB: a high resistance in series with its coil
questions.optionC: a low resistance in parallel with its coil
questions.optionD: a low resistance in series with its coil
questions.explanation:
A galvanometer acting as a voltmeter will have a high resistance in series with its coil.
##### △ Caution Students cannot use galvanometer for measuring current as it is a very sensitive instrument because it shows full deflection for a very small current.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A galvanometer of 50 Ohm resistance has 25 divisions. A current $ 4 \times 10^{-4} $ ampere gives a deflection of one division. To convert this galvanometer into a voltmeter having a range of 25 volts, it should be connected with a resistance of:
questions.explanation: Total current in the galvanometer. $$ I_{g}=25\times4\times10^{-4}A $$ $$ \mathrm{I_{g}=10^{-2}A} $$ The value of resistance connected in series to convert galvanometer into voltmeter of 25 V is: $$ \begin{array}{l} \displaystyle \mathrm{R} = \frac{\mathrm{V}}{\mathrm{I}_{g}} - \mathrm{G} \\ \displaystyle \qquad = \frac{25}{10^{-2}} - 50 \\ \displaystyle \qquad = 2450 \Omega \end{array} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: The magnetic potential energy, when a magnetic bar of magnetic moment $ \vec{m} $ is placed perpendicular to the magnetic field B is:
questions.explanation: The magnetic potential energy when a magnetic dipole of magnetic moment $ \vec{m} $ is placed perpendicular to a magnetic field $ \vec{B} $ is given by $ U = -\vec{m} \cdot \vec{B} $ $ \therefore U = -mB \cos 90^\circ = 0 $

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: The magnetic moment and moment of inertia of a magnetic needle as shown are, respectively, $ 1.0 \times 10^{-2} \, \text{Am}^{2} $ and $ \frac{10^{-6}}{\pi^{2}} \, \text{kg} \, \text{m}^{2} $. If it completes 10 oscillations in 10 s, the magnitude of the magnetic field is:
questions.explanation: Given, magnetic moment = $ 1 \times 10^{-2} $ Am $ ^{2} $ Moment of inertia, $ I = \frac{10^{-6}}{\pi^{2}} $ kgm $ ^{2} $ No. of oscillations = 10 time $ t = 10 $ s $$ \mathrm{T}=\frac{10}{10}=1\mathrm{~s} $$ $$ \mathrm{B}=\frac{1}{m}\left(\frac{2\pi}{\mathrm{T}}\right)^{2} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} \mathrm{B} &= \frac{10^{-6}}{\pi^2} \left( \frac{2\pi}{\mathrm{T}} \right)^2 \\ &1 \times 10^{-2} \\ \mathrm{B} &= \frac{10^{-6}}{\pi^2 \times 10^{-2}} (2\pi)^2 \\ &= \frac{10^{-6}}{10^{-2} \pi^2} \times 4\pi^2 \\ \mathrm{B} &= 4 \times 10^{-4} \, \mathrm{T} \end{aligned} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: The magnetic moment of an iron bar is M. It is bent in such a way that it forms an arc section of circle subtending an angle of 60^{\circ} at the centre. The magnetic moment of this arc section is:
questions.explanation: $$ \mathbf{M}^{\prime}=\mathbf{M}.\frac{\boldsymbol{\theta}}{2\pi} $$ Given: M is magnetic moment of iron bar $$ \theta=60^{\circ} $$ $$ \mathbf{M}=\mathbf{M}\frac{60^{\circ}}{2\pi} $$ $$ 60^{\circ}=\frac{60\pi}{180}=\frac{\pi}{3}radians $$ So. $$ \mathbf{M}^{\prime}=\mathbf{M}.\frac{\overline{3}}{2\pi}=\mathbf{M}.\frac{1}{6} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: In a uniform magnetic field of 0.049 T, a magnetic needle performs 20 complete oscillations in 5 seconds as shown. The moment of inertia of the needle is $ 9.8 \times 10^{-6} \, \text{kgm}^2 $. If the magnitude of magnetic moment of the needle is $ x \times 10^{-5} \, \text{Am}^2 $; then the value of 'x' is:
questions.explanation: Given B = 0.049 T, $$ \mathrm{T}=\frac{5}{20}=\frac{1}{4},\mathrm{I}=9.8\times10^{-6}\mathrm{kg}\mathrm{m}^{2} $$ $$ \mathrm{T}=2\pi\sqrt{\frac{\mathrm{I}}{m\mathrm{B}}} $$ $$ \mathrm{T}^{2}=\frac{4\pi^{2}\mathrm{I}}{m\mathrm{B}} $$ $$ m=\frac{4\pi^{2}\mathrm{I}}{\mathrm{T}^{2}\mathrm{B}} $$ On substituting the given values in eqn (i), we get $$ \begin{aligned}m&=\frac{4\pi^{2}\times9.8\times10^{-6}}{\left(\frac{1}{4}\right)^{2}\times4.9\times10^{-2}}\\&=1280\pi^{2}\times10^{-5}Am^{2}\\x&=1280\pi^{2}\end{aligned} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: An iron bar of length L has magnetic moment M. It is bent at the middle of its length such that the two arms make an angle 60^{\circ} with each other. The magnetic moment of this new magnet is:
questions.explanation: Magnetic pole strength remains the same $$ M_{new}=m L_{eff}=\frac{mL}{2}=\frac{M}{2} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A bar magnet is hung by a thin cotton thread in a uniform horizontal magnetic field and is in equilibrium state. The energy required to rotate it by $ 60^{\circ} $ is W. Now, the torque required to keep the magnet in the new position is:
questions.explanation: Since work done in rotating the magnet, $$W = MB (\cos \theta_0 - \cos \theta)$$ where, $M = \text{magnetic moment of the magnet}$$ $$B = \text{magnetic field}$$ $$W = MB (\cos 0^\circ - \cos 60^\circ)$$ $$= MB \left( 1 - \frac{1}{2} \right) = \frac{MB}{2}$$ $$MB = 2W$$ $$ \begin{array}{c} putting\mathbf{M}\mathbf{B}=2\mathbf{W} from(i),\\\tau=2\mathbf{W}\sin60^{\circ}\\=2\mathbf{W}\times\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}=\mathbf{W}\sqrt{3}\end{array} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A bar magnet of magnetic moment M placed at right angle to a magnetic induction B. If a force F is experienced by each pole of the magnet, the length of the magnet will be:
questions.explanation: The magnetic pole strength be m, then magnetic moment, $ M = ml $ From the given figure, $$ l=\frac{\pi}{3}\times r $$ $$ r=\frac{3l}{\pi} $$ ∴ New magnetic moment, $$ \begin{aligned}\mathbf{M}&=m\times r=m\times\frac{3l}{\pi}\\&=\frac{3}{\pi}\times ml=\frac{3\mathbf{M}}{\pi}\end{aligned} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A bar magnet of length l and magnetic dipole moment M is bent in the form of an arc as shown in figure. The new magnetic dipole moment will be:
questions.explanation: Here, $$ \begin{array}{c}B\\\longrightarrow\\\longrightarrow\\\longrightarrow\\\longrightarrow\end{array}\begin{array}{c}\\\longrightarrow\\\longrightarrow\\\longrightarrow\\\longrightarrow\end{array}\begin{array}{c}\\\longrightarrow\\\longrightarrow\\\longrightarrow\\\longrightarrow\\\end{array}\begin{array}{c}\\\longrightarrow\\\longrightarrow\\\longrightarrow\\\longrightarrow\\\end{array} $$ $$ \tau=\overset{\rightarrow}{\mathbf{M}}\times\overset{\rightarrow}{\mathbf{B}} $$ $$ \left|\tau\right|=MB\sin90^{\circ} $$ $$ \mathbf{F}l=\mathbf{M}\mathbf{B} $$ $$ l=\frac{\mathbf{M}\mathbf{B}}{\mathrm{F}} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A short bar magnet of magnetic moment 0.4 JT^{-1} is placed in a uniform magnetic field of 0.16 T. The magnet is in stable equilibrium when the potential energy is:
questions.explanation: We know that, U = -MB cos θ for stable equilibrium, θ = 0° U = -MB cos 0° = -MB = -(0.4)(0.16) = -0.064 J

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Two identical bar magnets are fixed with their centres at a distance d apart. A stationary charge Q is placed at P in between the gap of the two magnets at distance D from the centre O as shown in the figure. The force on the charge Q is:
questions.optionD: directed perpendicular to the plane of paper.
questions.explanation: The force on stationary charge Q is zero.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A bar magnet having a magnetic moment of $ 2 \times 10^{4} $ J T $ ^{-1} $, is free to rotate in a horizontal plane. A horizontal magnetic field, B = $ 6 \times 10^{-4} $ T exists in the space. The work done in taking the magnet slowly from a direction parallel to the field to a direction 60° from the field is:
questions.explanation: According to the question; When magnetic dipole is rotated from initial position $ \theta_{1} $ to final position $ \theta_{2} $, then $$ \mathbf{W}=\mathbf{M}\mathbf{B}(\cos\theta_{1}-\cos\theta_{2}) $$ $$ \mathrm{W}=\mathrm{MB}\left(\cos0^{\circ}-\cos60^{\circ}\right) $$ $$ W=\mathbf{M}\mathbf{B}\left(1-\frac{1}{2}\right)=\frac{2\times10^{4}\times6\times10^{-4}}{2} $$ $$ \mathbf{W}=6\mathbf{J} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A bar magnet of magnetic moment M is placed in magnetic field of induction B. The torque exerted on it is:
questions.explanation: A magnetic torque $ \tau $ acts on a bar magnet when it is placed in an external magnetic field B, which is given by, $$ \overrightarrow{\mathbf{r}}=\overrightarrow{\mathbf{M}}\times\overrightarrow{\mathbf{B}}=[\mathbf{M}]\left[\mathbf{B}\right]\sin\theta $$ where, $ \theta = \text{angle between M and B} $

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: An iron rod of susceptibility 599 is subjected to a magnetising field of 1200 A m^{-1}. The permeability of the material of the rod is (Take, $ \mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7} $ T m A^{-1}):
questions.explanation: Given, $$ \chi=599 $$ Magnetic field, B = 1200 Am $ ^{-1} $ Permeability of the material of the rod. $$ \begin{aligned}\mu_{\mathrm{m}}&=\mu_{\mathrm{o}}\left(1+\chi\right)\\&=4\pi\times10^{-7}\left(1+599\right)\\&=2.4\pi\times10^{-4}\mathrm{~T~m A}^{-1}.\end{aligned} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: The incorrect relation for a diamagnetic material (all the symbols carry their usual meaning and $\varepsilon$ is a small positive number) is:
questions.explanation:
The incorrect relation is $ 1 < \mu_r < 1 + \varepsilon $ For diamagnetic material, magnetic susceptibility is small and negative i.e., $-1 \leq \chi \leq 0$.
##### Caution Students should not get confused between relative permeability and magnetic susceptibility of diamagnetic, paramagnetic and ferromagnetic materials.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Match List-I with List-II. Choose the correct answer from the options given below:
questions.explanation:
MaterialMagnetic susceptibility $ \chi $
Diamagneticslightly less than 0
Paramagneticslightly greater than 0
Ferromagnetic$ \gg 1 $
Non-magnetic0

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: The magnetic moment of a diamagnetic atom is:
questions.explanation: The magnetic moment of a diamagnetic atom is equal to zero.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A thin diamagnetic rod is placed vertically between the poles of an electromagnet. When the current in the electromagnet is switched on, then the diamagnetic rod is pushed up, out of the horizontal magnetic field. Hence, the rod gains gravitational potential energy. The work required to do this comes from:
questions.optionA: the lattice structure of the material of the rod
questions.optionD: the induced electric field due to the changing field.
questions.explanation: Energy of current source will be converted into potential energy of the rod.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: The magnetic susceptibility is negative for:
questions.explanation: Magnetic susceptibility = $ \chi $ It is negative for diamagnetic material only.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: There are four light weight rod samples X, Y, Z, W respectively suspended by threads. A bar magnet is slowly brought near each sample and the following observations are noted: (I) X is feebly repelled (II) Y is feebly attracted (III) Z is strongly attracted (IV) W remains unaffected Which one of the following is true?
questions.optionB: W is of a ferromagnetic material
questions.optionD: Y is of a paramagnetic material
questions.explanation: (I) Paramagnetic material will be feebly attracted, (II) Diamagnetic material will be feebly repelled, (III) Ferromagnetic material will be strongly attracted (IV) Non-Magnetic material will remain unaffected by magnetic field.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: If a diamagnetic substance is brought near the north or the south pole of a bar magnet, it is:
questions.optionB: repelled by the north pole and attracted by the south pole
questions.optionC: attracted by the north pole and repelled by the south pole
questions.explanation: If a diamagnetic substance is brought near the north or the south pole of a bar magnet, it is repelled by both the poles.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Curie temperatures is the temperature above which:
questions.explanation: Curie temperature is that temperature above which a ferromagnetic material becomes paramagnetic material.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Nickel shows ferromagnetic property at room temperature. If the temperature is increased beyond Curie temperature, then it will show:
questions.explanation: Above curie temperature, ferromagnetic material behaves as paramagnetic material.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: If the magnetic dipole of an atom of diamagnetic material, paramagnetic material and ferromagnetic material are denoted by $\mu_{d}$, $\mu_{p}$ and $\mu_{f}$ respectively, then:
questions.explanation: $ \mu_p \neq 0 $, $ \mu_f \neq 0 $ and $ \mu_d = 0 $

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A diamagnetic material in a magnetic field moves:
questions.optionA: from stronger to the weaker parts of the field.
questions.optionB: from weaker to the stronger parts of the field.
questions.optionD: in none of the above directions. [AIPMT 20
questions.explanation:
A diamagnetic material in a magnetic field moves from stronger to the weaker part of the field.
##### Related Theory A diamagnetic material tends to move from stronger to weaker regions of the magnetic field and hence decreases the number of magnetic field lines passing through it.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: According to Curie's law, the magnetic susceptibility of a substance at an absolute temperature T is proportional to:
questions.explanation: From Curie Law, $ \chi_{m} \propto \frac{1}{T} $

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: In which type of material the magnetic susceptibility does not depend on temperature?
questions.explanation: Material's magnetic susceptibility is a measurement of how easily a specimen of that material may be magnetized in a magnetic field. Magnetic susceptibility ( $ \chi_{m} $) of a diamagnetic substance is temperature independent.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A diamagnetic substance is brought near a strong magnet, then it is:
questions.optionC: repelled by North pole and attracted by South pole.
questions.optionD: attracted by North pole and repelled by South pole.
questions.explanation:
When diamagnetic substances are placed in the magnetic field of a powerful magnet, they are weakly magnetized in the opposite direction of the field and repelled by the powerful magnet.
##### Related Theory Relative permeability of the diamagnetic substance is negative.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: For protecting a sensitive equipment from the external magnetic field, it should be:
questions.optionC: wrapped with insulation around it when passing current through it
questions.explanation: Iron is a ferromagnetic substance. Since, ferromagnetic substances have no magnetic lines of force. Hence, equipment can be protected by placing it within a ferromagnetic substance container. As a result, it is kept in an iron can. 6

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A square loop of side 1 m and resistance 1 Ω is placed in a magnetic field of 0.5 T. If the plane of loop is perpendicular to the direction of magnetic field, the magnetic flux through the loop is:
questions.explanation: Here, d = 1 m, R = 1 $ \Omega $, B = 0.5 T, angle, $ \theta = 0^{\circ} $ Area of square loop, A = $ d^{2} = 1 \, m^{2} $ Magnetic flux, $ \phi = BA \cos \theta $ $$ =0.5\times1\times\cos0^{\circ} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A big circular coil of 1000 turns and average radius 10 m is rotating about its horizontal diameter at 2 rad s^{-1}. If the vertical component of earth's magnetic field at that place is $ 2 \times 10^{-5} $ T and electrical resistance of the coil is 12.56 $ \Omega $, then the maximum induced current in the coil will be:
questions.explanation: Here, number of turns, (N) = 1000 Radius, (r) = 10 m Magnetic field, (B) = $ 2 \times 10^{-5} $ T $$ \mathrm{Resistance},(\mathrm{R})=12.56\ \Omega $$ Angular frequency of coil, $\omega = 2\ \mathrm{rad}\ \mathrm{s}^{-1}$ $$ \begin{aligned}num~induced~emf,&\mathrm{E}=\mathrm{N}\omega\mathrm{AB}\\&=\mathrm{N}\omega(\pi r^{2})\mathrm{B}\\&=1000\times2\times3.14\times(10)^{2}\times2\times10^{-5}\\&=12.56\mathrm{V}\end{aligned} $$ Maximum induced current. $$ \begin{aligned}I_{max}&=\frac{E}{R}\\&=\frac{12.56}{12.56}=1A\end{aligned} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: The magnetic flux linked with a coil (in Wb) is given by the equation $ \phi = 5t^{2} + 3t + 16 $. The magnitude of induced emf in the coil at the fourth second will be:
questions.explanation: Given: Magnetic flux, $ (\phi)=5t^{2}+3t+16 $ Induced emf. $$ \begin{aligned}|\mathbf{E}|&=\frac{d\phi}{dt}\\&=\frac{d}{dt}(5t^{2}+3t+16)\\&=10t+3\end{aligned} $$ when, $$ t=4\mathrm{~s}, $$ $$ \left|\mathrm{E}_{4}\right|=(10\times4)+3=43\mathrm{~V} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A coil of 800 turns and effective area 0.05 m² is kept perpendicular to a magnetic field 5 × 10⁻⁵ T. When the plane of the coil is rotated by 90° around any of its co-planer axis in 0.1 s. The emf induced in the coil will be:
questions.explanation: Here, $$ \mathrm{N=800\tau u r n s} $$ $$ A=0.05\ m^{2}=5\times10^{-2}\ m^{2} $$ $$ \mathbf{B}=5\times10^{-5}\mathrm{T} $$ $$ \Delta t=0.1\mathrm{~s~} $$ We know that, $$ e_induced=-{}\frac{\Delta\phi}{\Delta t} $$ $$ \begin{aligned}\Delta\phi&=\phi_{f}-\phi_{i}\\\phi_{f}&=0,\phi_{i}=\mathbf{N}\big(\overrightarrow{\mathrm{B}}.\overrightarrow{\mathrm{A}}\big)\\&=800\times5\times10^{-5}\times5\times10^{-2}\\&=2\times10^{-3}\end{aligned} $$ $$ \begin{aligned}e_{\mathrm{induced}}=&-\frac{\Delta\phi}{\Delta t}=\frac{-(0-2\times10^{-3})}{0.1}\\&=0.02\mathrm{~V}\end{aligned} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A uniform magnetic field is restricted within a region of radius r. The magnetic field changes with time at a rate $ \frac{dB}{dt} $. Loop 1 of radius R > r encloses the region r and loop 2 of radius R is outside the region of magnetic field as shown in the figure. Then, the $ \underline{emf} $ generated is:
questions.optionA: zero in loop 1 and zero in loop 2
questions.optionB: $ -\frac{d\mathrm{B}}{dt}\pi r^{2} $ in loop 1 and  $ -\frac{d\mathrm{B}}{dt}\pi r^{2} $ in loop 2
questions.optionC: $ -\frac{dB}{dt}\pi r^{2} $ in loop 1 and zero in loop 2
questions.optionD: $ -\frac{dB}{dt}\pi r^{2} $ in loop 1 and zero in loop 2
questions.explanation: We know that induced emf in the region is given by $$ e=-{\frac{d\Phi}{d t}}\quad\{\because\Phi=\mathrm{B A}=\pi r^{2}\mathrm{B} $$ $$ \because A=\pi r^{2} $$ $$ \frac{d\Phi}{dt}=-\pi r^{2}\frac{dB}{dt} $$ From question, Rate of change of magnetic flux associated with loop (1), $$ e_{1}=-\frac{d\Phi_{1}}{d t}=-\pi r^{2}\frac{d\Phi}{d t} $$ and Rate of change of magnetic flux associated with loop (2), $$ e_{2}=-\frac{d\Phi_{2}}{d t}=0\qquad\{\mathrm{s i n c e}\;\Phi_{2}=0\} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A thin semicircular conducting ring (PQR) of radius 'r' is falling with its plane vertical in a horizontal magnetic field B, as shown in figure. The potential difference developed across the ring when its speed is v, is:
questions.optionB: Bv $ \pi r^{2} $ and P is at higher potential
questions.optionC: $ \pi r Bv $ and R is at higher potential
questions.optionD: 2rBv and R is at higher potential.
questions.explanation: Rate of decrease of area of semicircular ring $$ =\frac{\mathrm{d}\mathbf{A}}{\mathrm{d}t}=(\pi r)\mathbf{v} $$ From Faraday's law of electromagnetic induction, $$ e = - \frac{d \Phi}{d t} = - \mathbf { B } \frac{d \mathbf { A }}{d t} = - \mathbf { B } ( \pi r \mathbf { v}) $$ $$ \begin{array}{c}\times\quad\times\quad\times\quad\times\quad\times\quad\times\quad\times\quad\times\\\times\quad\times\quad\times\quad r\quad\times\quad\times\quad\times\quad\times\quad\times\\\times\quad\times\quad\bullet\quad\times\quad\times\quad\times\quad\oplus\\P\longleftarrow2r\longrightarrow R\quad\times\quad\times\quad\times\quad\times\quad\times\quad\times\end{array} $$ As induced current in the ring produces magnetic field in upward direction, Hence, R is at higher potential.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A coil of resistance 400 Ω is placed in a magnetic field. If the magnetic flux $\phi$ (Wb) linked with the coil varies with time $t$ (s) as $\phi = 50t^{2} + 4$. The current in the coil at $t = 2$ s is:
questions.explanation: We know that, Induced emf of coil, $ e = \left| -\frac{d\phi}{dt} \right|_{t} $ Given that, $$ \phi=50t^{2}+4\mathrm{a n d}\mathrm{R}=400\Omega $$ $$ \begin{aligned}e&=\left|\frac{-d\phi}{d t}\right|_{t=2}=[100t]_{t=2}\\&=200\mathrm{~V}\end{aligned} $$ ∴ Current in the coil, $ I = \frac{E}{R} = \frac{200}{400} $ $$ I=0.5A $$ \[\begin{array}{r l}{\mathrm{(B)}~}&{\mathrm{W e~k n o w~t h a t,}\phi=\mathrm{B A}}\\ {\Rightarrow}&{}\\ {\mathrm{E_{i n}}=\frac{-d\phi}{d t}}\\ {\Rightarrow}&{\mathrm{E_{i n}}=-\frac{d}{d t}(\mathrm{B A})}\\ {\Rightarrow}&{\mathrm{E}=-\mathrm{B}\pi2r\frac{d r}{d t}}\\ {\mathrm{E}=\mathrm{B}\pi2r\frac{d r}{d t}}\\ {\mathrm{E}=\mathrm{E}=\mathrm{B}\pi2r\frac{d r}{d t}}\\ {E=\mathrm{E}=\mathrm{B}\pi2r\frac{d r}{d t}}\\ {E=\mathrm{E}=\mathrm{B}\pi2r\frac{d r}{d t}}\\ {E=\mathrm{E}=\mathrm{B}\pi2r\frac{d r}{d t}}\\ {E=\mathrm{E}=\mathrm{B}\pi2r\frac{d r}{d t

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A conducting circular loop is placed in a uniform magnetic field, B = 0.025 T with its plane perpendicular to the loop. The radius of the loop is made to shrink at a constant rate of 1 mm s^{-1}. The induced emf when the radius is 2 cm, is:

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: As a result of change in the magnetic flux linked to the closed loop as shown in the figure, an e.m.f. V volt is induced in the loop. The work done (joules) in taking a charge Q coulomb once along the loop is:
questions.explanation: As the induced electric field due to change in magnetic field is non-conservative. The work done due to change in flux = QV.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A rectangular coil of 20 turns and area of cross-section 25 sq cm has a resistance of 100 Ω. If a magnetic field which is perpendicular to the plane of coil changes at a rate of 1000 T/s, the current in the coil is:
questions.explanation: Total number of turns, N = 20 Area of coil, $$ \begin{array}{l} \text{A} = 25\ \text{cm}^2\\ = 25 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{m}^2 \end{array} $$ Change in magnetic field w.r.t. t $$ \frac{d\mathbf{B}}{dt}=1000\mathrm{T/s} $$ Resistance of coil, $ R = 100 \, \Omega $ We have, Induced current, $$ i=\frac{e}{\mathbf{R}} $$ $$ \begin{aligned}&=\frac{NA\frac{dB}{dt}}{R}\left[\because e=NA\frac{dB}{dt}\right]\\&=\frac{20\times25\times10^{-4}\times1000}{100}\\&=0.5A\end{aligned} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: An electron moves on a straight line path XY as shown. The abcd is coil adjacent in the path of electron. What will be the direction of current, if any induced in the coil?
questions.optionC: The current will reverse its direction as the electron goes past the coil
questions.explanation: When electron goes in straight line, electric flux first increases then decreases.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: In the Figure of circuit, the bulb will become suddenly bright, if
questions.explanation: When a circuit is broken, the induced e.m.f. is largest.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A wheel with 20 metallic spokes, each 1 m long, is rotated with a speed of 120 rpm in a plane perpendicular to a magnetic field of 0.4 G. The induced emf between the axle and rim of the wheel will be (1 G = 10^{-4} T):
questions.explanation: In the question, $$ \begin{aligned}\mathbf{B}&=0.4\mathbf{\Delta G}=0.4\times10^{-4}T\\l&=1\mathbf{\Delta m}\end{aligned} $$ $$ f=120\ rpm=\frac{120}{60}\ rps=2\ Hz $$ Induced emf between the axle and rim of the wheel is: $$ \begin{aligned}&e=\frac{1}{2}\mathbf{B}\omega l^{2}\\&=\frac{1}{2}\mathbf{B}(2\pi f)l^{2}=\pi\mathbf{B}f l^{2}\\&=3.14\times0.4\times10^{-4}\times2\times1\\&=2.51\times10^{-4}\mathrm{V}.\\ \end{aligned} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A cycle wheel of radius 0.5 m is rotated with constant angular velocity of 10 rad/s in a region of magnetic field of 0.1 T which is perpendicular to the plane of the wheel. The EMF generated between its centre and the rim is:
questions.explanation: According to the question, $$ \begin{aligned}e&=\frac{\mathbf{B}l^{2}\boldsymbol{\omega}}{2}\\&=\frac{1}{2}\bullet0.1\times\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{2}\times10=\frac{1}{8}\\&=0.125V.\end{aligned} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: The magnetic flux through a circuit of resistance R changes by an amount $\Delta\phi$ in a time $\Delta t$. Then the total quantity of electric charge Q that passes any point in the circuit during the time $\Delta t$ is represented by:
questions.explanation: According to the question on induced emf is: $$ \begin{aligned} \mathrm{V} &= \frac{\Delta \phi}{\Delta t} \\ \text{and current, } \mathrm{I} &= \frac{\mathrm{Q}}{\Delta t} = \frac{\Delta \phi}{\Delta t} \times \frac{1}{\mathrm{R}} \\ \Rightarrow \quad \mathrm{Q} &= \frac{\Delta \phi}{\mathrm{R}} \end{aligned} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A conductor of length 0.4 m is moving with a speed of 7 m/s perpendicular to a magnetic field of intensity 0.9 Wb/m². The induced emf across the conductor is:
questions.explanation: Given, length of conductor (l) = 0.4 m, $$ \mathrm{speed}(\mathbf{v})=7\mathrm{~m/s} $$ $$ (B)=0.9\ Wb/m^{2} $$ $$ \begin{aligned}[\theta=90^{\circ}]\text{as B is perpendicular to v}]\\=0.9\times0.4\times7\times\sin90^{\circ}\\=2.52\text{V}\end{aligned} $$ ##### Related Theory Magnitude of induced emf is directly proportional to the relative speed of coil magnet system.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Let us consider two solenoids X and Y, made from same magnetic material of relative permeability $\mu_{r}$ and equal area of cross-section. Length of X is twice that of Y and the number of turns per unit length in X is half that of Y. The ratio of self inductances of the two solenoids, $L_{X}: L_{Y}$ is:
questions.explanation: $ \mathbf{L} = \mu_0 \mu_r \times n \times \mathbf{A} \times \mathbf{N} $ $ \mathbf{L} = \mu_0 \mu_r n \times \mathbf{A} \times \frac{\mathbf{N}}{l} \times l $ $ \mathbf{L} = \mu_0 \mu_r \times n^2 \times \mathbf{A} \times l $ $ \mathbf{L} \propto n^2 l $ $ \frac{\mathbf{L}_{\mathrm{A}}}{\mathbf{L}_{\mathrm{B}}} = \frac{n_{\mathrm{A}}^2}{n_{\mathrm{B}}^2} \times \frac{l_{\mathrm{A}}}{l_{\mathrm{B}}} $ $ \mathbf{\frac{L_{\mathrm{A}}}{L_{\mathrm{B}}}} = \frac{1}{4} \times 2 = \frac{1}{2} $

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: The magnetic energy stored in an inductor of inductance $ 4 \, \mu\text{H} $ carrying a current of 2 A is:
questions.explanation: As we know that, $ \mathbf{U} = \frac{1}{2} \mathbf{L} \mathbf{I}^2 $ $ \mathbf{L} = \frac{1}{2} \times 4 \times 10^{-6} \times 2 \times 2 $ $ = 8 \, \mu\mathbf{J} $

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Two conducting circular loops of radii $ R_{1} $ and $ R_{2} $ are placed in the same plane with their centres coinciding. If $ R_{1} \gg R_{2} $, the mutual inductance M between them will be directly proportional to:
questions.explanation:
Let current in bigger loop be i. Magnetic field at the centre, $$ \mathbf{B}=\frac{\mu_{0}i}{2\mathbf{R}_{1}} $$ Magnetic flux through smaller coil, $$ \mathbf{\Phi}\phi=\mathbf{B}\mathbf{A}=\frac{\mathbf{\Phi}\phi}{i}=\frac{\mu_{0}i}{2\mathbf{R}_{1}}\times\mathbf{\pi}\mathbf{R}_{2}^{2} $$ $$ \phi=\frac{\mu_{0}\pi i R_{2}^{2}}{2R_{1}} $$ As per definition, $ \phi = Mi $ $$ \mathbf{M}=\frac{\phi}{i}=\frac{\frac{\mu_{0}\pi i\mathbf{R}_{2}^{2}}{2\mathbf{R}_{1}}}{i} $$ $$ \mathbf{M}=\frac{\mu_{0}\pi R_{2}^{2}}{2R_{1}} $$ $$ M\propto\frac{R_{2}^{2}}{R_{1}} $$
##### Related Theory When an emf is produced in a coil because of change in current in a coupled coil, the effect is called mutual inductance.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: The magnetic potential energy stored in a certain inductor is 25 mJ, when the current in the inductor is 60 mA. This inductor is of inductance:
questions.explanation: Given, $$ U=25mJ=25\times10^{-3}J $$ $$ I=60\ mA=60\times10^{-3}\ A $$ ∴ Energy stored in inductor. $$ \mathrm{E}=\frac{1}{2}\mathrm{~L}\times\mathrm{I}^{2} $$ $$ 25\times10^{-3}=\frac{1}{2}L\times(60\times(10^{-3}))^{2} $$ $$ \begin{aligned}L&=\frac{25\times2\times10^{6}\times10^{-3}}{3600}=\frac{500}{36}\\&=13.89Henry\end{aligned} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A long solenoid has 1000 turns. When a current of 4 A flows through it, the magnetic flux linked with each turn of the solenoid is $ 4 \times 10^{-3} $ Wb. The self-inductance of the solenoid is:
questions.explanation: From the question, Number of turns of the solenoid, N = 1000 Current, I = 4 A And magnetic flux, $ \phi = 4 \times 10^{-3} $ Wb $ \therefore $ self induction, $ L = \frac{\phi N}{I} $ $ = \frac{4 \times 10^{-3} \times 1000}{4} = 1 \, \text{H} $

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A current of 2.5 A flows through a coil of inductance 5 H. The magnetic flux linked with the coil is:
questions.explanation: Given: $ \begin{array}{l} \text{current I} = 2.5 \, \text{A} \\ \text{Inductance, L} = 5 \, \text{H} \\ \text{Magnetic flux, } \phi = ? \\ \text{We know, } \quad \phi = \text{LI} \\ \Rightarrow \quad = 5 \times 2.5 \, \text{Wb} = 12.5 \, \text{Wb} \end{array} $

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Two coils of self inductance 2 mH and 8 mH are placed so close together that the effective flux in one coil is completely linked with the other. The mutual inductance between these coils is:
questions.explanation: Mutual inductance between coils is, $$ \mathbf{M}=k\sqrt{\mathbf{L}_{1}\mathbf{L}_{2}} $$ $$ \begin{aligned}\mathrm{M}&=1\sqrt{2\times10^{-3}\times8\times10^{-3}}&\quad\{k=1\}\\&=4\times10^{-3}=4\mathrm{~mH}\end{aligned} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: In an inductor of self-inductance $ L = 2 \, \text{mH} $, current changes with time according to relation $ i = t^{2} e^{-t} $. At what time emf is zero?
questions.explanation: L = 2 mH, $ i = t^{2} e^{-t} $ $$ \begin{aligned}\mathrm{E}&=-L\frac{d\boldsymbol{i}}{d t}=-L\frac{d}{d t}[\boldsymbol{t}^{2}\boldsymbol{e}^{-t}]\\&=-\mathrm{L}[-\boldsymbol{t}^{2}\boldsymbol{e}^{-t}+2t\boldsymbol{e}^{-t}]\end{aligned} $$ when $$ E=0, $$ $$ -e^{-t}t^{2}+2t e^{-t}=0 $$ $$ t e^{-t}(t-2)=0 $$ Here, $ t \neq \infty $ and $ t \neq 0 $ $ \therefore $ t = 2 s

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A varying current in a coil changes from 10 A to zero in 0.5 s. If the average emf induced in the coil is 220 V, the self-inductance of the coil is:
questions.explanation: Emf induced in the coil of self-inductance L $$ e=-\frac{d\phi}{d t}=-\frac{d}{d t}(L i) $$ or $$ e=-L\frac{di}{dt} $$ $$ \left(\frac{di}{dt}=\text{rate of flow of current in coil}\right) $$ As, $$ di=i_{2}-i_{1}=0-10=-10\mathrm{A} $$ $$ dt=0.5s $$ $$ e=220\mathrm{~V} $$ $$ 220=-\mathrm{L}\ \frac{(-10)}{05} $$ or $$ L=\frac{220}{20}=11H $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: If the number of turns per unit length of a coil of solenoid is doubled, the self-inductance of the solenoid will:
questions.explanation: A long solenoid is one, whose length is significantly greater than its cross-section radius. If N is the total number of turns in the solenoid, A is the area of each turn and l is the length of the solenoid, then the self-inductance of the solenoid is, $$ \begin{aligned}\mathrm{L}&=\frac{\mu_{0}\mathrm{N}^{2}\mathrm{A}}{l}\\\mathrm{L}&=\mu_{0}n^{2}\mathrm{Al}\end{aligned} $$ (If the number of turns per unit length is $n$) So, $L \propto n^{2}$ When $n^{2}$ is doubled, L becomes 4 times.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: The variation of EMF with time for four types of generator are shown in the figures. Which amongst them can be called AC? (1) E (II) (III) Options:
questions.explanation: A current which changes its direction periodically is called alternating current.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A wire loop is rotated in a magnetic field. The frequency of change of direction of the induced emf is:
questions.explanation: Consider that the loop is starting to rotate from its initial position where it is perpendicular to the magnetic field. Thus, initially the maximum flux is passing through the loop. When the wired loop rotates, then the flux passing through the loop starts to decrease. When it becomes parallel to the magnetic field, then the flux becomes zero and then it starts to increase. Thus, there are two changes in one time period (i.e., twice per revolution).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A sheet is placed on a horizontal surface in front of a strong magnetic pole. A force is needed to: (I) hold the sheet there if it is magnetic (II) hold the sheet there if it is non-magnetic. (III) move the sheet away from the pole with uniform velocity if it is conducting. (IV) move the sheet away from the pole with uniform velocity if it is both, non-conducting and non-polar. Choose the correct statement(s) from the options given below:
questions.explanation: If sheet is magnetic, then force is required to hold the sheet because magnetic pole exert force on sheet. As sheet is conducting, eddy current form which opposes the way by which it is produced, so force is required. As sheet is non-conducting and non-polar, so no magnetization occurs in sheet; hence no force is required.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: In which of the following devices, the eddy current effect is NOT used?
questions.explanation: Eddy current are the loops of electrical current induced within conductor by a changing magnetic field. According to Faraday's law of induction, Eddy current flows in closed loops within conductors, in plane perpendicular to the magnetic field. Electric heaters are not based on the eddy current effect. It is based on joule's heating effect.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.subtopic: AC Voltage Applied to a Resistor
questions.question: The peak voltage of the AC source is equal to:
questions.optionA: the value of voltage supplied to the circuit
questions.optionB: the rms value of the source
questions.optionC: $ \sqrt{2} $ times the rms value of the AC source
questions.optionD: $ \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} $ times the rms value of the AC source
questions.explanation: We know that, $ V_{rms} = \frac{V_{o}}{\sqrt{2}} $ $ \Rightarrow $ $ V_{0}=\sqrt{2}V_{rms} $

questions.subject: Physics
questions.subtopic: AC Voltage Applied to an Inductor
questions.question: In the circuit shown below, the inductance L is connected to an AC source. The current flowing in the circuit is $ I = I_{0} \sin \omega t $. The voltage drop ( $ V_{L} $) across L is:
questions.explanation: $$ \mathbf{V}_{\mathrm{L}}=\mathrm{L}\frac{d\mathrm{I}}{dt} $$ The current in the circuit is $$ I\left(t\right)=I_{0}\sin\omega t $$ $$ \frac{d\mathrm{I}}{dt}=\frac{d}{dt}\mathrm{(I_{0}\sin\omega t)} $$ $$ =\mathrm{I}_{0}\omega\cos(\omega t) $$ $$ \mathrm{V_{L}}=\mathrm{L}\frac{d\mathrm{I}}{dt}=\mathrm{L}.\mathrm{I_{0}}\omega\cos\left(\omega t\right) $$ $$ \mathbf{V}_{\mathrm{L}}=\mathrm{L}\mathbf{I}_{0}\boldsymbol{\omega}\cos\left(\omega t\right) $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.subtopic: AC Voltage Applied to an Inductor
questions.question: A light bulb and an inductor coil are connected to an AC source through a key as shown in the figure below. The key is closed and after sometime an iron rod is inserted into interior of inductor. The glow of light bulb:
questions.explanation:
The diagram is shown in the figure. According to the question, the key is closed and when the iron rod is inserted inside the inductor, then inductance (L) of the coil increases.
When L increases, Then inductive reactance $ X_{L} = \omega L $ also increases. When $ X_{L} $ increases, Then current I decreases which is confirmed from the equation. $$ I=\frac{e}{X_{L}} $$ Since, $ X_{L} $ increases, hence I decreases and the glow of the bulb also decreases.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.subtopic: AC Voltage Applied to an Inductor
questions.question: A coil of self-inductance L is connected in series with a bulb B and AC source. Brightness of the bulb decreases when:
questions.optionA: frequency of the AC source is decreased.
questions.optionB: number of turns in the coil is reduced.
questions.optionC: a capacitance of reactance  $ X_{C} = X_{L} $ is included in the same circuit.
questions.optionD: an iron rod is inserted in the coil.
questions.explanation: We know that, $$ Z=\sqrt{R^{2}+X_{L}^{2}}=\sqrt{R^{2}+(2\pi fL)^{2}} $$ and $$ I=\frac{\mathbf{V}}{Z},\mathrm{P}=\mathrm{I}^{2}\mathrm{R} $$ When an iron rod is inserted in the coil, self-inductance increases and the current decreases. Hence, the brightness of the bulb decreases.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.subtopic: AC Voltage Applied to a Capacitor
questions.question: The steady state current in the circuit shown below is:
questions.explanation:
$$ \mathrm{R}=2\Omega,\mathrm{R}_{1}5\Omega,\mathrm{R}_{2}=3\Omega $$
$$ I=\frac{E}{R+R_{2}}=\frac{10}{2+3}=\frac{10}{5}=2\ A $$ ##### Related Theory In a steady state DC circuit, the capacitor is fully charged and acts as an open circuit.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.subtopic: AC Voltage Applied to a Capacitor
questions.question: A 10 µF capacitor is connected to a 210 V, 50 Hz source as shown in figure. The peak current in the circuit is nearly ( $ \pi = 3.14 $): 210 V. 50 Hz
questions.explanation:
Given: $$ \mathrm{V}_{r m s}=210\mathrm{V}, $$ $$ \begin{aligned}\mathrm{V}_{0}&=210\sqrt{2}\mathrm{~V}\\&\mathrm{C}=10\mu\mathrm{F}\end{aligned} $$
210 V, 50 Hz $$ \mathbf{V}_{0}=\mathbf{I}_{0}\mathbf{X}_{c} $$ $$ \mathrm{V}_{0}=\mathrm{I}_{0}\times\frac{1}{\omega\mathrm{C}} $$ $$ V_{0}=1_{0}\times\frac{1}{2\pi\times50\times10\times10^{-6}} $$ $$ l_{0}=210\sqrt{2}\times2\pi\times500\times10^{-6} $$ $$ l_{0}=0.93A $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.subtopic: AC Voltage Applied to a Capacitor
questions.question: An AC source is connected to a capacitor C. Due to decrease in its operating frequency:
questions.explanation:
Capacitive inductance. $$ X_{C}=\frac{1}{2\pi fC} $$ As frequency decreases, $X_{C}$ increases
So, conductive current decreases. Hence, displacement current also decreases.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.subtopic: AC Voltage Applied to a Capacitor
questions.question: A 40 μF capacitor is connected to a 200 V, 50 Hz AC supply. The rms value of the current in the circuit is, nearly:
questions.explanation: From the question. $$ \mathrm{C}=40\mu\mathrm{F}=40\times10^{-6}\mathrm{~F},\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{rms}}=200\mathrm{~V},f=50\mathrm{~Hz} $$ $$ \begin{aligned}X_{C}&=\frac{1}{2\pi f C}=\frac{1}{2\pi\times50\times40\times10^{-6}}\\&=\frac{10^{6}}{100\pi\times40}=\frac{250}{\pi}\Omega\end{aligned} $$ $$ I_{rms}=\frac{V_{rms}}{X_{C}}=\frac{200}{\underbrace{250}_{\pi}}=2.5A. $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.subtopic: AC Voltage Applied to a Capacitor
questions.question: A 100 Ω resistance and capacitor of 100 Ω reactance are connected in series across a 220 V source. When the capacitor is 50% charged, the peak value of the displacement current is:
questions.explanation: We know that, Impedance of R-C circuit. $$ Z=\sqrt{R^{2}+X_{c}^{2}} $$ Here $$ R=100\ \Omega,X_{C}=100\ \Omega $$ $$ \bullet \quad \bullet\bullet $$ $$ Z=\sqrt{(100)^{2}+(100)^{2}}=100\sqrt{2}\Omega $$ At 50% charged, $ V_{max} = 220\sqrt{2} $ V Peak value of current, $$ I_{max}=\frac{V_{max}}{Z}=\frac{220\sqrt{2}}{100\sqrt{2}}=2.2A $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.subtopic: AC Voltage Applied to a Capacitor
questions.question: The reactance of a capacitor of capacitance C is X. If both the frequency and capacitance be doubled, then new reactance will be:
questions.explanation: When an AC signal is coupled to a capacitor with capacitance C, the circuit's reactance is simply capacitive. We have, the capacitive reactance. $$ X_{C}=\frac{1}{\omega C}=\frac{1}{2\pi f C} $$ $$ x_{c}\propto\frac{1}{fC} $$ Considering two different cases of frequency and capacitance, we have $$ \frac{X^{\prime}}{X}=\frac{fC}{f^{\prime}C^{\prime}}=\frac{f\times C}{2f\times2C} $$ $$ [\because f^{\prime}=2f\text{and}C^{\prime}=2C] $$ $$ \frac{X^{\prime}}{X}=\frac{1}{4} $$ $$ \begin{aligned}X^{\prime}=\frac{X}{4}\end{aligned} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.subtopic: AC Voltage Applied to a Series LCR Circuit
questions.question: The amplitude of the charge oscillating in a circuit decreases exponentially as $Q = Q_{0}e^{-Rt/2L}$, where $Q_{0}$ is the charge at $t = 0$ s. The time at which charge amplitude decreases to $0.50 Q_{0}$ is nearly: [Given that $R = 1.5\Omega$, $L = 12\mathrm{mH}$, $\ln(2) = 0.693$].

questions.subject: Physics
questions.subtopic: AC Voltage Applied to a Series LCR Circuit
questions.question: In a series LCR circuit, the inductance L is 10 mH, capacitance C is 1 μF and resistance R is 100 Ω. The frequency at which resonance occurs is:
questions.explanation: Given, L = 10 mH = $ 10 \times 10^{-3} $ H $$ C=1\mu F=1\times10^{-6}F $$ $$ \mathrm{R=100\Omega} $$ Resonance frequency, $$ \begin{aligned}f_{0}&=\frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{1}{\mathrm{LC}}}\\&=\frac{1}{2\times3.14}\sqrt{\frac{1}{10\times10^{-3}\times10^{-6}}}\\&=\frac{1}{2\times3.14}\sqrt{\frac{10^{8}}{1}}\\&=\frac{10^{4}}{2\times3.14}=1592.3\mathrm{~Hz}=1.59\mathrm{~kJ}\end{aligned} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.subtopic: AC Voltage Applied to a Series LCR Circuit
questions.question: The net impedance of circuit (as shown in figure) will be:
questions.explanation: Here: $$ \mathrm{L}=\frac{50}{\pi}\mathrm{~mH}=\frac{50}{\pi}\times10^{-3}\mathrm{~H} $$ Then, $$ \begin{aligned}f&=50\ Hz\\ X_{L}&=2\pi f L\\&=2\pi\times50\times\frac{50}{\pi}\times10^{-3}\\&=5\ \Omega\end{aligned} $$ Similarly, $$ X_{C}=\frac{1}{2\pi fC} $$ $$ X_{C}=\frac{1}{2\pi\times50\times\frac{10^{3}}{\pi}\times10^{-6}}=10\ \Omega $$ As impedance. $$ Z=\sqrt{R^{2}+(X_{C}-X_{L})^{2}} $$ $$ Z=\sqrt{(10)^{2}+(10-5)^{2}} $$ $$ =5\sqrt{5}\Omega $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.subtopic: AC Voltage Applied to a Series LCR Circuit
questions.question: A series LCR circuit with inductance 10 H, capacitance 10 μF, resistance 50 Ω is connected to the AC source of voltage, V = 200 sin (100 t) volt. If the resonant frequency of the LCR circuit is $ v_{0} $ and the frequency of the AC source is v, then:
questions.explanation: Given. $$ \begin{aligned}&C=10\mu F,R=50\Omega,L=10H\\&\omega=2\pi f\\ \end{aligned} $$ $$ \mathrm{v}=\frac{\omega}{2\pi}=\frac{100}{2\pi}=\frac{50}{\pi}\mathrm{Hz} $$ $$ \begin{align*}Resonance~frequency,~v_{0}&=\frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{LC}}\\&=\frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{10\times10\times10^{-6}}}\\&=\frac{100}{2\pi}\\&=50\end{align*} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.subtopic: AC Voltage Applied to a Series LCR Circuit
questions.question: An inductor of inductance L, a capacitor of capacitance C and a resistor of resistance 'R' are connected in series to an AC source of potential difference 'V' volts as shown in figure. Potential difference across L, C and R is 40 V, 10 V and 40 V respectively. The amplitude of current flowing through LCR series circuit is $10\sqrt{2}$ A. The impedance of the circuit is:
questions.explanation:
Given. $$ \begin{array}{c}\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{L}}=40\mathrm{V}\\\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{C}}=10\mathrm{V}\\\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{R}}=40\mathrm{V}\\\mathrm{I}_{0}=10\sqrt{2}\end{array} $$ We know that. $$ \mathrm{V_{rms}=\sqrt{V_{R}^{2}+(V_{L}-V_{C})^{2}}} $$ ： $$ \mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{rms}}=\sqrt{1600+900}=50\mathrm{~V} $$ $ \therefore $ $$ I_{rms}=\frac{I_{0}}{\sqrt{2}}=\frac{10\sqrt{2}}{\sqrt{2}}=10\ A $$ $$ Z=\frac{\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{rms}}}{\mathrm{I}_{\mathrm{rms}}}=\frac{50}{10}=5\Omega $$
##### Mnemonics LEI the CIE For an inductive [L] circuit, the EMF(E) is ahead of the current (I). While for a capacitive circuit (C), the current (I) is ahead of EMF (E)

questions.subject: Physics
questions.subtopic: AC Voltage Applied to a Series LCR Circuit
questions.question: A circuit when connected to an AC source of 12 V gives a current of 0.2 A. The same circuit when connected to a DC source of 12 V, gives a current of 0.4 A. The circuit is:
questions.explanation: For AC source, Given. $$ \mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{rms}}=12\mathrm{~V},\mathrm{I}_{\mathrm{rms}}=0.2\mathrm{~A} $$ Impedance, $$ Z=\frac{V_{rms}}{I_{rms}}=\frac{12}{0.2}=60\Omega $$ and for DC source, Given. $$ V=12V,I=0.4A $$ Resistance. $$ \mathrm{R}=\frac{\mathrm{V}}{\mathrm{I}}=\frac{12}{0.4}=30\ \Omega $$ Since, Z > R, we conclude there must be another component in the series, which is inductor. ∴ It is a series LR circuit.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.subtopic: AC Voltage Applied to a Series LCR Circuit
questions.question: Figure shows a circuit that contains three identical resistors with resistance $ R = 9.0 \, \Omega $ each, two identical inductors with inductance $ L = 2.0 \, \text{mH} $ each and an ideal battery with emf $ E = 18 \, \text{V} $. The current i through the battery just after the switch is closed, is:
questions.explanation:
Just after the switch is closed, the inductor behaves as open circuit and the capacitor behaves short circuit. The equivalent circuit of the situation is shown below. Now, these two resistors are in parallel.
$$ \begin{array}{c} \mathrm{R}_{eq}=\frac{\mathrm{R}_{1}\mathrm{R}_{2}}{\mathrm{R}_{1}+\mathrm{R}_{2}}=\frac{\mathrm{R}.\mathrm{R}}{2\mathrm{R}}=\frac{\mathrm{R}}{2}=\frac{9}{2}\Omega\\\Rightarrow\quad\text{Current}=\frac{\mathrm{E}}{\mathrm{R}_{eq}}=\frac{18}{\frac{9}{2}}=4\mathrm{~A} \end{array} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.subtopic: AC Voltage Applied to a Series LCR Circuit
questions.question: A series R-C circuit is connected to an alternating voltage source. Consider two situations: (i) When capacitor is air filled. (ii) When capacitor is mica filled. Current through resistor is i and voltage across capacitor is V then:
questions.explanation: When capacitor is filled with mica, then capacitance C increases, so $X_{C}$ decreases. In case (2), $X_{C}$ decreases, so voltage across capacitor decreases. $$ \mathbf{V}_{1}>\mathbf{V}_{2} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.subtopic: AC Voltage Applied to a Series LCR Circuit
questions.question: A coil has resistance 30 Ω and inductive reactance 20 Ω at 50 Hz frequency. If an AC source of 200 V, 100 Hz is connected across the coil, the current in the coil will be:
questions.explanation: According to the question $$ \begin{aligned}\mathrm{R}&=30\Omega\\\mathrm{X_{L}}&=20\Omega\\f&=50\mathrm{Hz}\\\mathrm{X_{L}}&=\omega\mathrm{L}=2\pi\mathrm{fL}\\20&=2\pi\times50\times\mathrm{L}\end{aligned} $$ We know when frequency of AC source in changed to 100 Hz $$ X_{L}^{\prime}=\omega^{\prime}L=2\pi\times100\times L $$ $$ \mathbf{X}_{\mathrm{L}}^{\prime}=2\pi\times50\times2\times\mathrm{L} $$ from eq. (i), we get $$ X_{L}^{^{\prime}}=20\times2=40\ \Omega $$ Impedance $$ \begin{aligned}Z&=\sqrt{R^{2}+(X_{L}^{^{\prime}})^{2}}\\&=\sqrt{(30)^{2}+(40)^{2}}\\&=\sqrt{900+1600}\end{aligned} $$ $$ Z=50\ \Omega $$ Current, $$ I=\frac{V}{Z}=\frac{200}{50}=4A $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.subtopic: AC Voltage Applied to a Series LCR Circuit
questions.question: An AC voltage is applied to a resistance R and an inductor L in series. If R and the inductive reactance are both equal to 3 Ω, the phase difference between the applied voltage and the current is:
questions.explanation: We know that $$ \tan\phi=\frac{\mathrm{X_{L}}}{\mathrm{R}} $$ and $$ \phi=\frac{3}{3} $$ $ \tan \phi = 1 $ $$ \phi=45^{\circ} $$ $$ \phi=\frac{\pi}{4}\mathrm{~r a d} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.subtopic: AC Voltage Applied to a Series LCR Circuit
questions.question: In the given circuit, the reading of voltmeter $ V_{1} $ and $ V_{2} $ are 300 V each. The reading to the voltmeter $ V_{3} $ and ammeter A are respectively:
questions.explanation:
For series LCR circuit. Voltage. $$ \mathbf{V}=\sqrt{\mathbf{V}_{R}^{2}+(\mathbf{V}_{L}-\mathbf{V}_{C})^{2}} $$ since $$ \mathbf{V}_{L}=\mathbf{V}_{C} $$ Hence, $$ \mathbf{V}=\mathbf{V}_{R}=220\mathrm{~V} $$ ∴ $$ I=\frac{V}{R}=\frac{220}{100}=2.2A $$
##### Related Theory Series LCR circuit offers minimum impedance at resonance and current at this frequency is maximum.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.subtopic: AC Voltage Applied to a Series LCR Circuit
questions.question: What is the value of inductance L for which the current is maximum in a series LCR circuit with $C = 10\ \mu\mathrm{F}$ and $\omega = 1000\ \mathrm{s}^{-1}$?
questions.optionB: cannot be calculated unless R is known
questions.explanation:
According to question, current is maximum at resonance $$ \begin{aligned}\omega^{2}&=\frac{1}{\mathrm{LC}}\\\mathrm{L}&=\frac{1}{\omega^{2}\mathrm{C}}\\&=\frac{1}{(1000)^{2}(10\times10^{-6})}\\&=0.1\mathrm{~H}=100\mathrm{~mH}\end{aligned} $$
##### Related Theory For resonance to occur in LCR-Circuit, the presence of both capacitor (C) and inductor (L) is necessary. Resonance cannot be obtained in LR and LC circuit.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.subtopic: AC Voltage Applied to a Series LCR Circuit
questions.question: In a circuit, L, C and R are connected in series with an alternating voltage source of frequency f. The current leads the voltage by $45^{\circ}$. The value of C is:
questions.explanation: Phase difference between voltage and current is $$ \begin{array}{c}\tan\phi=\frac{\mathrm{X_{C}}-\mathrm{X_{L}}}{\mathrm{R}}\\\tan\left(\frac{\pi}{4}\right)=\frac{\frac{1}{\omega\mathrm{C}}-\omega\mathrm{L}}{\mathrm{R}}\\\Rightarrow\quad\mathrm{R}=\frac{1}{\omega\mathrm{C}}-\omega\mathrm{L}\ $ \mathrm{R}+2\pi f\mathrm{L})=\frac{1}{2\pi f\mathrm{C}}\\\text{or}\quad\mathrm{C}=\frac{1}{2\pi f(\mathrm{R}+2\pi f\mathrm{L})}\end{array} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.subtopic: AC Voltage Applied to a Series LCR Circuit
questions.question: A coil of 40 Henry inductance is connected in series with a resistance of 8 Ω and the combination is joined to the terminals of a 2 V battery. The time constant of the circuit is:
questions.explanation: According to the question, Time constant for LR circuit is, $ \tau = \frac{L}{R} $ $$ \tau=\frac{40}{8}=5s $$ ：

questions.subject: Physics
questions.subtopic: AC Voltage Applied to a Series LCR Circuit
questions.question: An LCR series circuit is connected to a source of alternating current. At resonance, the applied voltage and the current flowing through the circuit will have a phase difference of:
questions.explanation: An LCR circuit's impedance (Z) is given by, $$ Z=\sqrt{\mathbf{R}^{2}+\left(\omega\mathbf{L}-\frac{1}{\omega\mathbf{C}}\right)^{2}} $$ At resonance, $ X_{L}=X_{C} $ i.e. $$ \omega L=\frac{1}{\omega C}or Z=R $$ As a result, the circuit behaves as if it just has R. So, phase difference = 0

questions.subject: Physics
questions.subtopic: AC Voltage Applied to a Series LCR Circuit
questions.question: The time constant of C-R circuit is:
questions.explanation: We have. $$ \mathrm{R}=\frac{\mathrm{V}}{i}\text{and}\mathrm{C}=\frac{q}{\mathrm{V}} $$ $$ \mathrm{RC}=\frac{\mathrm{V}}{\mathrm{i}}\frac{\mathrm{q}}{\mathrm{V}}=\frac{\mathrm{q}}{\mathrm{i}}=\frac{\mathrm{i}\times\mathrm{t}}{\mathrm{i}}=\mathrm{t} $$ $$ [{\cal R}C]=[t]=[T] $$ CR is referred to as the CR circuit's time constant.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.subtopic: Power in AC Circuit: The Power Factor
questions.question: A series LCR circuit containing 5.0 H inductor, 80 μF capacitor and 40 Ω resistor is connected to 230 V variable frequency AC source. The angular frequencies of the source at which power transferred to the circuit is half the power at the resonant angular frequency are likely to be:
questions.explanation: Given, $$ \begin{aligned}&L=5\ H\\&C=80\ \mu F\\ \end{aligned} $$ $$ \mathrm{R}=40\ \Omega $$ $$ V=230V $$ Let the frequencies be, $$ \omega^{\prime}=\omega_{R}\pm\Delta\omega $$ where, ∴ $$ \Delta\omega=\frac{R}{2L} $$ $$ \Delta\omega=\frac{40}{2\times5}=4rad/s $$ And $$ \omega_{R}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{LC}}=50rad/s $$ $\therefore$ Frequencies are 50 - 4 and 50 + 4 = 46 rad/s and 54 rad/s

questions.subject: Physics
questions.subtopic: Power in AC Circuit: The Power Factor
questions.question: A series L-C-R circuit is connected to an AC voltage source. When L is removed from the circuit, the phase difference between current and voltage is $\frac{\pi}{3}$. If instead C is removed from the circuit, the phase difference is again $\frac{\pi}{3}$ between current and voltage. The power factor of the circuit is:
questions.explanation:
For LCR circuit phase difference, $$ \phi=\tan^{-1}\left[\frac{\mathbf{X}_{L}-\mathbf{X}_{C}}{\mathbf{R}}\right] $$ When L is removed, then $ X_{L} = 0 $ $$ \phi=\tan^{-1}\left(\frac{-\mathrm{X}_{\mathrm{c}}}{\mathrm{R}}\right) $$ $ \tan \phi = \left| \frac{X_c}{R} \right| $
$$ \phi=\frac{\pi}{3} $$
When C is removed. then $$ x_{c}=0 $$ $$ \phi=\tan^{-1}\left(\frac{\mathbf{X}_{L}}{\mathbf{R}}\right) $$ $$ \phi=\left(\frac{\mathbf{X}_{\perp}}{\mathbf{R}}\right) $$ From eq. (i) and eq. (ii), we get $$ \mathbf{X}_{\mathrm{L}}=\mathbf{X}_{\mathrm{C}} $$ $\therefore$ Impedance, $Z=\sqrt{R^{2}+(X_{L}-X_{C})^{2}}$ $$ Z=R $$ Power Factor, $$ \cos\phi=\frac{\mathrm{R}}{\mathrm{Z}}=1 $$ ∴ Power factor, $ \cos\phi=1 $.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.subtopic: Power in AC Circuit: The Power Factor
questions.question: The potential differences across the resistance, capacitance and inductance are 80 V, 40 V and 100 V respectively in an L-C-R circuit. The power factor of this circuit is:
questions.explanation: For this, we know the formula, $$ \tan\phi=\frac{V_{L}-V_{C}}{V_{R}}=\frac{100-40}{80}=\frac{3}{4} $$ $$ \phi=37^{\circ} $$ ∴ Power factor = $ \cos\phi = \cos37^{\circ} $ $$ =0.8 $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.subtopic: Power in AC Circuit: The Power Factor
questions.question: An inductor of 20 mH, a capacitor of 100 μF and a resistor of 50 Ω are connected in series across a source of emf, V = 10 sin 314t. The power loss in the circuit is:
questions.explanation: Given, Inductance, $ L = 20 \, \text{mH} = 20 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{H} $ Capacitance. $$ C=100\mu F=100\times10^{-6}F $$ Resistance. $$ R=50\Omega $$ $$ \mathbf{V}=10\sin314t $$ and equation of emf is, $$ \mathbf{V}=\mathbf{V}_{0}\sin\omega t $$ Compare eq. (i) and (ii), we get $$ V_{0}=10\ V\ and\ \omega=314\ rad\ s^{-1} $$ Power of the AC circuit is, $$ \begin{aligned}\mathrm{P}&=\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{rms}}\mathrm{I}_{\mathrm{rms}}\cos\phi\\&=\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{rms}}\left(\frac{\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{rms}}}{Z}\right)\left(\frac{\mathrm{R}}{Z}\right)=\left(\frac{\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{rms}}}{Z}\right)^{2}\mathrm{R}\end{aligned} $$ $$ \mathrm{P}=\left(\frac{\mathrm{V}_{0}}{\sqrt{2}.Z}\right)^{2}.฿ $$ Impedance, $$ \begin{aligned}\boldsymbol{Z}&=\sqrt{\mathbf{R}^{2}+(\overline{\mathbf{X}_{\mathrm{L}}-\mathbf{X}_{\mathrm{C}}})^{2}}\\&=\sqrt{\mathbf{R}^{2}+\left(\omega\mathbf{L}-\frac{1}{\omega\mathbf{C}}\right)^{2}}\end{aligned} $$ $$ \begin{array}{l}=\sqrt{(50)^{2}+\left(314\times20\times10^{-3}-\frac{1}{314\times100\times10^{-6}}\right)^{2}}\\=\sqrt{2500+(-25.56)^{2}}\approx56\Omega\end{array} $$ Putting the value of $Z = 56\ \Omega$ in eq. (iii), we get $$ \mathbf{P}=\left(\frac{10}{\sqrt{2}\times56}\right)^{2}\times50 $$ $$ P=\frac{100}{2\times3136}\times50 $$ $$ \mathrm{P}=0.79\mathrm{~W} $$ ∴ Power loss = 0.79 W

questions.subject: Physics
questions.subtopic: Power in AC Circuit: The Power Factor
questions.question: A resistance 'R' draws power 'P' when connected to an AC source. If an inductance is introduced such that the impedance of the circuit becomes 'Z', the power drawn will be:
questions.explanation: According to the question, the power drawn is $$ P=V_{rms}\cdot I_{rms}=V_{rms}\cdot\frac{V_{rms}}{R} $$ $$ \Rightarrow\mathrm{~\boldmath~V~}_{\mathrm{r m s}}^{2}=\mathrm{P R} $$ When inductor is connected in series with the resistor, then power drawn is, $$ \mathbf{P}^{\prime}=\mathbf{V}_{\mathrm{rms}}\ .I_{\mathrm{rms}}\cos\ \phi $$ where, $ \phi = \text{phase difference} $ ∴ $$ \mathrm{P}^{\prime}=\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{rms}}.\frac{\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{rms}}}{\mathrm{Z}}.\frac{\mathrm{R}}{\mathrm{Z}} $$ $$ \frac{V_{rms}^{2}}{R}\cdot\frac{R^{2}}{Z^{2}} $$ $ \therefore $ $$ \mathrm{P}^{\prime}=\frac{\mathrm{P}.\mathrm{R}^{2}}{\mathrm{Z}^{2}}=\mathrm{P}\left(\frac{\mathrm{R}}{\mathrm{Z}}\right)^{2} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.subtopic: Power in AC Circuit: The Power Factor
questions.question: The instantaneous value of alternating current and voltage in a circuit are given as: $ i = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \sin (100\pi t) $ ampere $ e = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \sin \left(100\pi t + \frac{\pi}{3}\right) $volt The average power (in Watts) consumed in the circuit is:
questions.explanation: $$ \begin{aligned}\mathrm{P}&=\mathrm{V}_{\text{rms}}\ \mathrm{I}_{\text{rms}}\cos\phi\\&=\frac{1}{2}\times\frac{1}{2}\times\cos\left(\frac{\pi}{3}\right)=\frac{1}{8}\mathrm{W}\end{aligned} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.subtopic: Power in AC Circuit: The Power Factor
questions.question: In an AC circuit, the emf (e) and the current (i) at any instant are given respectively by: $ e = E_0 \sin \omega t $ $ i = I_0 \sin (\omega t - \phi) $ The average power in the circuit over one cycle of AC is:
questions.explanation: Power = rate of work done in one complete cycle, $$ \mathbf{P}_{\omega}=\frac{\mathbf{W}}{\mathrm{T}} $$ $$ \mathrm{P}_{\omega}=\frac{(\varepsilon_{0}\mathrm{I}_{0}\cos\phi)\mathrm{T}/2}{\mathrm{T}} $$ $$ \mathbf{P}_{\omega}=\frac{\varepsilon_{0}\mathbf{I}_{0}\cos\phi}{2} $$ where, $ \cos \phi $ is called the power factor of an AC circuit.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.subtopic: Power in AC Circuit: The Power Factor
questions.question: A coil of inductive reactance 31 Ω and a resistance of 8 Ω. It is placed in series with a condenser of capacitative reactance 25 Ω. The combination is connected to an AC source of 110 V. The power factor of the circuit is:
questions.explanation: $ X_L = 31\ \Omega, X_C = 25\ \Omega, R = 8\ \Omega $ ∴ Impedance of series LCR is $$ \mathbf{Z}=\sqrt{\mathbf{R}^{2}+(\mathbf{X}_{L}-\mathbf{X}_{C})^{2}} $$ $$ \mathrm{Z}=\sqrt{8^{2}+(31-25)^{2}} $$ $$ \mathrm{Z}=\sqrt{64+36} $$ $$ Z=\sqrt{100}=10\Omega $$ Power factor, $ \cos\phi=\frac{R}{Z}=\frac{8}{10}=0.8 $

questions.subject: Physics
questions.subtopic: Power in AC Circuit: The Power Factor
questions.question: For a series LCR circuit, the power loss at resonance is:
questions.explanation: Power loss is given by, $$ \mathbf{P}=\mathbf{V}\mathbf{I}\cos\boldsymbol{\phi} $$ At resonance, current and voltage are in the same phase, that is $ \phi = 0 $. $ \therefore $ $$ \begin{aligned}\mathbf{P}&=\mathbf{V}\mathbf{I}\cos0^{\circ}\\&=\mathbf{V}\mathbf{I}=\mathbf{I}^{2}\mathbf{R}\end{aligned} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A step-up transformer is connected to an AC mains supply of 220 V to operate at 11000 V, 88 watt. The current in the secondary circuit, ignoring the power loss in the transformer, is:
questions.explanation:
Given, Primary voltage, $ V_{P} = 220 \, V $ Secondary voltage, $ V_{S} = 11,000 V $ Power. $$ \mathbf{P}=88\ \mathrm{W} $$ $$ \mathbf{P}_{\mathbf{P}}=\mathbf{P}_{\mathbf{S}}=\mathbf{P} $$ $$ \mathbf{P}_{\mathbf{S}}=\mathbf{V}_{\mathbf{S}}\mathbf{l}_{\mathbf{S}} $$ $$ I_{S}=\frac{P_{s}}{V_{s}} $$ $$ I_{S}=\frac{88W}{11000V} $$ $$ l_{S}=0.008A $$ $$ \mathrm{I_{S\delta}=8\ m A} $$
##### Related Theory The relationship between current voltage and number of turns in the primary and secondary coils of the transformer is given by, $$ \frac{I_{P}}{I_{S}}=\frac{V_{S}}{V_{P}}=\frac{N_{S}}{N_{P}}. $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: In an ideal transformer, the turns ratio is $ \frac{N_{p}}{N_{s}}=\frac{1}{2} $. The ratio $ V_{s}:V_{p} $ is equal to (the symbols carry their usual meaning):
questions.explanation: Given: $$ \frac{\aleph_{p}}{\aleph_{s}}=1:2 $$ $$ \eta=\frac{\mathrm{V_{s}}}{\mathrm{V_{p}}}=\frac{\mathrm{N_{s}}}{\mathrm{N_{p}}} $$ ∴ $$ \mathrm{\frac{v_{s}}{v_{p}}}={\frac{2}{1}} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A 12 V, 60 W lamp is connected to the secondary winding of a step down transformer, whose primary winding is connected to AC mains of 220 V. Assuming the transformer to be ideal, what is the current in the primary winding?
questions.explanation: Let us consider current in the primary is $ I_{p} $. As the transformer is ideal $$ \begin{array}{r} \begin{aligned}\mathrm{P}_{i}&=\mathrm{P}_{0}\\ 220\times\mathrm{I}_{p}&=60\end{aligned}\end{array} $$ or $$ I_{p}=\frac{60}{220}=\frac{3}{11}=0.27A $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A step down transformer connected to an AC mains supply of 220 V is made to operate at 11 V, 44 W lamp. Ignoring power losses in the transformer, what is the current in the primary circuit?
questions.explanation: Given, step-down transformer $$ \begin{aligned}\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{P}}&=220\mathrm{~V}\\\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{S}}&=11\mathrm{~V}\\\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{S}}&=44\mathrm{~W}\\\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{P}}&=\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{S}}\\\mathrm{I}_{\mathrm{S}}&=\frac{\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{s}}}{\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{s}}}=4\mathrm{~A}\\\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{P}}\mathrm{I}_{\mathrm{P}}&=\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{S}}\mathrm{I}_{\mathrm{S}}\\\mathrm{I}_{\mathrm{P}}&=\frac{\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{s}}\mathrm{I}_{\mathrm{s}}}{\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{P}}}=\frac{11\times4}{220}=\frac{4}{20}\\&=\frac{1}{5}=0.2\mathrm{~A}\end{aligned} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A transformer having efficiency of 90% is working on 200 V and 3 kW power supply. If the current in the secondary coil is 6 A, the voltage across the secondary coil and the current in the primary coil respectively are:
questions.explanation: According to the question, Efficiency = 90% = 0.9 Input voltage, $ V_{P} = 200 \, V $ Input power, $ P_{P} = 3000 $ W Secondary coil current, $ I_{S} = 6 \, A $ $$ \begin{aligned}V_{P}I_{P}&=3000\\ I_{P}&=\frac{3000}{V_{P}}=\frac{3000}{200}=15A\end{aligned} $$ $$ \therefore\text{Now efficiency}=\frac{\text{Output}}{\text{input}}=\frac{V_{S}I_{S}}{V_{P}I_{P}} $$ $$ 0.9=\frac{V_{s}\times6}{200\times15} $$ $$ \mathrm{V_{S}}=450~\mathrm{V} $$ ∴ Secondary coil current = 450 V.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: The primary winding of transformer when connected to a DC battery of 10 Volt draws a current of 1 mA. The number of turns of the primary and secondary windings are 50 and 100 respectively. The voltage in the secondary and the current drawn by the circuit in the secondary are respectively:
questions.optionC: Zero volt and therefore no current
questions.explanation: Transformer can not work on DC.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A 220 V input is supplied to a transformer. The output circuit draws a current of 2.0 A at 440 V. If the efficiency of the transformer is 80%, the current drawn by the primary windings of the transformer is:
questions.explanation: Efficiency, $$ ↳\Downarrow $$ $$ \begin{aligned}\eta\%&=\frac{P_{out}}{P_{in}}\times100=\frac{V_{s}i_{s}}{V_{p}i_{p}}\times100\\80&=\frac{2\times440}{220\times i_{p}}\times100\\i_{P}&=5A\end{aligned} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: The primary and secondary coils of a transformer have 50 and 1500 turns respectively.
