If the magnetic flux $ \phi $ linked with the primary coil is given by $ \phi = \phi_{v} + 4t $, where $ \phi $ is in webers, t is time in seconds and $ \phi_{v} $ is a constant, the output voltage across the secondary coil is:
questions.explanation: According to the question, $$ \mathrm{N_{P}=50,N_{S}=1500} $$ and $$ \phi=\phi_{\mathrm{v}}+4\mathrm{t} $$ Voltage across primary coil, $$ \mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{P}}=\frac{d\phi}{d t}=\frac{d}{d t}(\phi_{\mathrm{v}}+4\mathrm{t}) $$ $$ =4V $$ Now $$ \mathrm{\frac{V_{S}}{V_{P}}}={\mathrm{\frac{N_{S}}{N_{P}}}} $$ $$ V_{S}=\frac{1500}{50}\times4=120V $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A transformer is used to light a 100 W and 110 V lamp from a 220 V mains. If the main current is 0.5 amp, the efficiency of the transformer is approximately:
questions.explanation: The efficiency of transformer, $$ \begin{array}{l} \eta =\frac{P_S}{V_A I_P} \times 100 \\ =\frac{100}{220 \times 0.5} \times 100 \approx 90\% \end{array} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: The core of a transformer is laminated because:
questions.optionA: ratio of voltage in primary and secondary may be increased.
questions.optionB: energy, losses due to eddy currents may be minimised.
questions.optionC: the weight of the transformer may be reduced.
questions.optionD: rusting of the core may be prevented.
questions.explanation: The core of a transformers is laminated to minimise the energy losses due to eddy currents.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A step-up transformer operates on a 230 V line and supplies current of 2 A to a load. The ratio of the primary and secondary windings is 1:25. The current in the primary coil is:
questions.explanation:
For step-up transformer $$ \frac{\mathbf{V}_{\mathrm{S}}}{\mathbf{V}_{\mathrm{P}}}\;=\;\frac{\mathbf{N}_{\mathrm{S}}}{\mathbf{N}_{\mathrm{P}}}\;=\;\frac{\mathbf{l}_{\mathrm{P}}}{\mathbf{l}_{\mathrm{S}}} $$ Here, $$ \mathrm{\frac{N_{p}}{N_{s}}} = \frac{1}{25} $$ or $$ l_{S}=2\ A $$ $$ \frac{1_{P}}{2}=\frac{25}{1} $$ or $$ l_{P}=25\times2=50A $$
##### Related Theory We have the change in flux of the primary and secondary coils is proportional to the number of turns in the primary and secondary coils, respectively. So, $$ \frac{\phi_{P}}{N_{P}}=\frac{\phi_{S}}{N_{S}} $$ or $$ \frac{1}{N_{P}}\times\frac{d\phi_{P}}{dt}=\frac{1}{N_{S}}\frac{d\phi_{S}}{dt} $$ $$ \frac{V_{S}}{V_{P}}=\frac{N_{S}}{N_{P}} $$ $$ \left(a s V\propto\frac{d\phi}{d t}\right) $$ If there is no power loss, $$ VI=constant $$ $$ I=\frac{1}{V}\times\mathrm{constant} $$ or $$ \frac{I_{P}}{I_{S}}=\frac{V_{S}}{V_{P}}or\frac{I_{P}}{I_{S}}=\frac{N_{S}}{N_{P}} $$ $I_{p}$ and $I_{s}$ are currents in primary and secondary coils respectively.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: The primary winding of transformer has 500 turns whereas its secondary has 5000 turns. The primary is connected to an AC supply of 20 V-50 Hz. The secondary will have an output of:
questions.explanation:
The transformer converts high-voltage AC current into low-voltage AC current without changing the frequency. The voltage across the input is divided by the voltage across the output. We have, The voltage is given by, $$ \mathrm{\frac{Vs}{V_{p}}}={\frac{N_{s}}{N_{p}}} $$ $ N_{S} $ = Number of turns in secondary coil $ N_{P}= $ Number of turns in primary coil $$ \mathbf{V}_{\mathrm{S}}=\frac{\mathbf{N}_{\mathrm{s}}}{\mathbf{N}_{\mathrm{p}}}\mathbf{V}_{\mathrm{P}} $$ Substitute the given values, we get $$ =\frac{5000}{500}\times20=200V $$ Thus, output has voltage 200 V and frequency 50 Hz.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A parallel plate capacitor is charged by connecting it to a battery through a resistor. If I is the current in the circuit, then in the gap between the plates:
questions.optionA: displacement current of magnitude equal to I flow in the same direction as I. @THE_RDX 07
questions.optionB: displacement current of magnitude equal to I flows in a direction opposite to that of I.
questions.optionC: displacement current of magnitude greater than I flows but can be in any direction.
questions.explanation: A capacitor a being charged then the displacement current is $$ I=\varepsilon\frac{d(\phi)}{dt} $$ $$ I=\varepsilon_{0}\frac{d}{dt}\left(EA\right) $$ $$ \mathrm{I}=\varepsilon_{0}\frac{d}{dt}\left(\frac{\mathrm{V}}{d}\mathrm{A}\right) $$ $$ \mathrm{I}=\frac{\varepsilon_{0}\mathrm{A}}{d}\frac{d}{dt}\left(\frac{\mathrm{Q}}{\mathrm{C}}\right) $$ $$ \mathrm{I}=\frac{d\mathrm{Q}}{dt}=\mathrm{I}_{\text{conduction}}\quad\left(\because\mathrm{C}=\frac{\varepsilon_{0}\mathrm{A}}{d}\right) $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A capacitor of capacitance ‘C’ is connected across an AC source of voltage V, given by $ V = V_{0}\sin\omega t $. The displacement current between the plates of the capacitor, would then be given by:
questions.explanation: Displacement Current, $$ I_{d}=\frac{CdV}{dt} $$ $$ \mathrm{I}_{d}=\mathrm{C}\frac{d}{dt}\ (\mathrm{V}_{0}\sin\omega t) $$ $$ I_{d}=CV_{0}\omega\cos\omega t $$ $$ \frac{\mathrm{H}}{\mathrm{E}}=\sqrt{\frac{\varepsilon}{\mu}} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: If the ratio of relative permeability and relative permittivity of a uniform medium is 1:4. The ratio of the magnitude of electric field intensity (E) to the magnetic field intensity (H) of an EM wave propagating in that medium is: (Given that $ \sqrt{\frac{\mu_{0}}{\varepsilon_{0}}}=120\pi $):
questions.explanation: For relative permittivity and permeability, $$ \frac{\mathrm{H}}{\mathrm{E}}=\sqrt{\frac{\varepsilon_{r}\varepsilon_{0}}{\mu_{r}\mu_{0}}} $$ Given. $$ \frac{\mu_{r}}{\varepsilon_{r}}=\frac{1}{4} $$ $$ \frac{\mathrm{H}}{\mathrm{E}}\;=\;\sqrt{\frac{4\varepsilon_{0}}{1\mu_{0}}}\quad\mathrm{\quad\left(\because\mathrm{Given}\frac{\varepsilon_{0}}{\mu_{0}}=\frac{1}{120\pi}\right)} $$ $$ \begin{aligned}\frac{H}{E}&=\frac{2}{120\pi}\\&=\frac{E}{H}=60\pi\end{aligned} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: The property which is not of an electromagnetic wave travelling in free space is that:
questions.optionA: the energy density in electric field is equal to energy density in magnetic field.
questions.optionB: they travel with a speed equal to  $ \frac{1}{\sqrt{\mu_0\varepsilon_0}} $
questions.optionC: they originate from charges moving with uniform speed.
questions.explanation:
Electromagnetic waves do not originate from charges moving with uniform speed. So, this statement is wrong regarding an EM wave travelling in free space.
##### Related Theory The electromagnetic waves carry equal energy densities in electric and magnetic fields. The source of EM waves is accelerated charge instead of a charge moving with uniform velocity.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: In a plane electromagnetic wave travelling in free space, the electric field component oscillates sinusoidally at a frequency of $ 2.0 \times 10^{10} $ Hz and amplitude $ 48 \, \text{Vm}^{-1} $. Then the amplitude of oscillating magnetic field is: (Speed of light in free space = $ 3 \times 10^{8} \, \text{ms}^{-1} $)
questions.explanation: Given: $$ \mathbf{v}=2.0\times10^{10}Hz $$ $$ \mathrm{E}_{0}=48\mathrm{~V}\mathrm{m}^{-1} $$ $$ c=3\times10^{8}\,\mathrm{m s}^{-1} $$ We know that, $$ \begin{aligned}\mathrm{B}_{0}&=\frac{\mathrm{E}_{0}}{c}=\frac{48}{3\times10^{8}}\\&=1.6\times10^{-7}\mathrm{~T}\end{aligned} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: For a plane electromagnetic wave propagating in x-direction, which one of the following combination gives the correct possible directions for electric field (E) and magnetic field (B) respectively?
questions.explanation: For a plane EM wave, the E and B field are mutually perpendicular. Hence (A), (C) and (D) are not possible.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: The magnetic field in a plane electromagnetic wave is given by: $ B_{y} = 2 \times 10^{-7} \sin(\pi \times 10^{3} x + 3\pi \times 10^{11} t) $. Calculate the wavelength.
questions.explanation: The magnetic field in a plane electromagnetic wave is: $$ \mathbf{B}_{y}=\mathbf{B}_{0}\sin\left(kx-\omega t\right) $$ The given magnetic field in a plane electromagnetic wave is, $$\mathrm{B}_{y}=2\times10^{-7}\sin(\pi\times10^{3}x+3\pi\times10^{11}t)$$ comparing eq. (i) and eq. (ii) we get, $$ \begin{array}{c}k=\pi\times10^{3}\\\Rightarrow\quad\quad\quad\quad\frac{2\pi}{\lambda}=\pi\times10^{3}\\\Rightarrow\quad\quad\quad\quad\lambda=\frac{2}{10^{3}}\\=2\times10^{-3}m.\end{array} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: The ratio of contribution made by the electric field and magnetic field components, to the intensity of an electromagnetic wave is (where, $ c = \text{speed of electromagnetic waves} $):
questions.explanation: We know that, $ \frac{E_{0}}{B_{0}} = c $ Where, $ E_{0} $ = Peak value of electric field $ B_{0} $ = Peak value of magnetic field Intensity of electromagnetic wave, $$ 1=\mathrm{U}_{av}\cdot c $$ and $$ \mathrm{U}_{a\mathrm{v}}=u_{\mathrm{E}}+u_{\mathrm{B}} $$ where $u_{E}$ is average energy contribution by electric field and $u_{B}$ is average energy contribution by magnetic field. $$ \begin{aligned}u_{\mathrm{E}}&=\frac{1}{2}\varepsilon_{0}\mathrm{E}_{0}^{2}=\frac{1}{2}\varepsilon_{0}(c\mathrm{B}_{0})^{2}\\&=\frac{1}{2}\varepsilon_{0}c^{2}\mathrm{B}_{0}^{2}=\frac{1}{2\mu_{0}}\mathrm{B}_{0}^{2}\end{aligned} $$ $$ \left(\because c=\frac{1}{\sqrt{\mu_{0}\varepsilon_{0}}}\right) $$ $$ u_{\mathrm{B}}=\frac{1}{2\mu_{0}}\mathrm{B}_{0}^{2} $$ $$ u_{\mathrm{E}}:u_{\mathrm{B}}=1:1 $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: For a transparent medium relative permeability and permittivity $ \mu_r $ and $ \varepsilon_r $ are 1.0 and 1.44 respectively. The velocity of light in this medium would be:
questions.explanation: Velocity of light in medium is, $$ \mathbf{v}=\frac{c}{\sqrt{\mu_{r}\varepsilon_{r}}} $$ $$ \begin{aligned}&\mathrm{v}=\frac{3\times10^{8}}{\sqrt{1\times1.44}}\\ &\mathrm{v}=2.5\times10^{8}\mathrm{m s}^{-1}\\ \end{aligned} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: In an electromagnetic wave in free space the root mean square value of the electric field is $ E_{rms} = 6 \, V/m $. The peak value of the magnetic field is:
questions.explanation: According to question, $$ E_{rms}=6V/m $$ Peak value of electric field $$ \begin{aligned}&E_{0}=\sqrt{2}E_{rms}\\&E_{0}=\sqrt{2}\times6V/m\\ \end{aligned} $$ We know that. $$ c=\frac{E_{0}}{B_{0}} $$ $$ \begin{aligned}B_{0}&=\frac{E_{0}}{c}=\frac{\sqrt{2}\times6}{3\times10^{8}}\\&=\frac{8.48}{3}\times10^{-8}\end{aligned} $$ $$ \mathbf{B}_{0}=2.83\times10^{-8}\mathrm{T} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: An electromagnetic wave of frequency $ v = 3.0 $ MHz passes from vacuum into a dielectric medium with relative permittivity $ \varepsilon = 4.0 $ Then:
questions.optionA: wavelength is doubled and frequency becomes half.
questions.optionB: wavelength is halved and frequency remains unchanged.
questions.optionC: wavelength and frequency both remain un
questions.optionD: wavelength is doubled and frequency unchanged.
questions.explanation:
Velocity of electromagnetic wave in vacuum. $$ c=\frac{1}{\sqrt{\mu_{0}\varepsilon_{0}}}={\bf v}_{vacuum} $$ Velocity of electromagnetic wave in medium $$ \begin{array}{l}\mathrm{V}_{med}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{\mu_{0}\mu_{r}\varepsilon_{0}\ \varepsilon_{r}}}\\ \\ =\frac{c}{\sqrt{\mu_{r}\varepsilon_{r}}}\end{array} $$ $\mu_{0}$ and $\varepsilon_{0}$ is in vacuum and $\mu_{r}$ and $\varepsilon_{r}$ is in medium. For dielectric medium $\mu_{r}=1$ $$ \mathbf{v}_{\mathrm{med}}=\frac{c}{\sqrt{\varepsilon_{r}}}Given\varepsilon_{r}=4 $$ $$ \mathbf{v}_{\mathrm{med}}=\frac{c}{\sqrt{4}}=\frac{c}{2} $$ ∴ Wavelength of the wave in medium, $$ \begin{aligned}\lambda_{med}&=\frac{\mathbf{v}_{med}}{\mathbf{v}}\\&=\frac{c}{2\mathbf{v}}=\frac{\lambda_{vacuum}}{2}\end{aligned} $$
##### Related Theory Maxwell concluded that electromagnetic wave is transverse in nature and the light is an electromagnetic wave.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: The electric field of an electromagnetic wave in free space is given by: $ \vec{E} = 10 \cos (10^7 t + kx) $ $ \hat{J} $ V/m, where $ t $ and $ x $ are in seconds and metres respectively. It can be inferred that: (I) the wavelength $ \lambda $ is 188.4 m. (II) the wave number $ k $ is 0.33 rad/m. (III) the wave amplitude is 10 V/m. (IV) the wave is propagating along +x direction Which one of the following pairs of statements is correct?
questions.explanation: It is given that electric field of electromagnetic wave is: $$ \stackrel{\rightarrow}{\mathrm{E}}=10\cos\left(10^{7}t+kx\right)\stackrel{\leftarrow}{\mathrm{j}}\mathrm{V/m} $$ Here, $$ c=\frac{\omega}{k} $$ $$ 3\times10^{8}=\frac{10^{7}}{k} $$ $$ k=\frac{1}{30}=0.033rad/m $$ $$ \frac{2\pi}{\lambda}=\frac{1}{30} $$ $$ \lambda=188.4m $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: The electric field part of an electromagnetic wave in a medium is represented by: $ E_{x} = 0 $; $ E_{y}=2.5\frac{N}{C}\cos\left[\left(2\pi\times10^{6}\frac{\text{rad}}{\text{s}}\right)t\quad-\left(\pi\times10^{-2}\frac{\text{rad}}{\text{m}}\right)x\right] $ $ E_{z}=0 $ The wave is:
questions.optionA: moving along y-direction with frequency  $ 2\pi \times 10^{6} $ Hz and wavelength 200 m.
questions.optionB: moving along x-direction with frequency  $ 10^{6} $ Hz and wavelength 100 m
questions.optionC: moving along x direction with frequency  $ 10^{6} $ Hz and wavelength 200 m.
questions.optionD: moving along -x direction with frequency  $ 10^{6} $ Hz and wavelength 200 m.
questions.explanation: The standard equation is, $ E_{y} = E_{0} \cos (\omega t - kx) $ and $$ \mathrm{E}_{y}=2.5\frac{\mathrm{N}}{\mathrm{C}}\cos\left[\left(2\pi\times10^{6}\frac{\mathrm{rad}}{\mathrm{s}}\right)t-\left(\pi\times10^{-2}\frac{\mathrm{rad}}{\mathrm{m}}\right)x\right] $$ Compare the above equations we have, $$ \omega=2\pi f $$ $$ =2\pi\times10^{6} $$ and $$ f=10^{6}\mathrm{H z} $$ ∴ we know that, $$ \frac{2\pi}{\lambda}=k $$ $$ \begin{aligned}&k=\pi\times10^{-2}m^{-1}\\&\lambda=200m\\ \end{aligned} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: The wavelength of light of frequency 100 Hz is:
questions.explanation: We have, relation between velocity of light (c), frequency (v), and wavelength ( $ \lambda $) is given by $$ c=v\lambda $$ Thus, wavelength, $$ \lambda=\frac{c}{\nu} $$ Given, $ c = 3 \times 10^{8} $ m/s, $$ \mathbf{v}=100~\mathrm{H z} $$ ∴ $$ \begin{aligned}\lambda&=\frac{3\times10^{8}}{100}\\&=3\times10^{6}m\end{aligned} $$

questions.question: The minimum wavelength of X-rays produced by an electron accelerated through a potential difference of V volts is proportional to:
questions.explanation: Energy, E = h $ \nu $ = $ \frac{hc}{\lambda} $ If K.E. is maximum, thus $ \lambda $ is minimum. So, energy of emitted X-ray = energy of electron $$ \frac{hc}{\lambda_{\min}}=e\mathsf{V} $$ $$ \begin{array}{l}\Rightarrow\quad\lambda_{\min}=\frac{hc}{e}\times\frac{1}{V}\\ \Rightarrow\quad\lambda_{\min}\propto\frac{1}{V}\end{array} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: The energy of the EM waves is of the order 15 keV. To which part of the spectrum does it belong?
questions.explanation: We know, $$ E=\frac{hc}{\lambda} $$ $ \Rightarrow $ $$ \begin{aligned}\lambda&=\frac{hc}{E}=\frac{6.62\times10^{-34}\times3\times10^{8}}{15\times10^{3}\times1.6\times10^{-19}}\\&=0.828\times10^{-10}m\\\lambda&=0.828\mathring{A}\end{aligned} $$ Since, the order of wavelength is between 10 nm and 0.001 nm, it belongs to X-ray.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.subtopic: Reflection of Light by Spherical Mirrors
questions.question: An object is placed at a distance of 40 cm from a concave mirror of focal length 15 cm. If the object is displaced through a distance of 20 cm towards the mirror, the displacement of the image will be:
questions.explanation:
In this problem, we consider two cases
In Case (i) When object is placed, $ u_{1} = -40 $ cm and focal length of mirror, f = -15 cm Using the mirror formula, $$ \frac{1}{f}=\frac{1}{v_{i}}+\frac{1}{u_{i}} $$ $$ -\frac{1}{15}=\frac{1}{v_{1}}-\frac{1}{40} $$ $$ \frac{1}{v_{i}}=\frac{1}{-15}+\frac{1}{40}=-\frac{5}{120} $$ $$ v_{1}=-24cm $$ Case (ii) When object is displaced by 20 cm towards the mirror Then, $$ u_{2}=-20\mathrm{c m} $$ $$ \frac{1}{f}=\frac{1}{\mathbf{v}_{2}}+\frac{1}{u_{2}} $$ $$ -\frac{1}{15}=\frac{1}{v_{2}}-\frac{1}{20} $$ $$ \frac{1}{\mathrm{v}_{2}}=\frac{1}{20}-\frac{1}{15}=-\frac{1}{60} $$ $$ \mathbf{v}_{2}=-60\mathrm{c m} $$ So, image shifts away from mirror by $$ =60-24=36cm $$

questions.subtopic: Reflection of Light by Spherical Mirrors
questions.question: A rod of length 10 cm lies along the principal axis of a concave mirror of focal length 10 cm in such way that its end closer to the pole is 20 cm away from the mirror. The length of the image is:
questions.explanation: Image position of end A $$ \frac{1}{\mathrm{v}_{\mathrm{A}}}+\frac{1}{-20}=\frac{1}{-10} $$ $$ \Rightarrow $$ $$ \mathrm{v_{A}=-20cm} $$ Image position of end B $$ \mathrm{\frac{l}{v_{8}}+\frac{l}{-30}=}\quad\frac{1}{-10} $$ $$ \Rightarrow $$ $$ \mathrm{V_{B}=-15c m} $$ ∴ Length of the image is $$ \mathrm{A}_{1}\mathrm{B}_{1}=\left|\mathrm{v}_{\mathrm{A}}\right|-\left|\mathrm{v}_{\mathrm{B}}\right|=20-15=5\mathrm{~cm} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.subtopic: Reflection of Light by Spherical Mirrors
questions.question: If two mirrors are kept inclined at $60^{\circ}$ to each other and a body is placed at the middle, then total number of images formed, is:
questions.explanation: Many images are created when an object is held between two plane mirrors $M_{1}$ and $M_{2}$ inclined at an angle $\theta$ due to multiple reflections of light from the mirrors. The total number of images formed. $$ n=\frac{360^{\circ}}{\theta}-1 $$ Here, $ \theta = 60^{\circ} $ $$ n=\frac{360^{\circ}}{60^{\circ}}-1=6-1=5 $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Given below are two statements: Statement I: Image formation needs regular reflection and / or refraction. Statement II: The variety in colours of objects we see around us is due to the constituent colours of the light incident on them. In the light of the above statements, choose the most appropriate answer from the options given below:
questions.optionA: Statement I is correct but Statement II is incorrect.
questions.optionB: Statement I is incorrect but Statement II is correct.
questions.optionC: Both statement I and Statement II are correct.
questions.optionD: Both Statement I and Statement II are incorrect.
questions.explanation: For an image to form, regular reflection or refraction is necessary. Regular reflection occurs when light rays reflect off a smooth surface to form clear images. Refraction involves the bending of light as it passes from one medium to another, which also contributes to image formation (e.g., lenses forming images by refraction). The colours of objects are perceived based on the wavelengths of light they reflect. Objects absorb certain wavelengths of light and reflect others. The reflected light enters our eyes and is interpreted as colour. The colour of the light source (constituent colours) plays an important role in this perception. So, both statements are correct.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A light ray falls on a glass surface of refractive index $\sqrt{3}$, at an angle of $60^{\circ}$. The angle between the refracted and reflected rays would be:
questions.explanation:
By Snell's law. $$ \mathrm{1\sin60^{\circ}=\sqrt{3}\sin r} $$ $$ \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}=\sqrt{3}\sin r $$ $$ \sin r=\frac{1}{2} $$ $$ r=30^{\circ} $$
$$ 60^{\circ}+\theta+30^{\circ}=180^{\circ} $$ $$ \theta=90^{\circ} $$ So, the angle between refracted and reflected ray is $ 90^{\circ} $.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: An air bubble in a glass slab with refractive index 1.5(near normal incidence) is 5 cm deep when viewed from one surface and 3 cm deep when viewed from the opposite face. The thickness (in cm) of the slab is:
questions.explanation:
Let, t = thickness of glass slab.
From question. $$ \begin{array}{r} \begin{aligned}\frac{x}{\mu}+\frac{t-x}{\mu}&=3+5\\ \frac{t}{\mu}&=8cm\end{aligned} \\ \therefore \quad t=\mu\times8&=8\times\frac{3}{2}=12cm \end{array} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: The frequency of a light wave in a material is $2 \times 10^{14}$ Hz and wavelength is 5000 Å. The refractive index of material will be:
questions.explanation: Refractive index, $ \mu = \frac{c}{v\lambda} $ $$ \mu=\frac{3\times10^{8}}{2\times10^{14}\times5000\times10^{-10}}=3 $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A small coin is resting on the bottom of a beaker filled with liquid. A ray of light from the coin travels up to the surface of the liquid and moves along its surface. How fast is the light travelling in the liquid?
questions.explanation: $$ \frac{1}{\mu}=\sin\theta_{c}=\frac{3}{5} $$ $$ \upmu=\frac{5}{3} $$ And $$ \begin{array}{l} \text{v} =\displaystyle {\lim_{\text{mu } m \to 8}} 37 ( 10 i^{8}- \frac{3-10^{8}}{5/3}- \frac{1}{3}+ \dots) \\\qquad=\displaystyle {\lim_{\text{fg } h \to 8}} 9 ( 10^{8}-1.8+10^{8}-1 \text{ ms}) \\ \qquad = 9 \text{ cm} \end{array} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A beam of light composed of red and green ray is incident obliquely at a point on the face of rectangular glass slab. When coming out on the opposite parallel face, the red and green ray emerge from:
questions.optionA: two points preparing in two different non parallel directions
questions.optionC: one point propagating in two different directions
questions.optionD: one point propagating in the same directions
questions.explanation: Red and green rays emerge from two points propagating in two different parallel directions.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Electromagnetic radiation of frequency v, velocity v, and wavelength $ \lambda $, in air, enters a glass slab of refractive index $ \mu $. The frequency, wavelength and velocity of light in the glass slab will be respectively is:
questions.explanation: When an electromagnetic wave travels through another medium, the frequency is unchanged, but the wavelength and velocity are $\frac{1}{\mu}$ times. Thus, after passing from air to a glass slab with a refractive index of $(\mu)$, frequency remains $v$, and wavelength becomes $\lambda' = \frac{\lambda}{\mu}$ and velocity of light $v' = \frac{v}{\mu}$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Time taken by sunlight to pass through a window of thickness 4 mm, whose refractive index is $ \frac{3}{2} $, is:
questions.explanation: Let x be thickness of window, v be velocity of light entering in window, so time taken by sunlight to pass through the window $$ \begin{aligned}t=&\frac{x}{\mathbf{v}}=\frac{x}{\underbrace{c}_{\ \mu}}=\frac{\mu x}{c}&\left[\because\mathbf{v}=\frac{c}{\mu}\right]\\t=&\frac{1.5\times4\times10^{-3}}{3\times10^{8}}=2\times10^{-11}\mathbf{s}\end{aligned} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A beam of monochromatic light is refracted from vacuum into a medium of refractive index 1.5. The wavelength of refracted light will be:
questions.optionA: dependent on intensity of refracted light
questions.explanation: We have, refractive index, $$ \upmu=\frac{c}{\mathbf{v}}=\frac{\mathbf{v}\lambda_{\mathbf{v}}}{\mathbf{v}\lambda_{\mathbf{m}}} $$ As, v remains constant during refraction. $$ \lambda_{m}=\frac{\lambda_{v}}{\mu} $$ $$ \lambda_{\mathrm{m}}<\lambda_{\mathrm{v}} $$ Hence, in the second medium, the wavelength falls.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Light travels a distance x in time $ t_{1} $ in air and 10x in time $ t_{2} $ in another denser medium. What is the critical angle for this medium?
questions.explanation: For air. $$ \mathrm{c}=\frac{x}{t_{1}} $$ For medium. $$ \mathbf{v}=\frac{10x}{t_{2}} $$ Refractive index. $$ \mathrm{\mu}=\frac{c}{v}=\frac{x \times t_{2}}{t_{1} \times 10 x}=\frac{t_{2}}{10 t_{1}} $$ As, $$ \sin\mathrm{~c~}=\frac{1}{\mu}=\frac{10t_{1}}{t_{2}} $$ [Using (iii)] $$ \mathrm{c}=\sin^{-1}\left(\frac{10t_{1}}{t_{2}}\right) $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Two transparent media A and B are separated by a plane boundary. The speed of light in those media are $ 1.5 \times 10^{8} \, m/s $ and $ 2.0 \times 10^{8} \, m/s $ respectively, The critical angle for a ray of light for these two media is:
questions.explanation: For medium A, speed of light, $ v_{A} = 1.5 \times 10^{8} $ m/s For medium B, speed of light, $ v_{B} = 2.0 \times 10^{8} $ m/s For medium A, $ n_{A} = \frac{c}{v_{A}} = \frac{3 \times 10^{8}}{1.5 \times 10^{8}} = 2 $ For medium B, $ n_{B} = \frac{c}{v_{B}} = \frac{3 \times 10^{8}}{2 \times 10^{8}} = 1.5 $ Critical angle, $ \sin c = \frac{n_{B}}{n_{A}} $ $ c = \sin^{-1}\left(\frac{1.5}{2}\right) $ $ = \sin^{-1}(0.750) $

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: If the critical angle for total internal reflection from a medium to vacuum is $45^{\circ}$, then velocity of light in the medium is:
questions.explanation: It is given that critical angle, $ i_{c} = 45^{\circ} $. We know $$ \begin{aligned}\mu&=\frac{1}{\sin i_{\mathrm{c}}}=\frac{1}{\sin45^{\circ}}\\&=\frac{1}{\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}}=\sqrt{2}\end{aligned} $$ Velocity of light in air = $ \sqrt{2} $ Velocity of light in medium $$ ↳\Downarrow $$ $$ \mathrm{\frac{3 \times 10^{8}}{v_{m}}}={\sqrt{2}} $$ $$ \mathbf{v}_{m}=\frac{3}{\sqrt{2}}\mathrm{~\boldmath~\times~}10^{8}\mathrm{m/s}. $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: In total internal reflection, when the angle of incidence is equal to the critical angle for the pair of media in contact, what will be angle of refraction?
questions.explanation: At $i = i_{c}$, refracted ray grazes with the surface. So, angle of refraction is $90^{\circ}$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Which of the following is NOT due to total internal reflection?
questions.optionA: Difference between apparent and real depth of a pond.
questions.explanation: Difference between apparent and real depth of a pond is due to refraction.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: The speed of light in medium $M_{1}$ and $M_{2}$ is $1.5 \times 10^{8} \mathrm{~m/s}$ and $2.0 \times 10^{8} \mathrm{~m/s}$ respectively. A ray of light enters from medium $M_{1}$ to $M_{2}$ at an incidence angle $i$, if the ray suffers total internal reflection, the value of $i$ is:
questions.explanation: We know that, $$ \begin{aligned}&for total~internal~reflection\\&\Rightarrow\quad\sin i>\sin c\\&\Rightarrow\quad\sin45^{\circ}>\frac{1}{\mu}\\&\Rightarrow\quad\mu>\sqrt{2}\\&\Rightarrow\quad\mu>1.4\end{aligned} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A ray of light travelling in a transparent medium of refractive index \mu, falls on surface separating the medium from air at an angle of incidence of 45^{\circ}. For which of the following value of \mu, the ray can undergo total internal reflection?
questions.explanation: In total internal reflection, the angle of incidence $i$ is greater than or equal to critical angle $c$. $$ \begin{aligned}i&\geq c\\\mu_{1}&=2and\mu_{2}=\frac{3}{2}\end{aligned} $$ $$2 \sin i \geq \frac{3}{2} \sin 90^{\circ}$$ $$ i\geq\frac{3}{4} $$ $$ i\geq\sin^{-1}\left(\frac{3}{4}\right) $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A boy is trying to start a fire by focusing sunlight on a piece of paper using an equiconvex lens of focal length 10 cm. The diameter of the sun is $ 1.39 \times 10^{9} $ m and its mean distance from the earth is $ 1.5 \times 10^{11} $ m. What is the diameter of the sun's image on the paper?
questions.explanation: From the relation, Here, and $$ \begin{aligned}\frac{1}{\textrm{O}}&=\frac{\textrm{v}}{u}\\\textrm{O}&=1.39\times10^{9}\textrm{m}\\\textrm{v}&=0.1\textrm{m}\\u&=1.5\times10^{11}\textrm{m}\\l&=\frac{0.1}{1.5\times10^{11}}\times1.39\times10^{9}\\&=9.2\times10^{-4}\textrm{m}\end{aligned} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.subtopic: Refraction at Spherical Surfaces and by Lenses
questions.question: Two thin lenses are of same focal length (), but one is convex and the other one is concave. When they are placed in contact with each other, the equivalent focal length of the combination will be:
questions.explanation:
When two lenses placed in contact with each other, their power will be added. $$ \therefore\quad\mathrm{P}_{\text{combination}}=\mathrm{P}_{\text{convex}}+\mathrm{P}_{\text{concave}} $$ $$ \begin{aligned}\frac{1}{f_{comb}}&=\frac{1}{f_{convex}}+\frac{1}{f_{concave}}\\&=\frac{1}{f}+\frac{1}{(-f)}=0\end{aligned} $$ $$ f_{comb}=\frac{1}{0}=\infty $$
##### Caution Students must know that some of the terms on the right side in algebraic sum of individual power may be positive (for convex lens) and some negative (for concave lens).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.subtopic: Refraction at Spherical Surfaces and by Lenses
questions.question: In the figure shown here, what is the equivalent focal length of the combination of lenses (Assume that all layers are thin)?

questions.subject: Physics
questions.subtopic: Refraction at Spherical Surfaces and by Lenses
questions.question: A biconvex lens has radii of curvature 20 cm each. If the refractive index of the material of the lens is 1.5, the power of the lens is:
questions.explanation: Given: $$ \boldsymbol{\mu}=1.5,\mathbf{R}_{1}=+\mathbf{R},\mathbf{R}_{2}=-\mathbf{R} $$ $$ \mathrm{R}_{1}=+20\mathrm{~cm~and~}\mathrm{R}_{2}=-20\mathrm{~cm~} $$ Lens maker's formula, $$ \begin{aligned}\frac{1}{f}&=(\mu-1)\left(\frac{1}{\mathrm{R}_{1}}-\frac{1}{\mathrm{R}_{2}}\right)\\\frac{1}{f}&=(1.5-1)\left(\frac{1}{20}-\frac{1}{(-20)}\right)\\\frac{1}{f}&=0.5\left(\frac{1}{20}+\frac{1}{20}\right)\\\frac{1}{f}&=\frac{1}{20}\\f&=20\mathrm{~cm}\\&=100\quad5\mathrm{D}\end{aligned} $$ Now Power, $ P = \frac{100}{20} = 5D $

questions.subject: Physics
questions.subtopic: Refraction at Spherical Surfaces and by Lenses
questions.question: A point object is placed at a distance of 60 cm from a convex lens of focal length 30 cm. If a plane mirror was put perpendicular to the principal axis of the lens and at a distance of 40 cm from it, the final image would be formed at a distance of:
questions.optionA: 20 cm from the lens, it would be a real image
questions.optionB: 30 cm from lens, it would be a real image
questions.optionC: 30 cm from the plane mirror, it would be a virtual image
questions.optionD: 20 cm from the plane mirror, it would be a virtual image
questions.explanation:
Using lens formula, for first refraction from convex lens. $$ \frac{1}{\mathbf{v}_{1}}-\frac{1}{u}=\frac{1}{f} $$ $$ v_{1}=?,\quad u=-60\ cm,f=30\ cm $$ $$ \begin{array}{llll}\Rightarrow&\quad\frac{1}{\mathbf{v}_{1}}+\frac{1}{60}=\frac{1}{30}&\\ \Rightarrow&\quad\mathbf{v}_{1}=60\mathrm{~cm}\end{array} $$
Here, $I_{1}$ is the first image by the lens, The plane mirror will produce an image at distance 20 cm to the left of it. For second refraction from convex lens, $$ u=-20cm,v=?,f=30cm $$ From lens formula, $$ \begin{array}{llll}\Rightarrow&\quad\frac{1}{\mathbf{v}}-\frac{1}{(-20)}=\frac{1}{30}&\\ &\quad\mathbf{v}=-60\mathrm{~cm}\end{array} $$ Thus, the final image is virtual and at a distance, 60 cm - 40 cm = 20 cm from the plane mirror.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.subtopic: Refraction at Spherical Surfaces and by Lenses
questions.question: A convex lens ‘A’ of focal length 20 cm and a concave lens ‘B’ of focal length 5 cm are kept along the same axis with a distance ‘d’ between them. If a parallel beam of light falling on ‘A’, leaves ‘B’ as a parallel beam, then the distance ‘d’ in cm will be:
questions.explanation: Given, Focal length of A = $ f_{A} = +20 \, cm $ [convex] Focal length of B = $ f_{B} = -5 \, cm $ [concave] Distance between A and B = d For parallel beam of light entering A to remain parallel while exiting B, net focal length of combination should be infinite. $$ \begin{aligned}\frac{1}{f}&=\frac{1}{f_{A}}+\frac{1}{f_{B}}-\frac{d}{f_{A}f_{B}}=0\\\frac{1}{20}&-\frac{1}{5}+\frac{d}{100}=0\\&\quad-\frac{15}{100}+\frac{d}{100}=0\\d&=15\end{aligned} $$ $$ d=15\mathrm{c m} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.subtopic: Refraction at Spherical Surfaces and by Lenses
questions.question: A plano convex lens of unknown material and unknown focal length is given. With the help of a spherometer, we can measure the:
questions.optionB: radius of curvature of the curved surface
questions.explanation: The first known spherometer was invented by French Optician Robert Aglae Cauchoix in 1810. They started manufacturing in the nineteenth century primarily for the use of opticians in grinding lenses and curved mirrors.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.subtopic: Refraction at Spherical Surfaces and by Lenses
questions.question: The power of a biconvex lens is 10 D and the radius of curvature of each surface is 10 cm. Then, the refractive index of the material of the lens is:
questions.explanation: From question, power of biconvex lens is, P = 10 D $$ f=\frac{1}{\mathrm{P}}=\frac{1}{10}=0.1\mathrm{~m}=10\mathrm{~cm} $$ $$ \mathrm{R}_{1}=\mathrm{R}_{2}=10\mathrm{~cm} $$ Now, from lens maker's formula $$ \frac{1}{f}=(\mu-1)\left(\frac{1}{\mathbf{R}_{1}}-\frac{1}{\mathbf{R}_{2}}\right) $$ $$ \frac{1}{10}=(\mu-1)\left(\frac{1}{10}-\frac{1}{-10}\right) $$ $$ \begin{array}{c}\frac{1}{10}=(\mu-1)\left(\frac{2}{10}\right)\\\Rightarrow\quad1=(\mu-1)\times2\\\Rightarrow\quad\mu-1=\frac{1}{2}\\\Rightarrow\quad\mu=1+\frac{1}{2}=\frac{3}{2}\end{array} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.subtopic: Refraction at Spherical Surfaces and by Lenses
questions.question: Two similar thin equiconvex lenses, of focal length $f_{each}$, are kept co-axially in contact with each other such that the focal length of the combination is $F_{1}$. When the space between the two lenses is filled with glycerine (which has the same refractive index ($\mu = 1.5$) as that of glass) then the equivalent focal length is $F_{2}$. The ratio $F_{1}:F_{2}$ will be:
questions.explanation: First find the focal length of lens (1), $$ \frac{1}{f_{1}}=\left(\frac{\mu_{2}}{\mu_{3}}-1\right)\left(\frac{1}{\mathbf{R}_{1}}-\frac{1}{\mathbf{R}_{2}}\right) $$ $ R_{1} = \text{Radius of } 1^{\text{st}} \text{ surface} $, $ R_{2} = \text{Radius of } 2^{\text{nd}} \text{ surface}; $ $$ \begin{aligned}\frac{1}{f_{1}}&=\left(\frac{1.5}{1}-1\right)\left(\frac{1}{\mathbf{R}}-\frac{1}{-\mathbf{R}}\right)\\&=\frac{1}{2}\times\frac{2}{\mathbf{R}}=\frac{1}{\mathbf{R}}\end{aligned} $$ Now, focal length of (2), $$ \frac{1}{f_{2}}=\left(\frac{1.5}{1}-1\right)\left(\frac{1}{R}-\frac{1}{-R}\right)=\frac{1}{R} $$ From combination of these two $$ \frac{1}{\mathrm{F}_{1}}=\frac{1}{f_{2}}+\frac{1}{f_{2}}=\frac{1}{\mathrm{R}}+\frac{1}{\mathrm{R}}=\frac{2}{\mathrm{R}} $$ $$ \mathbf{F}_{1}=\frac{\mathbf{R}}{2} $$ Now focal length of concave (3) $$ \frac{1}{f_{3}}=\left(\frac{\mu_{2}}{\mu_{3}}-1\right)\left(\frac{1}{R_{1}}-\frac{1}{R_{2}}\right) $$ $$ \begin{aligned}\frac{1}{f_{3}}&=\left(\frac{1.5}{1}-1\right)\left(-\frac{1}{\mathrm{R}}-\frac{1}{\mathrm{R}}\right)\\&=\frac{1}{2}\times\frac{-2}{\mathrm{R}}\\&=-\frac{1}{\mathrm{R}}\end{aligned} $$ ∴ Focal length. $$ \frac{1}{\mathbf{F}_{2}}=\frac{1}{f_{1}}+\frac{1}{f_{2}}+\frac{1}{f_{3}} $$ $$ \frac{1}{F_{2}}=\frac{1}{R}+\frac{1}{R}-\frac{1}{R}=\frac{1}{R} $$ $ \therefore F_{2}=R $ According to question,

questions.subject: Physics
questions.subtopic: Refraction at Spherical Surfaces and by Lenses
questions.question: An equi-convex lens has power P. It is cut into two symmetrical halves by a plane containing the principle axis. The power of one part will be:
questions.explanation:
$$ \frac{\mathrm{F}_{1}}{\mathrm{F}_{2}}=\frac{\mathrm{R}/2}{\mathrm{R}}=\frac{\mathrm{R}}{2\times\mathrm{R}}=\frac{1}{2} $$
In both the cases, (i) and (ii) focal length does not change. Hence, power does not change. So, power of one part is also P.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.subtopic: Refraction at Spherical Surfaces and by Lenses
questions.question: A double convex lens has focal length 25 cm. The radius of curvature of one of the surfaces is double of the other. Find the radii, if the refractive index of the material of the lens is 1.5.
questions.explanation: Focal length of lens is, $$ \frac{1}{f}=(\mu-1)\left(\frac{1}{\mathbf{R}_{1}}-\frac{1}{\mathbf{R}_{2}}\right) $$ $$ \therefore\quad\frac{1}{25}=(1.5-1)\left(\frac{1}{R}+\frac{1}{2R}\right) $$ $$ \frac{1}{25}=0.5\left(\frac{3}{2R}\right) $$ $$ \Rightarrow\quad2R=37.5\mathrm{c m} $$ $$ \therefore\quad\mathrm{R}=18.75\mathrm{~cm} $$ Hence, radii are 18.75 cm, 37.5 cm.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.subtopic: Refraction at Spherical Surfaces and by Lenses
questions.question: Two identical glass \left(\mu_{g}=\frac{3}{2}\right) equi-convex lenses of focal length f each are kept in contact. The space between the two lenses is filled with water \left(\mu_{w}=\frac{4}{3}\right). The focal length of the combination is:
questions.explanation:
The diagram shows the actual situation Let R = Radius of curvature of the lens and $ \frac{1}{f_{eq}} = \frac{1}{f_1} + \frac{1}{f_2} + \frac{1}{f_3} $ $$ f_{1}=f_{3}=\frac{R}{2\left(\frac{3}{2}-1\right)} $$ $$ \boldsymbol{R}=f $$ $$ f_{2}=\frac{-\mathbf{R}}{2\left(\frac{4}{3}-1\right)}=-\frac{3}{2}\mathbf{R}=-\frac{3}{2}f $$ $$ \begin{aligned}&\therefore\quad\frac{1}{f_{eq}}=\frac{1}{f_{1}}+\frac{1}{f_{2}}+\frac{1}{f_{3}}\\&\Rightarrow\frac{1}{f_{eq}}=\frac{1}{f}+\left(-\frac{2}{3f}\right)+\frac{1}{f}\\&\Rightarrow\frac{1}{f_{eq}}=\frac{4}{3f}\\&\Rightarrow\quad f_{eq}=\frac{3f}{4}\end{aligned} $$
Radius of curvature of plano-convex lens, R = 20 cm Refractive index of plano-convex lens, $ \mu = 1.5 $ Using mirror formula. $$ \frac{1}{f}=(\mu-1)\left[\frac{1}{\mathbf{R}}\right] $$ $$ \Rightarrow $$ $$ \frac{1}{f}=(1.5-1).\frac{1}{R} $$ $$ \rightarrow $$ $$ \frac{1}{f}=\frac{0.5}{R} $$ Focal length of concave medium, $$ \begin{array}{l}\frac{1}{f_{\mathrm{concave}}}=(\mu_{1}-1)\left(-\frac{1}{\mathrm{R}}-\frac{1}{\mathrm{R}}\right)\\ \\ =(1.7-1)\left[-\frac{1}{\mathrm{R}}-\frac{1}{\mathrm{R}}\right]\end{array} $$ $$ \begin{array}{c} *\\ *\end{array} $$ $$ \frac{1}{f_{\mathrm{concave}}}=\frac{-0.7\times2}{R}=-\frac{1.4}{R} $$ Equivalent focal length will be, $$ \frac{1}{f_{\mathrm{eq}}}=\frac{0.5}{\mathrm{R}}-\frac{1.4}{\mathrm{R}}+\frac{0.5}{\mathrm{R}}=-\frac{0.4}{\mathrm{R}} $$ $$ f_{eq}=-50\ cm $$
##### Related Theory When a concave lens and a convex lens of the same focal length are in contact, then the power of the lens combination will be zero and its focal length will be infinite.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.subtopic: Refraction at Spherical Surfaces and by Lenses
questions.question: Two identical thin plano-convex glass lenses (refractive index 1.5) each having radius of curvature of 20 cm are placed with their convex surface in contact at the centre. The intervening space is filled with oil of refractive index 1.7. The focal length of the combination is:

questions.subject: Physics
questions.subtopic: Refraction at Spherical Surfaces and by Lenses
questions.question: A plano-convex lens fits exactly into a plano-concave lens. Their plane surface are parallel to each other. If lenses are made of different materials of refractive indices \mu_{1} and \mu_{2} and R is the radius of curvature of the curved surface of the lenses, then the focal length of the combination is:
questions.explanation: Lens maker's formula: $$ \frac{1}{f}\;=\;(\mu-1)\!\left(\frac{1}{\mathrm{R}_{1}}-\frac{1}{\mathrm{R}_{2}}\right) $$ For plano-convex lens: $$ \mathbf{R}_{1}=\infty,\mathbf{R}_{2}=-\mathbf{R} $$ Using (i), $$ \begin{aligned}\frac{1}{f_{1}}&=\left(\mu_{1}-1\right)\left(\frac{1}{\infty}-\frac{1}{-\mathbf{R}}\right)\\\frac{1}{f_{1}}&=\frac{1}{f_{2}}=\frac{\mu_{1}-1}{\mathbf{R}}\end{aligned} $$ For plano-concave lens: $$ \mathbf{R}_{1}=-\mathbf{R},\mathbf{R}_{2}=\infty $$ Using (i), $$ \begin{aligned}\frac{1}{f_{2}}&=\left(\mu_{2}-1\right)\left(\frac{1}{-\mathbf{R}}-\frac{1}{\infty}\right)\\\frac{1}{f_{2}}&=\frac{1-\mu_{2}}{\mathbf{R}}\end{aligned} $$ Thus focal length of combination, $$ \begin{aligned}\frac{1}{\mathrm{F}}&=\frac{1}{f_{1}}+\frac{1}{f_{2}}\\&=\frac{\mu_{1}-1}{\mathrm{R}}+\frac{1-\mu_{2}}{\mathrm{R}}\end{aligned} $$ $$ \mathbf{F}=\frac{\mathbf{R}}{\mu_{1}-\mu_{2}} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.subtopic: Refraction at Spherical Surfaces and by Lenses
questions.question: When a biconvex lens of glass having refractive index 1.47 is dipped in a liquid, it acts as a plane sheet of glass. This implies that the liquid must have refractive index:
questions.explanation:
According to the question,
$$ \upmu_{g}=1.47 $$ and $$ f=\infty $$ Using the formula, $$ \frac{1}{f}=(\mu-1)\left(\frac{1}{R_{1}}-\frac{1}{R_{2}}\right) $$ $ \Rightarrow $ $$ \frac{1}{\infty}=\left(\frac{\mu_{g}}{\mu_{2}}-1\right)\left(\frac{1}{R_{1}}-\frac{1}{R_{2}}\right) $$ $$ 0=\left(\frac{\mu_{g}}{\mu_{2}}-1\right)\left(\frac{1}{R_{1}}-\frac{1}{R_{2}}\right) $$ Since, $ \left(\frac{1}{R_{1}}-\frac{1}{R_{2}}\right)\neq0 $ $$ \begin{array}{ll}\therefore&\quad\left(\frac{\mu_{g}}{\mu_{2}}-1\right)=0\\\Rightarrow&\quad\frac{\mu_{g}-\mu_{2}}{\mu_{2}}=0\\\Rightarrow&\quad\mu_{g}=\mu_{2}\end{array} $$ This shows that the liquid must have a refractive index equal to that of glass.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.subtopic: Refraction at Spherical Surfaces and by Lenses
questions.question: A concave mirror of focal length f_{1} is placed at a distance of d from a convex lens of focal length f_{2}. Beam of light coming from infinity and falling on this convex lens concave mirror combination returns to infinity. The distance d must be equal:
questions.explanation: $$ d=2f_{1}+f_{2} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.subtopic: Refraction at Spherical Surfaces and by Lenses
questions.question: A converging beam of rays is incident on a diverging lens. Having passed through the lens, the rays intersect at a point 15 cm from the lens on the opposite side. If the lens is removed, the point where the rays meet, will move 5 cm closer to the lens, the focal length of the lens is:
questions.explanation: In the question, $ u = 10 \, \text{cm} $, $ v = 15 \, \text{cm} $ $$ \begin{aligned}\frac{1}{f}&=\frac{1}{v}-\frac{1}{u}\\&=\frac{1}{15}-\frac{1}{10}\\&=\frac{10-15}{150}=-\frac{5}{150}\end{aligned} $$ $$ \frac{1}{f}=-\frac{1}{30} $$ $$ f=-30\mathrm{~c m}. $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.subtopic: Refraction at Spherical Surfaces and by Lenses
questions.question: A biconvex lens has a radius of curvature of magnitude 20 cm. Which one of the following options describes the image formed of an object height 2 cm placed 30 cm from the lens?
questions.explanation: Here, $$ f=20cm $$ ∴ Using the formula, $$ \begin{array}{r} \left[\begin{array}{cc} \frac{1}{f}\;=\;\frac{1}{\mathrm{v}}-\frac{1}{u} & \end{array} \right] \\ \Rightarrow \left[\begin{array}{cc} \frac{1}{20}\;=\;\frac{1}{\mathrm{v}}+\frac{1}{30} & \\ \frac{1}{\mathrm{v}}\;=\;\frac{1}{20}-\frac{1}{30}=\frac{10}{600} & \end{array} \right] \\ \Rightarrow \left[\begin{array}{cc} \mathrm{v}=60\mathrm{~c m} \\ \mathrm{N o w},\qquad\quad\frac{h_{i}}{h_{o}}\;=\;2 \end{array} \right] \\ \Rightarrow \left[\begin{array}{cc} h_{i}=2h_{o} & \end{array} \right] \\ \pm=2\times|h_{o}|=4\mathrm{~c m} \end{array} $$ The image will be real, inverted, magnified and height of image is 4 cm.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.subtopic: Refraction at Spherical Surfaces and by Lenses
questions.question: A lens having focal length f an aperture of diameter d forms an image of intensity I. Aperture of diameter $ \frac{d}{2} $ in central region of lens is covered by a black paper. Focal length of lens and intensity of image now will be respectively:
questions.explanation: We know that, Intensity, $ I \propto A^{2} $ then. $$ \begin{aligned}\frac{\mathrm{I}_{2}}{\mathrm{I}_{1}}&=\left[\frac{\mathrm{A}_{2}}{\mathrm{A}_{1}}\right]^{2}\\&=\frac{\pi r^{2}-\frac{\pi r^{2}}{4}}{\pi r^{2}}=\frac{3}{4}\\\mathrm{I}_{2}&=\frac{3}{4}\mathrm{I}_{1}\end{aligned} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.subtopic: Refraction at Spherical Surfaces and by Lenses
questions.question: Two thin lenses of focal length $ f_{1} $ and $ f_{2} $ are in contact and co-axial. The power of the combination is:
questions.explanation: We can write, $$ \begin{aligned}\mathbf{P}&=\mathbf{P}_{1}+\mathbf{P}_{2}\\\mathbf{P}&=\frac{1}{f_{1}}+\frac{1}{f_{2}}\\\mathbf{P}&=\frac{f_{1}+f_{2}}{f_{1}f_{2}}\end{aligned} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.subtopic: Refraction at Spherical Surfaces and by Lenses
questions.question: A glass tube having a curved surface at one face is shown below with refractive index 1.5. Its centre of radius of curvature R lies inside the glass tube. If a particle is placed at point P, it forms the real image at point Q. The point O cut PQ such that $ OP = 2OQ $, then the value of OP is:
questions.explanation: When object is in the rarer medium then $$ \begin{aligned}&\frac{\mu_{1}}{\mathbf{v}}-\frac{\mu_{2}}{u}=\frac{\mu_{1}-\mu_{2}}{R}\\&Now,\quad\mathbf{v}=O Q\\&\Rightarrow\quad\frac{1.5}{O Q}+\frac{1}{2O Q}=\frac{1.5-1}{R}\\&\Rightarrow\quad\frac{3+1}{2O Q}=\frac{0.5}{R}\\&\quad O Q=4R\\&\therefore\quad O P=2O Q=2\times4R=8\end{aligned} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.subtopic: Refraction at Spherical Surfaces and by Lenses
questions.question: A convex lens and a concave lens, each having same focal length of 25 cm, are put in contact to form a combination of lenses. The power in diopters of the combination is:
questions.explanation: Focal length of convex lens, $$ f_{1}=25\ cm $$ focal length of concave lens, $$ f_{2}=-25\ cm $$ Power of combination is: $$ \boldsymbol{P}=\boldsymbol{P}_{1}+\boldsymbol{P}_{2} $$ $$ \mathrm{P}=\frac{100}{f_{1}}+\frac{100}{f_{2}} $$ $$ \mathrm{P}=\frac{100}{25}+\frac{100}{-25}=0 $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.subtopic: Refraction at Spherical Surfaces and by Lenses
questions.question: A convex lens is dipped in a liquid whose refractive index is equal to the refractive index of the lens. Then its focal length will:
questions.explanation: When refractive index of lens is equal to the refractive index of liquid, the lens behave like a plane surface with focal length infinity.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.subtopic: Refraction at Spherical Surfaces and by Lenses
questions.question: An equiconvex lens is cut two halves along (i) XOX' and (ii) YOY' as shown in the figure. Let $f, f^{\prime}, f^{\prime\prime}$ be the focal length of the complete lens, of each half in case (i) and each half in case (ii), respectively. Choose the correct statement from the following:
questions.explanation:
Since, the lens is equiconvex, the radius of curvature of each half is same.
Using lens maker's formula, $$ \frac{1}{f}\;=\;(\mu-1)\left(\frac{1}{\mathbf{R}_{1}}-\frac{1}{\mathbf{R}_{2}}\right) $$ Here $$ \boldsymbol{R}_{1}=\boldsymbol{R} $$ $$ \mathbf{R}_{2}=-\mathbf{R} $$ (by convention) $$ \frac{1}{f}=(\mu-1)\frac{2}{\mathbf{R}},~(\mu-1)\frac{1}{\mathbf{R}}=\frac{1}{2f} $$ If we cut the lens along XOX', then two halves of the lens will be having the same radii of curvature and $$ \therefore\quad\downarrow\quad\downarrow $$ $$ \begin{aligned}\mathbf{R}_{1}&=\mathbf{R},\mathbf{R}_{2}=-\mathbf{R}\\\frac{1}{f^{\prime}}&=(\mu-1)\left(\frac{2}{\mathbf{R}}\right)\\\frac{1}{f^{\prime}}&=\frac{2}{2f}=\frac{1}{f}\\f^{\prime}&=f\end{aligned} $$ But when we cut along YOY' then $$ \boldsymbol{R}_{1}=\boldsymbol{R} $$ $$ \mathbf{R}_{2}=\infty $$ $$ \begin{aligned}\frac{1}{f^{\prime \prime}}&=(\mu-1)\left(\frac{1}{R}-\frac{1}{\infty}\right)\\&=(\mu-1)\frac{1}{R}=\frac{1}{2f}\end{aligned} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.subtopic: Refraction at Spherical Surfaces and by Lenses
questions.question: A bulb is located on a wall. Its image is to be obtained on a parallel wall with the help of a convex lens. If the distance between parallel walls is $d$, then the required focal length of the lens placed in between the walls is:
questions.explanation:
$$ f^{\prime \prime}=2f $$
Since, $$ \mathbf{v}+u=d $$ $ \Rightarrow $ $$ \textbf{v}=d-u $$ From lens formula. $$ \frac{1}{f}=\frac{1}{\mathrm{v}}-\frac{1}{u} $$ $$ ↳\Downarrow $$ $$ \begin{aligned}\frac{1}{f}&=\frac{1}{d-u}-\frac{1}{-u}\\&=\frac{d}{(d-u)u}\\f&=\frac{du-u^{2}}{d}\end{aligned} $$ Differentiating eq. (i), we have. $$ \frac{df}{du}=\frac{1}{d}(d-2u) $$ for maximum value of $$ f_{1}=\frac{df}{du}=0 $$ $$ \begin{array}{l}\Rightarrow \quad \displaystyle\frac{1}{d}(d-2u)=0\\ \Rightarrow \quad \displaystyle d=2u\\ \Rightarrow \quad \displaystyle u=\frac{d}{2}\end{array} $$ putting these value in eq. (i), $$ f=\frac{d}{4} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.subtopic: Refraction at Spherical Surfaces and by Lenses
questions.question: A planoconvex lens is made of a material of refractive index $\mu = 1.5$. The radius of curvature of curved surface of the lens is 20 cm. If its plane surface is silvered, the focal length of the silvered lens will be:
questions.explanation: When a ray strikes the convex surface of a planoconvex lens, it is first refracted and then reflected from the plane surface, then refracted from the convex surface. As a result, there are two refractions and one reflection. So, the focal length of a planoconvex lens, $$ \frac{1}{\mathrm{F}}=\frac{2}{f_{1}}+\frac{1}{f_{m}} $$ If the plane surface is silvered, then $$ f_{m}=\frac{\mathrm{R}_{2}}{2}=\frac{\infty}{2} $$ by lens maker's formula $$ \begin{aligned}\frac{1}{f_{1}}&=(\mu-1)\left(\frac{1}{\mathrm{R}_{1}}-\frac{1}{\mathrm{R}_{2}}\right)\\&=\quad(\mu-1)\left(\frac{1}{\mathrm{R}}-\frac{1}{\infty}\right)=\frac{(\mu-1)}{\mathrm{R}}\end{aligned} $$ $$ \begin{aligned}\frac{1}{F}&=\frac{2(\mu-1)}{R}+\frac{1}{\infty}\\&=\frac{R}{2(\mu-1)}\end{aligned} $$ $$ F=\frac{R}{2(\mu-1)} $$ Or Given, $$ R=20cm,\mu=1.5 $$ Hence, $$ \begin{aligned}F&=\frac{20}{2(1.5-1)}&=\frac{20}{2\times0.5}\\ &=20cm\\ \end{aligned} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.subtopic: Refraction at Spherical Surfaces and by Lenses
questions.question: A planoconvex lens is made of material of refractive index 1.6. The radius of curvature of the curved surface is 60 cm. The focal length of the lens is:
questions.explanation: By lens maker's formula, $$ \frac{1}{f}=(\mu-1)\left(\frac{1}{R_{1}}-\frac{1}{R_{2}}\right) $$ We have the radius of curvature of a plane surface for a planoconvex lens is infinite. i.e., $$ \mathsf{R}_{2}=\infty $$ Given. $$ R_{1}=60cm,\mu=1.6 $$ Substituting the given values in Eq. (i), we get $$ \begin{aligned}\frac{1}{f}&=(1.6-1)\left(\frac{1}{60}-\frac{1}{\infty}\right)\\&=0.6\times\frac{1}{60}\end{aligned} $$ $$ f=100\mathrm{c m} $$ ∴ The focal length of plane convex lens is 100 cm or 1 m.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.subtopic: Refraction at Spherical Surfaces and by Lenses
questions.question: A convex lens of focal length 80 cm and a concave lens of focal length 50 cm are combined together. What will be their resulting power?
questions.explanation:
When focal length is in cm, $ P = \frac{100}{f} $ D Here, $$ f_{1}=80\ cm,f_{2}=-50\ cm $$ We have, combined power of two lenses $$ \mathbf{P}_{\mathrm{eq}}=\mathbf{P}_{1}+\mathbf{P}_{2} $$ $$ \mathrm{P_{eq}}=\frac{1}{f_{1}(\mathrm{m})}+\frac{1}{f_{2}(\mathrm{m})} $$
$$ \mathrm{P}=\frac{100}{80}-\frac{100}{50}=-0.75\mathrm{D} $$ ##### Related Theory The ability of a lens to converge a beam of light falling on it is known as power of lens. It is measured as the reciprocal of the focal length of lens. i.e., $$ \mathrm{P}=\frac{1}{f_{(\mathrm{i n}\mathrm{m})}} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A light ray enters through a right angled prism at point P with the angle of incidence $ 30^{\circ} $ as shown in figure. It travels through the prism parallel to its base BC and emerges along the face AC. The refractive index of the prism is:
questions.explanation:
$$ \begin{aligned}r_{1}+r_{2}&=90^{\circ}\\r_{2}&=90^{\circ}-r_{1}\end{aligned} $$
For surface AB $$ 1\times\sin30^{\circ}=\mu\sin r, $$ For surface AC $$ \begin{aligned}\mu\sin\left(90^\circ-r_{1}\right)&=1\\\Rightarrow\quad\mu\cos r,&=1\end{aligned} $$ $$ \mathrm{OS}r,=\frac{1}{\mu} $$ From eqn (i) and (ii), we get $$ \frac{1}{2}=\mu\sqrt{1-\frac{1}{\mu^{2}}} $$ or $$ \frac{1}{4}\;=\;\mu^{2}\left(1-\frac{1}{\mu^{2}}\right) $$ or $$ \frac{1}{4}=\mu^{2}-1 $$ $$ \implies $$ $$ \mu^{2}\;=\;1+\frac{1}{4} $$ $$ \bullet \quad \bullet\ast $$ $$ \mu=\frac{\sqrt{5}}{2} $$
##### Related Theory For emergence of ray along face of the prism, incidence angle at that face should be equal to the critical angle of glass air interface.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A ray is incident at an angle of incidence i on one surface of a small angle prism (with angle of prism A) and emerges normally from the opposite surface. If the refractive index of the material of the prism is \mu, then the angle of incidence is nearly equal to:
questions.explanation:
The ray diagram is,
From ray diagram. $$ \begin{aligned}&A+a+90^{\circ}=180^{\circ}\\ \Rightarrow\quad&a=90^{\circ}-A\\ Also\quad&r+a=90^{\circ}\\&r=90^{\circ}-a\\&=90^{\circ}-(90^{\circ}-A)=A\\ \Rightarrow\quad&r=A\end{aligned} $$ From snell's law, $$ \mu_{1}\sin i=\mu_{2}\sin r $$ $$ \sin i=\mu\sin A(Here,\mu_{1}=1,\mu_{2}=\mu) $$ For small, $ \sin\theta\approx\theta $
$$ i=\mu\mathbf{A}. $$ ##### Related Theory Prism is a homogeneous solid transparent medium bounded by two plane surfaces inclined at an angle.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: The refractive index of the material of a prism is $\sqrt{2}$ and the angle of the prism is $30^{\circ}$. One of the two refracting surfaces of the prism is made a mirror inwards, by silver coating. A beam of monochromatic light entering the prism from the other face will retrace its path (after reflection from the silvered surface) if its angle of incidence on the prism is:
questions.explanation:
For retracing its path, light ray should be normally inclined on silvered face.
Applying Snell's law at P, $$ \frac{\sin i}{\sin30^{\circ}}=\frac{\sqrt{2}}{1} $$ $$ \sin i=\sqrt{2}\times\frac{1}{2}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}=\sin45^{\circ} $$ $ \Rightarrow $ $$ i=45^{\circ} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A thin prism having refracting angle $10^{\circ}$ is made of glass of refractive index 1.42. This prism is combined with another thin prism of glass of refractive index 1.7. This combination produces dispersion without deviation. The refracting angle of second prism should be:
questions.explanation: The dispersion without deviation of prism is, $$ \left(\mu-1\right)\mathrm{A}+\left(\mu^{\prime}-1\right)\mathrm{A}^{\prime}=0 $$ $$ \Rightarrow $$ $$ \left|\left(\mu-1\right)\mathbf{A}\right|=\left|\left(\mu^{\prime}-1\right)\mathbf{A}^{\prime}\right| $$ $$ \Rightarrow\quad(1.42-1)10=(1.7-1)A^{\prime} $$ $$ 4.2=0.7\mathrm{A}^{\prime} $$ $$ \Rightarrow $$ $$ \mathrm{A^{\prime}}=6^{\circ} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: The angle of incidence for a ray of light at a refracting surface of a prism is $45^{\circ}$. The angle of prism is $60^{\circ}$. If the ray suffers minimum deviation through the prism, the angle of minimum deviation and refractive index of the material of the prism respectively, are:
questions.explanation: From figure, it is clear that, Here angle of incidence, i = 45° angle of refraction, $ r = r' = 30^\circ $ angle of emergence, $ e = 45^{\circ} $ ∴ Minimum deviation suffered by the ray. $$ \delta_{d}=i+e-(r+r^{\prime})=90^{\circ}-60^{\circ}=30^{\circ} $$ Using the formula. $$ \mu=\frac{\sin\left(\frac{\mathbf{A}+\delta}{2}\right)}{\sin\frac{\mathbf{A}}{2}} $$ $$ \mu=\frac{\sin\left(\frac{60^{\circ}+30^{\circ}}{2}\right)}{\sin\frac{60^{\circ}}{2}} $$ $$ \mu=\frac{\sin45^{\circ}}{\sin30^{\circ}} $$ $$ \mu=\frac{1/\sqrt{2}}{1/2}=\frac{2}{\sqrt{2}}=\sqrt{2} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A beam of light consisting of red, green and blue colours is incident on a right angled prism. The refractive index of the material of the prism for the above red, green and blue wavelength are 1.39, 1.44 and 1.47, respectively. The prism will:
questions.optionA: separate the blue colour part from the red and green colours
questions.optionB: separate all the three colours from one another
questions.optionC: not separate the three colours at all
questions.optionD: separate the red colour part from the green and blue colour.
questions.explanation: We know that, $$ \mu=\frac{1}{\sin i_{c}}=\frac{1}{\sin45^{\circ}}=\sqrt{2} $$ $$ \because\quad(\mu_{red}=1.39)<\mu $$ $$ \bullet \quad \bullet\bullet $$ $$ \mu_{b}>\mu;\;\mu_{g}>\mu $$ Only red colour do not suffer total internal reflection.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: The angle of prism is A. One of its refracting surfaces is silvered. Light rays falling at an angle of incidence 2A on the first surface returns back through the same path after suffering reflection at the silvered surface. The refractive index \mu, of the prism is:
questions.explanation: MO'P is a prism and the diagram is given, $$ \begin{array}{l}\angle MON=90^{\circ}-A\\ \therefore \quad \angle r=90^{\circ}-(90^{\circ}-A)\\ \Rightarrow \quad \angle r=A\end{array} $$ ∴ From Snell's law, $$ \begin{array}{c}\frac{\sin i}{\sin r}=\mu\\\Rightarrow\quad\mu=\frac{\sin\left(2\mathrm{A}\right)}{\sin\mathrm{A}}\\\therefore\quad\mu=\frac{2\sin\mathrm{A}\cos\mathrm{A}}{\sin\mathrm{A}}=2\cos\mathrm{A}\\\Rightarrow\quad\mu=2\cos\mathrm{A}\end{array} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: For the angle of minimum deviation of a prism to be equal to its refracting angle, the prism must be made of a material whose refractive index:
questions.explanation:
Since, $$ \mu=\frac{\sin\left(A+\frac{\delta_{m}}{2}\right)}{\sin\frac{A}{2}} $$
Values of A varies from 0 to $ 90^{\circ} $. $$ \bullet \quad \bullet\bullet $$ $$ A_{min}=2\cos\frac{90^{\circ}}{2}=\sqrt{2} $$ $$ \mathrm{A}_{max}=2\cos0^{\circ}=2 $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A thin prism of angle $15^{\circ}$ made of glass of refractive index, $\mu_{1}=1.5$ is combined with another prism of glass of refractive index, $\mu_{2}=1.75$. The combination of the prism produces dispersion without deviation. The angle of the second prism should be:
questions.explanation: The formula using for without deviation is, $$ \frac{A}{A^{\prime}}=\frac{\mu^{\prime}-1}{\mu-1} $$ putting the given value from question $$ \frac{15^{\circ}}{A^{\prime}}=\frac{1.75-1}{1.5-1} $$ $$ \frac{15^{\circ}}{A^{\prime}}=\frac{0.75}{0.5} $$ $$ \mathbf{A}^{\prime}=\frac{0.5\times15^{\circ}}{0.75}=10^{\circ} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A ray of light is incident on a $60^{\circ}$ prism at the minimum deviation position. The angle of refraction at the first face (i.e. incident face) of the prism is:
questions.explanation: Here, refracting angle of prism $$ \mathbf{A}=r_{1}+r_{2} $$ And for minimum deviation, $$ r_{1}=r_{2}=r $$ $$ A=2r $$ $ \Rightarrow $ $$ r=\frac{A}{2}=\frac{60^{\circ}}{2}=30^{\circ} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: For the given incident ray as shown in the figure, the condition of the total internal reflection of this ray, the minimum refractive index of prism will be:
questions.explanation: For given condition and use snell's law $$ 45^{\circ}=\mu\sin\left(90-\theta_{C}\right) $$ $$ \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}=\mu\cos\theta_{C}=\mu\sqrt{1-\frac{1}{\mu^{2}}} $$ $$ \mu^{2}=1+\frac{1}{2}=\frac{3}{2} $$ $$ \upmu=\sqrt{\frac{3}{2}} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Angle of deviation ($\delta$) by a prism (refractive index $\mu$) and supposing the angle of prism A to be small) can be given by:
questions.explanation: The refractive index of a prism is given by, $$ \mu=\frac{\sin\frac{\left(\mathbf{A}+\delta\right)}{2}}{\sin\left(\frac{\mathbf{A}}{2}\right)} $$ For a very thin prism, we can assume that $ \sin\theta \approx \theta $ $$ \mu=\frac{\frac{A+\delta}{2}}{\frac{A}{2}} $$ or $$ \mu=\frac{\mathbf{A}+\delta}{\mathbf{A}} $$ $$ \mathrm{O r}, $$ $$ \boldsymbol{\delta}=\mu\mathbf{A}-\mathbf{A}=(\mu-1)\mathbf{A} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: There is a prism with refractive index equal to $\sqrt{2}$ and the refracting angle equal to $30^{\circ}$. One of the refracting surface of the prism is polished. A beam of monochromatic will retrace its path if its angle of incidence over the refracting surface of the prism is:
questions.explanation:
When, the ray QR retraced its path,
$$ \angle ARQ=90^{\circ},\angle r=30^{\circ} $$ $$ \angle AQR=90^{\circ}-30^{\circ}=60^{\circ} $$ By snell's law. $ \sin i \times 1 = \sin r \times \mu $ $ \sin i = \mu \sin r = \sqrt{2} \sin 30^\circ $ [ $ \mu $ for air = 1] $$ \begin{array}{l}i=\sqrt{2} \times \frac{1}{2}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\\i=45^{\circ}\end{array} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A small telescope has an objective of focal length 140 cm and an eye piece of focal length 5.0 cm. The magnifying power of telescope for viewing a distant object is:
questions.explanation: Given: $ f_{o}=140\ cm; $ $ f_{e}=5\ cm $ Magnifying power for normal adjustment $$ \mathbf{M}=\left|\frac{-f_{o}}{f_{e}}\right|=\left|-\frac{140}{5}\right|=28 $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A lens of large focal length and large aperture is best suited as an objective of an astronomical telescope since:
questions.optionA: a large aperture contributes to the quality and visibility of the images.
questions.optionB: a large area of the objective ensures better light gathering power.
questions.optionC: a large aperture provides a better resolution
questions.explanation: Aperture ensure proper collection of light as well as better resolution.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: An astronomical refracting telescope will have large angular magnification and high angular resolution, when it has an objective lens of:
questions.optionA: large focal length and large diameter
questions.optionB: large focal length and small diameter
questions.optionC: small focal length and large diameter
questions.optionD: small focal length and small diameter
questions.explanation: For telescope, angular magnification is $ \frac{f_{o}}{f_{e}} $. So focal length of objective lens should be large and angular resolution = $ \frac{D}{1.22\lambda} $ should also be large. So, object lens should have large focal length ( $ f_{o} $) and large diameter (D).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A astronomical telescope has objective and eyepiece of focal lengths 40 cm and 4 cm respectively. To view an object 200 cm away from the objective, the lens must be separated by a distance:
questions.explanation:
Objective lens Eye piece lens
Using lens formula for objective lens, $$ \frac{1}{\textbf{v}}-\frac{1}{u}=\frac{1}{f} $$ $$ \begin{aligned}\frac{1}{\text{v}}&=\frac{1}{f}+\frac{1}{u}\\&=\frac{1}{40}+\frac{1}{-200}\\&=\frac{+5-1}{200}\end{aligned} $$ $ \Rightarrow $ v = 50 cm : Tube length $$ \begin{aligned}l&=|\mathbf{v}|+f_{e}\\&=50+4\\&=54\ cm\end{aligned} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: In an astronomical telescope in normal adjustment, a straight black line of length L is drawn on inside part of objective lens. The eye-piece forms a real image of this line. The length of this image is l. Then magnification of the telescope is:
questions.explanation:
We know that magnification of telescope, $$ \mathbf{M}=\frac{f_{o}}{f_{e}} $$ And also $$ \frac{f_{e}}{f_{e}+u}=-\frac{l}{L} $$ $$ \begin{array}{c} \Rightarrow\quad\frac{f_{e}}{f_{e}-(f_{o}+f_{e})}=-\frac{l}{L}\\ \Rightarrow\quad\frac{f_{e}}{f_{o}}=\frac{l}{L} \end{array} $$ From eq. (i) and (ii), $$ \textbf{M}=\frac{L}{l} $$
##### Mnemonics Magnifying power of Telescope: MBA for free $$ m=\left(\frac{B}{\alpha}\right)\times\left(\frac{f_{\theta}}{e}\right) $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: If the focal length of objective lens is increased, then magnifying power of:
questions.optionA: microscope will increase but that of telescope decrease
questions.optionB: microscope and telescope both will increase
questions.optionC: microscope and telescope both will decrease
questions.optionD: microscope will decrease but that of telescope will increase.
questions.explanation: $\because$ Magnifying power, $m \propto \frac{1}{f_{o}} \rightarrow$ microscope $$ \frac{f_{o}}{f_{e}}=m $$ $ m \propto f_o \rightarrow $ telescope The magnifying power of microscope decreases and magnifying power of telescope increases.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A microscope is focussed on a mark on a piece of paper and then a slab of glass of thickness 3 cm and refractive index 1.5 is placed over the mark. How should the microscope be moved to get the mark in focus again?
questions.explanation:
Shifting in microscope = upward shifting in mark $$ t\biggl(1-\frac{1}{\upmu}\biggr)\;=\;3\biggl(1-\frac{1}{1\cdot5}\biggr)=1\mathrm{~c m} $$
##### Mnemonics Magnifying power of simple microscope Microscope says, I add Detailed focus (microscope) $ \mathbf{m} = 1 + \left( \frac{D}{f} \right)_{\text{focus}}^{\text{Detailed}} $

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: An astronomical telescope of ten-fold angular magnification has a length of 44 cm. The focal length of the objective is:
questions.explanation: For an astronomical telescope, Therefore, $$ m=\frac{f_{o}}{f_{e}} $$ where, $ f_{o} $ = focal length of objective lens. $ f_{e}= $ focal length of eyepiece lens Length of telescope tube, $ L = f_{o} + f_{e} $ $$ m=10,L=44cm $$ $$ \frac{f_{o}}{f_{e}}=10 $$ and $$ f_{o}+f_{e}=44 $$ $$ f_{e}=\frac{f_{o}}{10} $$ Therefore, $$ f_{o}+\frac{f_{o}}{10}=44 $$ or $$ \frac{11f_{o}}{10}=44 $$ or $$ f_{o}=40\ cm $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.subtopic: Interference of Light Waves and Young's Experiment
questions.question: Two slits in Young's double slit experiment are 1.5 mm apart and the screen is placed at a distance of 1 m from the slits. If the wavelength of light used is $ 600 \times 10^{-9} $ m then the fringe separation is:

questions.subject: Physics
questions.subtopic: Interference of Light Waves and Young's Experiment
questions.question: Interference pattern can be observed due to superposition of the following waves: (I) $y = a \sin \omega t$ (II) $y = a \sin 2\omega t$ (III) $y = a \sin (\omega t - \phi)$ (IV) $y = a \sin 3\omega t$ Choose the correct answer from the options given below:
questions.explanation: For a sustainable interference, two waves should have same frequency with a constant phase difference. Wave (I) and (III) satisfy this condition whereas (II) and (IV) have different frequencies.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.subtopic: Interference of Light Waves and Young's Experiment
questions.question: If the monochromatic source in Young's double slit experiment is replaced by white light, then:
questions.optionA: there will be a central dark fringe surrounded by a few coloured fringes.
questions.optionB: there will be a central bright white fringe surrounded by a few coloured fringes.
questions.optionC: all bright fringes will be of equal width.
questions.explanation:
When we use white light in place of monochromatic light then only central fringe looks white and other fringe of different colour are observed. It is because central fringe for all the colour are formed at central point so it becomes white after mixing up. As fringe of other colours fall at different places we see fringes of all colour and a central bright fringe.
##### Related Theory Interference occurs when two waves are travelling in same medium but it is only observable when significant maxima and minima are sustained at one place for sufficient time. For example in Young's double slit experiment.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.subtopic: Interference of Light Waves and Young's Experiment
questions.question: For Young's double slit experiment, two statements are given below: Statement I: If screen is moved away from the plane of slits, angular separation of the fringes remains constant. Statement II: If the monochromatic source is replaced by another monochromatic source of larger wavelength, the angular separation of fringes decreases. In the light of the above statements, choose the correct In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options, given below:
questions.optionA: Statement I is true but Statement II is false.
questions.optionB: Statement I is false but Statement II is true.
questions.optionC: Both Statement I and Statement II are true.
questions.optionD: Both Statement I and Statement II are false.
questions.explanation: Angular separation, $ \theta = \frac{\lambda}{d} $ So, it is independent of D, which is the distance between screen and slit. As $ \lambda $ increases, $ \theta $ also increases. Thus, statement I is true but statement II is false.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.subtopic: Interference of Light Waves and Young's Experiment
questions.question: In a Young's double slit experiment, a student observed 8 fringes in a certain segment of screen when a monochromatic light of 600 nm wavelength is used. If the wavelength of light is changed to 400 nm, then the number of fringes he would observe in the same region of the screen is:
questions.explanation: Wavelength of light is inversely proportional to number of fringes observed; $$ \begin{aligned}So,\quad&n_{1}\bar{\lambda}_{1}=n_{2}\lambda_{2}\\\Rightarrow\quad&n_{2}=\frac{n_{1}\lambda_{1}}{\lambda_{2}}\\&=\frac{600\times8}{400}=12\end{aligned} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.subtopic: Interference of Light Waves and Young's Experiment
questions.question: Two coherent sources of light interfere and produce fringe pattern on a screen. For central maximum, the phase difference between the two waves will be:
questions.explanation: From Young's double slit experiment. phase difference = $ \frac{2\pi}{\lambda} $ × path difference. But for central maximum, path difference is zero for both coherent source in interference. Which means the path difference = 0 $\therefore$ Phase difference, $\phi = \frac{2\pi}{\lambda} \times 0 = 0$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.subtopic: Interference of Light Waves and Young's Experiment
questions.question: In Young's double slit experiment, if the separation between coherent source is halved and the distance of the screen from the coherent sources is doubled, then the fringe width becomes:
questions.explanation: From Young's double slit experiment, Fringe width $ \beta = \frac{\lambda D}{d} $ $$ \frac{\beta_{2}}{\beta_{1}}\;=\;\frac{\frac{\lambda_{2}\mathrm{D}_{2}}{d_{2}}}{\frac{\lambda_{1}\mathrm{D}_{1}}{d_{1}}} $$ ∴ Given, $$ \lambda_{1}=\lambda_{2},D_{2}=2D_{1},and d_{2}=\frac{d_{1}}{2} $$ putting these values in equation (i) $$ \frac{\beta_{2}}{\beta_{1}}=\frac{2D_{1}}{\left(\frac{d_{1}}{2}\right)}\times\frac{d_{1}}{D_{1}}=4 $$ $$ \beta_{2}=4\beta_{1} $$ fringe width becomes four times.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.subtopic: Interference of Light Waves and Young's Experiment
questions.question: In a double slit experiment, when light of wavelength 400 nm was used, the angular width of the first minima formed on a screen placed 1 m away, was found to be $0.2^{\circ}$. What will be the angular width of the first minima, if the entire experimental apparatus is immersed in water? ($\mu_{\mathrm{water}} = 4/3$)
questions.explanation: Angular width, $ \theta = 0.2^{\circ} $ Now, $$ \theta=\frac{\beta}{D}=0.2^{\circ} $$ In water, $$ \theta^{\prime}=\frac{\beta}{\mu D} $$ $ \therefore $ $$ \begin{aligned}\theta^{\prime}&=\frac{\theta}{\mu}=\frac{0.2}{4/3}\\&=0.15^{\circ}\end{aligned} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.subtopic: Interference of Light Waves and Young's Experiment
questions.question: In a Young's double slit experiment, if there is no initial phase-difference between the light from the two slits, a point on the screen corresponding to the fifth minimum has path difference:
questions.explanation: In Young's double slit experiment $ \Delta x = \text{path difference} = (2n - 1) \frac{\lambda}{2} $ So, Here $n = 5$ for $5^{\text{th}}$ minima $$ \mathrm{^{\bullet}} $$ $$ \Delta x=(2\times5-1)\frac{\lambda}{2}=\frac{9}{2}\lambda $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.subtopic: Interference of Light Waves and Young's Experiment
questions.question: In Young's double slit experiment, the separation d between the slits is 2 mm, the wavelength $\lambda$ of the light used is 5896 Å and distance D between the screen and slits is 100 cm. It is found that the angular width of the fringes is $0.20^{\circ}$. To increase the fringe angular width to $0.21^{\circ}$ (with same $\lambda$ and D) the separation between the slits needs to be changed to:
questions.explanation: In Young's double slit experiment, Angular width of fringe is: ⇄ $$ \begin{aligned}\theta&=\frac{\lambda}{d}\\\theta&\propto\frac{1}{d}\\\frac{\theta_{1}}{\theta_{2}}&=\frac{d_{2}}{d_{1}}\\\theta_{1}&=0.20^{\circ}\\\theta_{2}&=0.21^{\circ}\\d_{1}&=2\mathrm{~mm}\end{aligned} $$ or In question, Then eq. (i) becomes, $$ \frac{0.20^{\circ}}{0.21^{\circ}}=\frac{d_{2}}{2mm} $$ $ \Rightarrow $ $$ d_{2}=2\times\frac{0.20}{0.21}=\frac{0.40}{0.21} $$ $ =1.90\ mm $

questions.subject: Physics
questions.subtopic: Interference of Light Waves and Young's Experiment
questions.question: Young's double slit experiment is first performed in air and then in a medium other than air. It is found that $5^{\text{th}}$ dark fringe in air is equal to $8^{\text{th}}$ bright fringe lies in medium. The refractive index of the medium is nearly:
questions.explanation: From the question, it is clear that $5^{\mathrm{th}}$ dark fringe in air = $8^{\mathrm{th}}$ bright fringe in medium $$ (2\times5-1)\frac{\lambda_{air}D}{2d}=8\frac{\lambda_{m}D}{d} $$ $$ 9\frac{\lambda_{air}\mathbf{D}}{2d}=8\frac{\lambda_{m}\mathbf{D}}{d} $$ $$ \mu=\frac{\lambda_{air}}{\lambda_{m}} $$ $$ \mu=\frac{8\times2}{9}=\frac{16}{9} $$ ∴ Refractive index of medium $$ \mu=\frac{16}{9}\cong1.78 $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.subtopic: Interference of Light Waves and Young's Experiment
questions.question: The interference pattern is obtained with two coherent light sources of intensity ratio n. In the interference pattern, the ratio $ \frac{\mathrm{I}_{\max}-\mathrm{I}_{\min}}{\mathrm{I}_{\max}+\mathrm{I}_{\min}} $ will be:
questions.explanation: Let, $$ \frac{1_{1}}{1,}=\frac{n}{1} $$ $$ \begin{aligned}\frac{\mathrm{I}_{\max}-\mathrm{I}_{\min}}{\mathrm{I}_{\max}+\mathrm{I}_{\min}}&=\frac{\left(\sqrt{\mathrm{I}_{1}}+\sqrt{\mathrm{I}_{2}}\right)^{2}-\left(\sqrt{\mathrm{I}_{1}}-\sqrt{\mathrm{I}_{2}}\right)^{2}}{\left(\sqrt{\mathrm{I}_{1}}+\sqrt{\mathrm{I}_{2}}\right)^{2}+\left(\sqrt{\mathrm{I}_{1}}-\sqrt{\mathrm{I}_{2}}\right)^{2}}\\&=\frac{4\sqrt{\mathrm{I}_{1}\mathrm{I}_{2}}}{2(\mathrm{I}_{1}+\mathrm{I}_{2})}\end{aligned} $$ Dividing numerator and denominator by $ I_{2} $, $\therefore$ Ratio will be, $\frac{2\sqrt{\frac{\mathrm{I}_{1}}{\mathrm{I}_{2}}}}{\left(\frac{\mathrm{I}_{1}}{\mathrm{I}_{2}}+1\right)}=\frac{2\sqrt{n}}{n+1}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.subtopic: Interference of Light Waves and Young's Experiment
questions.question: The maximum intensity in a Young's double slit experiment is I_{0}. Distance between slits is d=5\lambda, where \lambda is the wavelength of light used in the experiment. What will be the intensity in front of one of the slits on the screen placed at a distance D=10d?
questions.explanation: $$ \begin{array}{l}\Delta x=\mathrm{S}_{2}\mathrm{P}-\mathrm{S}_{1}\mathrm{P}\\ =\sqrt{\mathrm{D}^{2}+d^{2}}-\mathrm{D}\\ =\mathrm{D}\left(1+\frac{1}{2}\frac{d^{2}}{\mathrm{D}^{2}}\right)-\mathrm{D}\\ =\mathrm{D}\left[1+\frac{d^{2}}{2\mathrm{D}^{2}}-1\right]=\frac{d^{2}}{2\mathrm{D}}\end{array} $$ Now $$ \begin{aligned}\Delta x&=\frac{d^{2}}{2\times10d}=\frac{d}{20}\\&=\frac{5\lambda}{20}=\frac{\lambda}{4}\end{aligned} $$ $$ \begin{aligned}\Delta\phi&=\frac{2\pi}{\lambda}.\frac{\lambda}{4}=\frac{\pi}{2}\\&\left\{\because\operatorname{Phase}\operatorname{diff.}=\frac{2\pi}{\lambda}\times\operatorname{Path}\operatorname{diff.}\right\}\end{aligned} $$ So, intensity at the desired point is, $$ \mathrm{I}=\mathrm{I}_{0}\cos^{2}\frac{\Delta\phi}{2}=\mathrm{I}_{0}\cos^{2}\frac{\pi}{4}=\frac{\mathrm{I}_{0}}{2} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.subtopic: Interference of Light Waves and Young's Experiment
questions.question: Two slits in Young's experiment have widths in the ratio 1:25. The ratio of intensity at the maxima and minima in the interference pattern, $ \frac{I_{max}}{I_{min}} $ is:
questions.explanation: In Young's double slit experiment, two slits of width are in the ratio 1:25. ∴ Ratio of intensity, $$ \begin{aligned}&\frac{\mathrm{I}_{1}}{\mathrm{I}_{2}}=\frac{\mathrm{W}_{1}}{\mathrm{W}_{2}}=\frac{1}{25}\\ &\frac{\mathrm{I}_{1}}{\mathrm{I}_{2}}=\frac{1}{25}\\ \end{aligned} $$ ∴ From the question, $$ \frac{\mathrm{I}_{\max}}{\mathrm{I}_{\min}}=\frac{\left(\sqrt{\mathrm{I}_{2}}+\sqrt{\mathrm{I}_{1}}\right)^{2}}{\left(\sqrt{\mathrm{I}_{2}}-\sqrt{\mathrm{I}_{1}}\right)^{2}} $$ $$ \frac{\mathrm{I}_{\max}}{\mathrm{I}_{\min}}=\left[\frac{\sqrt{\frac{\mathrm{I}_{2}}{\mathrm{I}_{1}}}+1}{\sqrt{\frac{\mathrm{I}_{2}}{\mathrm{I}_{1}}}-1}\right]^{2} $$ $$ \frac{I_{max}}{I_{min}}=\left[\frac{5+1}{5-1}\right]^{2} $$ $$ \frac{I_{max}}{I_{min}}=\frac{6^{2}}{4^{2}} $$ $$ \frac{I_{max}}{I_{min}}=\frac{36}{16} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.subtopic: Interference of Light Waves and Young's Experiment
questions.question: In a double slit experiment, the two slits are 1 mm apart and the screen is placed 1 m away. A monochromatic light of wavelength 500 nm is used. What will be the width of each slit for obtaining ten maxima of double slit within the central maxima of single slit pattern?
questions.explanation:
$$ \frac{I_{max}}{I_{min}}=\frac{9}{4} $$
Given : $$ d=1\mathrm{~mm}=1\times10^{-3}\mathrm{~m} $$ $$ \mathrm{D=l m} $$ $$ \lambda=5\times10^{-7}m $$ As width of central maxima = width of 10 maxima $$ \frac{2\lambda\mathrm{D}}{a}\;=\;10\left(\frac{\lambda\mathrm{D}}{d}\right) $$ $$ a=\frac{d}{5}=\frac{10^{-3}}{5}=0.2\times10^{-3}m $$ $$ a=0.2\mathrm{~mm} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.subtopic: Interference of Light Waves and Young's Experiment
questions.question: In the Young's double-slit experiment, the intensity of light at a point on the screen (where the path difference is $\lambda$) is K, ($\lambda$ being the wavelength of light used). The intensity at point where the path difference is $\frac{\lambda}{4}$, will be:
questions.explanation: We know that $$ \mathrm{I}=\mathrm{I}_{0}\cos^{2}\left[\frac{2\pi(r_{1}-r_{2})}{2\lambda}\right] $$ and $ r_{1}-r_{2}=\lambda $ $$ \mathrm{K\ =I_{0}\cos^{2}\left[\frac{2\pi}{\lambda}\times\frac{\lambda}{2}\right]} $$ $$ \mathrm{K}=\mathrm{I}_{0}\cos^{2}\pi $$ $$ \mathrm{K\rho=I_{0}} $$ $$ \because\cos^{2}\pi=(-1)^{2}=1 $$ Now again, $ I = I_{0} \cos^{2}\left[\frac{2\pi(r_{1}-r_{2})}{2\lambda}\right] $ and $ r_{1}-r_{2}=\frac{\lambda}{4} $ $$ \begin{aligned}\mathrm{I}&=\mathrm{I}_{0}\cos^{2}\left[\frac{2\pi}{\lambda}\times\frac{\lambda}{8}\right]\\&=\mathrm{I}_{0}\cos^{2}\frac{\pi}{4}=\frac{\mathrm{I}_{0}}{2}\end{aligned} $$ $$ I=\frac{K}{2} $$ $$ [\because\mathrm{I}_{0}=\mathrm{K}~from~eq.~(i)] $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.subtopic: Interference of Light Waves and Young's Experiment
questions.question: In Young's double slit experiment, the slits are 2 mm apart and are illuminated by photons of two wavelength $\lambda_{1}=12000\ \mathrm{Å}$ and $\lambda_{2}=10000\ \mathrm{Å}$. At what minimum distance from the common central bright fringe on the screen 2 m from the slit will a bright fringe from one interference pattern coincide with a bright from the other?
