questions.explanation: According to the question, $$ n_{1}\lambda_{1}=n_{2}\lambda_{2} $$ So $$ \frac{n_{1}}{n_{2}}=\frac{\lambda_{2}}{\lambda_{1}}=\frac{10000}{12000}=\frac{5}{6} $$ So maximum $n_{1}$ and $n_{2}$ are 5 and 6 respectively $$ \begin{aligned}X_{mn}&=\frac{n_{1}\lambda_{1}D}{d}=\frac{5(12000\times10^{-10})(2)}{2\times10^{-3}}\\&=6\times10^{-3}m=6mm\end{aligned} $$ $$ =6\times10^{-3}m=6mm. $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.subtopic: Interference of Light Waves and Young's Experiment
questions.question: The periodic waves of intensities $I_{1}$ and $I_{2}$ pass through a region at the same time in the same direction. The sum of the maximum and minimum intensities is:
questions.explanation: The resultant intensity of two periodic waves at a point is given by $$ \mathbf{l}=\mathbf{l}_{1}+\mathbf{l}_{2}+2\sqrt{\mathbf{l}_{1}\mathbf{l}_{2}}.\cos\phi $$ Resultant intensity is minimum, if $$ \cos\phi=-1 $$ i.e. $$ I_{\min}=I_{1}+I_{2}-2\sqrt{I_{1}I_{2}} $$ Resultant intensity is maximum, if $$ \cos\phi=+1 $$ $$ \mathrm{I}_{\max}=\mathrm{I}_{1}+\mathrm{I}_{2}+2\sqrt{\mathrm{I}_{1}\mathrm{I}_{2}} $$ Therefore, the sum of the maximum and minimum intensities is $ \mathrm{I}_{\max} + \mathrm{I}_{\min} $. $ \mathrm{I}_{1}+\mathrm{I}_{2}+2\sqrt{\mathrm{I}_{1}\mathrm{I}_{2}}+\mathrm{I}_{1}+\mathrm{I}_{2}-2\sqrt{\mathrm{I}_{1}\mathrm{I}_{2}}=2(\mathrm{I}_{1}+\mathrm{I}_{2}) $

questions.subject: Physics
questions.subtopic: Interference of Light Waves and Young's Experiment
questions.question: A paper, with two marks having separation $d$, is held normal to the line of sight of an observer at a distance of 50 m. The diameter of the eye-lens of the observer is 2 mm. Which of the following is the least value of $d$, so that the marks can be seen as separate? (The mean wavelength of visible light may be taken as 5000 Å.)
questions.explanation: Angular limit of resolution of eye, $\theta = \frac{\lambda}{d}$, where $d$ is diameter of one where, $d$ is diameter of eye lens. Also, if Y is the minimum separation between two objects at distance D from eye then, $$ \theta=\frac{\mathrm{Y}}{\mathrm{D}} $$ $$ \frac{\mathrm{Y}}{\mathrm{D}}\;=\;\frac{\lambda}{d} $$ $$ \mathrm{Y}=\frac{\lambda\mathrm{D}}{d} $$ $$ \Rightarrow $$ Here, wavelength, $ \lambda = 5000 \, \text{\AA} = 5 \times 10^{-7} \, \text{m} $ D = 50 m Diameter of eye lens = 2 mm = 2 × 10^{-3} m From eq. (i), minimum separation is, $$ \begin{aligned}\mathrm{Y}&=\frac{5\times10^{-7}\times50}{2\times10^{-3}}\\&=12.5\times10^{-3}\mathrm{~m}\\&=1.25\mathrm{~cm}\end{aligned} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.subtopic: Interference of Light Waves and Young's Experiment
questions.question: Colours appear on a thin soap film and on soap bubbles due to the phenomenon of:
questions.explanation: We know that the colours for which the condition of constructive interference is satisfied are observed in a given region of the film. The path difference between the light waves reaching the eye changes when the position of the eye is changed. Therefore, colours appear on a thin soap film or on soap bubbles due to the phenomenon of interference.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.subtopic: Interference of Light Waves and Young's Experiment
questions.question: In a Fresnel biprism experiment, the two positions of lens give separation between the slits as 16 cm and 9 cm respectively. What is the actual distance of separation?
questions.explanation: Separation between slits are $$ r_{1}=16\mathrm{~cm}\mathrm{a n d}r_{2}=9\mathrm{~cm}. $$ Actual distance of separation $ d = \sqrt{r_1 r_2} $ $ =\sqrt{16 \times 9} = 12 \, \text{cm} $

questions.subject: Physics
questions.subtopic: Interference of Light Waves and Young's Experiment
questions.question: Interference was observed in interference chamber where air was present, now the chamber is evacuated, and if the same light is used, a careful observer will see:
questions.explanation: In vacuum, $ \lambda $ increases very slightly compared to that in air. As $$ \beta=\frac{\lambda D}{d} $$ Since, $$ \beta\propto\lambda, $$ therefore, width of interference fringe increases slightly.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.subtopic: Interference of Light Waves and Young's Experiment
questions.question: In Young's double slit experiment carried out with light of wavelength ($\lambda$) = 5000 Å, The distance between the slits is 0.2 mm and the screen is at 200 cm from the slits. The central maximum is at $x = 0$. The third maximum (taking the central maximum as zeroth maximum) will be at $x$ equal to:
questions.explanation: $$ \begin{aligned}X=n\lambda\frac{D}{d}\end{aligned} $$ $$ x=3\times5000\times10^{-10}\times\frac{2}{0.2\times10^{-3}} $$ $$ x=1.5\times10^{-2}m $$ $$ x=1.5cm $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.subtopic: Interference of Light Waves and Young's Experiment
questions.question: If yellow light emitted by sodium lamp in Young's double slit experiment is replaced by a monochromatic blue light of the same intensity,
questions.explanation: As $ \beta = \frac{\lambda D}{d} $ and $ \lambda_b < \lambda_y $, Fringe width $ \beta $ will decrease.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.subtopic: Interference of Light Waves and Young's Experiment
questions.question: In Young's experiment, two coherent sources are placed 0.90 mm apart and fringes are observed one metre away. If it produces second dark fringe at a distance of 1 mm from central fringe, the wavelength of monochromatic light used would be:
questions.explanation: For dark fringe, $$ x=(2n-1)\frac{\lambda D}{2d} $$ $ \therefore $ $$ \lambda=\frac{2Xd}{(2n-1)D} $$ $$ \lambda=\frac{2\times10^{-3}\times0.9\times10^{-3}}{(2\times2-1)\times1} $$ $$ \lambda=0.6\times10^{-6}m $$ $$ \lambda=6\times10^{-5}cm $$ $$ \frac{\mathrm{I}_{1}}{\mathrm{I}_{2}}=\frac{a_{1}^{2}}{a_{2}^{2}}=\frac{4}{1} $$ $$ \frac{a_{1}}{a_{2}}=\frac{\sqrt{4}}{1}=2:1 $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.subtopic: Interference of Light Waves and Young's Experiment
questions.question: Ratio of intensities of two waves are given by 4:1, Then the ratio of the amplitudes of the two waves is:

questions.subject: Physics
questions.subtopic: Interference of Light Waves and Young's Experiment
questions.question: The Young’s double slit experiment is performed with blue and with green light of wavelengths 4360 Å and 5460 Å respectively. If x is the distance of $ 4^{th} $ maxima from the central one, then:
questions.explanation: Distance of $ n^{\text{th}} $ maxima, $ X = n \lambda \frac{D}{d} \propto \lambda $ As $ \lambda_{b} < \lambda_{g} $ $ \therefore x_{blue} < x_{green} $ #### Related Theory Fringes with red light are thicker than those for blue light. $$ \lambda_{\mathrm{r e d}}<\lambda_{\mathrm{b l u e}} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.subtopic: Interference of Light Waves and Young's Experiment
questions.question: In Young's double slit experiment, the fringe width is found to be 0.4 mm. If the whole apparatus is immersed in water of refractive index $\frac{4}{3}$, without disturbing the geometrical arrangement, the new fringe width will be:
questions.explanation: $ \beta' = \frac{\beta}{\mu} = \frac{0.4}{\frac{4}{3}} = 0.3 \, \text{mm} $

questions.subject: Physics
questions.subtopic: Interference of Light Waves and Young's Experiment
questions.question: Interference is possible in:
questions.explanation: Interference is a wave phenomenon shown by both the light waves and sound waves.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Angular width of the central maxima in the Fraunhoffer diffraction for $\lambda = 6000\,\text{\AA}$ is $\theta_0$. When the same slit is illuminated by another monochromatic light, the angular width decreases by 30%. The wavelength of this light is:
questions.explanation: As, $$ \theta_{0}=\frac{2\lambda}{a} $$ $$ \frac{\theta^{\prime}}{\theta_{0}}=\frac{\lambda^{\prime}}{6000} $$ ⇔ $$ \begin{array}{l}\lambda^{\prime}=0.7\times6000(as\theta^{\prime}=0.7\theta_{0})\\=4200\mathring{A}\end{array} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A linear aperture whose width is 0.02 cm is placed immediately in front of a lens of focal length 60 cm. The aperture is illuminated normally by a parallel beam of wavelength $5 \times 10^{-5}$ cm. The distance of the first dark band of the diffraction pattern from the centre of the screen is:
questions.explanation: Here $f = D = 60\ \text{cm} = 0.6\ \text{m}$ Distance of first dark band from centre of screen, $$ \begin{aligned}y&=\frac{\lambda\mathrm{D}}{a}=\frac{5\times10^{-7}\times0.6}{2\times10^{-2}\times10^{-2}}\\&=15\times10^{-4}\mathrm{~m}=0.15\mathrm{~c}\end{aligned} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: In a diffraction pattern due to a single slit of width $a$, the first minimum is observed at an angle $30^{\circ}$ when light of wavelength $5000\,\mathrm{Å}$ is incident on the slit. The first secondary maximum is observed at an angle of:
questions.explanation: According to the question, first minimum is observed at angle $ 30^{\circ} $ at single slit of width a means $$ n=1,\theta=30^{\circ} $$ From Bragg's law $ a \sin \theta = n \lambda $ a sin 30° = λ $$ a=2\lambda $$ and the first secondary maxima. $$ a\sin\theta_{1}=\frac{3\lambda}{2} $$ $$ \theta_{1}=\frac{3\lambda}{2a} $$ $ \Rightarrow $ From eq. (i) and (ii), $$ \begin{array}{c} \text{From eq. (1) and (II),}\\ \sin\theta_{1}=\dfrac{3\lambda}{2(2\lambda)}\\\\ \Rightarrow\qquad\sin\theta_{1}=\dfrac{3}{4}\\\\ \Rightarrow\qquad\theta_{1}=\sin^{-1}\left(\dfrac{3}{4}\right)\\ \end{array} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: At the first minimum adjacent to the central maximum of a single slit diffraction pattern, the phase difference between the Huygen's wavelet from the edge of the slit and the wavelet from the midpoint of the slit is:
questions.explanation: Phase difference, $\Delta\phi = \frac{\Delta x}{\lambda} \times 2\pi = \frac{(a / 2)\sin\theta}{\lambda} \times 2\pi$ $$ \Delta\phi=\frac{\lambda}{2\lambda}\times2\pi=\pi\text{radian} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: For a parallel beam of monochromatic light of wavelength \lambda' diffraction is produced by a single slit whose width a' is of the order of the wavelength of the light. If D' is the distance of the screen from the slit, the width of the central maxima will be:
questions.explanation: From diagram $ \sin \theta = \frac{\lambda}{a} $ $ \sin\theta=\theta=\frac{Y}{D} $ (for small angle) $$ \frac{Y}{D}=\frac{\lambda}{a} $$ $$ Y=\frac{\lambda D}{a} $$ $\therefore$ Width of central maxima = 2Y $=\frac{2\lambda D}{a}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A beam of light of $\lambda=600\ nm$ from a distant source falls on single slit 1 mm wide and the resulting diffraction pattern is observed on a screen 2 m away. The distance between first dark fringes on either side of the central bright fringe is:
questions.explanation: For a dark fringe to form, $$ \begin{aligned}\frac{dy}{D}&=\lambda\\y&=\frac{D\lambda}{d}\\&=\frac{2\times600\times10^{-9}}{10^{-3}}=1.2mm\end{aligned} $$ Distance between the first dark fringes on either side of central bright fringe is $ 2 \times y = 2.4 \, mm $.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A parallel beam of fast moving electrons is incident normally on a narrow slit. A fluorescent screen is placed at a large distance from the slit. If the speed of the electrons is increased, which of the following statements is correct?
questions.optionA: Diffraction pattern is not observed on the screen in the case of electrons
questions.optionB: The angular width of the central maximum of the diffraction pattern will increase
questions.optionC: The angular width of the central maximum will decrease
questions.optionD: The angular width of the central maximum will be unaffected
questions.explanation: As speed of electrons is increased, so wavelength of electrons will decrease. Therefore, the angular width ( $ \propto\lambda $) of the central maximum of diffraction pattern will decrease.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A parallel beam of light of wavelength $\lambda$ is incident normally on a narrow slit. A diffraction pattern formed on a screen placed perpendicular to the direction of the incident beam. At the second minimum of the diffraction pattern, the phase difference between the rays coming from the two edges of slit is:
questions.explanation: We know that path difference for second minimum = 2$\lambda$. $\therefore$ Phase difference $=\frac{2\pi}{\lambda}\times2\lambda$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Which one of the following phenomena is NOT explained by Huygens construction of wavefront?
questions.explanation: Huygen's construction of wavefront does not apply to origin of spectra, which is explained by quantum theory.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A beam of unpolarized light of intensity $I_{0}$ is passed through a polaroid X, then through another polaroid Y, oriented at $60^{\circ}$ and finally through another polaroid Z, oriented at $45^{\circ}$ relative to Y as shown. The intensity of emergent light is:
questions.explanation: When unpolarized light passes through the first polaroid X, its intensity is reduced by half. $$ I_{1}=\frac{I_{0}}{2} $$ When polarised light passes through polaroid Y, According to Malus' law $$ I_{2}=I_{1}\cos^{2}60^{\circ} $$ On comparing eqn (i) and (ii) $$ \mathrm{I}_{2}=\frac{\mathrm{I}_{0}}{2}\cos^{2}60^{\circ} $$ $$ \mathrm{I}_{2}=\frac{\mathrm{I}_{0}}{2}\times\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{2}\left(\because\cos60^{\circ}=\frac{1}{2}\right) $$ $$ I_{2}=\frac{I_{0}}{8} $$ When polarised light passes through polaroid Z, $ \mathrm{I}_{3}=\mathrm{I}_{2}\cos^{2}45^{\circ} $ $$ \mathrm{I}_{3}=\frac{\mathrm{I}_{0}}{8}\cos^{2}45^{\circ}\left(\because\cos45^{\circ}=\sqrt{\frac{1}{2}}\right) $$ $$ \mathrm{I}_{3}=\frac{\mathrm{I}_{0}}{8}\left(\sqrt{\frac{1}{2}}\right)^{2}=\frac{\mathrm{I}_{0}}{16} $$ $$ I_{3}=\frac{I_{0}}{16} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: An unpolarised light beam strikes a glass surface at Brewster's angle. Then:
questions.optionA: the refracted light will be completely polarised.
questions.optionB: both the reflected and refracted light will be completely polarised.
questions.optionC: the reflected light will be completely polarised but the refracted light will be partially polarised.
questions.optionD: the reflected light will be partially polarised.
questions.explanation: When unpolarised light strikes a surface at Brewster's angle, the reflected light becomes completely polarized parallel to the surface. However, the refracted light remains partially polarized.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: The Brewster's angle, $ i_{b} $ for an interface should be
questions.explanation: According to Brewster's law, when a beam of unpolarised light is reflected from a transparent medium of refractive index $(\mu_{2})$, the reflected light is completely polarised at certain angle of incidence called the angle of polarisation $(i_{b})$. $$ i_{b}=\frac{\mu_{2}}{\mu_{1}} $$ For air, $ \mu_{1}=1 $ $$ \therefore\tan i_{b}=\mu_{2}>1 $$ $$ \Rightarrow\quad\tan i_{b}>1 $$ $$ \begin{array}{r} 90^{\circ} > i_{b} > 45^{\circ} \end{array} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Unpolarised light is incident from air on a plane surface of a material of refractive index $ \mu' $. At a particular angle of incidence 'i', it is found that the reflected and refracted rays are perpendicular to each other. Which of the following options is correct for this situation?
questions.optionB: Reflected light is polarised with its electric vector perpendicular to the plane of incidence
questions.optionC: Reflected light is polarised with its electric vector parallel to the plane of incidence
questions.explanation:
When reflected light rays and refracted rays are perpendicular, reflected light is polarised with electric field vector perpendicular to the plane of incidence.
Also, $ \tan i = \mu $ (Brewster's law).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Two polaroid P_{1} and P_{2} are placed with their axis perpendicular to each other. Unpolarised light I_{0} is incident on P_{1}. A third polaroid P_{3} is kept in between P_{1} and P_{2} such that its axis makes an angle 45^{\circ} with that of P_{1}. The intensity of transmitted light through P_{2} is:
questions.explanation:
According to the given question,
From the diagram. The intensity transmitted through $P_{3}$. $$ \begin{aligned}\mathrm{I}_{2}&=\frac{\mathrm{I}_{0}}{2}\cos^{2}45^{\circ}\\&=\frac{\mathrm{I}_{0}}{2}\times\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\right)^{2}=\frac{\mathrm{I}_{0}}{4}\end{aligned} $$ Again intensity transmitted through $P_{2}$ $$ \mathrm{I}_{3}=\frac{\mathrm{I}_{0}}{4}\cos^{2}45^{\circ}=\frac{\mathrm{I}_{0}}{4}\times\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\right)^{2}=\frac{\mathrm{I}_{0}}{8} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Which of the following phenomenon is NOT common to sound and light waves?
questions.explanation: Sound waves can not be polarised as they are longitudinal. questions.subject: Physics
questions.topic: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter
questions.question: In a discharge tube, ionization of enclosed gas is produced due to collisions between:
questions.explanation: Negative electrons and neutral atoms collides and causes the ionization of gas enclosed in a discharge tube.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.topic: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter
questions.question: J.J. Thomson's cathode ray tube experiment demonstrated that:
questions.optionA: cathode rays are streams of negatively charged ions
questions.optionB: all the mass of an atom is essentially in the nucleus
questions.optionC: the  $ \frac{e}{m} $ of electrons is much greater than the  $ \frac{e}{m} $ of protons
questions.optionD: the  $ \frac{e}{m} $ ratio of the cathode-ray particles changes when a different gas is placed in the discharged tube
questions.explanation: $$ \left(\frac{e}{m}\right)_{electron}>>\left(\frac{e}{m}\right)_{proton} $$ $$ \left[\because\left(\frac{e}{m}\right)_{proton}=\frac{1}{1837}\left(\frac{e}{m}\right)_{electron}\right] $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.topic: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter
questions.question: Which of the following is NOT the property of cathode rays?
questions.optionB: It does not deflect in an electric field
questions.explanation:
Cathode rays deflect in an electric field.
##### Related Theory Stopping potential increases linearly with the frequency of incident photons but independent of the intensity.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.topic: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter
questions.question: A source of light is placed at a distance of 50 cm from photocells and the stopping potential is found to be $V_{0}$. If the distance between the light source and photo cells is made 25 cm, the new stopping potential will be:
questions.explanation: Stopping potential is independent of distance, hence, new stopping potential will remain unchanged. Stopping potential = $ V_{0} $

questions.subject: Physics
questions.topic: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter
questions.question: In photoelectric emission process from a metal of work function 1.8 eV, the kinetic energy of most energetic electrons is 0.5 eV. The corresponding stopping potential is:
questions.explanation: In a photoelectric effect, Stopping potential = Maximum kinetic energy So, $$ e\mathbf{V}=\mathbf{K}\mathbf{E}_{\max} $$ $$ V=\frac{KE_{max}}{e}=\frac{0.5eV}{e}=0.5V $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.topic: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter
questions.question: Photoelectric emission occurs only when the incident light has more than a certain minimum:
questions.explanation: From Einstein's photoelectric equation, $$ \mathbf{E}=h(\mathbf{v}-\mathbf{v}_{0}) $$ $$ \mathsf{v}\ge\mathsf{v}_{0} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.topic: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter
questions.question: When monochromatic radiation of intensity I falls on a metal surface, the number of photoelectrons and their maximum kinetic energy are N and T respectively. If the intensity of radiation is 2I, the number of emitted electrons and their maximum kinetic energy are respectively:
questions.explanation: Kinetic energy of photoelectrons depends on the frequency of incident radiation and number of photoelectrons depend on the intensity.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.topic: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter
questions.question: The potential difference that must be applied to stop the fastest photoelectrons emitted by a nickel surface, having work function 5.01 eV, when ultraviolet light of 200 nm falls on it, must be:
questions.explanation: We know that energy of incident light $$ \mathrm{E}=\frac{h c}{\lambda}=6.2\mathrm{eV} $$ Using Einstein's photoelectric equation $$ \begin{aligned}\mathrm{E}&=\mathrm{W}+\mathrm{e}\mathrm{V}_{0}\\\mathrm{e}\mathrm{V}_{0}&=\mathrm{E}-\mathrm{W}\\&=(6.2-5.01)\end{aligned} $$ $$ \begin{aligned}&=1.2\ eV\\ \mathbf{V}_{0}&=1.2\ V\end{aligned} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.topic: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter
questions.question: The figure shows a plot of photocurrent versus anode potential for a photo sensitive surface for three different radiations. Which one of the following is a correct statement? Retarding potential Anode potential
questions.optionA: Curves $a$ and $b$ represent incident radiations of different frequencies and different intensities
questions.optionB: Curves $a$ and $b$ represent incident radiations of same frequency but of different intensities
questions.optionC: Curves b and c represent incident radiations of different frequencies and different intensities
questions.optionD: Curves $b$ and $c$ represent incident radiations of same frequency having same intensity
questions.explanation: Curves $a$ and $b$ represent incident radiations of same frequency but of different intensities.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.topic: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter
questions.question: The number of photoelectrons emitted for light of a frequency $v$ (higher than the threshold frequency $v_{0}$) is proportional to:
questions.explanation: The number of photoelectrons emitted is directly proportional to the intensity of light.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.topic: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter
questions.question: A 5 watt source emits monochromatic light of wavelength 5000 Å. When placed 0.5 m away, it liberates photoelectrons from a photosensitive metallic surface. When the source is moved to a distance of 1.0 m, the number of photoelectrons liberated will be reduced by a factor of:
questions.explanation: Intensity is given by $$ I=\frac{\mathrm{P}}{4\pi d^{2}} $$ where, P = Power of source d = distance We know (assume) that light to spread out uniformly in all direction, i.e., spherical source. $$ I\propto\frac{1}{d^{2}} $$ $$ \frac{\mathrm{I}_{1}}{\mathrm{I}_{2}}=\frac{d_{2}^{2}}{d_{1}^{2}} $$ $$ \frac{\mathrm{I}_{1}}{\mathrm{I}_{2}}=\left(\frac{1}{0.5}\right)^{2} $$ $$ \frac{I_{1}}{I_{2}}=4 $$ $$ I_{2}=\frac{I_{1}}{4} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.topic: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter
questions.question: A photocell employs photoelectric effect to convert:
questions.optionA: change in the frequency of light into a change in the electric current.
questions.optionB: change in the frequency of light into a change in electric voltage.
questions.optionC: change in the intensity of illumination into a change in photoelectric current.
questions.optionD: change in the intensity of illumination into a change in the work function of the photocathode.
questions.explanation:
Photocell employs photoelectric effect to convert change in the intensity of illumination into a change in photoelectric current.
##### Related Theory A photocell is a technological application of the photoelectric effect. It is a device which converts light signal into an electrical signal.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.topic: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter
questions.question: A photoelectric cell is illuminated by a point source of light 1 m away. When the source is shifted to 2 m then:
questions.optionA: each emitted electron carries one quarter of the initial energy.
questions.optionB: number of electrons emitted is half the initial number.
questions.optionC: each emitted electron carries half the initial energy.
questions.optionD: number of electrons emitted is a quarter of the initial number.
questions.explanation: For a point source, $ 1 \propto \frac{1}{r^{2}} $ As number of photoelectrons $ \propto $ intensity, so, number of photoelectrons is quarter of the initial number.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.topic: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter
questions.question: When intensity of incident light increases:
questions.optionC: kinetic energy of emitted photoelectrons increases
questions.optionD: kinetic energy of emitted photoelectrons decreases
questions.explanation: Einstein's photoelectric effect theory states that a single incident photon ejects a single electron. As a result, as the intensity increases, the number of incident photons increases, and the number of emitted electrons increases, the photo current increases.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.topic: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter
questions.question: The cathode of a photoelectric cell is changed such that the work function changes from $ W_1 $ to $ W_2 $ ( $ W_2 > W_1 $). If the current before and after change are $ I_1 $ and $ I_2 $, all other conditions remaining unchanged, then (assuming $ \mathrm{hv} > W_2 $):
questions.explanation: By work function of a metal, it means that the minimum energy required for the electron in the highest level of conduction band to get out of the metal. The work function has no effect on photoelectric current as long as $ h\nu > W_{0} $. The photoelectric current is proportional to the intensity of incident light. Since, there is no change in the intensity of light. Hence, $$ I_{1}=I_{2} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.topic: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter
questions.question: The threshold frequency for photoelectric effect on sodium corresponds to a wavelength of 5000 Å. Its work function is:
questions.explanation: When a photon of light with frequency v strikes a photosensitive metal surface, its energy (hv) is consumed in two ways. A portion of the photon's energy is utilized to liberate the electron from the metal surface, which is equal to the metal's work function $ W_{0} $. $$ \mathbf{W}_{0}=h\mathbf{v}_{0} $$ (where, $ v_{0} $ is threshold frequency) $$ \Rightarrow\quad\mathbf{w}_{0}=\frac{h c}{\lambda_{0}} $$ Here, $$ \begin{aligned}\lambda_{0}&=5000\text{Å }\\&=5000\times10^{-10}m\end{aligned} $$ $$ \mathbf{W}_{0}=\frac{6.63\times10^{-34}\times3\times10^{8}}{5000\times10^{-10}} $$ $$ =4\times10^{-19}\mathrm{N} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.topic: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter
questions.subtopic: Einstein's Photoelectric Equation: Energy Quantum of Radiation
questions.question: If \phi is the work function of photosensitive material in eV and light of wavelength of numerical value \lambda = \frac{hc}{e} metre, is incident on it with energy above its threshold value at an instant then the maximum kinetic energy of the photo-electron ejected by it at that instant (Take h-Planck's constant, c-velocity of light in free space) is (in SI units):
questions.explanation: The energy of a photon with wavelength $ \lambda $ is given by $ e = \frac{hc}{\lambda} $. For photoelectric effect, the relation for maximum kinetic energy of the ejected electron is, $$ \mathbf{K}_{\mathrm{m a x}}=e-\boldsymbol{\phi} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.topic: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter
questions.subtopic: Einstein's Photoelectric Equation: Energy Quantum of Radiation
questions.question: The work functions of Caesium (Cs), Potassium (K) and Sodium (Na) are 2.14 eV, 2.30 eV and 2.75 eV respectively. If incident electromagnetic radiation has an incident energy of 2.20 eV, which of these photosensitive surfaces may emit photoelectrons?
questions.explanation:
Given: Energy of photon, E = 2.20 eV Work function of, $ \mathrm{Cs}_{\phi}=2.14\ \mathrm{eV} $, $$ \mathrm{K}_{\phi}=2.30~\mathrm{e V}, $$ $$ \mathrm{Na}_{\phi}=2.75\mathrm{eV} $$ we know. electron emits when $ h\nu > \phi_{0} $ So, it is clear that energy of photon is more than the work function of Cs only. Hence, Cs can emit the photoelectrons.
##### Related Theory The number of photoelectrons emitted per second is proportional to the intensity of incident radiation.
##### Mnemonics Electron emission can be done by 3 process (1) The-Thermionic Emission (2) Field-Field Emission (3) Photo-Photoelectric Emission

questions.subject: Physics
questions.topic: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter
questions.subtopic: Einstein's Photoelectric Equation: Energy Quantum of Radiation
questions.question: When two monochromatic light of frequency v and $ \frac{v}{2} $ are incident on a photoelectric metal, their stopping potential becomes $ \frac{V_{s}}{2} $ and $ V_{s} $ respectively. The threshold frequency for this metal is:
questions.explanation: According to photoelectric effect, $$ h\mathbf{v}=h\mathbf{v}_{o}+\frac{e\mathbf{V}_{s}}{2} $$ $$ \mathbf{E}=h\mathbf{v}_{o}+\mathbf{e}\mathbf{V}_{o} $$ $$ \frac{h\mathbf{v}}{2}=h\mathbf{v}_{o}+e\mathbf{V}_{s} $$ $$ \frac{h\mathbf{v}}{2}=\frac{-e\mathbf{V}_{s}}{2} $$ $$ -h\mathbf{v}=e\mathbf{V}_{s} $$ Putting (iii) in eq (i), we get $$ h\mathbf{v}=h\mathbf{v}_{o}-\frac{h\mathbf{v}}{2} $$ So, $$ \mathbf{v}_{o}=\frac{3}{2}\mathbf{v} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.topic: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter
questions.subtopic: Einstein's Photoelectric Equation: Energy Quantum of Radiation
questions.question: The work function of a photosensitive material is 4.0 eV. The longest wavelength of light that can cause photon emission from the substance is (approximately):
questions.explanation: Work function of material is $$ \mathbf{W}=h\mathbf{v}=\frac{hc}{\lambda} $$ Here h = planck's constant $$ h=6.63\times10^{-34}\mathrm{J{-}s} $$ $$ c=\mathrm{speed~of~light}=3\times10^{8}\mathrm{~m/s} $$ $$ \lambda=wavelength~of~light $$ And $$ \mathbf{W}=h\mathbf{v}=4\times1.6\times10^{-19}\mathbf{J} $$ From eq. (i) and (ii) we can write, $$ 4\times1.6\times10^{-19}=\frac{6.63\times10^{-34}\times3\times10^{8}}{\lambda} $$ $$ \lambda=\frac{6.63\times10^{-34}\times3\times10^{8}}{4\times1.6\times10^{-19}} $$ $$ \begin{aligned}\lambda&=3.108\times10^{-7}m\\&=310nm\end{aligned} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.topic: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter
questions.subtopic: Einstein's Photoelectric Equation: Energy Quantum of Radiation
questions.question: When the light $ 2v_{0} $ (where $ v_{0} $ is threshold frequency), is incident on a metal plate, the maximum velocity of electrons emitted is $ v_{1} $. When the frequency of the incident radiation is increased to $ 5v_{0} $, the maximum velocity of electrons emitted from the same plate is $ v_{2} $. The ratio of $ v_{1} $ to $ v_{2} $ is:
questions.explanation: From Einstein's photoelectric equation, we can write, $$ \mathrm{E}=\mathbf{W}_{0}+\frac{1}{2}m\mathbf{v}^{2} $$ where $$ \mathbf{W}_{0}=\mathbf{W}or\mathbf{k}\;function $$ $$ \frac{1}{2}mv^{2}=Kinectic energy $$ and $$ \mathbf{E}=h\mathbf{v} $$ When incident frequency of light is $ v = 2v_{0} $, then eq. (i) become, $$ h(2\mathbf{v}_{0})=h\mathbf{v}_{0}+\frac{1}{2}m\mathbf{v}_{1}^{2} $$ $$ h\mathbf{v}_{0}=\frac{1}{2}m\mathbf{v}_{1}^{2} $$ When incident frequency of light is $ v = 5v_{0} $, then eq. (i) becomes, $$ \begin{aligned}h(5\mathbf{v}_{0})&=h\mathbf{v}_{0}+\frac{1}{2}m\mathbf{v}_{2}^{2}\\4h\mathbf{v}_{0}&=\frac{1}{2}m\mathbf{v}_{2}^{2}\end{aligned} $$ From eq. (ii) and eq. (iii), we have $$ \begin{aligned}&\frac{h\mathbf{v}_{0}}{4h\mathbf{v}_{0}}=\frac{\frac{1}{2}m\mathbf{v}_{1}^{2}}{\frac{1}{2}m\mathbf{v}_{2}^{2}}\\ \Rightarrow\quad&\frac{\mathbf{v}_{1}^{2}}{\mathbf{v}_{2}^{2}}=\frac{1}{4}\\ \Rightarrow\quad&\frac{\mathbf{v}_{1}}{\mathbf{v}_{2}}=1:2\\ \end{aligned} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.topic: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter
questions.subtopic: Einstein's Photoelectric Equation: Energy Quantum of Radiation
questions.question: The photoelectric threshold wavelength of silver is $ 3250 \times 10^{-10} $ m. The velocity of the electron ejected from a silver surface by ultraviolet light of wavelength $ 2536 \times 10^{-10} $ m is (Given, $ h = 4.14 \times 10^{-15} $ eVs and $ c = 3 \times 10^8 $ ms $ ^{-1} $):
questions.explanation: Given: Threshold wavelength, $ \lambda_{0} = 3250 \times 10^{-10} \, \text{m} $ Wavelength of UV light, $ \lambda = 2536 \times 10^{-10} $ m We know that Einstein's photoelectric equation is. $$ \begin{aligned}&\mathrm{E}=\mathrm{K}+\mathrm{W}\\&\mathrm{h}\mathrm{v}=\frac{1}{2}m\mathrm{v}^{2}+h\mathrm{v}_{0}\\ \Rightarrow\ \frac{1}{2}m\mathrm{v}^{2}=h\mathrm{v}-h\mathrm{v}_{0}=h(\mathrm{v}-\mathrm{v}_{0})\\&=\quad h c\left(\frac{1}{\lambda}-\frac{1}{\lambda_{0}}\right)\\ \Rightarrow\quad\mathrm{v}&=\sqrt{\frac{2h c}{m_{0}}\left(\frac{1}{\lambda}-\frac{1}{\lambda_{0}}\right)}\\&=\sqrt{\frac{2\times6.02\times10^{-34}\times3\times10^{8}}{9.1\times10^{-31}}\left(\frac{3250-2536}{3250\times2536}\right)}\\&=0.6\times10^{6}\mathrm{~m s}^{-1}\\&=6\times10^{5}\mathrm{~m s}^{-1}\end{aligned} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.topic: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter
questions.subtopic: Einstein's Photoelectric Equation: Energy Quantum of Radiation
questions.question: Photons with energy 5 eV are incident on a cathode C, on a photoelectric cell. The maximum energy of emitted photoelectrons is 2 eV. When photons of energy 6 eV are incident on C, no photoelectrons will reach the anode A, if the stopping potential of A relative to C is:
questions.explanation: From photoelectric equation, we have $$ h\mathbf{v}=\mathbf{W}+\frac{1}{2}m\mathbf{v}_{0}^{2} $$ where, $$ KE=h\mathbf{v}-\mathbf{W} $$ putting the given values. $$ 2eV=5eV-W $$ $$ \mathbf{W}=3\mathbf{V} $$ $$ V_{\text{cathode}} - V_{\text{anode}} = 3\,V $$ $$ \therefore\mathrm{V}_{\text{anode}}-\mathrm{V}_{\text{cathode}}=-3\mathrm{~V} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.topic: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter
questions.subtopic: Einstein's Photoelectric Equation: Energy Quantum of Radiation
questions.question: A photoelectric surface is illuminated successively by monochromatic light of wavelength \lambda and \frac{\lambda}{2}. If the maximum kinetic energy of the emitted photoelectrons in the second case is 3 times that in the first case, the work function of the surface of the material is: $ (h = \text{Planck's constant}, c = \text{speed of light}) $
questions.explanation: From Einstein's photoelectric equation, where, $$ \begin{array}{r}{h\mathbf{v}=\mathbf{W}+\mathbf{K}_{\mathrm{m a x}}}\\ {\mathbf{W}=\mathrm{w o r k f u n c t i o n}}\\ {\mathbf{K}_{\mathrm{m a x}}=\mathrm{k i n e t i c e n e r g y}}\\ {\mathbf{K}_{\mathrm{m a x}}=h\mathbf{v}-\mathbf{W}}\end{array} $$ $$ \mathbf{K}_{\max}=\frac{hc}{\lambda}-\mathbf{W} $$ In the question, maximum kinetic energy of emitted electron is 3 times that in first case. $$ 3K_{\max}=\frac{hc}{\frac{\lambda}{2}}-W $$ From eq. (i) and (ii), $$ \mathbf{W}=\frac{hc}{2\lambda} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.topic: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter
questions.subtopic: Einstein's Photoelectric Equation: Energy Quantum of Radiation
questions.question: When the energy of the incident radiation is increased by 20%, the kinetic energy of the photoelectrons emitted from a metal surface increased from 0.5 eV to 0.8 eV. The work function of metal is:
questions.explanation: From Einstein's photoelectric equation, $$ h\mathbf{v}=\mathbf{W}+(\mathbf{K}.\mathbf{E}.)_{\max} $$ $$ \left(\mathbf{K.E.}\right)_{\max}=h\mathbf{v}-\mathbf{W} $$ In first condition, $$ 0.5=\mathrm{E}-\mathrm{W} $$ In second condition, $$ 0.8=1.2\mathrm{E}-\mathrm{W} $$ From (ii) and (iii), $$ -0.3=-0.2\mathrm{E} $$ $$ E=\frac{0.3}{0.2}=1.5eV $$ Putting in eq. (ii) we get, $$ \begin{aligned}&0.5=1.5-\mathbf{W}\\&\mathbf{W}=1.5-0.5=1\mathbf{eV}\\ \end{aligned} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.topic: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter
questions.subtopic: Einstein's Photoelectric Equation: Energy Quantum of Radiation
questions.question: For photoelectric emission from certain metal, the cut-off frequency is v. If radiation of frequency 2v impinges on the metal plate, the maximum possible velocity of the emitted electron will be (m is the electron mass):
questions.explanation: We know that, $$ \frac{1}{2}m v_{\mathrm{max}}^{2}=h v $$ $$ \mathbf{v}_{\max}^{2}=\frac{2h\mathbf{v}}{m} $$ $$ \mathbf{v}_{\mathrm{max}}=\sqrt{\frac{2h\mathbf{v}}{m}} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.topic: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter
questions.subtopic: Einstein's Photoelectric Equation: Energy Quantum of Radiation
questions.question: Two radiations of photon energies 1 eV and 2.5 eV, successively illuminate a photosensitive metallic surface of work function 0.5 eV. The ratio of the maximum speeds of the emitted electron is:
questions.explanation: $$ \begin{aligned}\mathrm{v}_{\max}&=\sqrt{\frac{2}{m}(\mathrm{KE}_{\max})}\\ &=\sqrt{\frac{2}{m}(\mathrm{E}_{ph}-\mathrm{W})}\end{aligned} $$ $$ \frac{\mathbf{v}_{1}}{\mathbf{v}_{2}}=\sqrt{\frac{1-0.5}{2.5-0.5}}=\sqrt{\frac{0.5}{2}}=1:2 $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.topic: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter
questions.subtopic: Einstein's Photoelectric Equation: Energy Quantum of Radiation
questions.question: The threshold frequency for a photosensitive metal is $3.3 \times 10^{14}$ Hz. If light of frequency $8.2 \times 10^{14}$ Hz is incident on this metal, the cut off voltage for the photoelectric emission is nearly:
questions.explanation: Given. Threshold frequency, $ v_{0} = 3.3 \times 10^{14} $ Hz and light of frequency, $ v = 8.2 \times 10^{14} $ Hz Then we know from Einstein's photoelectric equation, $$ \mathbf{e V}_{0}=\mathbf{E}-h\mathbf{v}_{0} $$ $$ \mathbf{V}_{0}=\frac{\mathrm{E}-h\mathbf{v}_{0}}{e} $$ $$ \mathbf{V}_{0}=\frac{h(\mathbf{v}-\mathbf{v}_{0})}{e} $$ $$ \mathrm{V}_{0}=\frac{6.62\times10^{-34}(8.2\times10^{14}-3.3\times10^{14})}{1.6\times10^{-19}} $$ $$ \mathrm{V}_{0}=\frac{6.62\times10^{-34}}{1.6}\times4.9\times10^{33} $$ $$ V_{0}=\frac{6.62\times4.9\times10^{-1}}{1.6} $$ $$ \mathrm{V}_{0}=2\mathrm{v o l t} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.topic: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter
questions.subtopic: Einstein's Photoelectric Equation: Energy Quantum of Radiation
questions.question: The work function of a surface of a photosensitive material is 6.2 eV. The wavelength of the incident radiations for which the stopping potential is 5 V lies in the:
questions.explanation: Using Einstein's photoelectric equation where. $$ \begin{aligned}\mathrm{KE}_{\max}&=\mathrm{E}-f\\f&=\text{work function}\\\mathrm{KE}_{\max}&=\text{Maximum kinetic energy}\\h\mathbf{v}&=\mathbf{e}\mathrm{V}_{0}+f\\h\mathbf{v}&=5\mathrm{eV}+6.2\mathrm{eV}=11.2\mathrm{eV}\\\lambda&=\left(\frac{12400}{112\mathrm{eV}}\right)=1.104\times10^{-7}\mathrm{n}\end{aligned} $$ Hence, the radiation lies in ultraviolet region.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.topic: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter
questions.subtopic: Einstein's Photoelectric Equation: Energy Quantum of Radiation
questions.question: When photons of energy hv fall on an aluminium plate (of work function $E_{0}$), photoelectrons of maximum kinetic energy K are ejected. If the frequency of radiation is doubled, the maximum kinetic energy of the ejected photoelectrons will be:
questions.explanation: According to given situation, $$ \begin{aligned}&and\\ &\Longrightarrow\\ \end{aligned} $$ $$ \begin{aligned}h\mathbf{v}&=\mathbf{E}_{0}+\mathbf{K}\\2h\mathbf{v}&=\mathbf{E}_{0}+\mathbf{K}^{\prime}\\\mathbf{K}^{\prime}&=\mathbf{K}+h\mathbf{v}\end{aligned} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.topic: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter
questions.subtopic: Einstein's Photoelectric Equation: Energy Quantum of Radiation
questions.question: The work functions for metal A, B and C are respectively 1.92 eV, 2.0 eV and 5 eV. According to Einstein's equation, the metals which will emit photoelectrons for a radiation of wavelength 4100 Å is/are:
questions.explanation: Energy of photon with wavelength 4100 Å is: $$ \begin{aligned}\frac{hc}{\lambda}&=4.845\times10^{-19}J\\&=3.024eV\end{aligned} $$ This incident electron would emit photon from metals, whose work function is less than its energy i.e., metal A and B.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.topic: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter
questions.subtopic: Einstein's Photoelectric Equation: Energy Quantum of Radiation
questions.question: A photosensitive metallic surface has work function $hv_{0}$. If photons of energy $2hv_{0}$ fall on this surface, the electrons come out with a maximum velocity of $4 \times 10^{6}$ m/s. When the photon energy is increased to $5hv_{0}$. Then maximum velocity of photoelectrons will be:
questions.explanation: According to the question, $$ 2h\mathbf{v}_{0}=h\mathbf{v}_{0}+\frac{1}{2}m(4\times10^{6})^{2} $$ $$ 5\ h v_{0}=h v_{0}+\frac{1}{2}m v_{2}^{2} $$ $\Rightarrow$ Divide eq. (ii) by eq. (i), we get $$ \begin{array}{l}\mathbf{v}_{2}^{2}=4\mathbf{v}_{1}^{2}\text{or}\mathbf{v}_{2}=2\mathbf{v}_{1}\\\mathbf{v}_{2}=2\times4\times10^{6}\\=8\times10^{6}\text{m/s}\end{array} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.topic: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter
questions.subtopic: Einstein's Photoelectric Equation: Energy Quantum of Radiation
questions.question: When ultraviolet rays incident on metal plate then the photoelectric effect does not occur, it occurs by incidence of:
questions.explanation: X-rays.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.topic: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter
questions.subtopic: Einstein's Photoelectric Equation: Energy Quantum of Radiation
questions.question: A light source is at a distance $d$ from a photoelectric cell, then the number of photoelectrons emitted from the cell is $n$. If the distance of light source and cell is reduced to half, then the number of photoelectrons emitted will become:
questions.explanation: We have, intensity of light source, $$ \mathrm{I}=\frac{1}{d^{2}} $$ where, d is the distance or light source from the cell. So, for two different cases for intensities, we have, $$ \begin{aligned}&\Rightarrow\quad\frac{\mathrm{I}_{1}}{\mathrm{I}_{2}}=\left(\frac{d_{2}}{d_{1}}\right)^{2}=\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{2}=\frac{1}{4}\\&\Rightarrow\quad\mathrm{I}_{2}=4\mathrm{I}_{1}\end{aligned} $$ As number of photoelectrons emitted is directly proportional to intensity, so number of photoelectrons emitted will become 4 times, i.e. 4n.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.topic: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter
questions.subtopic: Einstein's Photoelectric Equation: Energy Quantum of Radiation
questions.question: Einstein's work on photoelectric effect gives support to:
questions.explanation: In 1905, Einstein observed that the photoelectric effect could be explained if the energy in light was concentrated in small packets, or photons, instead of spread out over wavefronts. The energy hν is contained in each photon of light with frequency ν. Hence, Einstein's work on the photoelectric effect supports, $$ E=h\mathbf{v} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.topic: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter
questions.subtopic: Einstein's Photoelectric Equation: Energy Quantum of Radiation
questions.question: The energy of a photon of light is 3 eV. Then the wavelength of photon must be:
questions.explanation: If photon energy is expressed in (eV) and wavelength is expressed (in Å), then photon energy is given by, $$ \mathrm{E}=\frac{h c}{\lambda}=\frac{12375}{\lambda(\mathring{\mathrm{A}})}\mathrm{e}\mathrm{V}\mathring{\mathrm{A}} $$ $$ \begin{aligned}\mathrm{E}&=\frac{\overline{\lambda}}{\lambda}=\frac{\overline{\lambda(\mathring{A})}}{\mathrm{e}\mathrm{VA}}\\\therefore\quad\lambda&=\frac{12375}{\mathrm{E}(\mathrm{eV})}(\mathrm{eV})\mathring{A}\quad&[\because h c=12375\mathrm{eV}-\mathring{A}]\\&=\frac{12375}{3}\mathring{A}\\&=4125\mathring{A}=412.5\mathrm{~nm}\end{aligned} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.topic: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter
questions.subtopic: Einstein's Photoelectric Equation: Energy Quantum of Radiation
questions.question: If the threshold wavelength for a certain metal is 2000 Å, then the work function of the metal is:
questions.explanation: Given wavelength, $$ \lambda=2000\text{Å }=2000\times10^{-10} $$ $$ \begin{array}{l} \text{W}_{0}=\displaystyle \frac{h c}{\lambda}=\frac{6.6 \times 10^{-34} \times 3 \times 10^{8}}{2000 \times 10^{-10}} \\ \qquad = 9.9 \times 10^{-19} \text{J} = \frac{9.9 \times 10^{-19}}{1.6 \times 10^{-19}} \\ \qquad = 6.2 \text{eV} \end{array} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.topic: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter
questions.subtopic: Particle Nature of Light: The Photon
questions.question: If $c$ is the velocity of light in free space, the correct statements about photon among the following are: (I) The energy of a photon is $E = h\nu$ (II) The velocity of a photon is $c$ (III) The momentum of a photon, $p = \frac{h\nu}{c}$. (IV) In a photon-electron collision, both total energy and total momentum are conserved. (V) Photon possesses positive charge. Choose the correct answer from the options given below:
questions.explanation:
Energy $ E = h\nu $ velocity = c = v $ \lambda $ $$ p=\frac{h}{\lambda}=\frac{\frac{h}{c}}{v}=\frac{h\nu}{c} $$ $$ \mathrm{E_{e x d.}=0}, $$ So momentum is conserved and energy is also conserved.
##### Related Theory Photons are chargeless massless particles that carry momentum $p = \frac{\hbar v}{c}$, where $\mathbf{v}$ is the frequency of light and $c$ is the speed of light.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.topic: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter
questions.subtopic: Particle Nature of Light: The Photon
questions.question: The number of photons per second on an average emitted by the source of monochromatic light of wavelength 600 nm, when it delivers the power of $ 3.3 \times 10^{-3} $ watt will be $ (h = 6.6 \times 10^{-34} $Js $:
questions.explanation: Given, $$ \begin{aligned}&\lambda=600nm\\&\lambda=600\times10^{-9}m\\&\lambda=6\times10^{-7}m\\&P=3.3\times10^{-3}watt\\ \end{aligned} $$ We know power, $P = \frac{E}{t} = \frac{nhv}{t}$ $$ \bullet \quad \bullet\bullet $$ $$ \frac{n}{t}=\frac{\mathrm{P}}{h\mathrm{v}}=\frac{\mathrm{P}\lambda}{h c} $$ $$ \bullet \quad \bullet\bullet $$ $$ \frac{n}{t}=\frac{3.3\times10^{-3}\times6\times10^{-7}}{6.6\times10^{-34}\times3\times10^{8}} $$ $$ \frac{n}{t}=10^{16} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.topic: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter
questions.subtopic: Particle Nature of Light: The Photon
questions.question: Light with an energy flux of $ 25 \times 10^{4} $ Wm $ ^{-2} $ falls on a perfectly reflecting surface at normal incidence. If the surface area is 15 cm $ ^{2} $, the average force exerted on the surface is:
questions.explanation: We can write, $$ \begin{aligned}\mathrm{F}_{a v}&=\frac{\Delta\mathrm{P}}{\Delta t}\\&=\frac{2\mathrm{I}}{c}\times\mathrm{A}\\&=\frac{2\times25\times10^{4}}{3\times10^{8}}\times15\times10^{-4}\\&=\frac{10\times25\times10^{4}\times10^{-4}}{10^{8}}\\&=2.5\times10^{-6}\mathrm{N}\end{aligned} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.topic: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter
questions.subtopic: Particle Nature of Light: The Photon
questions.question: A 200 W sodium street lamp emits yellow light of wavelength 0.6 μm. Assuming it to be 25% efficient in converting electrical energy to light, the number of photons of yellow light it emits per second is:
questions.explanation: Using the formula, $ P = \frac{N}{t} \times \frac{hc}{\lambda} $ $ = 200 \times 0.25 $ $ \frac{N}{t} = 50 \times \frac{\lambda}{hc} $ $ = \frac{50 \times 0.6 \times 10^{-6}}{6.6 \times 10^{-34} \times 3 \times 10^{8}} $ $ = 1.5 \times 10^{20} $

questions.subject: Physics
questions.topic: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter
questions.subtopic: Particle Nature of Light: The Photon
questions.question: A source S_{1} is producing, 10^{15} photon/s of wavelength 5000\ \mathrm{Å}. Another source S_{2} is producing 1.02 \times 10^{15} photons per second of wavelength 5100\ \mathrm{Å}. Then, ratio of powers of S_{2} and S_{1} is equal to:
questions.explanation: Number of photons emitted per second $$ \begin{aligned}&(1)\text{Number of photons cimbs:}\\&\quad n=\frac{P}{\left(\frac{\hbar c}{\lambda}\right)}\\&\quad\Rightarrow\quad P=\frac{\hbar c\lambda}{\lambda}\\&\quad\Rightarrow\quad\frac{P_{1}}{P_{2}}=\frac{n_{2}\lambda_{1}}{n_{1}\lambda_{2}}=\frac{1.02\times10^{15}\times5000}{10^{15}\times5100}\\&\quad\quad=1\end{aligned} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.topic: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter
questions.subtopic: Particle Nature of Light: The Photon
questions.question: The momentum of a photon of energy 1 MeV in kg m/s will be:
questions.explanation: Energy of photon, $ E = 1 \, MeV $ Momentum of photon, $ p = \frac{E}{c} $ $$ \begin{aligned}p&=\frac{E}{c}=\frac{1\times10^{6}\times1.6\times10^{-19}}{3\times10^{8}}\\&=5\times10^{-22}kg m/s\end{aligned} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.topic: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter
questions.subtopic: Particle Nature of Light: The Photon
questions.question: The value of Planck's constant is:
questions.explanation: The value of planck's constant $$ h=6.63\times10^{-34}J s $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.topic: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter
questions.subtopic: Particle Nature of Light: The Photon
questions.question: Momentum of a photon of wavelength $ \lambda $ is:
questions.explanation: We have, The energy of light E = $ \frac{hc}{\lambda} $ The energy of light $ E = mc^{2} $ From (i) and (ii), we get $$ \begin{array}{l}\frac{hc}{\lambda}=mc^{2}\\\frac{h}{\lambda}=mc=Momentum\end{array} $$ Thus, the momentum of the photon = $ \frac{h}{\lambda} $

questions.subject: Physics
questions.topic: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter
questions.subtopic: Particle Nature of Light: The Photon
questions.question: The wavelength of a 1 keV photon is $ 1.24 \times 10^{-9} $ m. What is the frequency of 1 MeV photon?
questions.explanation: We have, $$ E=\frac{h c}{\lambda} $$ or $ E\lambda = hc = \text{constant} $ Therefore, $ E_{1}\lambda_{1}=E_{2}\lambda_{2} $ $$ 1\times10^{3}eV\times1.24\times10^{-9}=1\times10^{6}eV\times\lambda_{2} $$ Now, We have, $$ \lambda=1.24\times10^{-12} $$ SO. $$ \mathsf{v}=\frac{c}{\lambda} $$ $$ v=2.4\times10^{20}Hz $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.topic: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter
questions.question: An electron and an alpha particle are accelerated by the same potential difference. Let $\lambda_{e}$ and $\lambda_{\alpha}$ denote the de-Broglie wavelength of the electron and the alpha particle, respectively, then:
questions.explanation: To find the ratio between $\lambda_{e}$ to $\lambda_{\alpha}$ $$ \begin{aligned}\frac{\lambda_{e}}{\lambda_{\alpha}}&=\frac{\frac{n}{\sqrt{2m_{e}(2e)\mathrm{V}}}}{\frac{h}{\sqrt{2m_{\alpha}e\mathrm{V}}}}&\\&=\frac{\sqrt{2m_{\alpha}e\mathrm{V}}}{\sqrt{m_{e}e\mathrm{V}}}\\ &=\frac{\sqrt{2(4m_{p})e\mathrm{V}}}{\sqrt{m_{e}e\mathrm{V}}}\\ &=2\sqrt{2}\frac{\sqrt{m_{p}}}{\sqrt{m_{e}}}\\ &=2\sqrt{2}\times42.8\quad\left[\because\sqrt{\frac{m_{p}}{m_{e}}}=42.8\right]\\ &=121\\ \lambda_{e}&=121\lambda_{\alpha}\\ \lambda_{e}&>\lambda_{\alpha}\\ \end{aligned} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.topic: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter
questions.question: An electromagnetic wave of wavelength \lambda' is incident on a photosensitive surface of negligible work function. If m' is mass of photoelectron emitted from the surface that has de-Broglie wavelength \lambda_{d}, then:
questions.explanation: Given, $ Wavelength = \lambda $ $$ \boldsymbol{W}=0 $$ $$ mass=m_{e} $$ de-Broglie wavelength = $ \lambda_{d} $ We know from Einstein's relation Since $$ \begin{aligned}&h\mathbf{v}=\mathbf{W}+\mathbf{K}\mathbf{E}\\&\mathbf{W}=\mathbf{0}\\&h\mathbf{v}=\mathbf{K}\mathbf{E}\\ \end{aligned} $$ From de-Broglie hypothesis we know, $$ \lambda_{d}=\frac{h}{p} $$ and $$ p=\sqrt{2m K E} $$ $$ \bullet \quad \bullet\ast $$ $$ \lambda_{d}=\frac{h}{\sqrt{2mKE}} $$ ∴From eq. (ii), $$ \lambda_{d}^{2}=\frac{h^{2}}{2mKE} $$ $$ \bullet \quad \bullet $$ $$ KE=\frac{h^{2}}{2m\lambda_{d}^{2}} $$ From eq. (i), $$ \frac{h c}{\lambda}=KE $$ ∴ $$ \lambda=\frac{hc}{KE} $$ From eq. (iii) and (iv), $$ \lambda=\frac{hc}{h^{2}}\times2m\lambda_{d}^{2} $$ $$ \lambda=\frac{c}{h}2m\lambda_{d}^{2} $$ $$ \lambda=\left(\frac{2mc}{h}\right)\lambda_{d}^{2} $$ ##### △! Caution The de-broglie wavelength is inversely proportional to momentum $ \lambda \propto \frac{1}{p} $. Some students may assume a direct relationship or overlook the inverse proportionality.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.topic: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter
questions.question: The de-Brogie wavelength of an electron moving with kinetic energy of 144 eV is nearly:
questions.explanation: Kinetic energy of electron $$ \mathrm{K E=144~e V} $$ Means $$ \mathrm{eV}=144~\mathrm{eV} $$ $$ \mathrm{V}=144~\mathrm{V} $$ ∵ de-Broglie wavelength. From eq. (i), $$ \lambda=\frac{12.27}{\sqrt{\mathbf{V}}}\mathring{\mathbf{A}} $$ $$ \begin{aligned}\lambda&=\frac{12.27}{\sqrt{144}}\mathring{A}\\&=\frac{12.27}{12}\mathring{A}=1.02\mathring{A}\\&=102\times10^{-3}nm.\end{aligned} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.topic: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter
questions.question: A proton and \alpha-particle are accelerated from rest to the same energy. The de-Broglie wavelength \lambda_{p} and \lambda_{\alpha} are in the ratio:
questions.explanation: The de-Broglie wavelength of any moving particle associated with momentum, $$ \lambda=\frac{h}{p} $$ where, $h = \mathrm{planck's}$ constant Now $$ K.E.=\frac{1}{2}m v^{2} $$ $$ K.E.=\frac{1}{2m}(mv)^{2}=\frac{p^{2}}{2m} $$ $$ \Rightarrow $$ $$ p=\sqrt{2mKE} $$ ∴ From (i) and (ii), $$ \lambda=\frac{h}{\sqrt{2mKE}} $$ For Proton. $$ \lambda_{P}=\frac{h}{\sqrt{2m_{P}KE_{P}}} $$ For $ \alpha $-particle $$ \lambda_{\alpha}=\frac{h}{\sqrt{2m_{\alpha}\mathrm{KE}_{\alpha}}} $$ From (iii) and (iv), $$ \frac{\lambda_{p}}{\lambda_{\alpha}}=\frac{\sqrt{2m_{\alpha}kE_{\alpha}}}{\sqrt{2m_{p}kE_{p}}} $$ $$\{\because\mathrm{~S i n c e~K E}_{\alpha}=\mathrm{K E}_{\mathrm{P}}\mathrm{~a n d~}m_{\alpha}=4m_{p}\}$$ $$ \frac{\lambda_{p}}{\lambda_{\alpha}}=\sqrt{\frac{4m_{p}}{m_{p}}}=\frac{2}{1} $$ $$ \lambda_{\mathbf{P}}:\lambda_{\alpha}=2:1 $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.topic: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter
questions.question: An electron of mass $m$ with a velocity $\mathbf{v} = \mathbf{v}_0 \hat{i} \left( \mathbf{v}_0 > 0 \right)$ enters an electric field $E = -E_0 \hat{i} \left( E_0 = \text{constant} > 0 \right)$ at $t = 0$. If $\lambda_0$ is its de-Broglie wavelength initially, then its de-Broglie wavelength at time $t$ is:
questions.explanation: From question $$ \mathbf{v}=\mathbf{v}_{0}\hat{i}\quad\mathrm{a n d}\quad\mathbf{E}=-\mathbf{E}_{0}\hat{i} $$ Now initial de-Broglic wavelength is, $$ \lambda_{0}=\frac{h}{m\mathrm{v}_{0}} $$ Acceleration of electron. $$ a=\frac{e\mathrm{E}_{0}}{m} $$ Velocity after $t$ is, $$ \mathbf{v}=\left(\mathbf{v}_{0}+\frac{e\mathbf{E}_{0}}{m}t\right) $$ [This is obtained from, $ v = u + at $] So, $$ \lambda=\frac{h}{m\mathrm{v}}=\frac{h}{m\left(\mathrm{v}_{0}+\frac{e\mathrm{E}_{0}}{m}t\right)} $$ $$ \lambda=\frac{h}{mv_{0}\left(1+\frac{eE_{0}}{mv_{0}}t\right)}=\frac{\lambda_{0}}{\left(1+\frac{eE_{0}}{mv_{0}}t\right)} $$ Thus $$ \lambda=\frac{\lambda_{0}}{\left(1+\frac{e\mathrm{E}_{0}}{m\mathrm{v}_{0}}t\right)} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.topic: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter
questions.question: The de-Broglie wavelength of a neutron in thermal equilibrium with heavy water at a temperature T (Kelvin) and mass m, is:
questions.explanation: de-Broglie wavelength $$ \lambda=\frac{h}{p} $$ $$ \lambda=\frac{h}{\sqrt{2m(KE)}} $$ $$ \lambda=\frac{h}{\sqrt{2m\left(\frac{3}{2}\mathrm{KT}\right)}} $$ $$ \lambda=\frac{h}{\sqrt{3mKT}} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.topic: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter
questions.question: Electrons of mass $m$ with de-Broglie wavelength $\lambda$ fall on the target in an X-rays tube. The cut-off wavelength $(\lambda_0)$ of the emitted X-rays is:
questions.explanation: From de-Broglie equation $$ \lambda=\frac{h}{p} $$ $$ p=\frac{h}{\lambda} $$ $$ \mathrm{E}=\frac{p^{2}}{2m}=\frac{h^{2}}{2m\lambda^{2}} $$ $$ \therefore $$ $$ Also~in~X-ray,\lambda_{0}=\frac{hc}{E} $$ $$ \lambda_{0}=\frac{2mc\lambda^{2}}{h} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.topic: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter
questions.question: An electron of mass $m$ and a photon have same energy E. The ratio of de-Brogie wavelength associated with them is:
questions.explanation: For electron, $$ \lambda_{e}=\frac{h}{\sqrt{2mE}} $$ for photon. $$ \lambda_{ph}=\frac{hc}{E} $$ $$ \begin{array}{l}\frac{\lambda_{e}}{\lambda_{ph}}=\frac{h}{\sqrt{2mE}}\times\frac{\mathrm{E}}{hc}\\=\left(\frac{\mathrm{E}}{2m}\right)^{\frac{1}{2}}\times\frac{1}{c}\end{array} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.topic: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter
questions.question: Light of wavelength 500 nm is incident on a metal with work function 2.28 eV. The de-Broglie wavelength of the emitted electron is:
questions.explanation: Energy of photon, (E) = $ \frac{12400}{5000} $ $ = 2.48 \, eV $ Work function, W = 2.28 eV Using Einstein's photoelectric equation $$ \mathbf{E}=\mathbf{W}+\mathbf{K}\mathbf{E}_{max} $$ $$ 2.48=2.28+(\mathrm{K.E.})_{\max} $$ $$ \therefore\quad(K.E.)_{\max}=0.20\ eV $$ for electron. $$ \lambda=\frac{h}{\sqrt{2mE}} $$ ⇔ $$ \lambda=28\textup{\AA} $$ So $$ \lambda\geq2.8\times10^{-9}\mathrm{m} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.topic: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter
questions.question: If the kinetic energy of the particle is increased to 16 times its previous value, the percentage change in the de-Broglie wavelength of the particle is:
questions.explanation: Using de-Broglie hypothesis, $$ \lambda=\frac{h}{p} $$ Hence, $$ \lambda_{1}=\frac{h}{p}=\frac{h}{\sqrt{2mK}} $$ and $$ \begin{array}{l}\lambda_{2}=\frac{h}{2m\times16K}\\=\frac{h}{4\sqrt{2mK}}=\frac{\lambda_{1}}{4}\end{array} $$ Using eq. (i) ∴ $$ \lambda_{2}=\frac{1}{4}\times\lambda_{1}=25\%of\lambda_{1} $$ % Change in wavelength $$ \begin{aligned}&=\frac{\lambda_{1}-\lambda_{2}}{\lambda_{1}}\times100=1-\frac{\lambda_{2}}{\lambda_{1}}\times100\\&=75\%\end{aligned} $$ Thus, 75% changes in the wavelength.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.topic: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter
questions.question: The de-Broglie wavelength of neutrons in thermal equilibrium at temperature T is:
questions.explanation: Using de-Broglie wavelength, $$ \begin{aligned}\lambda&=\frac{\sqrt[n]{2m\mathrm{K_{B}}\mathrm{T}}}{\quad}\\ \lambda&=\frac{6.63\times10^{-34}}{0.83\times10^{-2}\times2\times1.6\times10^{-19}\times0.25}\\ &=\frac{3.08\times10^{-34}\times10^{25}}{\sqrt{\mathrm{T}}}\mathrm{m}=\frac{30.8}{\sqrt{\mathrm{T}}}\mathring{A}\end{aligned} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.topic: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter
questions.question: If the momentum of an electron is changed by p, then the de-Brogie wavelength associated with it changes by 0.5%. The initial momentum of electron will be:
questions.explanation: From de-Broglie hypothesis $$ \left|\frac{\Delta p}{p}\right|=\frac{\Delta\lambda}{\lambda} $$ $$ \frac{\Delta p}{p_{i}}=\frac{\Delta\lambda}{\lambda} $$ $$ p_{i}=\frac{p}{\underline{0.5}} $$ $$ p_{i}=\frac{1000}{5}p=200p $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.topic: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter
questions.question: An α-particle moves in a circular path of radius 0.83 cm in the presence of a magnetic field of 0.25 Wb/m². The de-Broglie wavelength associated with the particle will be:
questions.explanation: We know that, $$ \mathrm{R}=\frac{m\mathrm{v}}{q\mathrm{B}}\text{and}\lambda=\frac{h}{m\mathrm{v}} $$ $$ \begin{aligned}\lambda&=\frac{h}{mv}=\frac{h}{RqB}\\\lambda&=\frac{6.63\times10^{-34}}{0.83\times10^{-2}\times2\times1.6\times10^{-19}\times0.25}\\&=0.01\mathring{A}\end{aligned} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.topic: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter
questions.question: Electrons used in an electron microscope are accelerated by a voltage of 25 kV. If the voltage is increased to 100 kV, then the de-Broglie wavelength associated with the electrons would:
questions.explanation: We know that, $ \lambda = \frac{12.27}{\sqrt{\mathrm{v}}} $ $$ \begin{array}{ll}\Rightarrow&\frac{\lambda_{1}}{\lambda_{2}}=\sqrt{\frac{\mathrm{V}_{2}}{\mathrm{V}_{1}}}\\ \Rightarrow&\lambda_{2}=\lambda_{1}\sqrt{\frac{\mathrm{V}_{1}}{\mathrm{V}_{2}}\\\therefore&\lambda_{2}=\lambda_{1}\sqrt{\frac{25}{100}}\\\Rightarrow&\lambda_{2}=\lambda_{1}\sqrt{\frac{1}{4}}=\frac{\lambda_{1}}{2}\end{array} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.topic: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter
questions.question: A particle of mass 1 mg has the same wavelength as an electron moving with a velocity $ 3 \times 10^{6} \, \text{ms}^{-1} $. The velocity of the particle is:
questions.explanation: de-Broglie wavelength, associated with electron moving with velocity v, $$ \begin{aligned}\lambda&=\frac{h}{m\mathrm{v}}\\\lambda_{e}&=\frac{h}{9.1\times10^{-31}\times3\times10^{6}}\end{aligned} $$ So. Wavelength of particle of mass 1 mg moving with velocity v, $$ \lambda_{p}=\frac{h}{10^{-3}\times\mathrm{v}} $$ $$ \begin{array}{l} \text{As given,} \quad \lambda_{e} =\lambda_{p} \\ \Rightarrow \quad \frac{h}{10^{-6} \times \text{v}} =\frac{h}{9.1 \times 10^{-31} \times 3 \times 10^{6}} \\ = 2.7 \times 10^{-18} \text{ m/s} \end{array} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.topic: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter
questions.question: If particles are moving with the same velocity, then the de Broglie wavelength is maximum for:
questions.explanation: de-Broglie wavelength is maximum for $ \beta $-particle.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.topic: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter
questions.question: The wavelength associated with an electron, accelerated through a potential difference of 100 V, is of the order of:
questions.explanation: $$ \begin{aligned}\lambda&=\frac{h}{\sqrt{2meV}}&\\&=\frac{6.63\times10^{-34}}{\sqrt{2\times9.1\times10^{-31}\times1.6\times10^{-19}\times100}}\\&=1.227\times10^{-10}=1.227\mathring{A}\\ \end{aligned} $$ This wavelength corresponds to X-ray region of EM spectrum.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.topic: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter
questions.question: The de-Broglie wave corresponding to a particle of mass $m$ and velocity $v$ has a wavelength associated with it:
questions.explanation: The de-Broglie wavelength associated with the particle of mass m moving with velocity v is, $$ \begin{aligned}\lambda&=\frac{h}{p}\\\lambda&=\frac{h}{mv}\end{aligned} $$ where, m and v are the mass and the velocity of the particle and h is Planck's constant.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.subtopic: Alpha-Particle Scattering and Rutherford's Nuclear Model of Atom
questions.question: When an $ \alpha $-particle of mass m moving with velocity v bombards on a heavy nucleus of charge Ze, its distance of closest approach from the nucleus depends on m as:
questions.explanation: At distance of closest approach, the kinetic energy of the particle will convert completely into electrostatic potential energy. Here, $$ K.E=\frac{1}{2}m v^{2} $$ Electrostatic potential energy, $$ \begin{aligned}U&=\frac{K Q q}{d}\\ &=K\frac{(2e)(Z e)}{d}\end{aligned} $$ $$ \frac{1}{2}m\mathbf{v}^{2}=\mathbf{K}\frac{(2e)(\mathbf{Z}e)}{d} $$ $$ d\propto\frac{1}{m} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.subtopic: Alpha-Particle Scattering and Rutherford's Nuclear Model of Atom
questions.question: An alpha nucleus of energy $\frac{1}{2}mv^{2}$ bombards a heavy nuclear target of charge Ze. Then the distance of closest approach for the alpha nucleus will be proportional to:
questions.explanation: The energy of projectile is directly proportional to $ Z_{1}Z_{2} $.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.subtopic: Alpha-Particle Scattering and Rutherford's Nuclear Model of Atom
questions.question: In a Rutherford scattering experiment when a projectile charge $Z_{1}$ and mass $M_{1}$ approaches a target nucleus of charge $Z_{2}$ and mass $M_{2}$, the distance of closest approach is $r_{0}$. The energy of the projectile is:
questions.explanation: From question $$ \frac{1}{2}m\mathbf{v}^{2}=\frac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_{0}}\frac{(2e)(Z e)}{r_{0}} $$ $$ r_{0}=\frac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_{0}}\frac{2Z e^{2}}{\frac{1}{2}m\mathbf{v}^{2}} $$ $$ r_{0}\propto\frac{1}{m} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.subtopic: Bohr Model of the Hydrogen Atom
questions.question: The radius of inner most orbit of hydrogen atom is $5.3 \times 10^{-11}$ m. What is the radius of third allowed orbit of hydrogen atom?

questions.subject: Physics
questions.subtopic: Bohr Model of the Hydrogen Atom
questions.question: Let $T_{1}$ and $T_{2}$ be the energy of an electron in the first and second excited states of hydrogen atom, respectively. According to the Bohr's model of an atom, the ratio $T_{1}:T_{2}$ is:
questions.explanation: For first excited state, $ n_{1}=2 $ For second excited state, $ n_{2}=3 $ $$ \frac{T_{1}}{T_{2}}=\frac{n_{2}^{2}}{n_{1}^{2}}=\frac{3^{2}}{2^{2}}=\frac{9}{4} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.subtopic: Bohr Model of the Hydrogen Atom
questions.question: The total energy of an electron in the $ n^{\text{th}} $ stationary orbit of the hydrogen atom can be obtained by:
questions.explanation: The energy of an electron in Bohr's orbit of hydrogen atom is given by the expression, $$ \mathrm{E}_{n}=-\frac{2\pi^{2}m e^{4}Z^{2}}{n^{2}h^{2}(4\pi\varepsilon_{0})^{2}}=-13.6\frac{Z^{2}}{n^{2}}\mathbf{e}\mathbf{V} $$ Since, Z = 1 for hydrogen atom. So, the above equation further simplifies to, $$ \mathrm{E}_{n}=-\frac{13.6}{n^{2}}\mathrm{~e V} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.subtopic: Bohr Model of the Hydrogen Atom
questions.question: For which one of the following, Bohr model is NOT valid?
questions.explanation: Bohr's model is not valid for singly ionised neon atom $ \mathrm{(Ne^{+})} $.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.subtopic: Bohr Model of the Hydrogen Atom
questions.question: The total energy of an electron in an orbit is -3.4 eV. Its kinetic and potential energies are, respectively:
questions.explanation: In Bohr's model of H-atom, $$ \mathbf{K.E}=|\mathbf{T E}|=\frac{|\mathbf{U}|}{2} $$ $$ \mathrm{K.E=3.4~e V} $$ $$ \mathrm{U}=-6.8\;{\mathrm{e V}} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.subtopic: Bohr Model of the Hydrogen Atom
questions.question: The radius of the first permitted Bohr orbit for the electron, in a hydrogen atom equals to 0.51 Å and its ground state energy equals to -13.6 eV. If the electron in the hydrogen atom is replaced by muon (μ⁻) [Charge same as electron and mass is 207 mₑ], the first Bohr radius and ground state energy will be:
questions.explanation: $$ \begin{array}{c}{m_{\upmu}=207~m_{\mathrm{e}}}\\ {r=0.51~\mathring{\mathrm{A}}}\end{array} $$ $$ r_{n}\propto\frac{1}{m} $$ $$ r_{1}=\frac{0.51\times10^{-10}}{207}=2.56\times10^{-13}m $$ Since $$ \mathrm{E}\propto m_{e} $$ $$ \circ\circ $$ $$ \mathrm{E}_{1}=-13.6\times207=-2.8\mathrm{k e V} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.subtopic: Bohr Model of the Hydrogen Atom
questions.question: The ratio of kinetic energy to the total energy of an electron in a Bohr orbit of the hydrogen atom is:
questions.explanation: We have that, KE = -(total energy) So, kinetic energy: total energy = 1 : - 1

questions.subject: Physics
questions.subtopic: Bohr Model of the Hydrogen Atom
questions.question: An electron in hydrogen atom makes a transition $n_{1} \to n_{2}$ where, $n_{1}$ and $n_{2}$ are principal quantum numbers of the two states. Assuming Bohr's model to be valid, the time period of the electron in the initial state is eight times that in the final state. The possible values of $n_{1}$ and $n_{2}$ are:
questions.explanation: Time period of an electron in Bohr orbit is, then $$ \begin{aligned}\mathrm{T}&=\frac{4\varepsilon_{0}^{2}h^{3}n^{3}}{m e^{4}}\\\mathrm{T}&\propto n^{3}\\\frac{\mathrm{T}_{1}}{\mathrm{T}_{2}}&=\frac{n_{1}^{3}}{n_{2}^{3}}\\\mathrm{T}_{1}&=8\mathrm{T}_{2}\\\left(\frac{8\mathrm{T}_{2}}{\mathrm{T}_{2}}\right)&=\frac{n_{1}^{3}}{n_{2}^{3}}\\\left(\frac{n_{1}}{n_{2}}\right)^{3}&=(2^{3})\\\frac{n_{1}}{n_{2}}&=2\\n_{1}&=2n_{2}\end{aligned} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.subtopic: Bohr Model of the Hydrogen Atom
questions.question: An electron of a stationary hydrogen atom passes from the fifth energy level to the ground level. The velocity that the atom of mass $m$ acquired as a result of photon emission will be: (R = Rydberg constant and $h = \text{Planck's constant}$)
questions.explanation: According to the question, Electron Electron passes from fifth energy level to ground level means, $$ \begin{aligned}\mathrm{E}_{5}-\mathrm{E}_{1}&=\frac{hc}{\lambda}\\\Longrightarrow\quad\frac{\mathrm{Rh}c}{25}-\mathrm{Rh}c&=\frac{hc}{\lambda}\\\Longrightarrow\quad\frac{24}{25}\mathrm{R}&=\frac{1}{\lambda}\end{aligned} $$ But $$ p=\frac{h}{\lambda} $$ and $$ \mathbf{v}=\frac{\boldsymbol{h}}{m\lambda}=\frac{24}{25}\frac{\mathbf{R}\boldsymbol{h}}{m} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.subtopic: Bohr Model of the Hydrogen Atom
questions.question: The energy of a hydrogen atom in the ground state is -13.6 eV. The energy of a He $ ^{+} $ion in the first excited state will be:
questions.explanation: We know that, $ E = \frac{-13.6}{n^{2}} Z^{2} $ for first excited state n = 2 and for $ \mathrm{He}^{+} $, Z = 2 $$ \mathrm{E}=\frac{-13.6}{2^{2}}\times2^{2}=-13.6\mathrm{eV} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.subtopic: Bohr Model of the Hydrogen Atom
questions.question: The electron in the hydrogen atom jumps from excited state $(n=3)$ to its ground state $(n=1)$ and the photons thus emitted irradiate a photosensitive material. If the work function of the material is $5.1\ \mathrm{eV}$, the stopping potential is estimated to be (the energy of the electron in $n^{\mathrm{th}}$ state $E_{n}=-\frac{13.6}{n^{2}}\mathrm{eV}$):
questions.explanation: For n = 1, $ \mathrm{E}_{1} = -\frac{13.6}{(1)^{2}} = -13.6\,\mathrm{eV} $ For n = 3, $$ \mathrm{E}_{3}=-\frac{13.6}{\left(3\right)^{2}}=-1.51\mathrm{eV} $$ So, required energy $$ \begin{aligned}\mathrm{E}&=\mathrm{E}_{3}-\mathrm{E}_{1}\\&=-\left(1.51\right)-\left(-13.6\right)=12.09\mathrm{eV}\end{aligned} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.subtopic: Bohr Model of the Hydrogen Atom
questions.question: The ionization energy of the electron in the hydrogen atom in its ground state is 13.6 eV. The atoms are excited to higher energy levels to emit radiations of 6 wavelengths. Maximum wavelength:
questions.explanation: We know that, Number of spectral lines, $$ \begin{aligned}\mathrm{N}&=\frac{n(n-1)}{2}\\\frac{n(n-1)}{2}&=6\\\Rightarrow n^{2}-n-12&=0\\\Rightarrow(n-4)(n+3)&=0\\\Rightarrow n&=4\end{aligned} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.subtopic: Bohr Model of the Hydrogen Atom
questions.question: The ground state energy of hydrogen atom is -13.6 eV. When its electron is in the first excited state, its excitation energy is:
questions.explanation: Ground state energy of hydrogen atom $$ E_{1}=-13.6eV $$ Energy of electron in first excited state $ (n=2) $ $$ \mathrm{E}_{2}=-\frac{13.6}{\left(2\right)^{2}}\mathrm{eV} $$ ∴ Excitation energy, $$ \begin{aligned}\Delta E&=E_{2}-E_{1}\\&=-\frac{13.6}{4}-(-13.6)\\&=-3.4+13.6=10.2eV\end{aligned} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.subtopic: Bohr Model of the Hydrogen Atom
questions.question: The total energy of electron in the ground state of hydrogen atom is -13.6 eV. The kinetic energy of an electron in the first excited state is:
questions.explanation: Energy of $ n^{\text{th}} $ orbit of hydrogen atom is given by, $$ \mathrm{E}_{n}=-\frac{13.6}{n^{2}}\mathrm{eV} $$ for ground state, n = 1 $$ \mathrm{E}_{1}=-\frac{13.6}{1^{2}}=-13.6\mathrm{eV} $$ $$ \mathrm{E}_{2}=-\frac{13.6}{2^{2}}=-3.4\,\mathrm{eV} $$ $$ \mathbf{K}\mathbf{E}=|\mathbf{E}_{2}|=3.4~e\mathbf{V} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.subtopic: Bohr Model of the Hydrogen Atom
questions.question: The total energy of an electron in the first excited state of hydrogen atom is about -3.4 eV. Its kinetic energy in this state is:
questions.explanation: $$ K.E.=\left|\frac{1}{2}P.E\right| $$ But P.E is negative, $$ \begin{array}{r l}{\therefore\quad\mathrm{T o t a l~e n e r g y}=\left|\frac{1}{2}\mathrm{P.E}\right|-{\mathrm{P.E}}}\\ {\quad=\left|\frac{1}{2}\mathrm{P.E}\right|=-3.4\mathrm{e V}}\\ {\quad}\\ {\mathrm{K~E}=+3.4\mathrm{e V}}\end{array} $$ $$ \mathrm{K.E=+3.4e V} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.subtopic: Bohr Model of the Hydrogen Atom
questions.question: The Bohr model of atom:
questions.optionA: assumes that the angular momentum of electrons is quantised
questions.optionC: predicts continuous emission spectra for atoms
questions.optionD: predicts the same emission spectra for all types of atoms
questions.explanation: The Bohr model of atom assumes that the angular momentum of electrons is quantised.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.subtopic: Bohr Model of the Hydrogen Atom
questions.question: When hydrogen atom is in its first excited level, its radius is:
questions.optionA: four times its ground state radius
questions.optionC: same as its ground state radius
questions.optionD: half of its ground state radius
questions.explanation: The radius of $ n^{\text{th}} $ orbit of hydrogen, $$ r_{n}=\frac{\varepsilon_{0}n^{2}h^{2}}{\pi m e^{2}Z} $$ $$ r_{n}=\frac{n^{2}a_{0}}{Z}\text{or}r_{n}\propto\frac{n^{2}}{Z} $$ For ground state, $n=1$ Atomic number, $Z = 1$ For first excited state, $n = 2$ $$ \frac{r_{2}}{r_{1}}=\left(\frac{2}{1}\right)^{2}=4 $$ $$ r_{2}=4r_{1} $$ Hence, radius of first excited state is four times the radius of ground state.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.subtopic: Bohr Model of the Hydrogen Atom
questions.question: Consider an electron in the n^{\mathrm{th}} orbit of a hydrogen atom in the Bohr model. The circumference of the orbit can be expressed in terms of de-Broglie wavelength \lambda of that electron as:
questions.explanation: In terms of the wavelength of the wave corresponding with an electron, the circumference of an orbit in an atom is given by, $$ 2\pi r_{n}=n\lambda\quad[r_{n}=\text{radius of any }n\text{ orbit}] $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.subtopic: Bohr Model of the Hydrogen Atom
questions.question: To explain his theory, Bohr used:
questions.explanation: Bohr used the concept of quantization of angular momentum. For stationary orbits, Angular momentum, $$ L=\frac{nh}{2\pi} $$ where, n = 1, 2, 3, ..., etc.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.subtopic: The Line Spectra of the Hydrogen Atom
questions.question: Some energy levels of a molecule are shown in the figure with their wavelengths of transitions. Then: $$ \lambda_{3}>\lambda_{2},\lambda_{1}=2\lambda_{2} $$
questions.explanation: $$ \mathrm{E}=\frac{h c}{\lambda}\Rightarrow\lambda=\frac{h c}{\mathrm{E}}\Rightarrow\lambda\propto\frac{1}{\mathrm{E}} $$ Energy of photon of wavelength $ \lambda_{1} $ $$ \Delta\mathrm{E}_{1}=-\left(\frac{5\mathrm{E}}{2}\right)+4\mathrm{E}=\frac{3\mathrm{E}}{2} $$ Energy of photon of wavelength $ \lambda_{2} $, $$ \Delta\mathrm{E}_{2}=-2\mathrm{E}-(-3\mathrm{E}) $$ $$ \Delta\mathrm{E}_{2}=-2\mathrm{E}+3\mathrm{E}=\mathrm{E} $$ Energy of photon of wavelength $ \lambda_{3} $, $$ \Delta\mathrm{E}_{3}=-2\mathrm{E}-(-4\mathrm{E}) $$ $$ =2\mathrm{E} $$ So. $$ \lambda_{2}=2\lambda_{3} $$ $$ \lambda_{2}>\lambda_{1} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.subtopic: The Line Spectra of the Hydrogen Atom
questions.question: The spectral series which corresponds to the electronic transition from the levels $n_{2}=5,6,\ldots$ to the level $n_{1}=4$ is:
questions.explanation: The spectral series given in the question shows the electronic transitions from higher energy levels ( $ n_{2}=5,6,\ldots $) to the level ( $ n_{1}=4 $), this series is the Brackett series.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.subtopic: The Line Spectra of the Hydrogen Atom
questions.question: In hydrogen spectrum, the shortest wavelength in the Balmer series is $\lambda$. The shortest wavelength in the Brackett series is:
questions.explanation: Shortest wavelength in Balmer series is $ \lambda $. For Balmer series, $ n_{1}=2 $, $ n_{2}=\infty $ $$ \begin{aligned}We know,\frac{1}{\lambda}&=R\left(\frac{1}{n_{1}^{2}}-\frac{1}{n_{2}^{2}}\right)\\\frac{1}{\lambda}&=\frac{R}{2^{2}}\end{aligned} $$ For Bracket series, $ n_{1}=4 $, $ n_{2}=\infty $ So, $$ \begin{aligned}\frac{1}{\lambda^{\prime}}&=\mathbf{R}\left(\frac{1}{n_{1}^{2}}-\frac{1}{n_{2}^{2}}\right)\\\frac{1}{\lambda^{\prime}}&=\frac{\mathbf{R}}{4^{2}}\end{aligned} $$ Divide eq (i) by (ii), we get we get $$ \frac{\lambda^{\prime}}{\lambda}=\frac{1\times4^{2}}{2^{2}}=4 $$ $$ \lambda^{\prime}=4\lambda $$ #### ! Caution Students are often confused in finding the correct option. For it just find out the relation of the physical quantity and calculate the answer.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.subtopic: The Line Spectra of the Hydrogen Atom
questions.question: If an electron in a hydrogen atom jumps from the $3^{\mathrm{rd}}$ orbit to the $2^{\mathrm{nd}}$ orbit, it emits a photon of wavelength $\lambda$. When it jumps from the $4^{\mathrm{th}}$ orbit to the $3^{\mathrm{rd}}$ orbit, the corresponding wavelength of the photon will be:
questions.explanation: Using Rydberg formula, we have, $$ \begin{aligned}\frac{1}{\lambda}&=R\left(\frac{1}{n_{1}^{2}}-\frac{1}{n_{2}^{2}}\right)\\&=R\left(\frac{1}{2^{2}}-\frac{1}{3^{2}}\right)=\frac{5R}{36}\end{aligned} $$ and $$ \frac{1}{\lambda^{\prime}}=R\left(\frac{1}{3^{2}}-\frac{1}{4^{2}}\right)=\frac{7R}{144} $$ On dividing eqns. (i) by (ii), we get $$ \begin{aligned}\frac{\lambda^{\prime}}{\lambda}&=\frac{5R}{36}\times\frac{144}{7R}=\frac{20}{7}\\\lambda^{\prime}&=\frac{20}{7}\lambda\end{aligned} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.subtopic: The Line Spectra of the Hydrogen Atom
questions.question: The transition from the state $n=3$ to $n=1$ in a hydrogen-like atom results in ultraviolet radiation. Infrared radiation will be obtained in the transition from:
questions.explanation: Infrared radiation found in Paschen, Bracket and pfund series and it is obtain when electron transitions from high energy level to minimum $3^{rd}$ energy level.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.subtopic: The Line Spectra of the Hydrogen Atom
questions.question: Electron in hydrogen atom first jumps from third excited state to second excited state and then from second excited to the first excited state. The ratio of the wavelengths $\lambda_{1}$ and $\lambda_{2}$ emitted in the two cases is:
questions.explanation: We know that, $$ \begin{array}{c}n=4\\\downarrow\quad\mathrm{E}_{1}=\frac{hc}{\lambda_{1}}\\\downarrow\quad\mathrm{E}_{2}=\frac{hc}{\lambda_{2}}\\\downarrow\quad n=2\end{array} $$ Energy $$ \mathrm{E}=\frac{h c}{\lambda}=13.6\left[\frac{1}{n_{2}^{2}}-\frac{1}{n_{1}^{2}}\right] $$ According to the question, $$ \mathrm{E}_{1}=\frac{hc}{\lambda_{1}}=13.6\left[\frac{1}{3^{2}}-\frac{1}{4^{2}}\right] $$ And $$ \mathrm{E}_{2}=\frac{hc}{\lambda_{2}}=13.6\left[\frac{1}{2^{2}}-\frac{1}{3^{2}}\right] $$ On dividing eq. (ii) by (i), we get $$ \begin{aligned}\frac{\lambda_{1}}{\lambda_{2}}&=\frac{\frac{1}{4}-\frac{1}{9}}{\frac{1}{9}-\frac{1}{16}}=\frac{20}{7}\\&=20:7\end{aligned} $$

questions.subject: Physics
